title: The Dental Visit: Bridging Patients to Mental Healthcare Through Connection
description:
The U.S. faces a growing mental health crisis, fueled by post-pandemic isolation and loneliness. As a dental professional, you play a vital role in recognizing signs of mental health concerns and guiding patients to life-changing resources. In this dentist-led presentation, experts from multiple health care fields will equip you with practical tools to identify issues during medical history updates or patient interactions, respond with empathy, and confidently refer patients—even if you’re unfamiliar with mental health networks. You’ll gain communication strategies and collaboration skills to connect patients with the help they need.
Learning Objectives:
Recognize the nation’s mental health crisis and learn of the U.S. Surgeon General’s public health advisory on the importance of social connection for individual health, and for clinicians to ask brief questions about loneliness and connection
Understand the role of empathy and adopt the best communication practices when addressing a patient presenting mental health needs
Implement questions during a medical update that will open conversations about mental health
Know how and when to use appropriate and validated tools for screening and learn about the science behind them, such as the Patient Health Questionnaire-2 (PHQ-2)
Learn how to refer patients to mental health resources, including starting points, communication best practices, and integration models used in clinics
This presentation, recorded in January 2026, was made possible through the generous support of the ADA’s Council on Dental Practice (CDP) and the Dental Team Wellness Advisory Committee (DWAC), in their continued effort to prioritize mental health and wellness for dental professionals and patients.

title: Putting Your Oxygen Mask on First: Prioritizing Health and Well-Being in Dentistry
description:
Dentistry is a profession devoted to improving health, yet its demands can take a toll on clinicians’ physical and emotional well-being. Wellness is more than a personal concern —it is essential for workforce stability and patient safety. Like other healthcare professionals, dental teams are vulnerable to burnout, anxiety and depression.
This course explores current data on dental professionals’ wellness and provides actionable strategies for self-care. Discover how the ADA champions mental health, combats stigma and offers resources to help you thrive in practice.
Learning Objectives
Recognize the importance of safeguarding your mental and physical health
Examine ADA’s advocacy efforts to support dental workforce well-being
Understand the significance of Health Workforce Well-Being Day and ADA’s ongoing initiatives
Explore the Dental Well-Being Index (WBI), an anonymous and validated tool developed by Mayo Clinic to assess well-being across six domains
Access ergonomic and mental health resources available at ADA.org/Wellness

title: Sports Dentistry: An Introduction to Interdisciplinary Dentistry for Sports Medicine
description:
Sports Dentistry in the United States continues to advance through technological innovations and its growing integration with Sports Medicine. This specialized field plays a critical role in safeguarding athletes’ oral health, offering an added layer of healthcare oversight and providing an added scope of access to care for sports athletes and exercise participants alike.
Sports Dentistry, a branch of Sports Medicine, focuses on the prevention, diagnosis, and treatment of dental injuries and oral conditions associated with athletic activity. It supports athletes and teams across all age groups and levels of participation. The Academy for Sports Dentistry has long championed progress in this area, driving improvements through emerging technologies and deeper collaboration with Sports Medicine.
Learning Objectives:
Examine the relationship between sports and oral health by addressing access to essential dental care for athletes. Review age-specific risk factors and community needs, understand varying levels of sports participation and oversight, and explore how sports dentistry collaborates with sports medicine and athletic training teams to provide comprehensive care.
Develop an understanding of dental injuries associated with sports and exercise, including essential triage principles. Review major categories of dental trauma—such as tooth injuries, alveolar hard tissue involvement, and combined injuries—and examine the concept of dental triage following medical triage and sports medicine protocols.
Explain the role of protective sports mouthguards in athlete safety, emphasizing the design and use of customized, professionally fitted appliances for prescriptive fit. Review available mouthguard types—from generic and semi-fitted to fully customized designs—and evaluate their advantages and disadvantages as communicated by sports dentistry professionals.
Understand the principles of oral health advocacy and current guideline standards for sports dental appliances and i

title: Beyond the Mirror: Body Dysmorphic Disorder and Its Impact on Dental Care
description:
Explore your role as a dental professional in recognizing and managing body dysmorphic disorder (BDD) in your patients, particularly those seeking esthetic procedures. Accurate identification of BDD is essential due to ethical and safety concerns, including the prevention of overtreatment and dissatisfaction with outcomes. Drawing from a systematic review, the course provides insights into patient characteristics, screening tools and strategies for informed decision-making. You will learn how to mitigate medicolegal risks while enhancing patient care.
Learning Objectives:
Define body dysmorphic disorder (BDD) and explain its relevance in dental care.
Identify key characteristics and prevalence trends of BDD among dental patients.
Recognize ethical and clinical challenges associated with esthetic treatment in patients with BDD.
Apply screening tools and referral strategies to support informed, safe and ethical care.

title: Beyond Blood Sugar: Understanding Type-1 Diabetes in Dental Practice (1.5 CE)
description:
Type 1 diabetes presents unique challenges for dental care, requiring a clear understanding of not just its systemic and oral health implications but also its life experience. This course reviews the autoimmune nature of the disease, its impact on oral health, and the considerations for safe and effective treatment for this patient population. Participants will learn evidence-based strategies for risk assessment, glycemic management during procedures, and emergency preparedness, while emphasizing collaboration with medical teams. The goal is to equip dental professionals with the knowledge and confidence to deliver comprehensive care that supports both oral health and overall well-being for patients living with Type 1 diabetes.
Learning Objectives:
Describe the autoimmune nature and pathophysiology of Type 1 diabetes and its relevance to dental care
Recognize oral manifestations and systemic complications associated with Type 1 diabetes
Evaluate risk factors and implement evidence-based preventative measures to support this population
Implement protocols for glycemic management during routine dental visits and reduce the risk of emergencies during dental procedures
Integrate interdisciplinary collaboration to optimize outcomes for patients living with Type 1 diabetes.
Review care approaches for pediatric and adolescent patients with Type 1 diabetes, considering developmental and behavioral factors
Examine the role of technology, including continuous glucose monitors and Insulin pumps, and their role in supporting glucose management

title: The Biofilm Breakdown: How It Fuels Periodontal Disease and What to Do About It
description:
Biofilm isn’t just plaque—it’s an organized microbial army waging war on your patients’ periodontal health. This fast-paced, practical course equips you with the latest tools and techniques to break down biofilm, enhance treatment outcomes, and promote long-term periodontal stability. From air polishing and biofilm therapy to lasers and chemotherapeutics, you’ll dive into real-world applications that you can immediately implement in your practice. Say goodbye to the same old scraping and scaling—this session will change the way you think about biofilm management and periodontal care.
Learning Objectives
Understand what biofilm really is—how it forms and why it’s the key driver of periodontal disease
Go beyond traditional scaling, build on conventional periodontal treatments with cutting-edge biofilm management techniques
Upgrade your toolbox, explore the latest advancements in air polishing, biofilm therapy, chemotherapeutics and laser technology for superior patient care
Create a Biofilm Battle Plan: Learn how to integrate effective biofilm control strategies into daily practice to improve patient outcomes and prevent disease recurrence

title: Pediatric Dentistry Essentials: Confident Care and Strategies for Young Smiles
description:
Designed for both general dentists and pediatric specialists, this dynamic, on-demand course will strengthen clinical decision-making and treatment planning skills, empowering you to deliver safe, effective and empathetic care tailored to the unique needs of young patients.
This evidence-based course equips you with strategies for managing urgent situations, preserving pulpal vitality and addressing conditions like molar-incisor hypomineralization.
Learning Objectives:
Explore the principles behind managing urgent dental situations in children
Recognize the importance of diagnosing and managing molar-incisor hypomineralization
Learn how to preserve pulpal vitality using minimally invasive techniques

title: Tiny Smiles: The Importance of the Age 1 Visit
description:
Discover how early dental care can shape a lifetime of healthy smiles. This innovative course equips you with essential knowledge and practical tools—guided by expert insights—to confidently deliver the age 1 dental visit and engage caregivers in meaningful ways. By the end of this course, you’ll be empowered to perform high-quality assessments, offer tailored guidance, and make effective referrals for infants and toddlers—ensuring every child gets the best start to lifelong oral health.
Learning Objectives:
Learn the purpose and importance of the age 1 dental visit, conduct anticipatory guidance and risk assessments and best practices for referring to dental specialists
Acquire skills and confidence to conduct age 1 dental exams such as knee to knee exams with caregivers
Develop effective communication strategies to educate & communicate proven prevention methods & promote oral health practices tailored to caregivers with young kids

title: ADA Clinical Recommendations: Optimizing Radiation Safety in Dentistry | Patient Selection for Dental Radiography and Cone Beam Computed Tomography
description:
This course reviews the ADA’s first updated dental X-ray recommendations in over a decade, offering guidance on when 2-D and 3-D CBCT imaging is clinically necessary to reduce radiation exposure. Developed by an expert panel and endorsed by the AAOMR, these evidence-based guidelines expand upon the 2012 ADA/FDA recommendations and provide scenario-based direction for patient care. This course includes information on diagnostic imaging, following the 2024 recommendations on radiation safety and regulatory issues.
Learning Objectives:
To review best practice for radiation safety and protection.
To provide an update on patient shielding recommendations based on the current evidence.
To discuss recommended imaging modalities and frequency of radiographic examinations for the most common clinical indications in a general and pediatric dentistry office.

title: Pediatric Dental Anxiety and Pain: Understanding, Assessing, and Managing
description:
Dental anxiety in children can significantly impact treatment outcomes and long-term oral health. This course provides a comprehensive approach to understanding the drivers of pediatric dental anxiety and assessing its impact on care. You will learn evidence-based pain management strategies and explore both behavioral and pharmacologic tools to reduce fear and discomfort. Emphasis is placed on creating an emotionally safe environment that fosters trust and positive experiences for young patients.
Learning Objectives
Understand drivers of pediatric dental anxiety
Apply evidence-based pain management strategies
Integrate behavioral & pharmacologic tools
Deliver emotionally safe care

title: Wellness Reboot: 10 Health Topics to Discuss with Your Patients
description:
When patients seek ways to improve their health, ensure you're prepared to help them with the necessary lifestyle changes. In this course, you will learn how to confidently discuss nutrition, smoking cessation, vaping, trends in weight loss and preventative and screening services like hypertension, diabetes and HPV prevention. You will also understand these issues' impact on oral health.
Learning Objectives
Learn how to talk to your patient about quitting smoking and how to make smoking cessation referrals using tools provided by the ADA
Explain impact of artificial sweeteners on oral health
Understand and convey the oral health risks/benefits of GLP-1s
Learn how to screen for hypertension/high blood pressure, then make appropriate references if treatment is needed
Implement vaccinations in your office and educate patients on the importance of each
Discuss weight loss tools and their impact on oral health
Understand how to start conversations about mental health and substance use disorders

title: Unconscious Bias: Knowing What You Don’t Know
description:
Unconscious Bias is a learned stereotype that is automatic, unintentional, deeply ingrained, universal and able to influence behavior. Unconscious bias seeps into decisions that affect recruitment, retention, hiring, access to healthcare, banking, housing, education, the justice system, providing services, interpersonal interactions and outcomes in ways that can disadvantage both individuals and groups of people. We all have some form of unconscious bias, and the key is to recognize that we have it and employ techniques to mitigate it.
In this course, you will:
Define and identify unconscious bias
Learn how subconsciously, decisions are being made in your mind
Learn how unconscious bias affects everyday life
Learn ways to mitigate your bias

title: Documentation and Coding Made Easy
description:
Unlock the key to successful dental claims with this focused 90-minute course connecting documentation and coding. Accurate use of the CDT code set is crucial for proper claim reporting and reimbursement, yet coding and documentation errors remain the leading causes of denials, rejections, and payer audits.
This course dives into the most common coding and clinical documentation mistakes, offering practical strategies to avoid them and enhance your storytelling. Learn why record specificity is vital for both compliance and accuracy, and discover solutions.
Learning objectives
Identify critical documentation pitfalls and improve your clinical notes
Implement the S.O.A.P. note format for clinical notes
Prepare your team for CDT updates

title: The Practice Side of Fluoride: Patient Education and Addressing Misinformation
description:
Effectively communicating the benefits of fluoride and addressing misinformation is essential for healthcare professionals and public health advocates.
Presenters Jessica Robertson, D.M.D., and Greg Millwood, D.M.D., outline practical strategies for educating your patients about fluoride, offering clear and relatable explanations of its benefits. You will also learn grassroots approaches to engage with fluoridation skeptics and develop techniques to navigate misinformation with confidence and credibility. You will be equipped to foster informed discussions and promote evidence-based decision-making with fluoride-containing products.
Learning objectives
Learn strategies to share positive aspects of fluoridation in practical terms
Understand the principles of addressing patient misinformation
Learn “grassroots” responses for fluoridation skeptics

title: Advanced Pain Control in Dentistry Overview: Local Anesthesia and Analgesia
description:
Pain and dentistry are tightly linked, unfortunately. In contrast, our ability to keep patients comfortable is one of the factors that make a "good" dentist. In this course, you will review local anesthetics and local anesthesia techniques to get your patients comfortable during procedures. We will also review how to provide effective pain control after, and sometimes before, your procedures.
Learning Objectives
Utilize improved knowledge of local anesthetics available for use by North American dentists
Review standard and alternative techniques to provide profound local anesthesia for patients
Provide effective pain relief for your patients with an improved knowledge of analgesic pharmacology
Examine pain control protocols to provide better comfort for patients
This Online Course can be taken as a stand-along course, or as a complement to the new Live Webinar on Dec. 12: Safe & Effective Ways to Treat the Anxious Patient (1 CE) and Online Course: Optimizing Patient Intake for Sedation Dentistry (1 CE), also by Dr. Nkansah.

title: Optimizing Patient Intake for Sedation Dentistry
description:
Go beyond routine intake forms and instead focus on identifying your patient’s medical risks, dental anxiety, anesthesia limitations and appropriate sedation options. Building on knowledge of medical emergencies, you will learn how to take a more complete “snapshot” of the patient’s psychological and physiological profile to support better clinical decisions, safer anesthesia administration and improved patient comfort. This is not an anxiety treatment course, but a training session in risk awareness, planning and decision-making to help you anticipate and prevent complications.
Learning Objectives
Evaluate patient histories and risk factors to identify potential medical complications, anxiety risks and anesthesia limitations before treatment
Integrate psychological “snapshots” of patient anxiety into clinical decision-making around anesthesia and sedation
Compare and apply appropriate minimal and moderate sedation options to improve patient comfort and safety during care
This Online Course can be taken as a stand-along course, or as a complement to the new Live Webinar on Dec. 12: Safe & Effective Ways to Treat the Anxious Patient (1 CE) and Online Course: Advanced Pain Control in Dentistry Overview: Local Anesthesia and Analgesics (1 CE), also by Dr. Nkansah.

title: Treating Patients with Special Health Care Needs in Your Practice
description:
Many patients with special health care needs (SHCNs) and complex medical conditions face significant barriers to timely, comprehensive dental care. As a result, they often remain with pediatric dentists well into adulthood, or are referred to hospital-based clinics, where wait times can extend from months to years. These delays frequently turn preventable issues into complex problems—issues that could have been addressed in a general dental practice with timely intervention.
This course offers the knowledge, skills and confidence to safely and effectively treat patients with SHCNs and medical complexities in a private practice setting. You will explore the challenges of transitioning patients from pediatric to general dental care, discover how private practitioners can reduce reliance on overburdened hospital systems and learn practical clinical and communication strategies for this unique population. Special emphasis will be placed on the personal and professional fulfillment that comes from serving patients whose care requires extra patience, compassion and dedication.
Learning Objectives:
Explain the ethical considerations and guiding principles involved in providing care for patients with special health care needs (SHCNs) and complex medical conditions
Identify common medical comorbidities and apply appropriate dental management considerations
Initiate and conduct medical consultations with confidence to promote effective medical–dental integration
Implement business protocols and team training strategies to support the delivery of specialized care
Determine appropriate treatment modifications and select materials suited to patient-specific needs
Navigate legal and administrative requirements, including informed consent, power of attorney, and guardianship arrangements
Expand care delivery options by offering portable dentistry services

title: Conversations About Over the Counter Oral Health Products
description:
Today’s consumers are increasingly aware of the connection between oral health and systemic health and are becoming more conscientious in their oral care purchases. Yet with over 5,000 OTC oral care products and more than 1,800 brands on the market, many struggle to determine what’s truly effective. This self-paced course will guide the entire team in communicating key factors used in oversight, such as those through the ADA Seal of Acceptance, to determine the effectiveness of a product.
Learning Objectives:
Recognize how growing consumer awareness of oral-systemic health influences purchasing behavior
Describe the oversight process of over-the-counter products and its marketing
Summarize key indicators for patients to utilize in validation of products

title: Medicare Claims Submissions & Administrative Modifiers: Updates for Dentists Treating Medicare Beneficiaries
description:

Morgan, Jr., D.D.S., join Ian Hedges, ADA's director of Medicaid and Medicare program policy, to share their expertise and answer practitioner questions about how the new requirements affect treating Medicare beneficiaries and claims submission.

Differentiate the various Medicare programs and how dental services coverage is incorporated in those programs
Describe the Medicare provider enrollment process for dentists looking to treat Medicare beneficiaries
Identify the qualifying medical procedures in which dental services are intrinsically linked to clinical success and are covered under Medicare Part B
Apply knowledge in submitting a Medicare claim for dental services to a Medicare Administrative Contractor

title: Reverse Gum Recession and Fill Black Triangles Without Surgery: The Future of Regenerative Periodontics
description:
In this cutting-edge virtual training, you’ll learn how to stabilize and reverse gum recession, fill unsightly black triangles, and dramatically eliminate tooth sensitivity—all with a nonsurgical, minimally invasive injection performed chairside in minutes.
Whether you’re treating early recession or want to stop watching mild cases worsen, this course will introduce you to the future of regenerative periodontics.
Learning Objectives:
Understand how nonsurgical periodontal tissue regeneration works for treating gingival recession, fill black triangles, and eliminate hypersensitivity
Recognize treatment protocols to achieve the best esthetic and therapeutic outcomes with a regenerative dermal filler
Strategize how to integrate regenerative filler therapy into your dental practice

title: From Facts to Action: Defending Community Water Fluoridation
description:
Challenges to community water fluoridation are increasing. Dental professionals must lend their voices and knowledge to help preserve the most effective and cost-effective form of decay prevention for their community. This course will review the basics of water fluoridation, learn how to counter the most common misconceptions around fluoridation and how to be effective advocates in their states and communities, including talking with patients, and local decision-makers.
Learning Objectives:
Discuss the basic science of community water fluoridation and its benefits
Evaluate misinformation on neurocognitive effects and other aspects of community water fluoridation
Demonstrate the ability to advocate effectively for community water fluoridation

title: Scaling & Debridement of Single Implants with Peri-Implantitis (2026 CDT Codes: Clinical Practice Series Part 5)
description:
Focusing on single implants affected by peri-implantitis, this course provides a comprehensive understanding of implant maintenance procedures. Learn the essential techniques for debridement, prosthesis maintenance and patient education to ensure optimal implant health. Appropriate for dentists and dental hygienists.
Learning Objectives
Understand the importance of implant maintenance and hygiene
Identify signs of peri-implantitis and apply appropriate interventions
Explain the importance of implant maintenance for long-term success
Evaluate the stability and occlusion of the prosthesis
Conduct scaling and debridement of a single implant in cases of peri-implantitis without flap entry and closure
Educate patients on daily implant hygiene and maintenance routines

title: Implant Maintenance Procedures for Full-Arch Removable Implant Supported Dentures (2026 CDT Codes: Clinical Practice Series Part 4)
description:
Gain a comprehensive understanding of implant maintenance procedures, with a focus on full-arch removable implant-supported dentures. You will learn the essential techniques for debridement, prosthesis maintenance and patient education to ensure optimal implant health. Appropriate for dentists and dental hygienists.

Understand the importance of implant maintenance and hygiene.
Explain the importance of implant maintenance for long-term success.

title: The Why Behind CDT 2026 A-Panel Discussion (2026 CDT Codes: Clinical Practice Series Part 2)
description:
This engaging session invites dentists and their entire teams to go behind the scenes with the Current Dental Terminology (CDT). This moderated panel will discuss codes recently brought before the Code Maintenance Committee (CMC)—explaining what changes were accepted for CDT 2026 , and most importantly, why . Learn about the key considerations that shape code additions revisions, or deletions, and how those decisions impact everyday practice. Whether you are a dentist, hygienist, office manager, or billing specialist, this discussion will deepen your understanding of the coding process. Appropriate for dentists and dental team.
Learning Objectives
Gain insight into the CDT annual review process.
Understand the clinical and administrative reasoning behind recent code changes.
Understand how and why specific dental procedure codes can benefit your practice's documentation and billing needs.
Learn how you can participate in the CDT Code Maintenance Process.

title: The CDT Code: ​Why It Exists, How It’s Maintained and What’s Changed (2026 CDT Codes: Clinical Practice Series Part 1)
description:
Join us for an insightful session exploring the Current Dental Terminology (CDT) Code — the standardized language of dental procedures. In this presentation, ADA Senior Manager of Dental Codes Maintenance and Development Afton Dunsmoor takes you through the fascinating history behind the CDT Code to give you a clear understanding of its structure and how it is maintained and updated. Appropriate for dentists and dental team.
Learning Objectives
Explain the purpose and importance of the CDT Code in dental practice and insurance documentation.
Describe the historical development of the CDT Code and its role in standardizing dental terminology.
Identify the structural components of the CDT Code and how procedures are categorized and coded.
Outline the process by which the CDT Code is reviewed, maintained, and updated annually.

title: Antibiotics: Friend or Foe? Considerations for Antibiotic Use in Dentistry
description:
Acute dental pain and swelling associated with pulpal and periapical disease is extremely common. The most commonly reported oral-health-related reasons for Emergency Department visits were dental pain and intraoral swelling. Dentists and physicians often prescribe antibiotics in response to these symptoms and dentists were the third highest prescribing group for antibiotics in the U.S. It is estimated in reports from 2017 to 2019, between 30-85% of these prescriptions were suboptimal or not indicated.
Furthermore, patients’ use of antibiotics is often not ideal. Both inappropriate prescribing patterns and failure to thoroughly advise patients on the importance of using antibiotics as directed have led to an increase in the number of multi-drug-resistant bacteria.
This course will review the evidence regarding antibiotic usage in the dental setting, discuss the mechanisms of action of antibiotics commonly used in the dental setting, and establish the rationale for the American Dental Association (ADA) guidelines and best practices for their implementation in dental practices.

title: Ethical Considerations in Managing Patient Interactions & Comments
description:
This is a two-part course from the ADA Council on Ethics, Bylaws and Judicial Affairs.
Presentation 1 : Responding to Offensive Comments and Behavior from Patients
This presentation provides tools for preventing harassment and inappropriate conduct in dental offices. Elevate your practice by developing skills to identify, address, and prevent such issues, fostering a positive and inclusive workplace. You'll learn how to:
Identify forms of misconduct, unprofessional behavior and offensive comments
Use best practices when addressing offensive comments, misconduct and unprofessional behavior
Cultivate a dental practice that prioritizes respect and inclusivity for employees and patients
Learning Objectives
Participants will be able to identify the ethical risks and benefits responding or not responding to a patient’s offensive comments
Participants will understand how the ADA Principles of Ethics & Code of Professional Conduct applies to and offers guidance for how to manage the range of stakeholder considerations including staff, the patient and other dentists in the practice
Participants will consider a range of ethically permissible responses
Presentation 2 : Ethical Considerations of “Friending” Patients on Social Media
Social media is ubiquitous. From Instagram to Facebook to LinkedIn, professionals, acquaintances, friends and family are connecting through various channels for purposes of learning, catching up and/or networking. The blurring of professional and personal lines, however, can pose a challenge for dentists when patients of record make a “friend” request. This session will discuss some of the ethical pitfalls and benefits of accepting or rejecting these requests.
Learning Objectives:
Identify the ethical risks and benefits of connecting with patients through social media
Understand how the ADA Principles of Ethics & Code of Professional Conduct applies to an offers guidance for connecting with patients through social media

title: Lasers in Periodontics and Implant Dentistry
description:
Join Periodontist and Diplomate of the American Board of Periodontology Nasser Mansour Assery, B.D.S., M.S., F.R.C.D.(C) , for a comprehensive exploration of lasers in periodontics and implant dentistry. After an overview of the principles, mechanisms and types of lasers in periodontal therapy, you will delve into how lasers can be used in practice and how they affect hard and soft tissues and implants. To conclude the session, Dr. Assery provides you with case studies and examples comparing laser-assisted periodontal therapy and conventional therapy.
Learning Objectives:
1. Laser fundamentals
Explain the basic principles of lasers, including the types of lasers commonly used in periodontics and implant dentistry (e.g., diode, Nd;YAG, Er:YAG).
Describe how lasers interact with different types of tissues and how these interactions benefit periodontal treatment.
2. Identify clinical applications of lasers in periodontics
Outline the various periodontal and implant procedures where lasers can be used, such as pocket debridement, gingivectomy, and implant surface decontamination.
Discuss the benefits and limitations of laser use in treating periodontal disease.
Explain the photobiomodulatory effects of lasers on periodontal tissues.
3. Evaluate the evidence for laser efficacy in periodontal treatment
Look into evidence comparing laser-assisted periodontal therapy to conventional methods.

title: Single Dental Implant: From Surgical Placement to Restoration
description:
In this course, Periodontist and Diplomate of the American Board of Periodontology Nasser Mansour Assery, B.D.S., M.S., F.R.C.D.(C) , provides a comprehensive introduction to the surgical and restorative aspects of single dental implants, focusing on building foundational knowledge for dental professionals. This session emphasizes the placement of implants in a healed ridge and explores how various restorative decisions impact the long-term success and stability of the implant. Key topics include patient considerations, surgical considerations, prosthetic design, and implant maintenance protocols for successful outcomes. By the end of this course, participants will understand the interplay between surgical and restorative approaches, equipping them with the basic skills needed to ensure predictable results for single dental implants.
Learning objectives:
Identify absolute and relative contraindications for implant surgery.
Identify surgical and prosthetic factors that promote long term implant success.
Be able to establish a maintenance protocol for your implant patient.

title: Antibiotics Stewardship
description:
Antibiotic use is changing so that we can provide safer dental visits for patients. While using antibiotics has shown to be beneficial for certain conditions, these potential lifesavers can also result in a range of adverse reactions including serious population-level concerns such as antibiotic-resistant infections. This course — presented by Erinne Kennedy, D.M.D., M.P.H., M.M.S., assistant dean of curriculum and integrated learning
at Kansas City University College of Dental Medicine — will define the challenges with antibiotic use, outline the roles of the dental team in practicing antibiotic stewardship, and discuss best practices in other healthcare professions. We will review the current evidence base for antibiotic use in dentistry and review best practices for dental clinicians.
Learning Objectives:
Discuss tools to demonstrate a commitment to antibiotic stewardship within the dental setting.
Describe action items for implementation of a stewardship program
Identify tools to track and report antibiotic prescribing patterns
Identify resources to educate and train the entire dental team to promote responsible antibiotic prescribing

title: Artificial Intelligence's Revolutionary Impact on Dentistry
description: Artificial intelligence has arrived in the dental practice. Dental AI can now identify anatomy and disease with incredible levels of precision and present findings with compelling visuals for patient communication. The technology is poised to redefine how dentists are trained, practices operate, and insurers reimburse. In this course, Dr. Chris Balaban, who works at the forefront of this technology, leads you through the technology's current capabilities and how providers — as well as payers — are using dental AI today to improve patient care and outcomes.

title: Dentistry’s Role in Complete Health: We Make People Healthy! (2024)
description:
To address the burgeoning health crisis in the U.S., this special symposium aims to equip dental professionals with the knowledge and skills to play a pivotal role in addressing chronic conditions such as periodontal and periapical disease, systemic inflammation, metabolic syndrome, diabetes, cardiovascular disease, obesity, Alzheimer's and sleep disorders. These conditions, largely stemming from lifestyle choices, pose significant threats to both individual well-being and the national economy.
By taking a proactive and rational approach to complete patient health, dentistry can emerge as a frontline force in combating this crisis. The special ADA symposium—led by a distinguished team of researchers, clinicians, authors and educators—will empower you and your team to become leaders in the next frontier of dental care, emphasizing the mouth as the gateway to overall health and positioning dental professionals as healthcare superstars.

title: LGBTQIA+ Affirming Dental Practice: Pediatric Patients
description: Pediatric patients are some of the most vulnerable people we work with and have the lowest level of self-autonomy due to their age. When our pediatric patients are LGBTQIA+ they have added layers of vulnerability that can impact current and future health outcomes. Our role as pediatric providers is an important space for our young clients to feel seen, welcome and safe. This course will help you show your patients that you know the meaning of LGBTQIA+ terminology, recognize how we all learn about ourselves and the world through a developmental lens, discern the nuances of developmental messages that impact LGBTQIA+ pediatric patients differently than their peers, and learn practical ways to create an LGBTQIA+ pediatric clinical space.

title: Kids Don’t Grow Out of It, They Need Your Help—Here’s How: An ADA Children’s Airway Event
description:
As a dental professional, you hold the key to finding the causes behind poor behavior, developing bad habits and atypical growth. If the craniofacial respiratory complex is underdeveloped, the body must compromise—and no one can predict the effects of that compromise over a lifetime.
The sooner in life we recognize breathing resistance and bad habits, the sooner we can give the child the best opportunity to thrive. In this course, experts show you new skills to put therapy into practice. Gain enhanced behavioral management skills for young patients and communication skills for both the child and their caregivers/decision makers. (11.5 CE Credits)

title: Perinatal Mood and Anxiety Disorders: Supporting Dental Professionals, Teams and Families
description:
Perinatal Mood and Anxiety Disorders, including postpartum depression, can significantly impact dental professionals, their colleagues, and their families. The demands of dental practice schedules and workflows, and personal responsibilities, increase the risk of burnout and depression affecting anyone, regardless of gender. Colleagues and family members may recognize a change in their partner once the baby arrives. This presentation helps learners understand what perinatal or postpartum depression is, identify the risk factors and share when professional intervention is needed. Presenters highlight support resources, such as counseling, support groups, and employee assistance programs for anyone needing help, and explore national and state advocacy efforts surrounding depression, perinatal and postpartum depression.
Learning Objectives
Describe perinatal or postpartum depression
Identify risk factors for perinatal and postpartum depression
Understand when intervention for perinatal or postpartum depression is needed

title: Excellence in Oral Cancer: Let's Give them Something to Talk About (Dental Team Education)
description:
Consequences of an incomplete oral cancer evaluation, inadequate documentation, and delayed referral may result in delayed diagnosis, lives lost, and increased liability. As dental professionals, it is a professional responsibility to educate patients on risk factors, signs and symptoms of oral and oropharyngeal cancer, and the availability of a cancer prevention vaccine. What we know now, there are additional risk factors other than tobacco, alcohol, and HPV, and evidence shows there may be signs and symptoms present before we see “something suspicious” intraorally. Join Susan in this content-rich course to discuss these topics.
Learning Objectives
Discuss important information about the Human Papillomavirus
Recognize potential signs and symptoms of oral and oropharyngeal cancer that deserve further investigation
List additional risk factors for oral and oropharyngeal cancer

title: What Emergency Medicine Providers Want Dentists to Know About Opioids
description:
This webinar was recorded in March 2023
Emergency departments have a unique perspective on how to employ strategies to promote the judicious use of opioids and prioritize nonopioid multimodal evidence-based care when managing acute pain.
Emergency medicine providers have adopted a targeted approach to acute pain with a focus on appropriate transitions of care for continued treatment. There is a shift in emergency care to equip all providers with the tools necessary to tailor acute pain management, break pain crises, and educate the community on nonopioid strategies for pain and appropriate follow-up.
Learn about the emergency medicine provider’s perspective on opioids, effective pain management, and why streamlined referral programs are essential to patients receiving appropriate dental care.
Learning Objectives
•Discuss the evidence-based multi-modal nonopioid strategies utilized in the ED to address acute dental pain
• Review the importance of streamlined referral programs to connect emergency department patients directly with appropriate dental care and how to implement this strategy
• Explore the strategic initiatives set forth by the American College of Emergency Physicians to provide membership with guidance and support to appropriately manage acute pain in the era of fentanyl.
Disclaimer: The views of the presenter(s) do not necessarily represent the views of the American Dental Association

title: How Sweet It Is! Diabetes Mellitus, Dental Considerations and Patient Care Planning
description:
Diabetes mellitus remains the seventh leading cause of death in the United States. Of the estimated 29.1 million Americans that have diabetes, 21 million are diagnosed, while 8.1 million remain undiagnosed. Diabetes, and its systemic complications, are often accompanied by, and exacerbated by, numerous other co-morbid conditions.
This program will provide participants with an overview of diabetes mellitus, the arsenal of medications used in its treatment and the impact of both the disease and its treatment on dental therapy. Using case scenarios, special emphasis will be placed on the complex interplay between diabetes and the oral-systemic connection, as well as developing patient management strategies essential for successful treatment planning and proper care of our diabetic patients.
Learning objectives
Identify the different types of diabetes mellitus, including their symptoms and differential diagnosis
Describe the complex interplay of diabetes mellitus with oral-systemic inflammation and co-morbid diseases
Review the classes of medications used in the treatment of diabetes along with their mechanisms of action, adverse reactions and dental considerations
Describe the treatment modifications, patient management strategies and care planning techniques recommended for dental patients with diabetes

title: Why a Systems Change Approach for Oral Health? (Oral Health Systems Change Webinar Series Session 1)
description: The North Carolina Oral Health Collaborative (NCOHC) — a program of the Foundation for Health Leadership & Innovation (FHLI) — has pursued an approach to advancing oral health care access and equity through policy-driven systems change, accomplishing multiple legislative and regulatory changes affecting oral health care delivery, workforce, and payment in recent years. title: Amplifying Voices: Patients Who Have Special Needs — Creating an Inclusive Environment and Expanding Oral Health Access (Recorded Webinar)
description: This panel of dental professionals and patients will share tips for the whole team on making make the office more welcoming and comfortable for patients who have special healthcare needs. (1 CE Credit)

title: Highchair Dental Care: A Revolutionary Practice Model for Infants and Toddlers
description:
The Highchair Dental Care Practice Model offers an alternative standard to address infant oral health. This Practice Model draws attention to the importance of innovation and early interventions to prevent early childhood caries and other potential unfavorable sequelae.
About Highchair Dental Care
It is baby-friendly, and resourceful patient care that works well with most infants, toddlers and families.
It is a convenient and comforting way for caregivers and health professionals to establish the necessary routine for the oral care of the infant and toddler.
This practice model can be utilized in the dental setting for children up to three years of age.
Safety-regulated commercial highchairs are most often size-suitable and therefore recommended for use in the clinical health care environment.

title: Guideline on Antibiotic Use for the Urgent Management of Dental Pain and Intraoral Swelling
description: This course provides an overview of the ADA's clinical practice guideline on the use of antibiotics for the urgent management of pulpal- and periapical-related dental pain and intraoral swelling. It also addresses when to prescribe and when not to prescribe antibiotics. (1 CE Credit)
instructors: Erinne Kennedy (D.M.D., M.P.H., M.M.S., Assistant Dean Curriculum and Integrated Learning)

title: E-cigarettes, and Vaping: Current Trends, Neurobiology, Cessation, and the Role of the Dental Professional
description:
While electronic nicotine delivery systems (ENDS) such as e-cigarettes were initially marketed as a harm reduction and smoking cessation strategy, there is conflicting evidence regarding their effectiveness as a smoking cessation strategy.
Given increasing rates of use of ENDS, especially among youth, and emerging concerns about potential ENDS-related harm, dental providers are strategically positioned to support population-level tobacco reduction efforts and provide health information about ENDS.
There is a pressing need for continuing education opportunities to help dental providers improve ENDS-related knowledge and counseling practices.
Learning Objectives
Upon completion of this course, the dental professional should be able to:
Understand the origin and development of electronic nicotine delivery systems (ENDS) such as "e-cigarettes" and "Puff Bars"
Understand the mechanism of action and combustion science of ENDS
Discuss the epidemiology and trends associated with youth vaping, including use of flavored, nicotine and cannabis vaping products
Describe the neurobiology of nicotine and cannabis and the unique vulnerability of the developing adolescent brain
Use evidence-based screening and brief intervention strategy to help prevent and reduce youth vaping
Apply effective pharmacological and non-pharmacological cessation approaches for youth with an addiction to e-cigarettes and other vaping products
Understand the current knowledge about the effects of ENDS on oral health
Recognize the dental professional's role in appropriate education and recommendations

title: Ergonomics: What's Your Position? (Dental Team Education)
description: The dental profession is at high risk for musculoskeletal disorders (MSD), with 96% of practitioners reporting pain due to clinical work habits. At the conclusion of this course, participants will learn about the most common musculoskeletal disorders in dentistry, as well as ideal positioning for dental hygienists, dentists, and dental assistants. (1 CE Credit)

title: Ergonomics: More than Just Good Posture (Dental Team Education)
description: The dental profession is one of the most at-risk professions in the area of musculoskeletal injury due to multiple ergonomic risk factors. At the conclusion of this course, participants will be able to identify new ergonomic concerns as a result of the COVID-19 pandemic, learn about the importance of stretching and microbreaks, and the importance of operatory design and layout to preserve musculoskeletal health. (1 CE Credit)

title: The Art of Posterior Provisionals (Dental Team Education)
description: Provisional restorations provide an optimal situation for patients and clinicians in developing permanent restorations. Ideal permanent restorations, however, require time for clinicians to plan and laboratories to create them. Therefore, dentists satisfy their patients’ needs with a provisional restoration and ensure sufficient time to perfect the permanent restoration. (.75 CE Credits)

title: The Art of Anterior Provisionals (Dental Team Education)
description: The role of dental restorations used for provisional and indirect restorative procedures has changed dramatically in the past several years. These restorations are no longer regarded as temporary restorations but as provisional restorations with distinct functions and purposes. (.75 CE Credits)

title: Wisdom Teeth Medical Billing - Hidden Gem Module 1 (Dental Team Education)
description: This Dental Team Education module is designed to enable you to submit medical claims for wisdom teeth removal. Upon completion, the attendee will gain the knowledge of how to complete a CMS-1500 claim form and how ICD-10 diagnosis codes explain the treatment. (1 CE Credit)

title: What the Dental Laboratory Needs to Know (Dental Team Education)
description: Communication between the dentist, assistant, and laboratory technician is more critical than ever in the overall achievement of ultimate esthetic results. This Dental Team Education course will help you increase your artistic skill and improve patient satisfaction by learning about materials and techniques that can help provide the best dentistry has to offer. (2 CE Credits)

title: The Successful PPO Practice: Profitability without Sacrificing (Dental Team Education)
description: This strategic Dental Team Education course offers solutions for achieving 100 percent insurance collection and tips for faster insurance claim payments and denial prevention. Learn how YOUR practice can implement these proven solutions immediately. (1 CE Credit)

title: The Successful PPO Practice: How to decide what plans to pursue or drop? (Dental Team Education)
description: This Dental Team Education course will help you know what plans to pursue and what plans to avoid. Learn how YOUR practice can implement these systems and use the tools introduced in this session to take the stress out of participation. (1 CE Credit)

title: The Dynamic Duo: 5 Secrets for the Best Doctor-Assistant Team
description: This is a course originally presented at the ADA 2020 Virtual Connect Conference. (1 CE Credit)

title: The Dental Hygiene Detective: What your patient's mouth is telling you (Dental Team Education)
description: There are many clues that provide evidence of diseases, such as occlusal wear, recession, bleeding, fissured tongues, erythemic tissues, incipient lesions, and many more. Determining our patient's high-risk factors for oral disease and systemic health is multi-factorial and our role as dental professionals allows us to evaluate the first signs of disease. This Dental Team Education course will help you and your team review the patient's risk factors in-depth, while empowering the clinician to compare clinical technologies that align with the patient's lifestyle to reduce decrease disease. (1 CE Credit)

title: System Implementation is the Key to Practice Growth (Dental Team Education)
description: In this Dental Team Education course, presenter Denise Williams-Jones, founder and owner of Next Level to Success, will discuss the benefits of implementing systems to allow a smooth flow and have a more productive schedule. She will also recommend practical systems for you to create with your team to facilitate growth within the practice. (1 CE Credit)

title: Rubber Dam Isolation (Dental Team Education)
description: With today's heightened awareness of infection control, patient safety, and technique-sensitive dental materials, meticulous operative field isolation is mandatory. This management begins with rapid, profound knowledge of hemostasis. (1 CE Credit)

title: Maxillary Local Anesthesia for the Dental Professional (Dental Team Education)
description: In this Dental Team Education course, Katrina Sanders, RDH, M.Ed. guides participants through the common causes of injection failure, including anatomical and physiological conditions. Explore techniques used in private practice, including appropriate doses of anesthesia, anatomical and armamentarium considerations. (1 CE Credit)

title: Mandibular Local Anesthesia for the Dental Professional (Dental Team Education)
description: In this Dental Team Education course, Katrina Sanders, RDH, M.Ed. guides participants through the common causes of injection failure, including anatomical and physiological conditions. (1 CE Credit)

title: Making a Great Impression (Dental Team Education)
description: Even the most experienced clinician can encounter difficulties without troubleshooting bubbles, tears pull, voids, and tray contact. This Dental Team Education course provides an overview of the materials and techniques needed to master impression making. (1 CE Credit)

title: Help! My Filling Fell Out. To Bond or Not to Bond (Dental Team Education)
description: This course helps you to avoid trouble with techniques and procedures, and become a better dental clinician. It will provide you with tools and techniques to use to perform more predictable, faster, efficient, and profitable direct restorations in your practice. (1 CE Credit)

title: Gaining Control of Your Dental Inventory (Dental Team Education)
description: Although inventory control and product ordering is essential to the practice of every dental office; few have achieved an ideal balance between maintaining necessary inventory and curtailing overhead expenses; usually, they lack a system or the protocol that can make these tasks run smoothly and cost-effectively. In this Dental Team Education course, you will learn how successful inventory control begins with assessing the current situation and evaluating the practice’s systems’ strengths and weaknesses. (1 CE Credit)

title: Front Admin: You can Effectively Lead, Strengthen, and Unite Your Team (Dental Team Education)
description: In this Dental Team Education course, presenter Denise Williams-Jones, founder and owner of Next Level to Success, will discuss successful tips and strategies to use daily to encourage a strong collaboration between front and back office. She will also suggest encouraging communication for an improved daily environment and true connection with the team. (1 CE Credit)

title: Excellence in Oral Cancer: All Hands on Deck (Dental Team Education)
description: In this session, we will review a thorough visual and tactile, intraoral and extraoral evaluation, show techniques for optimal visualization and assessment, discuss components of a thorough oropharyngeal evaluation, and why Mallampati score and Tonsil grade are important to this evaluation. (1 CE Credit)

title: Demystifying the AAP classifications: A practical approach to implementation (Dental Team Education)
description: Proper identification of the patient's disease will allow the patient to have an effective treatment plan to prevent the progression of Periodontal Disease. This Dental Team Education module is designed to provide a practical application for clinicians to accurately assess and treat patient's periodontium. (1 CE Credit)

title: CBCT Medical Billing and Prior Authorization – Hidden Gem Module 2
description: This gem of a course will train administrative and clinical teams to find the chief complaint and deduce the most appropriate ICD-10 codes. The presentation also covers constructing a prior authorization through online portals or the insurance’s preferred method. (1 CE Credit)

title: Diagnostic Signs of Abuse
description: Dental professionals may be the first or only point of contact for domestic violence victims in a health care setting and since as much as 75% of physical abuse involves injuries to the head, face, or neck areas, they may be the most capable of recognizing the signs of abuse. This webinar will provide guidance to the dental professionals to evaluate signs of suspected abuse and neglect. (1 CE Credit)

title: Preventing HPV Cancers in Action Part 3: 'Building Blocs' — Medical-Dental Collaboration Models and More
description:
How does a dentist interested in promoting the HPV vaccine build a collaboration with a medical provider? What actions can states take to build collaborative partnerships that increase HPV vaccination? Please join Jessica Jack MD FAAP, Pediatrician, & Scott Hamilton, DDS, Pediatric Dentist, Denver Health, and Tommy Johnson, DMD, State Dental Director, Alabama Department of Public Health, to discuss partnership models and more.
This course is the third in the three-part series "HPV Cancers in Action" from the ADA Council on Advocacy for Access & Prevention (CAAP). More than ever, there is a need to increase awareness of HPV and of educating the public on the need for HPV immunization. The administration of the HPV vaccine is a safe and effective way to reduce the risk of HPV-related cancers, including certain head and neck cancers. HPV is now associated with more than 20,000 cases of head and neck cancer each year in the United States, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC).
Learning objectives
Understand how dentistry and medicine can collaborate to promote primary prevention of HPV
Understand the lessons learned from a pilot project including dentists, pediatricians, and efforts to increase pediatric vaccination rates
Experience an example of a multi-factorial, interdisciplinary collaboration to educate providers on HPV-related oral and oropharyngeal cancers and the importance of promotion of the HPV vaccine
This presentation was recorded on April 20, 2022, and reviewed in 2026.

title: Preventing HPV Cancers in Action Part 2: Dentists As Vaccinators
description:
The COVID-19 pandemic has increased awareness of dentists as vaccinators—including the HPV vaccine. Please join Rosie Wagner DDS, Founder, Smiles By Rosie Family Dentistry and Isaac Zeckel DDS, Chief Dental Director, HealthLinc, to discuss the benefits of dentists as immunizers, steps to becoming a dentist immunizer, medicolegal considerations, and ways to increase access to care by providing a new vaccination setting, with innovative vaccination approaches that utilize on-site interdisciplinary teams.
This course is the second in the three-part series "HPV Cancers in Action" from the ADA Council on Advocacy for Access & Prevention (CAAP). More than ever, there is a need to increase awareness of HPV and of educating the public on the need for HPV immunization. The administration of the HPV vaccine is a safe and effective way to reduce the risk of HPV-related cancers, including certain head and neck cancers. HPV is now associated with more than 20,000 cases of head and neck cancer each year in the United States, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC).
Learning objectives
Learn the steps in becoming an immunizer, including registration with one's state database, training on storage, handling, and administration of vaccines, and necessary equipment purchases
Learn about one FQHC’s collaborative practice to educate patients and administer the HPV vaccine while in the dental operator
Describe ways to increase access to care by providing a new vaccination setting
This presentation was recorded on April 4, 2022, and reviewed in 2026.

title: Preventing HPV Cancers in Action Part 1: The Critical Role of the Dentist
description:
The dentist plays a critical role in detecting oral cancers and in educating their patients on the importance of HPV vaccination. In this presentation, Gary Heyamoto, DDS, MAGD, President, Academy of General Dentistry Foundation (AGDF) discusses the importance of performing a consistent, thorough clinical oral exam, how to discuss HPV vaccination with patients, be able to describe Anterior Mouth and Posterior Mouth from a medical perspective and learn how dentists can participate, as an office, in fighting HPV oral cancers through awareness and promotion of the HPV vaccine.
This course is first in the three-part series "HPV Cancers in Action" from the ADA Council on Advocacy for Access and Prevention (CAAP). More than ever, there is a need to increase awareness of HPV and of educating the public on the need for HPV immunization. The administration of the HPV vaccine is a safe and effective way to reduce the risk of HPV-related cancers, including certain head and neck cancers. HPV is now associated with more than 20,000 cases of head and neck cancer each year in the United States, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC).
Learning objectives:
Understand the importance of performing a consistent, thorough clinical oral exam & be able to describe the anterior and posterior mouth from a medical perspective
Understand how to discuss HPV vaccination with patients
Learn how dentists can participate, as an office, in fighting HPV oral cancers
This presentation was recorded on March 23, 2022, and reviewed in 2026.

title: Human Trafficking: What ED Physicians Want Dentists to Know About Identifying At-Risk Patients
description: Human trafficking is more prevalent than most think. Many ask, "What can I do as a dentist?" Get the ED physician perspective on what dentists need to know to increase awareness, best practices for screening & what to do when faced with these potential sensitive situations. (1 CE Credit)

title: Communicating with Patients When Things Go Wrong in Dentistry
description:
Few challenges strike as much fear in a dentist’s heart as the need to talk to a patient who was harmed by their dental care. In this recorded webinar, national experts share best practices for communicating with patients who have been harmed by their dental care and describe how these skills reinforce the key behaviors needed to support patients and drive learning in dentistry.
By the conclusion of this webinar, attendees will be able to:
Identify three steps they can take today to enhance a culture of patient safety in their practice
Explain three reasons why transparency is essential to a culture of safety
Describe the critical communication skills required to respond effectively when patients are harmed by their dental care.
Presented by the ADA’s Council on Advocacy for Access and Prevention (CAAP).
American Dental Association on-demand webinar

title: The Top 200 Prescription Drugs Part 2
description: Our primary obligation and ultimate reasonability is the timely delivery of quality diagnostic, preventive, and therapeutic services, within the bounds of the clinical circumstances presented by patients. Consequently, we must possess knowledge and skills essential to determine the functional state of patients to undergo and respond to dental care. (4 CE Credits)

title: Pharmacotherapeutics for Dental Practitioners: Local Anesthetic Agents
description: The most common complaint causing a person to seek the services of an oral health care provider is pain. Pain is an unpleasant sensory and emotional experience associated with actual or potential tissue damage. Proper management of pain requires an understand¬ing of its complexity, an appreciation for the factors that determine its expression in the clinical setting, sound pharmacological strategies (local anesthesia) during the perioperative period, and implementation of disease-modifying procedures (primary dental care). (2 CE Credits)

title: Local Anesthesia Parts 6 & 7: Local and Systemic Complications
description: This program discusses complications occurring in and around the site of local anesthetic drug administration - facial nerve paralysis, hematoma, paresthesia, trismus and needle breakage, their prevention, recognition and management as well as the two systemic adverse drug reactions – allergy and overdose – are reviewed as each relates to the administration of local anesthetic drugs. (1.5 CE Credits)

title: Local Anesthesia Part 9: What's New in Dental Local Anesthesia?
description: Though local anesthetics are safe and effective, considerable research is on-going to make them even more effective. Computer-controlled local anesthetic delivery systems (C-CLAD) and buffered local anesthetic solutions promise to allow painless injections to be delivered to dental patients almost anywhere in the oral cavity. (1 CE Credit)

title: Local Anesthesia Part 8: Articaine - Is it the IDEAL local anesthetic for dentistry? Facts & Fiction
description: This program provides an in-depth look at articaine, its advantages and disadvantages when compared with other commonly used dental local anesthetics. We also discuss the ‘controversy’ alleging an increased risk of paresthesia following administration of 4% local anesthetics. (1 CE Credit)

title: Local Anesthesia Part 4 & 5: Mandibular & Supplemental Techniques
description: Techniques of anesthesia in the mandible are reviewed in this program, including the traditional inferior alveolar, Gow-Gates, Vazirani-Akinosi, and incisive nerve blocks as well as several non-traditional injection techniques including the periodontal ligament injection, intraosseous anesthesia and the administration of the local anesthetic articaine HCl by mandibular infiltration in adult patients. (1 CE Credit)

title: Local Anesthesia Part 3: Anatomy & Maxillary Techniques
description: Local anesthetic techniques providing pain control in the maxillary arch are described in this program following a review of the anatomy of the trigeminal nerve. Techniques include infiltration (supraperiosteal), anterior superior alveolar nerve block; middle superior alveolar nerve block; posterior superior alveolar nerve block; anterior superior alveolar nerve block; and palatal injection techniques. (1 CE Credit)

title: Local Anesthesia Part 2: Armamentarium, Patient Evaluation & Basic Injection Technique
description: The basic armamentarium for the administration of local anesthetics includes the syringe, cartridge and needle. Proper assembly and use of these items minimizes the risk of complications arising during injection, such as shattering of cartridges and needle breakage. (1 CE Credit)

title: Management of the Open Apex: Practical Endodontic Strategies for the General Dentist (Friday, May 15, 2026, Live Webinar)
description:
NOTE: This is a live webinar scheduled for Friday, May 15, 2026. It is not included with the ADA CE Online subscription.
Date: Friday, May 15, 2026
Time: 11 a.m. to 2 p.m. Central
Managing pediatric and adolescent patients with endodontic needs can be challenging, particularly when dealing with immature teeth and open apices.
As a general dentist, it is common to feel uncertain about when to treat, when to monitor, and when to refer. This Live Webinar will equip you with the clinically grounded knowledge needed to make confident decisions. You will receive practical guidance on managing endodontic issues in children and teens, helping you provide optimal care for your young patients.
Designed specifically for general dentists, this course demystifies the basics of pulpal diagnosis in younger patients, outlines predictable strategies for managing common dental issues like large carious lesions or traumatic dental injuries, and provides step-by-step guidance for treating immature teeth. You will also come to understand the breakdown in communication that often occurs between general practices and pediatric or endodontic specialists, and show you how to establish a reliable referral network that supports timely, appropriate care. You will also review restorative considerations for both short-term stabilization and long-term success.
Once you complete this course, you will have the confidence to make informed decisions, improve interdisciplinary collaboration and ensure optimal outcomes for young patients.
Learning Objectives
Identify endodontic diagnostic considerations that are unique to the young patient
Differentiate cases that can be managed in-office from those requiring referrals
Develop relationships to build a trusted specialist network for referrals
Describe and apply evidence-based protocols for managing dental trauma in children and teenagers
Explain the clinical steps and biologic goals of treating immature permanent teeth with open apices, including apexog

title: Fighting Resistance: The Dental Professional’s Role in Antibiotic Stewardship
description:
__Live CE Webinar: May 13th, 2026, 7:30-9:00 PM ET__
The rise of antibiotic-resistant bacteria is a growing concern, with new strains emerging that are increasingly difficult to treat. As antibiotic use increases, their effectiveness diminishes. In the field of dentistry, nearly 10% of all outpatient antibiotics are prescribed, with an estimated 80% of these prescriptions deemed unnecessary according to current guidelines. As dental professionals, it is crucial to stay informed about the latest prescribing guidelines and actively participate in antibiotic stewardship.
In this engaging presentation, attendees will gain insights into the current state of antibiotics, learn about appropriate usage, and explore non-antibiotic alternatives for patient treatment.

title: The Oral-Systemic Link: Why Daily Oral Care Is Preventive Medicine
description:
__Live CE Webinar: April 8th, 2026, 7:30-9:00 PM ET__
Oral diseases, particularly periodontal disease, are chronic inflammatory conditions with systemic implications that extend beyond the oral cavity. Emerging research continues to demonstrate strong associations between oral inflammation and systemic conditions such as diabetes mellitus, cardiovascular disease, adverse pregnancy outcomes, respiratory infections, and cognitive decline.
This course examines the biological pathways linking oral and systemic health and explores how daily oral hygiene practices function as preventive interventions. Participants will review current evidence, discuss implications for clinical practice, and develop strategies to communicate the importance of daily oral care as an essential component of whole-body health.
Emphasis will be placed on inflammation control, patient education, risk assessment, and the role of the dental professional in advancing preventive medicine through routine oral health practices.

title: Intraoral Radiographic Anatomy
description:
Intraoral radiography is part of the daily practice of dentistry. Radiographs provide important information to supplement the clinical examination, enabling the dentist to render a diagnosis and develop a treatment plan. Guidance regarding the prescription of radiographs can be found in course, Radiographic Selection Criteria (CE584) .
This course will focus on the anatomical structures that are recorded on intraoral radiographic images. The clinician must be familiar with the anatomical structures to ensure accurate orientation and/or arrangement prior to interpretation. Recognition of anatomical structures serves as a baseline to differentiate normal structures from abnormalities.
It should be understood that not every structure presented will be seen on every radiographic survey. However, enough structures will be recorded to provide the necessary guidance for arrangement and interpretation.
Syllabus: Dental Radiology
The information in this 12-module syllabus is intended (1) to meet elements of initial educational/training requirements for Dental Students, Dental Hygiene Students, and Dental Assistant Students related to dental radiography; (2) to provide a framework for an in-service training program in oral healthcare settings to meet annual educational/training requirements as mandated by federal, state, local and professional organizations; and (3) to serve as a resource for oral healthcare personnel wishing to review evidence-based information on specific topics related to dental radiography. title: Adverse Drug Reactions - Part II
description:
Adverse drug reactions (ADRs) may be characterized as (1) “on-target,” (2) “off-target,” (3) cytotoxic reactions, (4) immune-mediated reactions, and (5) idiosyncratic reactions.1 This course discusses common immune-mediated and idiosyncratic ADRs related to the top 200 drugs dispensed by U.S. community pharmacies in 2008 to inform and raise the awareness of oral healthcare providers of the spectrum of potential ADRs affecting their patient cohort. A discussion of less common ADRs that relate to dental therapeutics and/or manifest in the head and neck area is also provided.
Please note that this is Part II of a two-part series. Adverse Drug Reactions – Part I discusses common “on-target,” “off-target,” and cytotoxic reactions related to the top 200 drugs dispensed by U.S. community pharmacies in 2008, and less common ADRs that relate to dental therapeutics and/or manifest in the head and neck area.

title: A String around Your Finger: Do We Really Need to Floss?
description:
Controversies about the utility of flossing were thrust into the spotlight in 2016 in a controversy known amongst dental professionals as “Flossgate. As a part of the update to the 2015-2020 Dietary Guidelines for Americans, the federal government omitted a recommendation for flossing previously included in the guidelines in 2000, 2005, and 2015. [1](/en-us/ce-courses/ce550/references#1) Citing the reasons for the omission, the Dietary Guideline Advisory Committee (DGAC) stated they focused on food and nutrient intake (e.g., added sugar) and cited the 2011 Cochrane systematic review, [2](/en-us/ce-courses/ce550/references#2) which concluded there is currently a lack of strong evidence to support the use of floss as a preventative measure for caries and periodontal diseases, although the systematic review does acknowledge evidence that flossing results in a reduction in gingival inflammation and interdental plaque. The Surgeon General, Centers for Disease Control (CDC), the Department of Health and Human Services, and other federal and state health agencies continue to publish materials that support the importance of flossing and interdental cleaning, and that advice is available to the public through the National Institute of Dental and Craniofacial Health (NIDCR), [3](/en-us/ce-courses/ce550/references#3) CDC’s Division of Oral Health, [4](/en-us/ce-courses/ce550/references#4) and Healthy People 2020. [5](/en-us/ce-courses/ce550/references#5)

title: Advanced Instrumentation for the General Practice Dental Hygienist
description:
This course serves to broaden the horizons of the general practice dental hygienist in utilizing advanced instrumentation and /or instruments that are not typically in the general practice set up for initial or periodontal recall patient treatment. In addition, alternative fulcrums, patient and clinician positioning that can be utilized to better access certain areas of the mouth and address special dental needs.
In pre-pandemic times, the use of combination therapy, consisting of both hand and power instruments, was considered the norm. Yet, as the concern over aerosol transmitted viruses has drastically come into play, the recommendation for the reduction of the use of power instrumentation, such as the use of ultrasonic scaling and air polishing, must be considered. [1,](/en-us/ce-courses/ce569/references#1)[8](/en-us/ce-courses/ce569/references#8) Although a combination of manual and ultrasonic therapies is very efficient and recommended when treating moderate to deep periodontal pockets, manual scaling cannot be replaced. When dealing with initial PPD greater than 4 mm (medium to deep pockets), manual instrumentation was shown to be the superior in reducing PPD. [2,](/en-us/ce-courses/ce569/references#2) [9](/en-us/ce-courses/ce569/references#9)
The purpose of this course is to broaden the horizons of the general practice dental hygienist in treating some of the special needs of patients by utilizing advanced manual instrumentation and /or instruments that are not typically in the general practice set up for initial or periodontal recall patient treatment.

title: Oral Health Care: A Whole New Language
description:
This course is intended for anyone new to the field of oral health care: dental assisting, dental therapy, dental hygiene, dentistry, dental laboratory technology, or anyone already in the field who would like a dental vocabulary refresher. The most commonly used terms in oral health care will be defined. The professionals associated with oral health care will be discussed. Useful prefixes and suffixes often used to create dental/medical words are presented, as are some common abbreviations.
Although there are many medical terminology textbooks and programs available, they are somewhat daunting in their scope since they cover a wide range of information. The intent of this course is not to provide a dictionary or a comprehensive medical terminology course, but a short course that will limit itself to the words, phrases, prefixes, suffixes and abbreviations that a beginning oral health care provider needs to know. Websites that will provide additional information and links are also included.

title: Radiographic Selection Criteria
description:
__Please Note this course is currently under revision__.
This continuing education course will present and discuss the latest recommendations for prescribing radiographic examinations to assist dentists in the optimal application of radiation safety measures and the appropriate selection of patients for diagnostic imaging. 1,2 These recommendations recapitulate and update the 2012 document and provide decision-making guidance on applicable imaging modalities as well as the safety measures and practices that undergird and support imaging procedures and systems. 1-3 The updated guidance takes the form of consensus recommendations due to limitations in the available evidence acquired from a comprehensive review of dental radiation safety research, guidance from national and international agencies, and regulatory standards, 1,2 Clinicians should base imaging decisions on the patient’s medical and dental histories, clinical examination findings, disease risk assessments, and the presence of specific clinical conditions. 1 Prior to any radiographic examination, previous radiographs should be obtained and reviewed, and any imaging procedure, particularly cone beam computed tomography (CBCT), should be prescribed judiciously to minimize patient radiation exposure. 1 When properly utilized, radiographic imaging examinations contribute to dental treatment decisions that optimize patient care. 1

title: Geriatric Dentistry: Before You Call 911
description:
As our population continues to age, dental teams are increasingly at the frontline of caring for older adults and medically complex patients. This article offers an insightful overview of the essential steps for safely managing geriatric patients, from their first appointment to post-treatment follow-up.
Providing dental care to older adults has become a daily reality in most practices. This course has been thoughtfully designed for dental professionals who recognize the rapidly changing demographics across the United States and the growing need for specialized care. Geriatric dentistry goes beyond oral treatment, it emphasizes understanding each patient’s comprehensive medical and dental profile to enhance their comfort, safety, and overall quality of life.
