title: e-Poster Presentation
description: e-Poster presentations are concise, research-based lectures prepared by students. These pre-selected presentations are grounded in focused research in clinical, health-related dentistry. Presentation topics include original scientific research, clinical innovation case studies, and systematic literature reviews. Participants who attend the full session are eligible to receive three (3) hours of continuing education (CE) credit.Learning Objectives: Analyze current evidence-based research and clinical techniques across multiple dental specialties.Evaluate emerging dental technologies and recent clinical advancements.Identify best practices and key trends shaping the future of the dental profession.Engage with researchers to explore practical applications that enhance patient care.

title: Gingival Pathology: The Good, the Bad and the Ugly
description: This course will inform oral healthcare providers about gingival oral pathology. Benign lesions, reactive lesions, malignant lesions and ulcerative conditions will be discussed.Learning Objectives: Discuss the possible ways in which benign oral pathology presents on the gingivae. Recognize how malignant conditions present on the gingivae. Determine an appropriate treatment plan when gingival lesions present.

title: The Periodontal Ligament Injection: Say Goodbye to the Mandibular Block for Most Lower-Arch Dentistry
description: The proper use and administration of the periodontal ligament (PDL) injection for local anesthesia in the mandibular arch has been proven to be an efficient replacement for the inferior alveolar nerve block. Patients love the alternative local anesthetic protocol of the PDL injection over the mandibular block for many reasons. The proper administration of this injection technique will be a game-changer for your practice and can be immediately incorporated by any clinician.Learning Objectives: Learn how to give an effective PDL injection.Learn the armamentarium and anesthetics that make the PDL injection effective.Learn from actual patients what they feel about local anesthetics, the PDL injection and the mandibular block technique.Understand the anatomy that makes the PDL injection work so well.

title: Baby Bootcamp: Unlocking the Pediatric Code (Part 1 of 4)
description: This two-day interactive lecture and hands-on course equips general dentists with practical evidence-based skills for diagnosing, treatment-planning and managing pediatric patients from infancy through adolescence. Through lectures, case discussions and hands-on workshops, participants will explore pediatric diagnostics, restorative materials, pulp therapy and trauma management. The course also covers behavior guidance, parent communication, and recognition of child abuse and neglect. Emphasizing real-world applications, attendees will analyze pediatric case studies, perform hands-on procedures such as stainless-steel crowns and trauma-splinting, and develop effective strategies for managing children and parents in diverse clinical settings.Learning Objectives: Perform comprehensive pediatric assessment and diagnosis.Develop evidence-based pediatric treatment-planning and clinical decision-making.Manage pediatric dental trauma and emergency care.Apply behavior guidance, parent communication and recognition of abuse.Develop clinical application and hands-on proficiency with pediatric materials and techniques.
requirements: TPVS1, TPVS2, FPVS1 and FPVS2 are a 12-hour bundle.

title: Can Your Dental Team Defend You Against a Lawsuit?
description: Complete documentation is the gold standard for malpractice defense. Every team member plays a vital role in ensuring that documentation is complete. Team members all have responsibilities during patient care to ensure that the dentist and the practice meet accepted standards of care. By analyzing actual malpractice cases, see how the team can all contribute to defending against malpractice and preventing it before it happens.Learning Objectives: Review the concept of standard of care.Learn what is required in clinical documentation.Learn through actual malpractice cases how each member plays a vital role in defense.Understand what standards are accepted for many clinical situations.

title: Collaborative Approaches and Case Presentations with "Outside the Box" Techniques
description: This course will explore collaborative treatment-planning and innovative “outside the box” approaches to managing complex dental cases. It will highlight interdisciplinary strategies that integrate principles from periodontics, prosthodontics, implant dentistry, endodontics and orthodontics to achieve optimal functional and esthetic results. Emphasis will be placed on creative problem-solving, evidence-based decision-making and the application of unconventional yet clinically sound techniques. Through detailed case presentations, the course will illustrate how collaborative and innovative approaches can enhance treatment outcomes and expand clinical versatility.Learning Objectives: Analyze complex dental cases using a multidisciplinary and evidence-based framework.Identify opportunities to incorporate innovative or “outside the box” techniques in treatment-planning.Integrate principles from periodontics, prosthodontics, implant dentistry, endodontics and orthodontics to enhance patient outcomes.

title: Customer Service Skills: Seven Critical Questions that Help Your Patients Say "Yes"
description: Great clinical skills matter, but the right words and patient engagement can make the difference in the patient moving forward with your care. In this impactful course, you'll learn how to build trust from the first interaction and guide patients to confidently say "yes" to the treatment you seek to provide. Discover how small shifts in language and behavior can lead to great results, and walk away with seven powerful questions that inspire connection, confidence and commitment.Learning Objectives: Understand how the initial conversation sets the tone for the entire patient journey — and how to start strong.Discover how one word can shift a patient's mindset, build trust and open the door to deeper engagement.Learn seven powerful, strategic questions to guide patients toward saying "yes" to your care — and the outcome they want.

title: Holy Cow! I Did Not Know You Could Do That with Composite
description: This is not your father’s composite lecture. Dr. John Gammichia practices in the real world where cusps fracture, decay runs deep, and patients can’t always afford the “ideal” buildup and an onlay or crown. So, what do you do next? This lecture challenges traditional restorative thinking and explores how advanced composite techniques can be used to predictably restore large posterior defects. Beginning with Class I and Class II restorations, participants will review adhesion fundamentals and minimally invasive philosophies, then progress to managing large restorations, deep decay, cracked teeth, and multi-cusp composites involving two to five cusps. Dr. Gammichia also addresses concerns surrounding longevity, predictability, and profitability. Attendees will be challenged and will leave thinking, “Holy Cow! I Did Not Know You Could Do That with Composite.”Learning Objectives: Explain current principles of adhesion and minimally invasive dentistry as they relate to posterior composite restorations.Identify clinical situations where large multi-cusp composite restorations may be an appropriate alternative to indirect restorations.Evaluate strategies for managing deep decay, cracks, and extensive posterior defects using direct composite.Assess the longevity, limitations, and practice management considerations of large posterior composite restorations.

title: It's (Medically) Complicated! Successfully Interpreting Patients’ Complex Medication Histories
description: Dental professionals must frequently draw upon their knowledge of pharmacology for optimal care of today’s medically complex dental patients. However, armed with relatively brief exposure to such a vast subject during formal education and faced with ever-changing medication therapy guidelines, many dental professionals simply cannot keep up with the latest trends in disease state management. This program will provide an overview of the dental implications of the prescription medications, nonprescription medications, herbal products and nutritional supplements most frequently used by patients. Special emphasis will be given to delivering this essential information in a format that makes it practical and useful for all members of the dental team.Learning Objectives: Identify the prescription medications most frequently encountered in a patient’s medical history and discuss their indications for use, adverse reactions and impact on dental therapy.Discuss the nonprescription medications most often used by dental patients and explain their impact on dental therapy, including situations that preclude their use.Identify the most popular herbal products and nutritional supplements and discuss their purported uses and dental considerations.
instructors: Tom Viola, RPh, CCP, CDE, CPMP

title: Oral Cancer and the Dental Team - the First Line of Defense
description: This course equips oral healthcare professionals with essential knowledge about oral cancer, including the identification and management of potentially malignant conditions, with a particular focus on leukoplakia. Emphasizing the critical role of prevention, this course highlights evidence-based strategies that clinicians can implement to reduce the risk of oral squamous cell carcinoma.Learning Objectives: Discuss the etiology of potentially malignant conditions of the oral cavity.Recognize how potentially malignant conditions present in the oral cavity.Determine an appropriate treatment plan when potentially malignant conditions are discovered.

title: Powerful Partnerships: Building a Strong Dentist-Hygienist Relationship for a Thriving Practice
description: Top dental teams know that 60% or more of restorative treatment should be planned and enrolled in the hygiene operatory. Co-diagnosis is critical to achieving this goal through optimal patient confidence and case acceptance. Cooperative diagnosis creates a strong sense of team and an empowered environment, allowing hygienists to tap into their talents for education and enrollment. Achieving a high level of collaboration between the doctor and clinical team requires steps to calibrate treatment philosophies, enrollment processes and the use of technology. This interactive course outlines steps that will take diagnostic confidence and effectiveness through the roof!Learning Objectives: Learn how the actions in the first 20 minutes of the hygiene visit affect the entire practice.Optimize technology to increase case acceptance.Review a process to calibrate treatment philosophy between the team and doctor(s) to increase team confidence in restorative co-diagnosis.Learn a three-step system to engage patients in their diagnoses.Learn power words that dramatically increase effectiveness of treatment presentations.

title: The Easiest IPR System: GOST
description: David Galler, DMD, FAACA, created GOST — Galler Ostreicher Spacing Technique — to supercharge Invisalign® interproximal reduction (IPR) cases and create exact interproximal areas with no ledging, no estimating, no guesswork and no damage to adjacent teeth. This revolutionary new technique will take only minutes to create perfect minimally invasive interproximal spaces, even on the tightest teeth. The technique combines new innovations and new specialty patented instruments to make this integral part of adult orthodontic cases stress-free and 100% predictable. This technique is guaranteed to allow you to start treating advanced cases, to reduce refinements and eliminate midcourse corrections.Learning Objectives: Review the strategy and reasons for IPR in orthodontic cases.Understand the shortcomings of current methodologies.Learn the GOST (Galler Ostreicher Spacing Technique).Practice the technique on models.Present complicated orthodontic cases that were completed easily after GOST IPR was performed.

title: When Implants Fail: Understanding, Preventing and Treating Peri-Implantitis
description: Peri-implant diseases represent one of the most significant biological complications affecting the long-term success of dental implants. This course provides an evidence-based review of peri-implant mucositis and peri-implantitis, with emphasis on disease prevalence, etiology, contributing risk factors and diagnostic criteria. Participants will learn how to identify patients at increased risk, interpret clinical and radiographic findings, and establish accurate baseline records for early detection. Both non-surgical and surgical treatment strategies will be discussed, including cause-related therapy, adjunctive modalities, and contemporary surgical approaches for advanced disease. Through case-based presentations, this course will illustrate clinical decision-making from diagnosis through treatment and long-term maintenance, while highlighting the importance of preserving natural dentition in patients susceptible to peri-implant disease.Learning Objectives: Describe the prevalence, epidemiology, and classification of peri-implant mucositis and peri-implantitis based on current literature.Identify the primary etiologic factor and contributing local, systemic, and prosthetic risk factors associated with peri-implant diseases.Recognize clinical and radiographic signs used in the diagnosis and risk assessment of peri-implant diseases, including the importance of baseline documentation.Differentiate non-surgical and surgical treatment modalities for peri-implant diseases and determine appropriate indications for each.Apply evidence-based maintenance strategies and clinical decision-making principles to improve long-term implant outcomes and reduce the risk of disease recurrence.

title: Yes! Crown Lengthening CAN Enhance Restorative Results
description: Frustrated by not getting good impressions due to deep caries or fractures? Planning esthetic restorations and dealing with a "gummy smile"? Enhance your restorative results by learning about solutions to these everyday problems. Experience an in-depth look at the concepts and applications for both posterior and anterior crown lengthening.Learn indications and contraindications, plus key concepts like biologic width as it applies to improving restorative results. You will see how soft-tissue management alone is not enough and how bone management is a key to success.Learning Objectives: Achieve predictable impressions every time.Learn to apply principles of biologic width and ferrule as they apply to comfortable and esthetic restorations.Understand causes of red or sore gums around crowns.

title: From Clinical Dentistry to Organizational Leadership: Applying Clinical Principles to Entrepreneurial Growth
description: Dentistry today requires more than technical excellence; it demands leadership, innovation and the ability to manage complex care delivery systems. This course examines how the principles that guide high-quality clinical dentistry, diagnostic reasoning, treatment-planning, precision and evidence-based care can be effectively applied to the development and leadership of dental organizations. Dr. Bob Martino, a practicing dentist and entrepreneur, shares how his clinical training provided the foundation for building and leading multilocation dental practices and healthcare-related companies. Through clinical case examples and operational frameworks, participants will learn how to apply a clinician’s mindset to leadership, team development and systems-based practice growth. Emphasis will be placed on maintaining patient-centered outcomes and clinical quality while scaling operations or pursuing alternative professional pathways. The course will also explore the decision-making process for transitioning from full-time clinical practice into broader leadership or administrative roles, whether within group practice settings, dental services organizations or other healthcare enterprises, while ensuring adherence to professional ethics and quality standards.Learning Objectives: Apply evidence-based clinical reasoning and diagnostic frameworks to leadership and organizational decision making in dental settings.Identify and analyze parallels between clinical competencies and entrepreneurial skills, including treatment-planning, precision and risk management.Develop strategies to maintain clinical quality and patient-centered outcomes when scaling a dental practice up or expanding into multiple locations.Evaluate indicators and timing for transitioning from direct patient care to broader administrative or leadership roles within dental organizations or healthcare systems.Implement ethical, evidence-based management principles to guide innovation, operational efficiency and int

title: Insurance Reimagined: A Strategic Tool for Dental Wealth
description: Dentists face unique financial risks — from income dependency on clinical ability to practice ownership liabilities and long-term transition planning. Participants will gain a practical understanding of how different types of insurance — such as disability, life and specialty coverage — can be intentionally integrated into a comprehensive financial strategy. Through real-world examples and planning frameworks, attendees will learn how insurance decisions impact personal wealth, business continuity and generational outcomes, particularly for high-income professionals and practice owners.Learning Objectives: Understand the unique insurance risks dentists face, including income interruption, practice ownership exposure and transition-planning.Reframe insurance as a proactive wealth-building and financial-planning tool rather than a standalone expense.Identify key insurance solutions — such as disability and life insurance — and understand how they support cash flow, tax efficiency and long-term planning for dentists.Recognize common insurance planning gaps among dental professionals that can undermine personal wealth and practice value.Integrate insurance strategies into a broader financial plan that supports practice growth, retirement readiness and legacy goals.

title: Periodontal Disease: Window to Health and Wellness
description: Scientific literature is increasingly recognizing that the presence of periodontal disease is associated with a wide variety of health and medically related disorders. Periodontal disease [LH1.1]is associated with chronic inflammation as well as the oral and gut microbiomes. As such, the role of the dentist and hygienist is directly related to making people healthy. Having an understanding of this relationship opens up new options for participation in the patient’s overall well-being, an improved quality of life and improved longevity.Learning Objectives: Understand the role that oral disease(s), specifically periodontal disease, has on overall health.Understand the developing science that involves the oral and gut microbiomes and how these are related.Realize the expanding role of the dental hygienist within the practice of dentistry.Improve communication between dentists and their medical colleagues as it relates to improved prevention as part of healthcare.
instructors: Dennis R. Bailey, DDS, Dip ABDSM, FAASM, Dip ABOP

title: Fill the Void: A Hands-On Guide to Socket Grafting
description: Tooth extraction is just the beginning — what happens next can determine the long-term success of your restorative plan. This hands-on course demystifies socket preservation, guiding clinicians through practical, evidence-based techniques to maintain alveolar bone following extraction. Participants will learn how to select the right allograft materials, barrier membranes and closure strategies for predictable outcomes. Through detailed demonstrations and guided exercises, you’ll gain confidence in handling grafting materials and managing soft tissue in single-tooth sites.Learning Objectives: Identify clinical indications and contraindications for socket preservation following tooth extraction.Select appropriate grafting materials and membranes based on defect type and treatment goals.Demonstrate step-by-step socket grafting techniques, including graft placement and flap management.Recognize and manage common complications or pitfalls in socket preservation.
instructors: Mai-Ly Duong, DMD, MPH, MAEd, MAGD, FACD, FICD, FPFA

title: Enhance Your Liability IQ: Top Reasons Dentists Get Sued for Malpractice
description: Dentist’s Advantage and CNA released their third published analysis of dental claims from the last five years. Professional liability data provides insight into the underlying causes in liability cases because it can reveal missteps, clinical errors, patterns of communication and judgment failures that contributed to adverse events.Using case studies and claim data, dentists can examine top areas of liability through the experiences of their peers. Most importantly, this session provides you with practice recommendations that you can use to help prevent or defend against malpractice allegations while also enhancing patient safety.Learning Objectives: Identify the leading allegations made against dentists in malpractice lawsuits.Define average costs to defend dentists in malpractice lawsuits.Identify key risk management tools that dentists can incorporate into their practices to help improve patient safety and reduce risk and error.
instructors:
Jennifer Flynn, CPHRM
Ronald R. Zentz, RPh, DDS, FAGD, CPHRM

title: Baby Bootcamp: Unlocking the Pediatric Code (Part 2 of 4)
description: This two-day interactive lecture and hands-on course equips general dentists with practical evidence-based skills for diagnosing, treatment-planning and managing pediatric patients from infancy through adolescence. Through lectures, case discussions and hands-on workshops, participants will explore pediatric diagnostics, restorative materials, pulp therapy and trauma management. The course also covers behavior guidance, parent communication, and recognition of child abuse and neglect. Emphasizing real-world applications, attendees will analyze pediatric case studies, perform hands-on procedures such as stainless-steel crowns and trauma-splinting, and develop effective strategies for managing children and parents in diverse clinical settings.Learning Objectives: Perform comprehensive pediatric assessment and diagnosis.Develop evidence-based pediatric treatment-planning and clinical decision-making.Manage pediatric dental trauma and emergency care.Apply behavior guidance, parent communication and recognition of abuse.Develop clinical application and hands-on proficiency with pediatric materials and techniques.
requirements: TPVS1, TPVS2, FPVS1 and FPVS2 are a 12-hour bundle.

title: Classification of Periodontal and Peri-Implant Diseases
description: The course will provide an in-depth review of the current classification systems for periodontal and peri-implant diseases and conditions as established by the 2017 World Workshop on the Classification of Periodontal and Peri-Implant Diseases and Conditions. Participants will gain the knowledge and confidence to accurately diagnose periodontal, gingival and peri-implant conditions based on etiology, clinical presentation and radiographic findings. Emphasis is placed on accurate diagnosis, sound treatment-planning and prognosis-determination.Learning Objectives: Differentiate between various forms of periodontal, gingival and peri-implant diseases based on the 2017 classification criteria.Interpret clinical and radiographic findings to establish accurate diagnoses and assess disease severity.Apply the classification system to develop evidence-based treatment plans and determine prognosis.Integrate diagnostic and classification principles into comprehensive periodontal and implant maintenance protocols.

title: Communication Skills Workshop
description: Effective communication is the cornerstone of exceptional patient care and team performance. In this dynamic interactive workshop, participants will learn how to communicate with clarity, confidence and empathy, transforming everyday interactions into more opportunities for increased patient satisfaction and practice growth. This program explores how words, tone and nonverbal cues shape perception, influence decision-making and impact trust. Attendees will gain practical strategies to strengthen relationships with patients, overcome objections to increase patient satisfaction and improve productivity.Learning Objectives: Listen actively and respond intentionally.Present office protocols with confidence.Introduce treatment comfortablyNavigate difficult or emotional conversations with professionalism.Strengthen internal communications for smoother team alignment.Build credibility and trust that enhances patient acceptance.

title: Crown Lengthening Hands-On Workshop
description: This workshop provides an opportunity to experience crown lengthening principles and procedures for functional restorative issues as discussed in the morning prerequisite lecture. See how these techniques can solve common everyday restorative challenges like retention, open margins or inflamed tissue around crowns. Biologic width and ferrule are emphasized to enable participants to perform crown lengthening surgery if they choose or make more informed referrals if they prefer. Participants will perform flap surgery and suturing on pig jaws and review the required osseous recontouring on models.Learning Objectives: Understand surgical steps and techniques.Understand key principles of required osseous surgery.Review case examples in a treatment-planning session.

title: Dental Marketing Mastery: Schedule 37 More New Patients Per Month
description: During this course, Becky Spicer will share an all-new dental marketing approach that attracts and schedules profitable new patients without relying on deep discounts or low-reimbursement insurance. You will learn the tools and cutting-edge strategies necessary to leverage your dental practice online, get found by new patients and increase your practice income considerably. This course will cover brand new, never-before-shared secrets to attracting high-end new patients and will reveal how you can turn your front office staff into your most powerful asset. Plus, Spicer will share a carefully structured three-part dental marketing formula that helped one practice go from opening its doors to earning $5 million in a single year.Learning Objectives: Learn how to create marketing systems to attract and schedule more new patients and fully control your patient flow so you can stop relying on low-reimbursement insurance plans or inconsistent internal marketing. Learn how to differentiate your practice within your market, become remarkable and show patients why they should choose you over other dentists. Learn how to maximize your online visibility and show up consistently in front of your ideal patient. Learn how to develop a highly deliberate and dialed-in conversion system so you can generate more revenue from every patient and stop missing opportunities.

title: Holy Cow! I Did Not Know You Could Do That with Composite Hands-On Workshop
description: This afternoon hands-on workshop is designed to build on the concepts introduced in the morning lecture. After seeing what is possible with posterior composites, participants often ask, “How do I actually do that?” This workshop provides the answer. Attendees will begin developing the skills needed to create exquisite posterior composite restorations using the same materials and techniques Dr. Gammichia uses in daily practice. Participants will work through the complete restorative process for large three- and four-cusp posterior composites, focusing on adhesion, composite selection, layering techniques, and the development of both primary and secondary anatomy. Participants will return to their practices ready to “wow” their patients and change their practice.Learning Objectives: Apply proper adhesive protocols for large posterior composite restorations.Demonstrate composite layering techniques for restoring multi-cusp posterior teeth.Develop primary and secondary anatomy in large posterior composite restorations.Integrate lecture concepts into hands-on clinical techniques for immediate use in practice.

title: Next-Level Denture and Overdenture Techniques: Combining Traditional and Cutting-Edge Methods (Part 4 of 4)
description: Join Drs. Joseph Massad and Michael Scherer for an engaging two-day program that blends practical fundamentals with the latest innovations in removable and implant overdentures. Designed for dentists who want real-world techniques that they can apply immediately, this course covers patient assessment, impression accuracy, occlusal records and delivery protocols. Hands-on exercises include analog and digital impressions, jaw recording, implant overdenture attachments, and troubleshooting strategies.With decades of combined teaching and clinical experience, the presenters will share simplified, evidence-based methods to help you expand treatment options, improve patient satisfaction and build confidence in both standard removable and implant prosthodontics.Learning Objectives: Review simplified and practical concepts in removable and implant prostheses and define their advantages and disadvantages versus past methods.Describe the clinical techniques in developing the final prosthesis for transitional and definitive cases.Learn fundamental denture techniques to assist in fabricating the implant overdenture prosthesis.
instructors:
Joseph J. Massad, DDS
Michael D. Scherer, DMD, MS, FACP, Dip ABOI/ID
requirements: WPJM1, WPJM2, TPJM1 and TPJM2 are a 12-hour bundle.

title: Oral Manifestations of Sexually Transmitted Infections
description: This course is designed to inform the participant about how certain sexually transmitted infections manifest in the oral cavity. Prevention and management of sexually transmitted infections affecting the oral cavity will also be discussed.Learning Objectives: Discuss sexually transmitted infections and how they may affect the oral cavity.Describe oral presentations of select sexually transmitted infections.Determine how to properly manage patients presenting with oral manifestations of sexually transmitted infections.
topic: 730 Oral Medicine, Oral Diagnosis, Oral Pathology
instructors: Ashley Clark, DDS, FACD, FICD, FAAOMP
metadata: {"extracted_from":"agd-eventscribe","detail_url":"https://agd2026.eventscribe.net/ajaxcalls/PresentationInfo.asp?PresentationID=1716219","subject_code":"730 Oral Medicine, Oral Diagnosis, Oral Pathology","session_notes":""}

title: Placing Attachments for Clear Aligners
description: Almost every clear aligner system requires the placement of attachments on specific teeth for retention and to effect certain movements. The better the placement, the better the end result. Join David Galler, DMD, FAACA, as he teaches and demonstrates the most effective way to accurately and consistently place attachments on teeth for use in clear aligners. Perfecting this technique will result in fewer refinements and elimination of midcourse corrections. In this hands-on program, doctors will perfect placing attachments on typodonts in real time.Learning Objectives: Learn why attachments are critical to clear aligner therapy.Understand the purposes of the most common attachments used by Align Technology in their Invisalign® clear aligner system.Practice placing attachments on typodonts.Become proficient in removing excess adhesive material.

title: Porcelain Laminate Veneers: From No Prep to Full Prep and More
description: In this course, Dr. Nash will illustrate the use of ceramic laminates to enhance smiles. He will outline his five rules for no-preparation veneers and give examples of where they can be used. Minimal preparation to full preparation as well as 360-degree laminate cases will be shown in a step-by-step fashion using clinical images and video. Clinical situations where mandibular veneers contribute to both esthetics and function will be illustrated and discussed.Learning Objectives: Learn Dr. Nash’s five rules for no-prep veneers.Identify where preparation is needed.Discover preparation and placement techniques for porcelain veneers.

title: The Changing Standards of Care in 2026
description: Do you understand what the words “standards of care” mean? Do you know that the standards of care in dentistry are constantly evolving, informed by evidence-based dentistry and ongoing research? Do you know how standards of care are used in court to defend dentists from malpractice or to find them guilty of negligence? This course will cover standards of care for many clinical situations in 2026. It is imperative for clinicians and their staff to know the current standards of care and incorporate them into their practice. See actual malpractice cases that have been heard in court. You will leave with a much better understanding of these questions.Learning Objectives: Learn the standard of care for many clinical situations.Learn why standards of care are so important to follow in your practices.Learn latest changes for standards of care in 2026.

title: What Patients Aren’t Telling You About Their Self Medication
description: In today’s information-saturated world, many patients turn to over-the-counter (OTC) medications, dietary supplements, home remedies and even unregulated substances to manage their own health, including dental pain and anxiety. This course explores the growing trend of patient self-medication and the significant implications it has for oral healthcare. Dentists and dental hygienists will learn to identify self-medication behaviors, recognize potential risks and interactions, and engage patients in nonjudgmental, informed discussion. Emphasis is placed on pharmacologic literacy, medical history assessment, and strategies to ensure safe, effective and ethical dental care in a self-directed health culture.Learning Objectives: Identify prescription and OTC drugs, dietary supplements, and unregulated substances patients use to self-medicate.Recognize signs and risks of self-medication, including drug interactions, masking of symptoms, delayed diagnosis and adverse oral effects. Evaluate potential interactions between self-administered substances and dental medications or procedures.
instructors: Tom Viola, RPh, CCP, CDE, CPMP

title: Reignite and Reconnect: Dental Volunteerism
description: Volunteering profoundly impacts dental professionals, serving as a powerful catalyst for professional advancement, personal well-being and a purpose-driven reconnection to the field. This engagement facilitates authentic relationships within professional circles, transforming it from a charitable act into a vital strategy for long-term career resilience and holistic health. Physiologically, strategic volunteering acts as a critical buffer against burnout by triggering a neurochemical “helper’s high” that reduces cortisol levels and restores a sense of purpose. Research indicates that consistent service can significantly improve mental well-being by reducing symptoms of depression, anxiety and stress. By engaging in diverse volunteer opportunities, both within and outside of dentistry, professionals can bridge skill gaps, expand their networks, discover new growth opportunities and reconnect with their fundamental purpose as care providers.Learning Objectives: Analyze volunteerism as a fundamental strategy for fostering purpose-driven reconnection and long-term engagement within the dental profession.Evaluate the physiological and psychological benefits of clinical and community service, specifically their impact on mood regulation, stress reduction and the mitigation of emotional depletion.Assess how consistent volunteer engagement increases life satisfaction and expands high-value social and professional networks.Synthesize strategies for using service-based work to restore professional resilience, neutralize isolation, and achieve a renewed sense of clinical and personal fulfillment.

title: Keys to a Successful Out-of-Network Transition
description: Many of us spend hours negotiating with insurance companies without understanding how these plans even work. We feel increasingly held captive by the fee schedules dictated by our contracted relationships. We want to provide the best care possible, pay and retain our teams better, and maybe even make a profit, yet we feel overwhelmed by skyrocketing costs coupled with flattening compensation. How do we break free?In this course, Christal will share years of experience taking a four-location practice that once had a 50% HMO patient base to a practice that is primarily fee-for-service and out-of-network. She hopes to empower you to take the next steps to regain control of your fees in a way that honors your practice and patients.Learning Objectives: Understand how insurance networks actually work.Create a successful plan for unwinding insurance participation.Liberate your fee schedules in a way that contributes to growth and success for your practice.

title: Direct Implant Provisionals: Two-Shade Technique
description: When determining provisional restorative options for anterior implants, the option of placing a highly esthetic, direct provisional restoration quickly becomes the patient’s and clinician’s choice for anterior implants. The question becomes: How do you create one yourself, with no lab bill and minimal chair time? This lecture will guide you step by step to create the result both the patient and you will love.Learning Objectives: Learn to fabricate in-office direct implant provisionals that rival the dental lab’s.Achieve implant temporization that eliminates the Essix retainer.Learn how to control the tissue to achieve esthetic anterior implant results.Have peace of mind for your patients and yourself when temporizing anterior implants.Save time and money by avoiding a lab step by creating beautiful direct provisionals with the two-shade composite technique.

Registration will not be required, and seats will be available on a first-come, first-served basis."}

title: Burnout, Boundaries and Balance in Dentistry
description: Dentistry is a high-stress profession, with rising rates of burnout, anxiety and disengagement across the entire dental team. This session will explore the early signs of burnout and the impact of chronic stress on physical, emotional and professional well-being. Using evidence-based research, real-world dental practice scenarios and guided self-assessment tools, participants will learn practical strategies for boundary-setting, time management, effective communication and healthy coping. Attendees will leave with actionable techniques to improve resilience, restore balance and support long-term sustainability in dental practice.Learning Objectives: Identify early physiological, emotional and behavioral signs of burnout affecting dental professionals and team members.Differentiate healthy and unhealthy coping strategies commonly used in high-stress dental environments.Apply practical boundary-setting and communication techniques to reduce conflict and emotional overload in dental practices.Implement time-management strategies that support well-being while maintaining professional performance and patient care.Develop a personalized action plan to improve resilience, balance and long-term sustainability in dental careers.

title: Creating a Financial Integrity Scorecard: A Framework for Dental Professionals
description: Dental professionals often excel clinically but lack a comprehensive framework for financial independence, often struggling with liquidity, investment planning, and tax strategy. This program closes that gap by providing education and tools to build lasting financial health and confidence. Participants will leave with a "Financial Integrity Scorecard" to assess their status across five key areas, identifying if they are in a danger zone or reaching their optimal potential.Learning Objectives: Implement strategies to protect personal and business assets, secure income, and preserve legacy through estate and risk management.Apply methods to convert active income into recurring income streams and build multi-generational wealth.Design a comprehensive investment strategy including business assets, real estate, qualified plans, insurance, and alternative investments.Develop tax-smart financial habits to restructure debt and increase after-tax cash flow.

title: FE - Fellowship Exam
description: Registration for the exam closes on Wednesday, June 24.Taking the Fellowship Exam is one step toward earning the prestigious Fellowship Award (FAGD). Members can take the exam as early as their first year of active membership. The exam consists of 252 multiple-choice questions focusing on clinical applications of accepted dental knowledge, techniques, and procedures. The Fellowship Exam is offered as a paper-and-pencil test and is given in a four-hour session. A government-issued photo ID is required for entrance. Electronic devices, such as cell phones, PDAs, or computers, are not allowed and must be checked at the door before entering the exam room. Confidential test results are sent to each examinee approximately 10 weeks after the exam. Exam results are not given over the telephone or via email.The Fellowship Exam fee is separate from the meeting registration fee. To take the exam, you must include your dental license number and the state the license is held at the time of registration.Note: No CE credit hours are awarded for taking the exam.

title: Baby Bootcamp: Unlocking the Pediatric Code (Part 3 of 4)
description: This two-day interactive lecture and hands-on course equips general dentists with practical evidence-based skills for diagnosing, treatment-planning and managing pediatric patients from infancy through adolescence. Through lectures, case discussions and hands-on workshops, participants will explore pediatric diagnostics, restorative materials, pulp therapy and trauma management. The course also covers behavior guidance, parent communication, and recognition of child abuse and neglect. Emphasizing real-world applications, attendees will analyze pediatric case studies, perform hands-on procedures such as stainless-steel crowns and trauma-splinting, and develop effective strategies for managing children and parents in diverse clinical settings.Learning Objectives: Perform comprehensive pediatric assessment and diagnosis.Develop evidence-based pediatric treatment-planning and clinical decision-making.Manage pediatric dental trauma and emergency care.Apply behavior guidance, parent communication and recognition of abuse.Develop clinical application and hands-on proficiency with pediatric materials and techniques.

title: Dental Burnout
description: In this three-hour laugh-a-thon, David Galler, DMD, FAACA, will review all the crazy idiosyncrasies that dentists have. Take his patented 13-question quiz during the course to see if you suffer from mild, moderate or severe anxiety. Bring the whole team, and laugh, learn, and grow into a dental team that doesn't let the minutiae of daily dental stress get them down. Burnout of dentists and team members is on the rise across the country. Don't become cynical and start despising work. Recognize the signs. Laugh hard during this course.Learning Objectives: Provide insight into why dental offices are high stress.Learn strategies for successful communication skills and stress reduction.Understand the importance of teamwork.Learn how to live a happier life.

title: Endo Unlocked: Build Confidence, Master Technique
description: With the advent of new technologies and a focus on dentin preservation, endodontic treatment is rapidly progressing. This course is designed to present clinical and mindset tips to get you up to speed and proficient with modern endodontic treatment. Topics covered include risk awareness and preparation, diagnosis, cone-beam computed tomography, anesthesia, perforation prevention, safe access, effective canal location methods, working length determination, canal instrumentation, irrigation, and obturation. Endodontic treatment can feel stressful in a busy dental practice. Examples of daily in-office wellness offerings will be presented to help the dental practitioner and team remain calm, centered, and focused on safe, efficient and successful endodontic treatment.Learning Objectives: Learn the latest technology and concepts related to modern endodontic treatment.Gain clinical skills, confidence and knowledge related to endodontic treatment.Learn processes and techniques to create more predictable outcomes with endodontic cases.Learn wellness practices that help manage the stressors related to endodontic treatment in the dental office.
instructors: Brett Gilbert, DDS, FICD, Dip ABE

title: Enhancing Restorative Results with Soft-Tissue Grafting
description: Frustrated by recession around recently placed crowns or unsightly recession around a cuspid? Learn evidence-based solutions for these and other types of soft-tissue challenges that practitioners face daily, such as how to cover exposed roots and predictably stop or prevent recession. In this course, we will discuss methods to solve these problems and enhance restorative results by using both free gingival and connective tissue grafts. Gain peace of mind knowing the gingiva stays in place at the margins of newly placed esthetic crowns.Learning Objectives: Understand diagnostic criteria for each graft.See indications and limitations for each graft.Learn to predictably stop or prevent recession in the esthetic zone.Explore when and when not to graft.

title: From Gross Anatomy to Clinical Practice: Cadaveric Dissection of the Head and Neck for Dentists (Part 1 of 2)
description: This comprehensive course offers an in-depth revisit to head-and-neck anatomy — an area many clinicians have not explored since dental school. Through a detailed, clinically focused lecture followed by intensive hands-on cadaver dissection, participants will bridge traditional anatomical understanding with the latest discoveries and contemporary clinical relevance.Emphasis will be placed on the identification of key neurovascular structures, surgical landmarks and anatomical variations essential for safe and effective dental practice. This course is designed to enhance spatial understanding, procedural confidence and evidence-based clinical decision-making for general dentists and specialists alike.Learning Objectives: Identify critical neurovascular structures of the head and neck relevant to dentistry.Apply anatomical knowledge to improve injection techniques, implant placement and surgical approaches.Integrate evidence-based anatomical understanding into clinical decision-making.Correlate cadaveric findings with clinical imaging and surgical procedures to enhance safety and precision.
instructors:
Jessica Immonen, PhD, MS
Joe Iwanaga, DDS, PhD
requirements: FPJI1 and FPJI2 are a 6-hour bundle.

title: Fundamentals of Forensic Odontology
description: This introductory course explores the role of forensic odontology within the broader field of forensic science. Students will learn the fundamentals of dental identification, age estimation and bite mark analysis, along with the ability to recognize human abuse and neglect. The course highlights the forensic odontologist’s contributions to medicolegal investigations, emphasizing collaboration with law enforcement and medical examiners. By the end, participants will understand how dental evidence supports human identification and justice in forensic contexts.Learning Objectives: Explain the fundamental principles and scope of forensic odontology within the field of forensic science.Apply basic methods of dental identification, age estimation and bite mark analysis to forensic cases.Recognize indicators of human abuse and neglect through dental and oral evidence.Describe the role and responsibilities of the forensic odontologist in medicolegal investigations and interdisciplinary collaboration.

title: Porcelain Veneer Preparation Workshop
description: This hands-on workshop provides dentists with a practical, step-by-step approach to porcelain veneer preparation. Participants will prepare typodont models while guided demonstrations highlight ideal preparation design, depth control, margin placement, and tooth conservation. Clinical case studies will be presented to reinforce preparation principles and support predictable esthetic and functional outcomes.Learning Objectives: Demonstrate proper preparation techniques for porcelain veneers.Select and use appropriate burs for efficient and conservative preparation.Identify critical areas of preparation that impact veneer fit, esthetics, and longevity.Apply preparation principles to clinical case scenarios.

title: Transition Issues for Sellers and Buyers-Preparing to Sell or Purchase a Dental Practice
description: This course will dive into the process of preparing to either purchase or sell a dental practice. Potential buyers and sellers will be guided through the necessary steps. We will cover the process, including bank financing; legal issues, such as dealing with landlords and office leases; and the due diligence a dentist needs to perform when buying. We will also cover what a sale of a dental practice to a dental services organization (DSO) looks like and will delve into why a seller should or should not entertain selling to a DSO.Learning Objectives: Be prepared to take steps to buy or sell a dental practice.Discuss legal issues, including the purchase and sales agreement and associated agreements for sellers.Discuss issues to determine whether a seller should sell his or her practice to a DSO.Review the due diligence process buyers should go through when buying a practice and what sellers need to be ready for.
instructors:
Arthur Wiederman, CPA
Jason Wood, Esq.
Justin Klingshirn

title: Waiting to Inhale! The Fall and Rise of Cannabis, Cannabinoids and Terpenes
description: The U.S. Drug Enforcement Administration (DEA) considers cannabis to have no accepted medical use. Therefore, it is illegal to possess. Yet, almost every state has enacted legislation aimed at legalizing or decriminalizing cannabis for medical or recreational use. Despite this rise in social and legal acceptance, public health concerns regarding the use of cannabis remain, especially among dental professionals, since the major routes of administration of cannabis involve the oral cavity. This program will introduce dental professionals to the pharmacology of cannabis, including its various dosage forms, routes of administration and adverse reactions. Special emphasis will be placed on the effects of cannabis use on oral health and dental treatment, as well as the proposed uses of cannabis in dentistry for the management of patient anxiety and odontogenic pain.Learning Objectives: Discuss the history and various types of cannabis, as well as its current legal status. Describe the pharmacology of cannabis, including its mechanism of action, routes of administration, available formulations and proposed uses in dentistry. Explore the dental considerations of cannabis, including potential treatment modifications and strategies for patient care-planning.
instructors: Tom Viola, RPh, CCP, CDE, CPMP

title: What's on Your Radar? Putting Your Numbers to Work
description: This dynamic course shows attendees how to translate data into powerful insights that spark real change. Learn how the right statistics can motivate teams, boost productivity and improve practice performance. Learn what statistics really matter to improve team effectiveness and increase practice success.Learning Objectives: Learn how technology can assist in developing accurate performance indicators.Discover what data is important to monitor and when it's time to schedule strategic planning.Involve the team in knowing the numbers to engage them in practice growth.

title: Infection Control Update
description: This seminar will primarily focus on a practical discussion of the most recent dental infection control recommendations. Discussion sections include hand hygiene, sharps safety, recommended vaccinations, personal protective equipment, instrument and handpiece reprocessing, environmental surface disinfection, and dental water treatment.Learning Objectives: Describe application of standard precautions as the foundation of infection prevention.Understand the most recent updates for CDC and OSHA infection control recommendations and regulations.Comprehend practical strategies that can minimize dental unit waterline contamination.

Please select to participate in either FLJM1 OR FLJM2; you may not register for both sessions."}

title: Protecting Your License Against Common State Board of Dentistry Matters
description: According to the National Practitioner Data Bank, since 2010, there have been more reported adverse actions against dentists annually in the United States than medical malpractice reported payments. The presentation will highlight common dental license complaints seen in the Dentist’s Advantage/CNA program made to state dental boards. This session will focus on the disciplinary process; the role of dental boards; and the duties, responsibilities and rights of dentists in the process.Learning Objectives: Understand the role, authority and mission of state dental boards.Describe common dental license complaints made to state dental boards.Discuss possible outcomes of the disciplinary process along with strategies to proactively protect your license.

title: Leukoplakia in Practice: What to Recognize and How to Respond
description: This course offers a brief overview of leukoplakia with an emphasis on recognition and clinical decision-making. Participants will learn to identify leukoplakia, distinguish it from other white plaques, and consider key elements of the differential diagnosis. The course also addresses appropriate management when leukoplakia is discovered, including follow-up care, helping clinicians respond confidently and appropriately in clinical practice.Learning Objectives: Distinguish leukoplakia from other white plaques.Discuss the differential diagnosis of leukoplakia.Determine the proper course of action when leukoplakia is discovered.
topic: 730 Oral Medicine, Oral Diagnosis, Oral Pathology
instructors: Ashley Clark, DDS, FACD, FICD, FAAOMP

title: Oral Radiology Mysteries: Unseen Case Insights
description: In this presentation, we will dive into a variety of intriguing and complex cases encountered in oral radiology. Using two-dimensional imaging techniques like full-mouth series and panoramic radiographs, alongside the advanced capabilities of cone beam computed tomography, we’ll explore a range of radiographic findings. Each case will be discussed in a detailed case-presentation format, highlighting diagnostic challenges, management strategies and follow-up protocols. Prepare for a comprehensive exploration of how these imaging modalities aid in clinical decision-making and enhance patient care.Learning Objectives: Correlate radiographic findings with the final diagnosis.Develop comprehensive management plans.Plan the radiographic follow-up and be able to treatment-plan.
topic: 730 Oral Medicine or Oral Diagnosis or Oral Pathology

title: Unveiling the Untold Link: Oral Health – Total Health
description: “Unveiling the Untold Link: Oral Health–Total Health” explores the powerful, often overlooked connection between the mouth and the body. This course invites healthcare professionals, wellness educators and curious minds to reimagine oral health as a vital pillar of systemic wellness. Through clinical insights, cultural context and holistic strategies, participants will uncover how oral conditions can influence inflammation, chronic disease, mental health and even intimacy. Rooted in science and elevated by storytelling, this course empowers learners to advocate for whole-person care — starting with the smile.Learning Objectives: Identify key systemic conditions linked to oral health.Explain how chronic stress and cortisol overload can disrupt oral microbiomes and intimacy-related wellness.Integrate oral health education into broader wellness conversations with patients, clients or communities.Advocate for culturally competent, emotionally intelligent approaches to oral-systemic care across diverse populations.

title: Stop Overpaying Your Taxes and Protect Your Wealth!
description: A law firm that once sued you is now protecting you! We will reveal the tactics trial lawyers and the IRS use to take what you earn. Discover the tax and wealth creation strategies you are missing out on.Learning Objectives: Gain insights into asset protection strategies and methods to safeguard your high-valued belongings.Explore the essentials of estate-planning and develop a comprehensive plan to protect and transfer assets to your heirs while eliminating probate court.Learn about specific structures that will protect your license from the National Practitioner Data Bank.

title: Red, White and Urgent: Recognizing the Clinical Warning Signs of Oral Cancer
description: This course will review the essential steps of comprehensive oral cancer screening, with an emphasis on identifying the clinical features of suspicious red and white lesions within the oral cavity. Participants will explore diagnostic adjuncts and emerging technologies designed to aid in the early detection of oral cancer. The course will also provide a concise overview of referral pathways and management considerations for patients with potentially malignant oral lesions. The presentation will conclude with interactive clinical case discussions to reinforce key concepts.Learning Objectives: Review the steps of an extra-oral and intra-oral exam for oral cancer screening.Recognize clinical features of suspicious white and red lesions in the oral cavity.Gain familiarity with the technological advancements and products to assist in early oral cancer detection.Understand the referral pathway and how oral precancerous and cancerous lesions are managed.
topic: 730 Oral Medicine, Oral Diagnosis, Oral Pathology
instructors: Sonal S. Shah, DDS, FAAOMP, FAAOM, FPFA

title: Thriving Not Surviving: Leading a Progressive Inflammation Management Program
description: In this course, dentists and hygienists will learn leadership roles for successful implementation of guided biofilm therapy (GBT), including the development of a thriving inflammation management program in their respective dental practices. Attendees will gain actionable insights and strategies to confidently lead their teams, optimize patient outcomes, and transition from merely surviving to truly thriving in their approach to oral healthcare delivery.Learning Objectives: Understand the fundamental principles and benefits of GBT.Identify key leadership responsibilities for both dental hygienists and dentists in GBT implementation.Develop a strategic plan for integrating GBT into existing workflows.Measure and monitor the success of a progressive inflammation management program.

title: Where There’s a Mill, There’s a Way
description: Not only has dentistry evolved from the Stone Age to the digital age, it has also evolved from the limitations of single-unit chairside milling systems. This course explores the possibilities of a technology-based in-office system for fabrication of provisional and final crown-and-bridge restorations. From single-unit crowns to full-mouth restorations, the addition of an in-office mill improves quality, efficiency and profitability.Learning Objectives: Apply digital concepts to expand current clinical capabilities.Select the appropriate software and hardware needed for an in-office technology-based crown-and-bridge lab.Compare and contrast the capabilities of four-axis versus five-axis milling machines.
instructors: James W. Gibney, DMD, JD, MAGD

Registration will not be required, and seats will be available on a first-come, first-served basis."}

title: The Age of Clear Aligners, Part 1: Foundations for Today’s Emerging Dentist
description: Dentistry is constantly evolving. What were once hallmarks of the profession are often replaced by new advances and modern techniques. The decade from 2020–2030 will be remembered as the Age of Clear Aligners, as orthodontics has transitioned from a limited specialty to being considered everyday dentistry.In this introductory session, Galler will explore the major transitions that have taken place in dentistry over the past 30 years, with a focus on how clear aligner therapy has become integrated into the modern dental practice. Designed for students and new dentists, this course will provide foundational insight into current trends and tools shaping contemporary patient care.Learning Objectives: Review the history of clear aligners and its evolution in the dental practice.Understand the wave of clear aligner dentistry.Recognize the role of orthodontics in everyday general practice.
requirements: This course is an introductory session and available to students and new dentists only. Note: The Age of Clear Aligners, Part 2: From Diagnosis to Clinical Execution is available to all audiences.

Note: The Age of Clear Aligners, Part 2: From Diagnosis to Clinical Execution is available to all audiences."}

title: How AI-Powered Automatic Surgical Guide and Implant Crown Design Can Increase Your Production
description: Learn how AI shortens your learning curve and increases your confidence in implant planning, surgical guides, final implant crown design, and, more importantly, how you can maximize efficiency and production in implant treatment plans. Discover the basic principles of implant dentistry and the value of guided surgery. You no longer need to outsource these workflows and wait for a design approval—download your design in less than five minutes. Simply uploading your scan files and clicking “next” will let AI do the work automatically. As the clinician, you still maintain complete control and can make changes at any point in the process if necessary. Implant planning will never be the same. Discover the power of AI in your practice.Learning Objectives: Understand the value of guided surgery in a growing practice.Learn the steps to predictable planning and success.Understand how to avoid implant failure.Review important restorative principles of implant treatment planning.

title: We've Got This!" Creating Winning Solutions for Everyday Practice Challenges
description: Every dental practice encounters daily challenges that impact productivity, communication, team morale and patient experience. This course provides strategies to transform obstacles to efficiency into effective sustainable solutions. Participants will share their practice "pet peeves"—scheduling breakdowns, patient objections, team misalignment and through guided discussion, practical strategies to respond proactively will be developed.Solution-focused thinking will be emphasized. This program will empower dental teams and leaders to create consistent wins that support practice growth, patient satisfaction and a healthy workplace culture.Learning Objectives: Learn to identify common operational communication and workflow challenges that impact daily practice performance.Apply practical problem-solving frameworks to develop effective team driven solutions.Implement specific tools to enhance efficiency, case acceptance, and the overall patient experience.Develop effective communication tools for the dental team to enhance the patient and team experienceLearn to shift from reactive problem management to proactive leadership.

title: Advanced Issues in Dental Practice Sales and Purchases
description: This course will expound on the morning course and take a deeper dive into the process of selling and buying a dental practice. The presenters — a dental CPA and broker and a dental attorney — will provide more detailed information regarding the process and will use case studies to help show issues sellers and buyers will face. The course will cover allocation of purchase price and tax issues on a sale, issues buyers and sellers need to deal with regarding office leases, valuation of a dental practice, and purchase of real estate. Case studies of actual dental practice sales will be presented, and the course will conclude with a roundtable of the presenters answering questions from attendees.Learning Objectives: Gain a deeper understanding of the process of dental practice sales and purchases.Review case studies on actual sales and purchase transactions from legal, financial and tax standpoints.Discuss valuation of a dental practice and what makes a practice more or less valuable.Learn the mistakes made by sellers and buyers of practices during the process.
instructors:
Arthur Wiederman, CPA
Jason Wood, Esq.
Justin Klingshirn

title: Baby Bootcamp: Unlocking the Pediatric Code (Part 4 of 4)
description: This two-day interactive lecture and hands-on course equips general dentists with practical evidence-based skills for diagnosing, treatment-planning and managing pediatric patients from infancy through adolescence. Through lectures, case discussions and hands-on workshops, participants will explore pediatric diagnostics, restorative materials, pulp therapy and trauma management. The course also covers behavior guidance, parent communication, and recognition of child abuse and neglect. Emphasizing real-world applications, attendees will analyze pediatric case studies, perform hands-on procedures such as stainless-steel crowns and trauma-splinting, and develop effective strategies for managing children and parents in diverse clinical settings.Learning Objectives: Perform comprehensive pediatric assessment and diagnosis.Develop evidence-based pediatric treatment-planning and clinical decision-making.Manage pediatric dental trauma and emergency care.Apply behavior guidance, parent communication and recognition of abuse.Develop clinical application and hands-on proficiency with pediatric materials and techniques.
requirements: TPVS1, TPVS2, FPVS1 and FPVS2 are a 12-hour bundle.

title: Clark County Morgue "John Doe" Identification
description: This hands-on continuation of “Fundamentals of Forensic Odontology” provides practical experience in dental identification techniques. Participants will work alongside forensic professionals at the coroner’s office to perform dental charting, compare antemortem and postmortem records and apply identification protocols in real-world scenarios. Emphasis is placed on accurate documentation, ethical considerations and the odontologist’s role within medicolegal investigations. This experiential learning module reinforces foundational knowledge through direct application in a professional forensic setting.Learning Objectives: Perform dental charting and documentation for the purpose of human identification.Apply forensic odontology techniques in a real-world setting at the coroner’s office.Compare antemortem and postmortem dental records to support identification processes.Demonstrate professionalism, accuracy and ethical responsibility during medicolegal investigative procedures.

title: Endo Unlocked: Build Confidence, Master Technique — Hands-On Workshop
description: Building confidence in endodontics requires predictable strategies that improve efficiency, safety, and focus. This hands-on workshop teaches practical techniques for access preparation, locating the maximum number of canals, and instrumentation using two proven rotary filing systems. Emphasizing a 3D approach to root canal therapy, the course integrates cone beam computed tomography (CBCT) to enhance visualization of complex root canal anatomy and improve treatment outcomes. Participants will gain hands-on experience with instrumentation, ultrasonics, magnification, irrigation, obturation, and interpretation of CBCT volumes, providing a comprehensive approach to managing complex endodontic cases with greater confidence and predictability.Learning Objectives: Learn about the latest armamentarium for accessing teeth, locating canals, and effectively instrumenting and debriding root canal systems.Apply the learning principles of rotary instrumentation using two different rotary systems.Understand the anatomical and clinical necessity of chemical debridement through effective irrigation protocols.Practice 3D obturation of root canal systems using single-cone obturation techniques, followed by placement of an effective coronal seal.
instructors: Brett Gilbert, DDS, FICD, Dip ABE
requirements: This content is repeated on Saturday afternoon in SPBG2. Please select to participate in either FPBG2 OR SPBG2; you may not register for both sessions.

Please select to participate in either FPBG2 OR SPBG2; you may not register for both sessions."}

title: From Gross Anatomy to Clinical Practice: Cadaveric Dissection of the Head and Neck for Dentists (Part 2 of 2)
description: This comprehensive course offers an in-depth revisit to head-and-neck anatomy — an area many clinicians have not explored since dental school. Through a detailed, clinically focused lecture followed by intensive hands-on cadaver dissection, participants will bridge traditional anatomical understanding with the latest discoveries and contemporary clinical relevance.Emphasis will be placed on the identification of key neurovascular structures, surgical landmarks and anatomical variations essential for safe and effective dental practice. This course is designed to enhance spatial understanding, procedural confidence and evidence-based clinical decision-making for general dentists and specialists alike.Learning Objectives: Identify critical neurovascular structures of the head and neck relevant to dentistry.Apply anatomical knowledge to improve injection techniques, implant placement and surgical approaches.Integrate evidence-based anatomical understanding into clinical decision-making.Correlate cadaveric findings with clinical imaging and surgical procedures to enhance safety and precision.
instructors:
Jessica Immonen, PhD, MS
Joe Iwanaga, DDS, PhD
requirements: FPJI1 and FPJI2 are a 6-hour bundle.

title: Gingival and Connective Tissue Grafting Workshop
description: Learn practical techniques for placing two different soft-tissue grafts. See how gingival grafts are placed to stop or prevent recession and how connective tissue grafts are used to cover exposed roots. Both grafts will be discussed and practiced. After step-by-step techniques are shown, participants will place the two grafts, including practicing stable suturing methods, all on pig jaws. Two surgical videos will be shown to illustrate the technique before the exercise.Learning Objectives: Prepare recipient sites and obtain donor tissue for each graft.Learn specific suturing techniques for stable grafts and predictable results.Learn how to use specialized instruments.Understand medications and postoperative considerations.

title: Hygiene Department Pain Points
description: Is your hygiene department a pain in your assets? Join Emme Sanders, RDH, as we use collaboration and fun to discover four simple solutions to reduce your team’s fatigue.Learning Objectives: Discover four hygiene profitability metrics.Reduce last-minute cancellations.Review patient communication skills.Capture three ways to improve co-diagnosis.

title: The Weight is Over for Diabetes and Obesity with GLP-1 and GIP Agonists
description: Diabetes mellitus, as well as its numerous systemic complications, is often accompanied and is exacerbated by numerous other comorbid conditions. This program will provide participants with an overview of diabetes, the arsenal of medications used in its treatment, and the complex interplay between diabetes and the oral-systemic connection. Special emphasis will be placed on the use of new GLP-1 (glucagon-like peptide-1) agonists, such as Ozempic (semaglutide), and dual-action GLP-1/GIP (gastric inhibitory polypeptide) agonists, such as Mounjaro (tirzepatide), for the treatment of diabetes as well as their use in the management of obesity.Learning Objectives: Identify the different types of diabetes, including symptoms and differential diagnosis.Describe the complex interplay of diabetes and periodontal disease as well as the origins of the oral-systemic connection.Review the various classes of medications used in the treatment of diabetes and its numerous comorbidities.
instructors: Tom Viola, RPh, CCP, CDE, CPMP

title: Preventing Work-Related Repetitive-Motion Injuries
description: This course provides an overview of how to recognize, prevent and manage work-related repetitive-motion injuries. Participants will learn about common risk factors, proper exercise practices and simple adjustments that reduce strain during daily tasks.By understanding body mechanics and early warning signs, employees can take proactive steps to maintain comfort, improve productivity and prevent long-term musculoskeletal issues in the workplace.Learning Objectives: Identify common causes and symptoms of repetitive-motion injuries.Apply physical activity principles to reduce physical strain during work tasks.Demonstrate proper posture and movement techniques to prevent injury.Develop a personal action plan to promote long-term musculoskeletal health.

title: The Case for Sedation in the General Dental Office
description: Dental office sedation is generally thought of as exclusive to surgical-based offices, but the use of sedation in a general dental office has many applications. This program will explore the seven main reasons to sedate patients in a general dental office. Additionally, the lecture will compare and contrast the efficacy and safety of inhalation, oral conscious and intravenous routes of sedation. The program will conclude by discussing how these three modalities of sedation can be incorporated into a general dental office.Learning Objectives: List the seven reasons to sedate a patient in a general dental office.Understand the efficacy and safety of three main methods sedation (inhalation, oral and intravenous) in a general dental office.Understand how to incorporate sedation techniques into a general dental office.

Registration will not be required, and seats will be available on a first-come, first-served basis."}

title: Before the Scaler: Why Home Care Therapy Determines Periodontal Outcomes
description: Successful management of periodontal disease depends not only on professional intervention but also on patients’ ability to perform effective daily plaque control. This course examines the biological rationale, clinical evidence, and practical implementation of home care therapy as a critical therapeutic phase in periodontal treatment. Drawing from published case series and evidence-based guidelines, participants will explore how structured patient education, device selection, and behavioral reinforcement can lead to measurable improvements in gingival inflammation, probing depths, and readiness for professional care—often prior to scaling, root planing, or surgery. Emphasis is placed on integrating home care therapy into cause-related periodontal treatment to enhance clinical outcomes, improve patient engagement, and promote long-term periodontal stability.Learning Objectives: Describe the role of dental plaque biofilm in the pathogenesis of periodontal disease and its implications for cause-related therapy.Differentiate home care therapy from traditional oral hygiene instruction based on therapeutic intent and clinical outcomes.Identify appropriate patient-specific home care devices and techniques based on periodontal diagnosis, anatomy, dexterity, and motivation.Evaluate clinical indicators that demonstrate effective home care therapy prior to initiating professional periodontal treatment.Apply evidence-based strategies to educate, motivate, and reinforce patient compliance with home care therapy to improve long-term periodontal stability.

title: The Age of Clear Aligners, Part 2: From Diagnosis to Clinical Execution
description: Building on the foundational concepts introduced in The Age of Clear Aligners, Part 1, this session will take a deeper dive into how experienced general dentists are utilizing clear aligners and orthodontics to enhance clinical outcomes. As orthodontics becomes an integral part of the modern dental exam, understanding its practical application is essential for today’s practitioner.Galler will examine real-world cases and clinical workflows to demonstrate how aligners are incorporated into comprehensive treatment-planning, highlighting tools and techniques used by some of the most forward-thinking dentists in practice today.Learning Objectives: Learn the practical applications of aligners and orthodontics in the modern dental exam.Review clinical cases that highlight contemporary orthodontic concepts.Identify tools and techniques that improve patient outcomes in everyday practice.
requirements: This course is available to all audiences. Note: The Age of Clear Aligners, Part 1: Foundations for the Emerging Dentist is an introductory session and available to students and new dentists only.

Note: The Age of Clear Aligners, Part 1: Foundations for the Emerging Dentist is an introductory session and available to students and new dentists only."}

title: Mastering TMD: Diagnosis, Management and Treatment
description: This course provides a practical clinical overview of Temporo-Mandibular Joint Disorder (TMD), focusing on accurate diagnosis and appropriate management. It reviews etiology, common signs and symptoms, and frequent reasons TMD is misdiagnosed in practice. Participants learn a systematic approach to evaluating suspected TMD, including history, clinical exam and differential diagnosis. Non-invasive management options, case selection, sequencing of care and patient communication around diagnosis, treatment planning and expected outcomes are also covered.Learning Objectives: Identify the clinical signs and symptoms associated with TMD, including findings that are commonly overlooked or misinterpreted.Differentiate TMD from other conditions presenting with similar orofacial pain symptoms through clinical assessment and diagnostic criteria.Describe a systematic, step-by-step examination protocol for evaluating patients with suspected TMD.Discuss non-invasive management and treatment options for TMD and their appropriate clinical application.Formulate an initial treatment approach for TMD based on diagnosis, clinical findings and patient presentation.
