Registration will not be required, and seats will be available on a first-come, first-served basis."}

title: Beyond the Management of Caries: What Is Possible?
description: This interdisciplinary lecture will challenge conventional paradigms by reframing dental caries as a sentinel health event (SHE) –– a biologically and historically grounded indicator of systemic metabolic imbalance. Integrating perspectives from archaeological transitions, histological analysis and noncommunicable disease (NCD) epidemiology, Pariseau will present a theory that positions enamel not merely as a hard tissue, but also as a non-regenerative biosensor that visibly registers nutritional stress before other organs manifest pathology.Learning Objectives: Interpret dental caries through the lens of public health surveillance, recognizing it as an early indicator of systemic dysfunction aligned with the criteria for SHEs.Evaluate the historical and systemic consequences of dentistry’s separation from medicine and its impact on current healthcare delivery and policy.Critically assess the limitations of localized caries treatment in addressing the underlying drivers of NCDs.Formulate practical, context-specific interventions that empower dental teams to lead in the early detection and prevention of NCDs.

title: Predictable Class V Composites: Techniques for Healthy Gums and Smooth Margins
description: Class V composite restorations can be challenging due to moisture control related to crevicular fluid and gingival bleeding as well as difficult cervical margins. This course provides a practical, step-by-step approach to achieving predictable, long-lasting and esthetic Class V composite restorations while maintaining healthy, happy gums. Participants will learn how to use Mylar matrix bands to manage moisture, control crevicular fluid, reduce bleeding and cure composite against Mylar to create exceptionally smooth, well-sealed margins. The focus is on simple, repeatable techniques that can be immediately applied to support gingival health while improving marginal integrity and restoration longevity.Learning Objectives: Explain how crevicular fluid and gingival bleeding affect moisture control, marginal integrity and longevity of Class V composite restorations.Demonstrate proper placement and use of Mylar matrix bands to achieve accurate cervical adaptation and smooth margins.Implement repeatable Class V composite techniques that reduce gingival bleeding, improve restorative longevity and enhance patient satisfaction.
instructors:
Rachel J. Malterud, DMD, MPH, FAGD
Mark I. Malterud, DDS, MAGD

title: GTM - Getting There: Mastership
description: Aimed at all AGD members interested in AGD Awards, first-time meeting attendees and Fellowship awardees. This session will provide introductory information about AGD's Mastership Award, including up-to-date information about the requirements, how to read your award transcript, timelines, MasterTrack programs and other CE opportunities. All AGD members are encouraged to attend this session to learn about the value of AGD Mastership and the steps toward earning it.

title: Clear Aligner Boot Camp
description: This course will introduce the basic principles of orthodontic tooth movement and clear aligner therapy. It will aim to cover fundamental orthodontic concepts and patient selection criteria for initiating aligner treatment in the dental office. Clinical steps for bonding and interproximal reduction techniques will be discussed to highlight key procedures utilized in clear aligner therapy. The course will also address the importance of dental hygiene and maintenance for orthodontic patients. In addition, participants will explore the business skills necessary to successfully incorporate clear aligners into everyday practice.Learning Objectives: Discuss patient evaluation and selection for clear aligner treatment.Understand the basic principles of orthodontic tooth movement and clear aligner therapy.Identify the records required for orthodontic treatment and the workflow for ordering aligners.Review clinical steps for delivering aligners, including bonding attachments, performing interproximal reduction and monitoring aligner fit.Recognize the importance of oral hygiene and practice management considerations when incorporating clear aligner therapy into dental practice.

title: Designing Elective Treatment Consultations to Increase Case Acceptance—A Team Event
description: Successful elective treatment acceptance requires more than clinical excellence. It demands a well-designed consultation process supported by clear communication, consistency and team alignment. It begins with the team having a clear understanding of practice philosophy of care and learning how to articulate this effectively from the patients' first practice encounter to the exam, diagnosis/treatment planning and consultation. This program is uniquely delivered by three presenters, each presenting their critical role in establishing an exceptional patient experience—The Business Assistant, The RDH / Treatment Coordinator and the Doctor. They will illustrate how each role contributes to a patient-centered consultation process that leads to improved case acceptance.Learning Objectives: Identify each team member's role in elective treatment consultation.Discover the role of the RDH is introducing this type of care.Learn techniques to introduce this level of care to all patients.Demonstrate effective communication techniques that improve patient understanding, trust and engagement during elective treatment discussion.Apply team-based protocols to ensure consistent patient experiences from first contact to case presentation.Identify common consultation breakdowns and implement practical solutions to improve outcomes.Develop strategies to implement elective treatment discussions into the practice.
instructors:
Debra Engelhardt-Nash, AA, BFA
Ross W. Nash, DDS
Emme Sanders, RDH

title: SLLV1A - Are You Air Aware? Incorporating Airway Assessments into Routine Dental and Medical Appointments
description: Optimal health depends on functional, efficient breathing. As frontline providers, all healthcare practitioners are positioned to recognize signs of disordered breathing and sleep apnea to support collaborative airway care. Disordered breathing is common, often underdiagnosed, and can significantly impact both physical and mental health. Many of its warnings, especially in children—are visible in the head, neck, and oral structures, making medical and dental settings an ideal place for early identification. This introductory course will equip participants with foundational knowledge to recognize key red flags and contributing factors of disordered breathing in both children and adults. Attendees will leave with practical strategies and increased confidence to begin incorporating basic airway screening into routine wellness visits.Learning Objectives: Define disordered breathing and recognize its impact.Explain the association between disordered breathing, sleep, and systemic health outcomes across the lifespan.Identify common airway risk factors and observable clinical indicators in the head, neck, and oral structures that may contribute to compromised airway health.
instructors: Lancette VanGuilder, BS, RDH, PHEDH, CEAS, FADHA

title: SLLV1B - The Future of the Dental Hygiene Profession Starts Now
description: The dental hygiene profession is at a turning point. With shifting healthcare trends, expanding oral-systemic science and increasing public demand for accessible care, dental hygienists have a unique opportunity to redefine their profession. This dynamic session explores the current state of the dental hygiene profession, from workforce challenges to practice innovations, and empowers participants to embrace their role as elite healthcare providers. Attendees will examine key opportunities to reduce burnout, learn strategies to expand care delivery in their practice and communities, explore innovative products and discover how advocacy and leadership can create a new era and enhance both provider and patient satisfaction.Learning Objectives: Describe the key healthcare, societal and legislative trends shaping the future of healthcare.Identify innovative ways to expand dental hygiene care in a variety of settings.Explain the importance of advocacy and leadership in advancing autonomy, scope of practice and public access to care.
instructors: Lancette VanGuilder, BS, RDH, PHEDH, CEAS, FADHA

title: SLLV1C - Clean is a Mindset: Redefining Prevention Through Mindful Oral Care
description: Challenge the outdated oral hygiene routines and redefine homecare as a purpose-driven, mindful self-care practice. Grounded in the principles of Individually Trained Oral Prophylaxis (iTOP) and the Touch-to-Teach approach, this course moves beyond mechanical instruction and focuses on intentional brushing, personalized interdental care, and behavior-based coaching. Participants will learn how to transform daily oral care from an unconscious habit into a meaningful wellness ritual — empowering both providers and patients to think differently about prevention, consistency, and long-term wellness. This course is designed to create a mindset shift, not just a skill upgrade.Learning Objectives: Demonstrate modern, evidence-based brushing and interdental techniques through hands-on learning.Personalize oral hygiene tools and techniques to support comfort, confidence, and long-term success.Apply Touch-to-Teach coaching strategies that motivate patients and build sustainable habits.Inspire a purpose-driven mindset that connects daily oral care to overall health and well-being.
instructors: Lancette VanGuilder, BS, RDH, PHEDH, CEAS, FADHA

title: Elevating Dental Hygiene: Prevention, Awareness, and Growth
Endo Unlocked: Advanced Endodontic Techniques Workshop — Calcification, Retreatment, Managing MB2 and More
description: Managing complicated endodontic cases can be challenging for any clinician. Calcified canals are difficult to manage, but with proper technique, they can be cleaned and shaped with predictability. Retreatment also presents challenges for clinicians, but with sound technique and understanding, successful outcomes are possible in general practice. Separation of instruments can occur, creating a stressful and potentially damaging outcome. Modern techniques and armamentaria can help clinicians successfully retrieve separated instruments. Locating the second mesiobuccal canal (MB2) is challenging, but, after learning more about anatomy and using the right instruments, it is achievable. Participants will learn perforation prevention and repair techniques. This combination of lecture and hands-on workshop will allow each participant to learn and gain proficiency in advanced endodontic techniques and skills.Learning Objectives: Learn how to identify and negotiate calcified canal structures using modern armamentaria.Practice removal of gutta percha and root fillings to mimic the challenges encountered during endodontic retreatment.Learn about MB2 anatomy and anatomical cues of location, and practice locating and effectively instrumenting these structures using 3D-printed tooth anatomy.Understand how to prevent perforation of tooth structure, and learn techniques and materials to successfully repair these iatrogenic errors.
instructors: Brett Gilbert, DDS, FICD, Dip ABE

title: Facially Driven Diagnosis: The Foundation for Predictable Esthetic and Functional Success
description: Every restorative decision begins with diagnosis—and every diagnosis begins with the face.In this comprehensive session, Robert P. Korman, DDS, MAGD, introduces a structured, facially guided diagnostic system that unites esthetics, function, structure and biology into a single, predictable framework. Attendees will learn how facial reference points define the boundaries for restorative design, tissue management, and occlusal harmony. Drawing from interdisciplinary cases and clinical experience, Dr. Korman illustrates how a facially guided diagnostic approach leads to restorative precision, lasting esthetics and predictable functional harmony.Learning Objectives: Recognize how facial landmarks establish parameters for esthetic, functional and biologic design.Implement facially generated principles to improve diagnostic accuracy and case sequencing.Integrate biologic-width and tissue-response concepts into restorative and implant planning.Develop predictable protocols for interdisciplinary coordination and long-term maintenance.Apply facially driven diagnostic principles to enhance both esthetic and restorative outcomes.

title: Incorporating Digital Dentistry (Part 1 of 2)
description: In this course, you will learn to fully incorporate digital dentistry into your practice in a practical and profitable way. As part of a digital workflow, you will learn to scan, design and mill restorations in-office using a fully integrated CAD/CAM system. Additional topics will include material selection, preparation, final finish and cementation. A hands-on digital scanning exercise using an intraoral scanner with typodonts will be provided.Learning Objectives: Review the benefits of a digital workflow.Understand techniques for digitizing the clinical workflow.Apply intraoral scanning to capture precise digital impressions.Evaluate intraoral scan data for accuracy and completeness prior to digital design.Learn to digitally design a functional esthetic restoration.Make material choices based on their indications.Produce quality restorations in a single visit.

title: What You Need to Know About Medicare for Yourself and Your Parents
description: Discover the basics of Medicare eligibility, enrollment periods and plan types, including learning the options between Medicare Supplement Plans, Medicare Advantage (Medicare Part C) HMOs and PPOs, and prescription drug plans. Medicare is not a one-size-fits-all program, but rather an opportunity to find a plan that suits your individual needs for a given time as your health changes.Learning Objectives: Understand Medicare Eligibility Periods.Learn the difference between Medicare Supplements, Medicare Advantage and the Medicare Prescription Drug Plan.Understand which Medicare plan may work for you and/or your parents.

title: Focus On Smiles: The Art of Dental Photography
description: Dental photography has become an essential component of modern clinical practice, impacting diagnosis, treatment planning, case acceptance, communication and marketing. This session guides dental professionals through the fundamentals and advanced principles of high-quality dental photography. Attendees will gain a clear understanding of the science behind color and shade selection—hue, chroma, and value—and learn how these principles directly influence restorative outcomes. The course explores practical camera setup and operation, with emphasis on using DSLR systems, appropriate lens selection, flash configuration, and standardized photographic protocols. Participants will learn how to capture consistent, diagnostic-quality images, how to integrate photography into everyday practice, and how to utilize well-documented cases to enhance clinical communication, patient education and overall practice growth. This session also emphasizes how mastering dental photography can reduce clinician stress, improve workflow efficiency, and build greater confidence when presenting treatment—supporting both clinical excellence and practitioner well-being.Learning Objectives: Explain the scientific principles of color (hue, chroma, value) and their relevance to shade matching and restorative dentistry.Set up and operate a DSLR camera system for consistent, high-quality intraoral and extraoral images.Apply standardized photographic protocols to document cases accurately from diagnosis through completion.Use photography to improve patient communication and treatment acceptance.Integrate photographic workflows to enhance marketing, digital communication and interdisciplinary collaboration.
requirements: All Take the Floor sessions will be held within the exhibit hall. Registration will not be required, and seats will be available on a first-come, first-served basis.

Registration will not be required, and seats will be available on a first-come, first-served basis."}

title: Effortless Power: Recalibrate Your Well-Being
description: Dentistry requires sustained precision, decision-making, and emotional regulation in high-pressure clinical and academic environments. Over time, many dental professionals find that the internal effort required to perform their roles increases — even when skills, experience and commitment remain strong. Traditional approaches such as time off or productivity strategies often fail to address the underlying physiological patterns that drive stress, reactivity and diminished presence. This session introduces the concept of “effortless power” — a state in which clinical judgment, leadership and communication arise from nervous system regulation rather than force or over-effort. Drawing from real-world clinical and academic experiences and supported by neuroscience, this talk explores how subtle recalibration of the nervous system can improve decision-making, patient interactions, team dynamics and professional sustainability. Participants will gain practical insights into integrating brief, evidence-informed practices into daily clinical routines and meetings, allowing for greater clarity, steadiness and long-term effectiveness without adding additional workload.Learning Objectives: Recognize how nervous system dysregulation impacts clinical performance, leadership effectiveness, and team communication in dental and academic settings.Identify subtle physiological cues that contribute to internal resistance and reactivity, even in highly skilled and experienced professionals.Apply simple, evidence-informed recalibration practices that support clearer decision-making, improved patient interactions and sustainable professional performance.
instructors: Dhwani J. Shah, DDS, MS, MS

title: Facial and Dental Asymmetry, TMJ, and Orthodontics
description: Facial and dental asymmetry is frequently a manifestation of underlying skeletal imbalance, functional shifts and temporomandibular joint (TMJ) asymmetries rather than an isolated esthetic concern. This presentation provides a practical framework for diagnosing asymmetry through comprehensive clinical evaluation and CBCT-based 3D analysis, with emphasis on dental and facial signs, condylar position, occlusal plane discrepancies, and transverse skeletal deficiencies. Attendees will learn orthodontic strategies for managing asymmetry and improving TMJ health, including growth-guided treatment, skeletal expansion and selective anchorage mechanics. Real clinical cases will demonstrate predictable protocols to enhance facial balance, occlusion, airway function and joint stability in both adolescent and adult patients, allowing clinicians to confidently integrate asymmetry evaluation into everyday orthodontic and restorative treatment-planning.Learning Objectives: Identify clinical and radiographic signs of facial and dental asymmetry, including occlusal plane discrepancies, midline shifts, crossbites and mandibular functional shifts.Describe the relationship between facial asymmetry, occlusal imbalance and TMJ instability in both growing and adult patients.Interpret CBCT findings related to asymmetry and TMJ health, including condylar position, skeletal discrepancies and transverse maxillary deficiency.Differentiate between growth-related and acquired asymmetry, and determine appropriate timing and treatment approaches for pediatric and adult patients.Apply orthodontic and skeletal treatment strategies, including growth-guided mechanics and skeletal expansion, to improve facial symmetry while supporting TMJ stability.

Registration will not be required, and seats will be available on a first-come, first-served basis."}

title: The Agentic Practice: How Data, AI, and Autonomous Marketing Are Replacing the Traditional Dental Marketing Team
description: 2026 marks a fundamental shift in how dental practices grow. The conversation has moved past automation. The practices winning new patients today are not the ones with the biggest marketing budgets or the most posts. They are the ones that have built a system where data flows into insights, insights trigger action, and action compounds over time without a human having to initiate every step. This is the age of agentic marketing.Bryan Hwang spent years building enterprise-grade marketing infrastructure at Accenture, Cognizant, and Wipro for some of the world’s largest organizations before bringing that architecture exclusively to dentistry. In this Learning Lab, he translates what the Fortune 500 discovered years ago into a framework dental practice owners, DSOs, and office managers can deploy now.The session reframes marketing not as a service you purchase, but as an intelligence layer you build. Attendees will learn how AI agents are replacing manual campaign management, how the data already sitting inside your practice can be converted into growth actions in real time, and why the practices that understand this shift now will be nearly impossible to compete with in three years. This is not about posting more content or running another boosted ad. This is about building a living, breathing marketing system that thinks, learns and acts on your behalf.Learning Objectives: Understand the shift from traditional marketing automation to agentic marketing, and how AI systems that move from data to insights to action are replacing manual campaign management, freeing practice owners and office managers from reactive decision-making.Identify the data signals already present in your practice, including patient acquisition sources, lead response times, search visibility trends and ad performance patterns, and learn how to convert those signals into real-time growth decisions rather than monthly reports that arrive too late to act on.Evaluate the readiness of your practice or D

title: Beyond the Ovate: The E-Pontic Revolution in Soft-Tissue Design
description: It’s time to rethink the pontic. The E-pontic represents a biologically guided evolution in tissue design—preserving and enhancing soft-tissue form where the traditional ovate pontic has long fallen short. Robert Korman, DDS, MAGD, the codeveloper of the E-pontic concept, shares how this innovative design merges prosthetic contouring with laser-assisted tissue sculpting to achieve stable, natural-looking results around implants, pontics and natural teeth. Building on the pioneering foundation of Dr. Jimmy Eubank, Dr. Korman demonstrates how biologic form drives lasting esthetics, guiding clinicians toward soft-tissue predictability and restorative excellence. The concepts presented in this course will be beneficial and relevant for ceramists and dentists alike.Learning Objectives: Differentiate the E-Pontic from traditional ovate pontic designs in both form and biologic behavior.Understand how pontic morphology influences pontic recipient site development, tissue migration and long-term stability.Apply laser-assisted sculpting techniques to refine tissue contours conservatively.Integrate the E-pontic into interdisciplinary planning to enhance esthetic and periodontal outcomes.Adopt biologically driven pontic design as a foundation for predictable, long-term restorative success.

title: Concerns, Challenges and Treatment of Our Aging Patients: What Do We Need to Know?
description: This lecture will discuss topics related to caring for aging patients, including treatment planning, treatment challenges, and effective communication. It will also cover consent, patient care modifications, techniques that consider cost and age, and techniques to help patients understand the care they receive and need.Learning Objectives: Understand the complexities of aging as they pertain to treatment-planning for our patients.Develop knowledge of treatment concerns that impact oral healthcare treatment-planning for geriatric patients.Better understand evidence-based oral health options for geriatric patients.Understand adaptive devices that geriatric patients use and the impact on patient care.Identify and appraise the qualities of communication with geriatric patients.Understand the consent process for geriatric patients with diminished mental capacity.

title: Endo Unlocked: Build Confidence, Master Technique — Hands-On Workshop
description: Building confidence in endodontics requires predictable strategies that improve efficiency, safety, and focus. This hands-on workshop teaches practical techniques for access preparation, locating the maximum number of canals, and instrumentation using two proven rotary filing systems. Emphasizing a 3D approach to root canal therapy, the course integrates cone beam computed tomography (CBCT) to enhance visualization of complex root canal anatomy and improve treatment outcomes. Participants will gain hands-on experience with instrumentation, ultrasonics, magnification, irrigation, obturation, and interpretation of CBCT volumes, providing a comprehensive approach to managing complex endodontic cases with greater confidence and predictability.Learning Objectives: Learn about the latest armamentarium for accessing teeth, locating canals, and effectively instrumenting and debriding root canal systems.Apply the learning principles of rotary instrumentation using two different rotary systems.Understand the anatomical and clinical necessity of chemical debridement through effective irrigation protocols.Practice 3D obturation of root canal systems using single-cone obturation techniques, followed by placement of an effective coronal seal.
instructors: Brett Gilbert, DDS, FICD, Dip ABE
requirements: This content is repeated on Friday afternoon in fPBG2. Please select to participate in either FPBG2 OR SPBG2; you may not register for both sessions.

title: Incorporating Digital Dentistry (Part 2 of 2)
description: In this course, you will learn to fully incorporate digital dentistry into your practice in a practical and profitable way. As part of a digital workflow, you will learn to scan, design and mill restorations in-office using a fully integrated CAD/CAM system. Additional topics will include material selection, preparation, final finish and cementation. A hands-on digital scanning exercise using an intraoral scanner with typodonts will be provided.Learning Objectives: Review the benefits of a digital workflow.Understand techniques for digitizing the clinical workflow.Apply intraoral scanning to capture precise digital impressions.Evaluate intraoral scan data for accuracy and completeness prior to digital design.Learn to digitally design a functional esthetic restoration.Make material choices based on their indications.Produce quality restorations in a single visit.

title: Cracking the Code: Coding Essentials
description:
CDT and ICD-10 are evolving faster than ever—and practices that don’t keep up are leaving money on the table. With more than 175 CDT code additions, deletions, and revisions in just three years, and the introduction of ICD-10 as a completely new medical coding system, the rules of reimbursement have changed dramatically.This high-impact course cuts through the confusion and delivers the most current, practical coding and documentation strategies you need right now. Learn how to align clinical documentation with today’s codes to maximize legitimate reimbursement, reduce denials, and protect your practice—without increasing risk or workload.Packed with real-world insights and immediately usable techniques, this program empowers doctors and teams to code with confidence, capture every appropriate dollar, and elevate the financial performance of the practice. Stay compliant. Stay current. Get paid for the dentistry you deliver.Learning Objectives: 1. To assess if you losing money because you are using old codes to file insurance
claims and/or not utilizing optimized Medical coding for medical reimbursement
2. To provide the latest dental coding information on CDT and ICD
3. To equip the practice to obtain maximum legitimate reimbursement

title: Record Keeping, Board Protocols, & Important Information from the Oregon Board of Dentistry
description: At the conclusion of this course, attendees will be familiar with the mission of the Oregon Board of Dentistry and how ORS’s and OAR’s are utilized to regulate Dentists, Dental Hygienists, and Dental Therapists in Oregon.Learning Objectives: Review rule changes including important reminders to those who hold anesthesia permits.Address patient records and documentation issues. Overview of the Health Professional's Service Program (HPSP)Understand the investigation process and how to stay in compliance with the Dental Practice Act.
instructors:
Winthrop B. Carter, DDS, FICD
Angela M. Smorra, DMD

title: Elevate: Smile Design & Facial Aesthetics - Integrating Digital Dentistry with Modern Injectables
description: Dr. Olesya Salathe will guide attendees through a modern approach to smile design that begins with the face, not the teeth. Participants will learn how facial proportions, lip dynamics, gingival architecture, and occlusion inform esthetic decision-making, allowing clinicians to design smiles that complement the entire face rather than relying solely on tooth measurements. Emphasis will be placed on facial balancing and how subtle, intentional changes can significantly enhance overall harmony.The course will explore how digital diagnostics and Digital Smile Design are used to visualize outcomes, improve communication, and align expectations among the dentist, patient, and interdisciplinary team. Attendees will see how digital “test drives,” including mock-ups and provisional designs, allow patients to experience their proposed smile before irreversible treatment begins, increasing confidence and case acceptance.A central philosophy of this presentation is additive dentistry rather than subtractive dentistry. Dr. Salathe will demonstrate how clear aligner therapy can be strategically used to establish ideal tooth position prior to restorative care—whether for implants, veneers, or conservative esthetic enhancement—often minimizing tooth reduction and preserving natural structure. This orthodontic-restorative sequencing supports biologically respectful, long-lasting outcomes.Facial aesthetics will be incorporated through the thoughtful use of neurotoxins and dermal fillers as adjuncts to smile design. Attendees will gain insight into how injectables can support facial balance, lip posture, and perioral esthetics when used conservatively and in harmony with dental treatment, rather than as standalone cosmetic procedures.Beyond clinical concepts, the course will address how to integrate smile design, clear aligners, and facial aesthetics into daily practice through efficient workflows, team communication, and patient education. This course is ideal for dentists seeking t

title: Staying in the Zone: Understanding The Sedation Continuum
description:
This comprehensive course equips dentists and dental team members with a deep understanding of the sedation continuum and its essential role in safe patient management. Participants will explore the pharmacology, indications, contraindications, and dosing considerations for commonly used oral and intravenous sedation agents, with an emphasis on selecting appropriate medications based on patient factors and clinical goals. The course will also address documentation standards, informed consent, monitoring, and emergency preparedness to ensure regulatory compliance and high-quality care.In addition, attendees will receive an in-depth review of current Oregon sedation requirements along with the newly updated ADA Guidelines for the Use of Sedation and General Anesthesia by Dentists. These guidelines will be presented by one of their contributing authors, offering firsthand perspective on national best practices and how they translate to everyday clinical application. Practical strategies for integrating or expanding sedation services within a dental practice will be discussed, making this course valuable for both new and experienced providers.Learning Objectives: At the conclusion of this course, attendees will be able to:
• Define and apply the sedation continuum to ensure patient safety and compliance with state regulations and updated ADA Guidelines.
Compare and select oral and intravenous sedation agents using pharmacology, dosing parameters, and risk profiles.Implement documentation standards and interpret Oregon’s current guidelines to align practice policies and patient care.

title: The Understanding and the Application of Emotional Intelligence in the Practice of Dentistry Today
description: The understanding and the application of Emotional Intelligence in the practice of dentistry today has become and essential skillset. Daniel Goleman defines Emotional Intelligence as, “our ability to successfully recognize and manage our feelings, and thus, recognize and respond appropriately to the feelings of others.” The practice of dentistry is highly demanding on both the physical and emotional levels, and the most common way team members cope with stressors is through the repression of their feelings, by reacting too coldly, or by reacting in an emotionally unmanaged fashion that undercuts their ability to positively influence a situation toward a preferred outcome. High Emotional Intelligence involves developing the emotional self-disciple necessary to get the “circus under the tent,” —decipher highly emotional situations in a timely fashion, and respond to them strategically and appropriately. As we master this critically important behavioral skill, we become more effective communicators, educators, leaders, helpers, and friends.Learning Objectives: What is emotional intelligence and why it matters more today than ever before.The neuroscience behind Emotional Intelligence.How to enhance Emotional Intelligence, and leverage it to become more effective with patients, teammates, and others —including successfully managing conflicts and disagreements.How raising your team’s emotional intelligence allows your patients to feel heard, felt, cared-about, and appropriately cared-for. This enhances your brand image and subsequently helps build a practice through intention rather than by luck or third party influence.When enhanced emotional intelligence won’t solve an interpersonal problem but will help you to know when a relationship needs to be terminated to everyone’s benefit.

title: Sugar… Aw Honey, Honey: Diabetes Detection in the Dental Office
description:
Out of the backdrop of America’s declining health emerge two fire-breathing dragons that destroy tooth and bone: Periodontal Disease and Insulin Resistance/Diabetes. Epidemic in today's world, it is critical that dentists and hygienists recognize telltale signs of Diabetes and test for it. Fortunately the first ramifications of Diabetes are oral. This course will help you quickly recognize the risk factors and signs and learn how and when to test for it. Susan will also help you recognize the bi-directional interplay between hyperglycemia and Periodontal Disease, each making the other worse. You will confidently make good referrals and improve your communication skills with medical providers, who in turn, will refer patients back to you. Perhaps most importantly, Susan will challenge our acceptance of conventional “perio treatment” methods and inspire the dental profession to accept a more profound role in root cause analysis and facilitating health change behavior. This includes effective changes in diet, activity level and reducing overall inflammation. Learning Objectives: Learn the bi-directional relationship between uncontrolled (or undetected) Diabetes and active Periodontal DiseaseUncover root causes and contemporary treatment strategies of each: Periodontal Disease and Type 2 Diabetes (T2DM)Understand the pathology and progression of Insulin Resistance and to Pre-Diabetes and finally to Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T2DM)Learn the evidence-based screening, testing and diagnostic criteria for T2DMTouch on our role as facilitator and coach for preventing and/or improving lifestyle-related systemic disease
Discover a paradigm-shifting approach to comprehensive periodontal therapy that includes glycemic control.

title: The Exceptional Assistant: Direct Restorations
description: PART ONE: As a dental assistant, you have a unique opportunity to maximize your contribution to the practice. In this high-energy session, we’ll focus on essential skills that empower you in your role. These skills include the principles of bonding, composite resin materials, matrix systems, occlusion, esthetics, and polishing materials and techniques. We will use Class II composite as our model to build clinical knowledge, and we will expand to other types of composite bonding as well. You will leave this day with an expanded view of what is possible for you in your role as a chairside dental assistant, a leader in your practice, and an advocate for your patient.Learning Objectives: Learn the fundamentals of a Class II composite.Expand your knowledge of the principles of bonding and composite resins.Learn about the application and modification of matrix systems.Develop your understanding of how esthetics and occlusion are optimized with bonding.Update your knowledge of polishing materials and techniques.

title: The Latest and Greatest in Pediatric Dentistry
description: So, what’s new in the world of pediatric dentistry? As with other areas of dentistry, new developments are keeping pace in in care for children. Highlighted in this program include the following: indications for and treatment of primary pulp therapy have changed, the variety of full coverage crowns has exploded, faster and painless anesthesia is now a real possibility, and minimally invasive treatment is more popular. There is also a surprising new development that may increase the relationship between dentistry and medicine. Also, as parents become more demanding, there will be tips for handling some of their vexing behavior. Topics in this realm will include requesting no radiographs, crowns (vs large restorations) and whether or not to give their children fluoride. Know today’s techniques, concepts, and materials to treat your youngest patients with confidence! This stimulating course will bring all practitioners up to date with what’s changed and how to most effectively move ahead.Learning Objectives: Diagnose and reat primary pulps with the greatest rate of successPerform pain-free injections and increase working time on childrenDifferentiate primary teeth needing crowns versus large restorationsManage parent demands with greater success
instructors: Greg Psaltis, DDS, Certificate in Pediatric Dentistry

title: Transforming Smiles: Elevating Dental Hygiene Care
description: Dental hygiene can be a job or it can be a career. Do you enjoy your hygiene role or is something missing? Are you in a job or a career?Hygienists join the profession to help others and to be a part of the healthcare community while earning a good income. During day-to-day cllinical practice, hygienists often can't find the joy that was once envisioned. Clinical hygiene may be perfect for some, but for others not so much.. This program will examine options both in and out of clinical practice to achieve optimal career satisfaction and patient care benefits. Whether a new graduate or seasoned hygienist, the roles that are open to a hygienist's career path are numerous. We will examine the classic role of the dental hygienist and review next level roles for increased satisfaction both for patients and for the hygienist. Various products and technology to increase patient treatment may be discussed.Learning Objectives: define mission statements examine the role of mentors and networks in professional relationshipsreview products to assist hygienists in patient treatment and education evaluate and prepare alternatives to clinical dental hygiene practice

title: Whats New? Materials and Tech that Expedite Excellence
description:
With a constant stream of new products that claim to be bigger, better, faster flooding the industry, it is challenging to know which are effective and efficient… and where there is hype. In this fast-moving market, advertising makes every product appear to be the panacea. However, some of the best products and services don’t enter the market with much fanfare. Dental professionals can become bogged down or confused when trying to decipher which new materials and technologies are right for their practices. As editorial director with Dental Economics magazine, Dr. Joshua Austin authors the column Pearls for Your Practice and has personally worked with most new products and technologies that have entered the market. In this interactive, fast-moving course, Joshua shares the current trends in dental materials and technologies. Learn how materials have changed over the past few years and how those changes can affect your procedures and your practice.Learning Objectives: Explore the latest innovations and enhancements in intraoral scanning
Learn how the improved scanning amplifies practice productivity
Discover how these IOS devices can close the gap of patient communicationReview universal adhesives and their advantages
Discuss time saving placement techniques for bulk fill posterior composites
Analyze how bulk fill posterior composites compare to their traditional counterparts

title: Medical Emergencies in the Dental Office
description: Medical emergencies can and do occur in dental practices every day. Over the next fifteen years the segment of the population at highest risk for medical emergencies, those 65 and older, will become a larger percentage of our patients. The entire dental team must be prepared to recognize and respond to a variety of medical emergencies that could be precipitated by the stress of the dental environment. This course will discuss the diagnosis, prevention, and management of common medical emergencies that might occur in dental offices. This four-hour course is appropriate for the entire dental team and meets the medical emergency education requirement of the Oregon Board of Dentistry for license renewal and anesthesia permits.Learning Objectives: Participants will be able to identify those at increased risk for specific medical emergencies.Participants will be able to diagnose the common medical emergencies based on the signs and symptoms demonstrated by a patient.Participants will develop a rational approach to the management of medical emergencies in general as well as the specific protocols for managing each medical emergency discussed.Participants will be able to identify modifiable risk factors for medical emergencies and understand methods to prevent their occurrence in the dental environment.

title: CPR for the Dental Professional
description: New 2025 standards for CPR and AED use for adults, infants, and children with new choking standards. Successfull completion results in a 2 year certificate. You will review the use of a bag valve mask for rescue breathing as well as when to use naloxone to reverse a narcotic overdose. New books will be availble. Team work is emphasized after initial recognition of cardiac or respiratory arrest. AEDs (automatic external defibrillators) will be taught with a final "mini-code" scenario. There is a written "celebration of knowledge" at the end which is open book. Your entire office is encouraged to attend as a group since teamwork leads to successful resuscitation. Learn the different signs and symptoms of a "heart attack" in males and females, young and old. Be prepared to respond to a choking emergency. You will be using feedback manikins in order to evaluate your rate and depth of compressions and effectiveness of ventillations. How well you train helps you to respond with confidence to office emergencies.Learning Objectives: Upon completion, the participant wil be able to respond with confidence to assess patients for life threatening respiratory or cardiac arrestUpon completion, the participant will be able to perform CPR with AED usage and rescue breathing for anyone experiencing cardiac or respiratory arrestUpon completion, the participant will be able to recognize choking and perform choking interventions for adult, infant, child.

title: OHSU CaseCATS Student Poster Competition
Unequal Sleep: Addressing Disparities in Sleep Health by Age, Gender and Ethnicity
description: Course DescriptionSleep is a biological necessity, yet sleep disorders—particularly obstructive sleep apnea—remain profoundly underdiagnosed and undertreated in large segments of the population. This course explores sleep health through an equity-informed, trauma-aware lens, examining how age, gender, ethnicity, and social determinants of health influence who is diagnosed, who receives care, and who is left behind.Designed for dental professionals, this presentation expands the traditional biomedical model of sleep apnea to include lived experience, cultural context, and systemic barriers to care. Special attention is given to populations that are frequently overlooked or mischaracterized, including women, older adults, Indigenous communities, and patients with trauma histories. Participants will examine why common screening tools and referral pathways often fail these groups and how dentistry is uniquely positioned to close these gaps.The course will review the pathophysiology and health consequences of obstructive sleep apnea while emphasizing practical, practice-ready strategies for improved screening, patient communication, and interdisciplinary collaboration. Trauma-informed considerations will be discussed, including why CPAP therapy may be poorly tolerated for some patients and how oral appliance therapy can serve as an effective, patient-centered alternative.Drawing on clinical experience, public health principles, and community-based models of care, this presentation challenges providers to rethink access, compliance, and outcomes in sleep medicine. Attendees will leave with a deeper understanding of sleep as a social and cultural determinant of health, along with actionable tools to identify risk, reduce disparities, and deliver more inclusive, compassionate sleep care within their practices.This course is appropriate for general dentists, specialists, hygienists, and dental team members seeking to integrate sleep health into comprehensive, ethical, and eq

title: Classroom to Chairside: A Panel on Solving the Dental Workforce Shortage
description: The dental workforce shortage is a complex challenge impacting practices nationwide, driven by multiple factors such as recruitment and retention difficulties for dental assistants and hygienists. One critical upstream issue is the slow flow of the workforce pipeline. This course will explore practical solutions to strengthen that pipeline and address staffing gaps.Participants will gain insight into the pivotal role educators and workforce program leaders play in shaping the future dental workforce. The session will showcase successful strategies and partnerships between dental practices, local high schools, training programs, and workforce development initiatives. Through real-world examples and collaborative discussion, attendees will learn how to engage with community resources, build sustainable relationships, and implement actionable steps to attract and retain qualified team members.The panel will feature a diverse group of experts, including educators, workforce program leaders, healthcare organizations, and dentists who have successfully partnered with schools and training programs to create innovative solutions.Learning Objectives: Identify key factors contributing to the dental workforce shortage, including challenges in recruitment and retention for dental assistants and hygienists.Evaluate successful strategies and partnerships that dentists have implemented with local high schools, training programs, and workforce development initiatives. Develop actionable steps to engage in community-based solutions, such as outreach programs, mentorship, and advocacy efforts to strengthen the talent pipeline.Recognize the role of educators and workforce program leaders in addressing shortages and how dental practices can collaborate effectively with these stakeholders.Apply practical tools and resources to improve team stability and create sustainable workforce solutions within their own practices.

title: Ensuring Culturally and Linguistically Competent Dental Care – a patient-centered approach
description: This 2-hour interprofessional session will provide attendees with an overview of culturally competent care for patients from diverse sociocultural and linguistic backgrounds. Participants will build skills to implement a person-centered approach that is relevant and useful for all patients, not just those whose cultural backgrounds are markedly different than the clinician’s.This approach, which emphasizes interpersonal communication skills, empathy, and human interaction, is especially valuable as technological advances are rapidly changing the healthcare environment, including new tools that make it easier to communicate across language barriers and ensure high quality care for patients with limited English proficiency and hearing disabilities.Using case analyses and breakout group discussion, this will be an interactive, two-part session focused on: (1) general approaches for ensuring culturally competent care and (2) how to address language barriers using professional methods of interpretation.Learning Objectives: Describe the core principles of culturally competent careExplore patients’ perspective on their health issues to personalize treatment discussionsIdentify mistrust and engage in trust-building strategiesRecognize social determinants of health that impact careDescribe the pitfalls of poor communication for patients with limited English proficiencyImplement strategies to work effectively with professional interpreter services

title: Infection Control in the Dental Office
description:
Infection prevention and control are important elements of every patient encounter in the dental setting. The Oregon Board of Dentistry has specific regulations for infection control practice in every dental office. Dental professionals and patients are increasingly aware of the potential spread of infectious diseases from aerosols, droplets, contaminated surfaces and equipment, and the dental devices that are connected to water sources. This course will provide the most current information regarding evidence-based infection control practices. The presenter will review the latest infection control guidance from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), and provide easy to implement methods for ensuring a safe care environment.This course will include a review of appropriate selection and use of PPE, disinfection and sterilization, dental waterline management, bloodborne exposure prevention and post-exposure follow-up, and immunization recommendations. In addition to dental-specific infection control guidelines, this course will review related guidelines that apply to all healthcare settings.Learning Objectives: Describe the agencies that provide recommendations and regulations for infection control in dental settings.
Determine the appropriate steps needed in their practice to ensure compliance with infection control standards.Define the CDC Core Practices.

title: Advanced Local Anesthesia Techniques
description:
This advanced course equips dentists, hygienists and staff with a deeper, evidence-based understanding of local anesthesia and the skills needed to deliver injections safely, effectively, and confidently. Participants will learn advanced injection techniques designed to improve success rates, reduce patient discomfort, and enhance clinical outcomes. The session will also address persistent myths and misconceptions surrounding local anesthetics—such as articaine use, mandibular block alternatives, tachyphylaxis, and “failed anesthesia” scenarios—and replace them with current, research-supported guidance.In addition to technique refinement, the course provides practical strategies for preventing, recognizing, and managing complications, including nerve injuries, hematomas, positive aspirations, and local anesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST). A review of relevant pharmacology will help clinicians select the most appropriate anesthetic agents based on onset, duration, metabolism, maximum recommended doses, and patient-specific considerations. By integrating anatomy, pharmacology, and real-world clinical experience, this course empowers providers to elevate their anesthesia practice and deliver safer, more predictable care.Learning Objectives: At the conclusion of this course, attendees will be able to:
Identify key anatomical landmarks and neuroanatomy relevant to advanced local anesthesia techniques.Describe the pharmacology of local anesthetics, including maximum doses, onset, duration, and systemic effects.Recognize common myths and misconceptions about local anesthesia and contrast them with evidence-based facts.Explain potential complications and contraindications associated with various local anesthesia techniques.

title: Clinical Records Prevent Criminal Records
description:
Dr. Shelburne learned that lesson the hard way—serving time from August 20, 2008, to May 14, 2010. But what he gained from that experience has become a powerful roadmap for every dental professional who wants to protect their practice, their reputation, and their freedom.In this eye-opening, energizing program, Dr. Shelburne pulls back the curtain on the reality of Board complaints, insurance audits, malpractice claims, and even criminal allegations—and shows you exactly how to stay out of the danger zone. His message is simple, bold, and liberating: your best defense is rock-solid, bulletproof clinical records.You’ll discover how faulty systems and incomplete documentation can put even great clinicians at risk—and how easy it is to build protective protocols that actually work. Because in the eyes of the law, if it’s not in your clinical record, then you didn’t see it, you didn’t say it, you didn’t do it, it didn’t need to be done… and it doesn’t exist.This course empowers the entire dental team—not just the doctor—to be careful, concise, complete, and diligent. With the right systems, your records become your shield, your proof, and your peace of mind.Learn the no-nonsense, team-driven approach that can mean the difference between thriving in practice… and fighting for it.Learning Objectives: 1. To understand that what you don’t know can hurt you and that ignorance is no
excuse2: To learn to assimilate and maintain records that can both protect and defend. 3. Understand the necessity of due diligence and how to conduct records review and
internal audits to insure accuracy and excellence

title: Oral Cancer from Love and Other Drugs: The HPV Epidemic
description:
Historically, we have considered tobacco and alcohol as the primary culprits of oral cancer. Not anymore! HPV Oral Pharyngeal Cancer (HPV-OPC) accounts for about 73% of all oral pharyngeal cancers. These too are exacerbated by smoke, booze and our all-too-commonplace acid reflux, but certain HPV strains are much more aggressive, even by themselves—and they are easily detected in saliva.With the incidence of HPV-OPC on such a rambunctious rise, increasing at about 30% per year, we all need to be more sophisticated in our approach to screening, discussing preventive recommendations, and at considering the root cause—oral sex. Keeping in mind that 50% of all new HPV infections occur between the ages of 15 and 24, our efforts need to be focused on patients at every age. This lecture will arm you with relevant studies, prevention strategies, early detection efforts and communication points for some potentially uncomfortable conversations with patients. Learning ObjectiLearning Objectives: 1. Understand the growing prevalence of oral and oropharyngeal cancers and the lifestyle risk factors contributing to their rise.
2. Explain the connection between HPV infection—especially through oral sex—and the increasing incidence of oral pharyngeal cancers.3. Recognize the importance of the 9-valent HPV vaccination as well as salivary diagnostics in the prevention and early identification of HPV-related oral pharyngeal cancers.4. Identify the critical role of tobacco cessation in reducing the risk of oral and oropharyngeal cancers and the potential for these conversations in the dental chair5. Implement a comprehensive 3- to 5-minute routine oral cancer examination during preventive visits to enhance early detection efforts.6. Learn critical communication points for patients and appropriate medical referrals.

title: The Exceptional Assistant: Indirect Restorations
description: PART 2: In this dynamic session, we will delve into the intricacies of single crowns, using them as a model to build your clinical knowledge and skills. But that’s not all—we’ll also expand our focus to multiple crowns and veneers. We’ll dive into specific procedure skills, clinical preparation, and communication. Understanding the nuances of each step ensures seamless patient care and efficient teamwork. Effective communication and a commitment to excellence are your allies. Let’s elevate your role together!Learning Objectives: Learn the fundamentals of a single crown and a single crown provisional restoration.Expand your skills to tissue management, and the clinical materials and products that improve outcomes.Maximize your digital skills, including intraoral scanning, model printing, photography, and digital lab communication.Review traditional impression techniques and stone model fabrication.Develop your understanding of how esthetics and occlusion are optimized.Learn the importance of clear communication and tips for ensuring communication with your lab.

title: The Gummy Smile - Sugery to Final Restorations" Esthetic and Functional Crown Lengthening Procedures
description:
Providing functional and esthetic dental therapy to achieve great smiles is the treatment outcome we all hope to achieve for our patients. The interface between surgical and restorative partners to achieve these outcomes include the knowledge and skill sets which both partners need to understand and execute together as the dental team. Most importantly, given the skill sets are available, are 1) what does the patient want to achieve as their treatment outcome and 2) clear communication between the surgical and restorative partners with the patient to make the patient’s goal happen! This course will go in-depth for the entire dental team. Exploration of the surgical considerations to achieve these great smiles for our patients will be provided to attendees both didactically and through clinical cases presenting surgical procedures and final restorations during this course. “Pearls” from Kois, Robbins, Gargiulo, Vacek, Offenbacher, CDT codes, and current Portland clinicians will all be presented.Learning Objectives: At the conclusion of this course, attendees will be able to:
* Define the (dental) “esthetic zone” for our patients.
At the conclusion of this course, attendees will be able to:
* Know how to correct an unesthetic smile to achieve an esthetic smile surgically.At the conclusion of this course, attendees will be able to:
* Know once the surgical procedures are performed and the esthetic outcome is achieved, what
needs to be done restoratively to achieve the great smiles for our patients.
At the conclusion of this course, attendees will be able to:
* Know what an esthetic dental outcome will look like from our patients’ perspective.At the conclusion of this course, attendees will be able to:
* Minimize your risk for an adverse esthetic outcome for your patients.

title: The Mental Dental Connection: The Secret to Being a Better Clinician & Leader
description:
Dental professionals often examine the physical aspects of wellness yet we rarely explore or discuss the mental aspects. It is time to remove the taboo and face the facts. Anxiety, pain, depression, burnout. Dentistry can be difficult and take a toll. Studies show us that dental professionals experience a high incidence of mental illness. Additionally, many dental professionals don’t clearly understand the mental health conditions that our patients present with, which red flags to watch for or what to do/how to intervene. Presented in Dr. Joshua Austin’s trademark comedic style, this course explores strategies for working together to improve mental health so we can be our best. Learn how to recognize triggers and warning signs. Identify essential tools and resources. Discover how and when to refer to a mental health professional. Explore and understand mental health terms, common treatments and therapy options. Gain coping mechanisms and learn how to manage expectations – both our ownand those of our patients.Learning Objectives: Define mental health terms and analyze mental health statisticsDiscover mechanisms for coping with our personal and professional challenges
Illuminate common DSM-5 diagnoses and characteristics
Review neuropsychopharmacological agents and their impact on oral health.
Identify and examine our psychologic triggers and their importance
Explore the benefits of psychotherapy

title: Think and Work like a Pediatric Dentist
description: Pediatric dentistry is far more than just filling teeth on little people. Some of the unique aspects of caring for children include managing behavior, carefully watching for potential anomalies, establishing successful treatment plans, monitoring growth and development for the timing of orthodontic referrals and performing the appropriate restorative and preventive procedures. And, of course, let’s not forget about the parents! Many feel the parents are the biggest problem with a pediatric appointment, but we’ll discuss now to make them your entertainment as well as your best referral source. This course will stimulate your thinking about dental age, sequential care and management tools for successful care for children in a general practice. Part of the vastly different world of pediatrics includes recognizing that each examination with a pediatric patient is actually a new patient examination due to the child’s growth. If you have ever wondered “how pediatric dentists can handle kids,” you’ll find out here.Learning Objectives: Analyze "dental age" from chronological age and why this is important in treatment planningImplement tools for managing the behavior of parentsAppreciate why every pediatric dental examination is actually a new patient examEstablish handouts for parents that will reflect your discussion with them
instructors: Greg Psaltis, DDS, Certificate in Pediatric Dentistry

title: What's Sex Got To Do With It? Under The Radar Women's Health Conditions
description:
Women’s bodies are constantly evolving. From the moment of first menstruation to menopause, the body is undergoing constant evolution. Whether for better or worse, hormones and other factors play a role in how women experience daily life.There is a lot of discussion about the common effects of hormones throughout a woman’s life cycle. Yet there are some conditions that although men can acquire them, the predominant patient populations are women. Why do women get these conditions more than men? Some of these are well-known but others are not as common. Reviewing patient's medical histories is vital to understanding patients' conditions and medications. This program will review several conditions that plague women more than men but we, as health professionals, are not given as much information about. Learn about lesser discussed topics that can affect a women’s systemic health as well as their oral health and how we as dental professionals can assist patients in maintaining health. Various products and technologies will be discussed during the program. Learning Objectives: define autoimmune conditions and the role autoimmunity plays in a woman's life
explain the role of estrogen in TMDreview polymyalgia rheumatica and raynaud's phenomenondiscuss vertigo, skin disorders and other common and uncommon women's health conditions understand how these health conditions impact patient dental care and treatment

title: WORKSHOP: Prevent, Protect, Preserve! Teeth, Implants, and Advanced Instrumentation
description: It’s a vital time for dental professionals to acquire the latest protocols and products to optimize complete care for our patients’ natural teeth, implants, and most of all their overall health. Given the evolving information on preventing periodontal and peri-implant disease, protecting implants, and preserving restorations, it is imperative to stay informed on the latest advancements. This course will cover updated protocols for prophylaxis, implant maintenance, treating periodontal and peri-implant disease. We will delve into research surrounding air polishing powders, prophy pastes, and varnishes, along with which products to recommended to patients for their home-care regimen. Emphasis will be placed on what research recommends using now and why it is important. Participate in an interactive experience exploring advanced instrumentation and trialing the latest preventive, protective, and restorative products. Discover how prevention for natural teeth, protection of implants, and preservation of restorations are fundamental to providing comprehensive patient care and enhancing overall health outcomes! Learning Objectives: Apply current guidelines and clinical practices for the management of tooth- and implant-borne restorations, as well as the treatment of periodontal and peri-implant diseases.ntegrate innovative protocols, tools, and products derived from biofilm research into clinical practice for the care of natural teeth, implants, and the longevity of restorations.Understand the critical importance of biofilm-focused home care in promoting patients' oral and overall health.Demonstrate advanced instrumentation techniques and effectively utilize the latest preventive, protective, and restorative products in clinical practice.

title: CPR for the Dental Professional
description: New 2025 standards for CPR and AED use for adults, infants, and children with new choking standards. Successfull completion results in a 2 year certificate. You will review the use of a bag valve mask for rescue breathing as well as when to use naloxone to reverse a narcotic overdose. New books will be availble. Team work is emphasized after initial recognition of cardiac or respiratory arrest. AEDs (automatic external defibrillators) will be taught with a final "mini-code" scenario. There is a written "celebration of knowledge" at the end which is open book. Your entire office is encouraged to attend as a group since teamwork leads to successful resuscitation. Learn the different signs and symptoms of a "heart attack" in males and females, young and old. Be prepared to respond to a choking emergency. You will be using feedback manikins in order to evaluate your rate and depth of compressions and effectiveness of ventillations. How well you train helps you to respond with confidence to office emergencies.

title: The Heart of the Matter
description: Every 40 seconds, someone in the United States suffers a heart attack. As part of our standard of care, blood pressure measurements should be taken at every routine appointment. Up to 50% of heart attacks and strokes are triggered by oral pathogens. The dental professional plays a vital role in the screening, prevention, and management of heart disease.Learning Objectives: Discover the latest statistics on heart diseaseReview nitric oxide and the vital role it plays in oral and systemic healthIdentify best practices for screening patients for heart diseaseEvaluate practical approaches to a heart-healthy dietInvestigate home care therapies to reduce periodontal disease and promote heart health

title: Oregon Board of Dentistry Updates and Common Questions
description: This course will present an overview of current topics related to the Dental Practice Act. It will cover recent rule changes and how they impact the dental office. Common misunderstandings and issues that Board staff encounter frequently will be discussed. The presentation will also review continuing education requirements, the audit process, common issues seen in investigations, and changes to dental assisting rules.Learning Objectives: Learn a general overview about the Oregon Board of DentistryLearn about the recent rule changes and how they impact the dental practiceOverview of continuing education audits, investigations, and recent dental assisting rule changes

title: Implant Treatments for Successful Outcomes
description:
Dental implants are a known and predictable treatment option for missing teeth, where most patients today desire this therapy. Although implant survival is extremely high often above 90-95%, these favorable outcomes depend greatly on proper case selection, diagnosis and treatment planning, surgical and prosthetic execution, and management of complications. The informed consent process must discuss patient expectations vs. what can reasonably be delivered for each individual patient. Comprehensive clinical and radiographic examinations must also be performed to determine hard and soft tissue deficiencies that may need to be addressed. In addition, patients are continuously demanding more complicated treatment modalities with immediate placement, immediate loading, using the most state-of-the art materials and techniques. This presentation will update the practicing dentist on achieving the most ideal and successful implant outcomes, while understanding risks, benefits, alternatives, and indications. Successful outcomes can be achieved when fundamental principles are followed, and both clinicians and patients have realistic expectations. Learning Objectives: This course will discuss how appropriate diagnosis and treatment planning leads to favorable outcomes.
This course will discuss state-of-the-art implant techniques and materials. This course will help the practicing clinician improve implant outcomes.

title: Promoting Ethical Practices and Professional Integrity: Ethical Dilemmas Facing New (and Experienced) Dentists
description: As professional, we have an obligation to treat our patients and co-workers in an honest, ethical manner. This course will discuss how that might look in several real-life situations and answer questions course participants may have in a lively, interactive format.Learning Objectives: How do we ethically handle unintended dental outcomes by ourselves or our colleages.How do we behave ethically as an employee or a practice owner.How to be ethical in our involvement with insurance companies.A.I. and ethics

title: The Wonderful World of Special Care (Cultural Competency)
description: The Wonderful World of Special Care is an interactive course that puts the learner in the shoes of many of our patients. This course contains simulations that leave the learners with a newly formed empathy for our patients' struggles in the dental office. Step out of your world and into ours as you engage and feel what it is like to experience the world in a different way. Sit in the "Splash Zone" for a particularly interactive experience. Though this course is centered around special care dentistry, the concepts can be applied to make any patient more comfortable in the chair. This course focuses on Maslow's Hierarchy of needs and how meeting needs at multiple levels can influence patient behaviors to achieve better outcomes. This course often is a crowd favorite due to its interactive nature. All members of the team are welcome and this information is important to anyone who works with patients.Learning Objectives: better understand non-verbal communication alter their environments to influence behaviors better understand how Maslow's hierarchy of needs applies to treating dental patients

title: Creating Beautiful & Healthy Smiles Part 1: Esthetic Diagnosis & Treatment Planning
description: In the dynamic landscape of restorative dentistry, where teeth may be missing, damaged, or misaligned, and occlusion can deteriorate, clinicians face unique challenges. Where do you start and how do you finish when your normal reference points are missing and your goal is to restore your patient to a result that is beautiful, stable, and healthy? This course delves into these questions and provides answers.We begin the day with a lecture that covers the fundamentals of esthetics and function and their relationship to one another. These fundamentals will guide our detailed look at treatment planning and decision making in both the complex and simple cases that come your way.Learning Objectives: Facial and dental estheticsThe symbiotic relationship between esthetics and functionHow to assess cases without the usual esthetic reference points of teeth and gingivaDigital smile designThe esthetic risk of the patient

title: Dental Anatomy: Clinical Relevance and Digital Integration
description: Dental anatomy remains a timeless foundation for clinical excellence, yet its relevance is often overlooked in routine practice. This presentation bridges traditional anatomical knowledge with modern clinical applications, highlighting how digital technologies—such as intraoral scanning, CAD/CAM systems, and 3D modeling—are transforming the ways we teach, learn, and implement dental anatomy. Through case-based insights, attendees will explore how anatomical principles guide the creation of restorations, occlusal schemes, and alignments, while digital integration enhances diagnosis, treatment planning, and both restorative and esthetic outcomes. The session will also address the challenges and opportunities of this evolving integration, emphasizing the enduring value of anatomical precision in contemporary dentistry. In addition, participants will examine complex clinical scenarios, including patients with cleft palate and those affected by amelogenesis imperfecta, where unique tooth anomalies demand careful consideration. These examples will demonstrate how restoration and the achievement of optimal esthetic results are possible through the thoughtful application of anatomical principles combined with meticulous treatment planning, underscoring the indispensable role of anatomy in even the most demanding cases.Learning Objectives: Identify key dental anatomical structures and explain their significance in direct and indirect restorative procedures aimed at achieving optimal esthetic outcomes.Understand how digital technologies—including intraoral scanning, CAD/CAM systems, and 3D modeling—facilitate the visualization and application of anatomical principles in clinical practice.Integrate anatomical knowledge using hybrid analog-digital workflows to improve diagnostic accuracy, treatment planning, and restorative/esthetic results.Evaluate the clinical advantages and limitations of incorporating digital tools alongside traditional anatomical approachesDiscuss current

title: Getting the Upper Hand on Pain
description: Nearly 75% of hygienists and 40% of dentists experience hand and wrist pain. Fewer dental professionals fully recover from hand pain syndromes than they do from pain in the neck & shoulder, so early intervention is essential! Dr. Valachi brings powerful research to this CE course that focuses on ergonomic, therapeutic and exercise strategies to help dental professionals prevent hand, wrist & arm pain and extend their careers. Many pain syndromes mimic carpal tunnel syndrome but have an entirely different etiology! Learn how to recognize the symptoms of carpal tunnel, DeQuervains syndrome, cubital tunnel syndrome, osteoarthritis of the thumb, thoracic outlet syndrome, tennis elbow, anterior interosseous nerve syndrome and tennis elbow, and implement proven evidence-based interventions. Learn the ergonomic criteria for selecting truly ergonomic dental instruments, including diameter, taper, texture, sharpness and material. Learn about the one healthcare professional that can do the BEST differential diagnosis of hand & wrist pain syndromes, and how to find this specialist in your city. Team members will walk away with techniques they can apply immediately in the operatory and at home to work more comfortably and improve quality of life.Learning Objectives: Recognize symptoms of carpal tunnel syndrome, osteoarthritis of the thumb, deQuervains disease, tennis elbow, AIN and thoracic outlet syndrome.Discuss the 5 pain syndromes that mimic carpel tunnel syndrome, but have an entirely different etiology.Identify ergonomic selection criteria for hygiene & dental instruments.Implement self-treatment (exercise and therapeutic) strategies to prevent and treat many common hand, wrist and arm pain syndromes.Implement essential ergonomic strategies in the operatory to prevent hand, wrist and arm injuries.State the one healthcare professional who is most highly qualified to perform differential diagnosis of hand & wrist pain syndromes.
instructors: Bethany Valachi, PT, DPT, MS, CEAS

title: Is Dentistry a Team Sport?
description: Build trust. Be vulnerable. Embrace conflict. Be accountable. These are just a few of the prescriptions practice management coaches often write for dental practices seeking to create a winning and cohesive team. Certainly, these actions or attributes are important, even vital, to the functioning and overall success of a great team. But where does it really start? How do we acquire them? The strength of the team is each individual team member, and the strength of each team member is the team. Zen master Phil Jackson, one of the winningest coaches in the history of basketball, says, “In order to build and be a truly effective (winning) team, we must start with ideal team players.” In this interactive and hands-on presentation, Geri will use her 30+ years of experience in the dental industry to discuss and teach the core essence of the ideal team player and a winning cohesive team.Learning Objectives: Articulate three tangible characteristics of an ideal team player.Identify one area or characteristic a team player can improve in themselves and othersInitiate a team discussion about what it takes to build a more cohesive and higher performing team.

title: Revealing Deception: How Ordinary Practices Have Extraordinary Theft
description: Embezzlement in dental practices rarely looks dramatic—until the losses are uncovered. In this powerful 3-hour session, attendees will learn how ordinary, trusted team members exploit everyday systems to carry out extraordinary theft. Using real case examples and clear forensic insights, this course reveals the behavioral red flags, software manipulation tactics, and operational vulnerabilities that allow embezzlement to go undetected.
