Participants will leave with practical strategies to strengthen internal controls, improve owner oversight, and protect their practice from financial deception.Learning Objectives: Recognize behavioral and operational signs of embezzlement.Identify how common workflows create opportunities for theft.Detect manipulation patterns inside practice management softwareImplement proactive internal controls to prevent financial loss.Learn oversight systems for early detection.Learn how to oversee the revenue from a safegaurded aspect

title: The Dental Hygiene Detective
description: There are many clues that provide evidence of oral disease such as occlusal wear, recession, bleeding, fissured tongues, erythematic tissues, incipient lesions, and many more. Determining our patient’s high-risk factors for oral disease and systemic health is multifactorial, and our role as dental professionals allows us to evaluate and identify the first signs of disease. This program will help you and your team review patient risk factors in depth while empowering the clinician to compare clinical technologies that alight with the patient’s lifestyle to reduce disease. Recommended for all team members!Learning Objectives: Review the role of pH in caries risk assessment and learn to identify stages of demineralization and remineralizationLearn how to expedite risk assessments to enhance patient education and preventionPrioritize the patient’s high-risk factors to comprehensively decrease their risk of oral and systemic diseasesEvaluate the latest clinical and home care technologies to decrease patient oral and systemic disease riskDiscover how to integrate lasers into the workflow

title: Top Clinical Tips for Dental Team Members
description: Today, dental offices are busy places - often filled with distractions, lack of communication, in tandem with rapidly evolving technology and dental materials. Let's face it - dentistry is not always easy! This program will provide ideas to create a well-organized, patient-focused dental team that can orchestrate the patient journey beginning with the initial phone call and extending beyond their exit. Expect to learn specific details, “easy-to-implement” ideas, techniques, current materials, and tips for each team member to build a successful dental practice. Look for this knowledge and tips to help maximize your role within the dental team. Topics will range from clinical protocols, communication, and isolation, to digital dentistry and best practices. Get excited about creating your ultimate new “Top Tips CheckList” with ideas that work and can be implemented on Monday! Utilizing these tips will set the stage for a total team approach, healthy environment, boost case acceptance, and professional satisfaction.Learning Objectives: Update your restorative materials list with clinical confidenceBoost your education, communication, and case presentation skillsCapitalize on specific elective servicesUnderstand the value of cross-trainingLearn assessment skills that benefit the patient

title: CPR for the Dental Professional
description: New 2025 standards for CPR and AED use for adults, infants, and children with new choking standards. Successfull completion results in a 2 year certificate. You will review the use of a bag valve mask for rescue breathing as well as when to use naloxone to reverse a narcotic overdose. New books will be availble. Team work is emphasized after initial recognition of cardiac or respiratory arrest. AEDs (automatic external defibrillators) will be taught with a final "mini-code" scenario. There is a written "celebration of knowledge" at the end which is open book. Your entire office is encouraged to attend as a group since teamwork leads to successful resuscitation. Learn the different signs and symptoms of a "heart attack" in males and females, young and old. Be prepared to respond to a choking emergency. You will be using feedback manikins in order to evaluate your rate and depth of compressions and effectiveness of ventillations. How well you train helps you to respond with confidence to office emergencies.

title: Medical Emergencies in the Dental Office
description: Medical emergencies can and do occur in dental practices every day. Over the next fifteen years the segment of the population at highest risk for medical emergencies, those 65 and older, will become a larger percentage of our patients. The entire dental team must be prepared to recognize and respond to a variety of medical emergencies that could be precipitated by the stress of the dental environment. This course will discuss the diagnosis, prevention, and management of common medical emergencies that might occur in dental offices. This four-hour course is appropriate for the entire dental team and meets the medical emergency education requirement of the Oregon Board of Dentistry for license renewal and anesthesia permits.Learning Objectives: Participants will be able to identify those at increased risk for specific medical emergencies.Participants will be able to diagnose the common medical emergencies based on the signs and symptoms demonstrated by a patient.Participants will develop a rational approach to the management of medical emergencies in general as well as the specific protocols for managing each medical emergency discussed.Participants will be able to identify modifiable risk factors for medical emergencies and understand methods to prevent their occurrence in the dental environment.

title: OHSU CaseCATS Student Poster Competition
Systemic Health, Medications & Implant Osseointegration
description:
Systemic Health, Medications & Implant OsseointegrationAlthough dental implants are highly successful in healthy patients, medical risk factors can significantly compromise outcomes. Therefore, clinicians must be familiar with the effect of systemic health factors on implant osseointegration. Are there relative or absolute contraindications to implant placement? Or, should we just make sure our patients optimize their medical conditions before implant surgery is performed? Since implant survival and success is so high, with many studies citing > 90-95%, are there any medical contraindications to treatment planning implants? Is there concern with control of diabetes? Do patients need to quit smoking before implant surgery? Can patients receive implants if they are taking bisphosphonates or denosumab? How do we determine if patients post-radiation are candidates for implant placement? This presentation will discuss implant procedures in smokers, diabetics, osteoporotics, and patients taking various medications. These systemic medical conditions can cause significant delays in healing, or even failure of ossoeintegration.Learning Objectives: • This course will provide updates on medical risk factors and medications that may affect implant osseointegration.
• This course will discuss how to maximize osseointegration in medically compromised patients.• This course will discuss alternative protocols to improve outcomes in smokers, diabetics, and patients taking anticoagulants.

title: Special Care Show & Tell (Cultural Competency)
description: This course is just as the title suggests. Dr. Brooke will show things that help make it easier for her to treat her patients. It is like in kindergarten, where you bring in your cool toys to show your friends, but dental style. Dr. Brooke has many slides with numerous things she uses from websites to toothpastes to pharmacy compounds this course will build your toolbox to tackle unique problems. She even brings some of the "toys" so you can touch and feel them after class. Do you have some cool toys you like to use? Come in, shout them out! We are all here to learn and share and grow. Join us for an hour of show and tell fun. Thought this course is created with Special Care Dentistry in mind, these tools work well for all patients. We have found that even neurotypical patients respond well and enjoy these concepts and products.Learning Objectives: compare various bite blockscompare various toothbrushes compare various interproximal cleaning aids and much more

title: Complications in Implant Dentistry
description:
Complications in Implant Dentistry Dental implants are a known and predictable treatment option for missing teeth, where most patients today desire this therapy. Although implant survival is extremely high, these favorable outcomes depend greatly on proper case selection, minimizing medical risk factors, surgical and prosthetic execution, and prevention and management of complications. Complications can occur because of poor planning, failure of execution intraoperatively, and postoperatively. For the practicing clinician, we must focus on prevention of complications, diagnosing complications early, managing them as quickly as possible, and utilizing new technologies to minimize complications. Dentists must also understand when patients or complications are out of their scope, and consider referral to the appropriate specialist. This presentation will update the practicing clinician on achieving the most ideal and successful implant outcomes, and avoiding and managing complications. With patients becoming much more challenging, both from a medical and expectation perspective, prevention and management of complications is of the utmost importance.Learning Objectives: • This course will discuss common complications in dental implant therapy.
• This course will discuss strategies to identify and prevent unfavorable outcomes in the implant patient.• This course will discuss the use of new technologies to improve outcomes and prevent or minimize complications.

title: Infection Control in the Dental Office
description: Infection prevention and control are important elements of every patient encounter in the dental setting. The Oregon Board of Dentistry has specific regulations for infection control practice in every dental office. Dental professionals and patients are increasingly aware of the potential spread of infectious diseases from aerosols, droplets, contaminated surfaces and equipment, and the dental devices that are connected to water sources. This course will provide the most current information regarding evidence-based infection control practices. The presenter will review the latest infection control guidance from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), and provide easy to implement methods for ensuring a safe care environment.This course will include a review of appropriate selection and use of PPE, disinfection and sterilization, dental waterline management, bloodborne exposure prevention and post-exposure follow-up, and immunization recommendations. In addition to dental-specific infection control guidelines, this course will review related guidelines that apply to all healthcare settings.Learning Objectives: Describe the agencies that provide recommendations and regulations for infection control in dental settings.Determine the appropriate steps needed in their practice to ensure compliance with infection control standards.Define the CDC Core Practices.

title: I Found A Lump, Is This Normal? A Modern Approach to Clinical Oral Pathology
description: Oral and Maxillofacial hard and soft tissue diseases encompass a wide range of pathologic conditions ranging from indolent and incidental to malignant that may carry substantial morbidity and mortality. Dental practitioners are most often the first providers to identify these problems for patients. Given the multitude of oral diseases and treatment options, developing an accurate assessment and carrying out clinical decisions may be overwhelming. The purpose of this talk is to develop an algorithmic approach to diagnosis, treatment and referrals for a broad differential of hard and soft tissue pathology of the mouth and jaws.Learning Objectives: The participant will be able to develop a differential diagnosis for hard and soft tissue oral pathology based on history, physical exam, and dental radiographyThe participant will be able to identify common and uncommon oral diseases The participant will be able to risk stratify lesions into low, intermediate, and high risk categoriesThe participant will understand the rationale for tests and procedures to aid in diagnosis of oral pathologic conditionsThe participant will be able to describe CBCT findings for a variety of hard tissue diseases The participant will be able to apply an algorithmic approach to clinical decision making in the management of oral pathology

title: Become a WOW Dental Assistant
description: This comprehensive program will prepare you to excel as a dental assistant. This course emphasizes both technical excellence and the interpersonal skills that make truly exceptional dental assistants invaluable members of the dental team! Through examples and techniques, clinical skills development, and professional practice management, you will learn to support dentists consistently and efficiently. You will master the art of chairside assisting, learn to anticipate the dentist’s needs during procedures, ensure patient comfort, while maintaining the highest standards of dental care. You will become proficient in taking both digital and traditional impressions - understanding the required anatomy capture, along with fabricating temporary crowns. Detailed learning will include a dental materials update, how to cross-train, develop excellent patient rapport, methods to reduce patient anxiety, while promoting a safe environment, and scheduling appointments. Expect this program to enhance the importance of your role within a smoothly running practice, advance your career, and build your confidence.Learning Objectives: Build a comprehensive knowledge of current dental materialsFine tune your initiative and preparedness to create more value within the practiceImprove communication skillsRaise your happiness quotient and lower stressBecome more qualified, competent, and successful

title: Creating Beautiful & Healthy Smiles Part 2: Functional Diagnosis and Treatment Planning
description: After exploring the esthetic considerations of diagnosis and treatment planning (Part 1 - 8 am - 11 am), we’ll delve into the functional and occlusal considerations of creating a beautiful and healthy smile. We’ll continue to discuss treatment decision-making to harmonize esthetics and function to achieve optimal stability and naturally beautiful results.Learning Objectives: The fundamental concepts of occlusion and functionPosterior functional reference points and tooth relationshipsAnterior functional reference points and tooth relationshipsConnecting anterior and posterior function with estheticsThe functional risk of the patient

title: I Haven't Got Time for This Pain! Dental Pain Management for the Entire Team
description:
Pain management is a critical component of dental care, yet it requires a careful balance between efficacy and patient safety. Local anesthesia is foundational to peri-operative pain control in dentistry, but its effective and safe use requires more than just technical skill; it demands a deep understanding of pharmacology, patient-specific considerations, and evolving clinical protocols. In addition, the use of non-opioid and opioid analgesics used in the treatment of acute post-procedural dental pain also requires an in-depth understanding of the pharmacology, indications, and clinical considerations of the use of these agents. This course provides an in-depth review of both local anesthetics and analgesics used in dental practice, including mechanisms of action, dosage guidelines, pharmacokinetics, contraindications, and potential complications. Dentists and dental hygienists will learn to compare anesthetic and analgesic options, tailor pain management plans to individual patient needs, and navigate regulatory, ethical, and public health concerns. Special attention will be given to patient-specific treatment modifications and current best practices to enhance patient safety, comfort, and outcomes.Learning Objectives: Understand the pharmacology and mechanism of action of
opioid and non-opioid analgesics, as well as their misuseIdentify the intended roles of these analgesics in the
treatment of acute dental pain, as well as situations
which may preclude their useDiscuss best practices for prescribing opioid analgesics
as part of an individualized patient pain management
planDifferentiate between the two major classes of local
anesthetic agents with respect to their metabolism,
adverse effects, drug interactions and contraindicationsDiscuss the rationale for the use of vasoconstrictors in local
anesthetic solutions and their potential effects in common
organ system disease statesSpecify the various local anesthetic agent combinations
most commonly used in dentistr
instructors: Thomas Viola, RPh CCP CDE CPMP

title: It's Not You, It's Me
description:
Have you ever found yourself wondering why communication is so hard, or experienced the frustration of feeling likeyour message isn't getting through? Do you wish you could read people's minds or understand their actions?Join Geri Gottlieb in this interactive and captivating program designed to help you do just that (or at least comeclose)! During this session, Geri will guide you through the fascinating world of DiSC® styles. By understanding yourown unique communication style and learning to identify the styles of those around you, you'll gain practicalknowledge that will empower you to eliminate misunderstandings and foster harmonious relationships. Imaginebeing able to build stronger relationships, resolve conflicts more effectively, and enhance collaboration withanyone—colleagues, patients, friends, and family members. Geri's expertise and engaging approach will leave youinspired and equipped with effective strategies and techniques that can be immediately applied in your dailyinteractions. You'll gain a deeper understanding of yourself and others, enabling you to adapt your communicationapproach for maximum impact. Get ready to discover new ways to connect, influence, and inspire others.Learning Objectives: Discover effective strategies that will enhance their ability to articulate ideas clearly, listen empathetically,
and build rapport effortlessly.Gain a deeper understanding of how different communication styles impact interactions.Navigate any conversation with confidence, ensuring their message is heard loud and clear.

title: Neck, Back & Beyond: Preventing Pain with Evidence-Based Dental Ergonomics
description: One-third of dentists who retire early are forced to, due to a musculoskeletal disorder. Pain is NOT a necessary by-product of delivering dental care! Effectively preventing work-related pain in dentistry requires addressing numerous risk factors with proven, research-based interventions. Dr. Valachi brings 20 years dental ergonomic experience to teach truly effective strategies in the operatory to prevent back, neck and shoulder pain. Through engaging demonstrations, you'll discover how to implement the appropriate interventions to prevent chronic back, neck and shoulder pain.Most students are not taught in school how to properly position patients utilizing a double articulating headrest. Neck and back pain are often the result! Learn Dr. Valachi's 10 Step Patient Positioning Sequence that ensures optimal operator posture and comfort. Learn the secret to utilizing dental positioning aids to treat difficult patients (pedo, geriatric and wheelchair). You'll learn why one dental stool does NOT fit all bodies! Through a guided self-assessment, you'll determine your lumbar curvature and what style of stool and adjustment you should have to prevent back pain or injury. Many dental loupes on the market are not ergonomic and can actually create neck pain! Dr. Valachi teaches 3 ergonomic criteria to select truly ergonomic loupes that will preserve your neck health. Assistant face some of the greatest ergonomic challenges of the dental team! Assistants will discover valuable positioning and seating guidelines to prevent back pain. Join Dr. Bethany for this interactive session to transform your life, and help ensure your career longevity.Learning Objectives: Properly position patients, headrest & chair correctly to optimize your best working posture.Implement appropriate ergonomic interventions to prevent chronic back, neck and shoulder.Utilize dental ergonomic positioning aids and techniques to more comfortably treat difficult patients (pedo, geriatric, wheelchair & more).Id
instructors: Bethany Valachi, PT, DPT, MS, CEAS

title: Periodontal Surgery for the General Dentist
description:
You may want to expand your General Practice to include performing periodontal surgical procedures in your office! How do you decide and KNOW that your patient needs periodontal surgery? What is supposed to happen during the periodontal re-evaluation appointment for your patient? What kinds of periodontal surgery should you consider performing for your office? How do you "triage" your periodontal patients for surgery? How do you minimize your clinical risk management when performing periodontal surgery in your office? Clinical cases will be presented as part of this presentation. This learning opportunity will provide you with information to assist you to provide periodontal surgery in your office or expand your current periodontal surgical services in your office beyond what you are providing now.Exploration of the surgical opportunities which you should be able to provide with the knowledge from this course to perform predictable quality periodontal surgical procedures for your patients will be presented.The second part of this presentation will be presenting highlights from the Academy of Osseointegration and the American Academy of Periodontology 2025 Consensus on Prevention & Management of Peri-Implant diseases. Learning Objectives: At the conclusion of this course, attendees will be able to:
* Know what pre-surgical preparation is required before you perform surgery in your office.
At the conclusion of this course, attendees will be able to:
* Determine if your patient is a “good” candidate for you to perform the surgery or is it better to
refer the patient to the periodontist for surgery. “Triage your patients!”
At the conclusion of this course, attendees will be able to:
* Determine the “Rules of Engagement” for performing periodontal surgery. At the conclusion of this course, attendees will be able to:
* Know the limitations of performing periodontal surgery in the General Practice office.At the conclusion of this course, attendees will be able to:
* Minimi

title: Team Trust & Integrity: The Front Line of Financial Integrity
description: Front office teams are the heartbeat of financial integrity in a dental practice. From patient check-in to insurance verification to daily deposits, they sit at the first—and often only—line of defense against financial errors, inefficiencies, and potential fraud. This course teaches practices how to build a culture of trust, accountability, and ethical awareness, while supporting teams with the systems and structure they need to succeed. Using real-world examples and clear operational guidance, attendees will learn how to strengthen internal communication, prevent financial vulnerabilities, and ensure that every team member contributes meaningfully to the practice’s financial stability.Learning Objectives: Understand how team culture affects financial accuracy and security.Identify daily workflows that impact financial integrityImprove transparency, documentation, and communicationRecognize behaviors that support or undermine trustImplement systems that empower teams to protect the revenue cycleStrengthen accountability through clear role expectations and consistent follow-up

title: The RDH Toolkit: Evidence-Based Products & Protocols for Optimal Patient Outcomes
description: How we decontaminate the oral cavity matters. The demands of fast-paced lifestyles have increased our patient’s oral diseases. To be effective in our chairside treatments, having updated technologies is requisite. To be effective in our chairside treatments, having updated technologies is requisite.This course is designed to review the newest technology and its techniques and discuss how to improve patient care while reducing stress on the clinician.Learning Objectives: Identify how to enhance patient care and communication by leveraging chairside technologiesEvaluate the clinician and patient benefits of the technologiesReview power instrumentation for the dental hygienistUnderstand the science behind the clinical innovationsDetermine how the armamentarium will fit into everyday workflow

title: WORKSHOP: Socket Bone Grafting Hands-On Workshop
description: This course will allow participants to learn how to predictably assess a socket after extraction, and then proceed with the appropriate socket grafting technique(s) utilizing todays materials and methodologies. Proper materials selection (bone grafting materials, membranes, sutures, etc.) will be discussed and highlighted as well as the benefits of utilizing blood biologics. Realistic socket hard & soft tissue management as well as suturing will be discussed and practiced by all!
instructors: Bobby Birdi, DMD MS FRCDC FACP DABP

title: WORKSHOP: Understanding Sutures & Flap Design for the General Dentist
description: NOTE: This course is a 4-hour hands-on workshop. There is a 2-hour pre-requisite webinar to the hands-on course and it should be viewed by all participants in preparation and prior to the course. The total CE is 6 hours. Oral Surgery procedures are an integral part of everyday dental practice. General Dentists perform extractions, periodontal surgery and implant placements that often require specific suturing skills. However, some clinicians may experience challenges such as suture loosing, flap dehiscence, tearing, and the inability to stabilize flaps. Improper suturing can lead to complications and even treatment failures.Whether dentists have been practicing for years or a relatively short period of time mastering suturing and soft tissue management is an essential skill that will predictably improve the final post-surgical results.The goal of this hands-on course is to train general dentists in predictable and correct suturing techniques and flap designs. Attendees will learn about different types of suture material and needle configuration and indications for their use. The hands-on portion of the course will include lots of firsthand “over the shoulder” tips, tricks, and instructions. Participants will be confident with different suture types and experience the exercise of the most commonly used suturing techniques and flaps in dental practice. Doctors at any level who are interested in improving their suturing skills will be provided with practical information on how to suture properly and improve surgical treatment outcomes. Learning Objectives: Comprehend predictable wound healing, suture mechanics and the importance of correct suturing.Select the proper threads, needles, and hand instruments.Understand why and how to choose a particular type of suture materiaLearn to perform and apply the most useful suturing techniques in divers clinical situations and understand the theory of these different suturing techniques in surgical exodontia, bone grafting, impla
instructors: Thomas G. Wiedemann, MD, PhD, DDS

title: CPR for the Dental Professional
description: New 2025 standards for CPR and AED use for adults, infants, and children with new choking standards. Successfull completion results in a 2 year certificate. You will review the use of a bag valve mask for rescue breathing as well as when to use naloxone to reverse a narcotic overdose. New books will be availble. Team work is emphasized after initial recognition of cardiac or respiratory arrest. AEDs (automatic external defibrillators) will be taught with a final "mini-code" scenario. There is a written "celebration of knowledge" at the end which is open book. Your entire office is encouraged to attend as a group since teamwork leads to successful resuscitation. Learn the different signs and symptoms of a "heart attack" in males and females, young and old. Be prepared to respond to a choking emergency. You will be using feedback manikins in order to evaluate your rate and depth of compressions and effectiveness of ventillations. How well you train helps you to respond with confidence to office emergencies.

title: Immediate Implant Placement
description:
Immediate implant placement and loading is a practice that continues to gain traction in the field of implant dentistry, largely driven by patients and clinicians to reduce treatment time and improve satisfaction. Although implant survival rates are high with immediately placed implants, there is often an increase in complications and a lack of primary implant stability. How can we improve these issues, to meet the needs of both patients and clinicians? And, can we always deliver immediate treatment for patients seeking it? This presentation will discuss indications, contraindications, and specific protocols for immediate implant placement. It will focus on diagnosis, treatment planning, surgical techniques, and materials to facilitate predictability and long-term success with immediate implants. This presentation will also discuss when immediate implants are not indicated. With increasing demand for immediate implant placement and loading, we must meet the demands of our patients without compromising function, esthetics, and long-term stability.Learning Objectives: • This course will discuss evidence-based approaches for immediate implant placement.
• This course will discuss techniques and materials to facilitate immediate implant placement.• This course will discuss clinical cases and strategies to avoid complications with immediate implants.

title: Locum Life: Exploring the Possibilities of Locum Tenens
description: Have you heard of Locum Tenens? Have you considered taking a Locum Tenens position? Have you done a Locum Tenens but wonder what's next? After this course you'll be able to answer all of this and more including learning "What IS a Locum Tenens?" This talk will follow and present the experiences of Ian Dickinson, DMD on his journey as he navigated his "Locum Life" all over the state of Oregon in 2023 and 2024. Other points of interest in this course will include: Analysis of career options in the Dental field, dealing with headhunters, and how you can finally achieve your dream of having someone pay you to live at the beach!Learning Objectives: At the conclusion of this course, attendees will understand what a Locum Tenens is.At the conclusion of this course, attendees will be able to have a greater grasp of their career options in the field of dentistryAt the conclusion of this course, attendees will be able to weigh the pros and cons of engaging in a Locum Tenens job.

title: Infection Control in the Dental Office
description:
Infection prevention and control are important elements of every patient encounter in the dental setting. The Oregon Board of Dentistry has specific regulations for infection control practice in every dental office. Dental professionals and patients are increasingly aware of the potential spread of infectious diseases from aerosols, droplets, contaminated surfaces and equipment, and the dental devices that are connected to water sources. This course will provide the most current information regarding evidence-based infection control practices. The presenter will review the latest infection control guidance from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), and provide easy to implement methods for ensuring a safe care environment.This course will include a review of appropriate selection and use of PPE, disinfection and sterilization, dental waterline management, bloodborne exposure prevention and post-exposure follow-up, and immunization recommendations. In addition to dental-specific infection control guidelines, this course will review related guidelines that apply to all healthcare settings.Learning Objectives: Describe the agencies that provide recommendations and regulations for infection control in dental settings.
Determine the appropriate steps needed in their practice to ensure compliance with infection control standards.Define the CDC Core Practices.

title: Common Oral Pathology
description: This course is designed to inform the participant about various oral pathologic lesions of the oral cavity. The oral health care professional should be able to identify common lesions and understand how to manage patients with such conditions.Learning Objectives: Identify common lesions of the oral cavity during routine examinationDetermine how to properly manage patients presenting with various oral pathologic lesionsDiscuss the treatment plan and prognosis of various oral pathologic lesions with patients

title: Creating Excitement in Hygiene with Lasers
description: This course is designed for everyone—whether you’re brand-new to lasers or have been using them for years. Discover the newest trends in laser therapy for the dental hygienist and explore how laser use has evolved over time.We’ll dive into the questions every hygienist has: Should laser therapy be done at the start of the appointment or after your hygiene therapy is complete? How do lasers compare to antibiotics, pre-procedural rinses, and SRP? What’s the real difference between an initiated tip and an uninitiated tip? And how have settings changed as laser technology has advanced?You’ll also learn how super-pulsed lasers work, how wavelengths impact tissue interaction, and why your settings matter more than you might think. We’ll walk through the most common challenges clinicians face when trying to integrate lasers into hygiene—and how to overcome them with confidence.You’ll leave this course with a clear, step-by-step plan for bringing lasers into your practice, including where to start and how to seamlessly add laser therapy into ANY hygiene schedule.And if you’re already experienced with lasers, you’ll gain strategies to elevate your current protocols and incorporate advanced laser modalities into your daily workflow.Come ready to learn, ask questions, and walk away with practical tools you can use the very next day in the operatory.Learning Objectives: Explore cutting-edge trends in laser therapy and uncover how laser use has transformed hygiene care—backed by the latest researchUnderstand how wavelength, settings, and laser technology influence clinical results in hygiene therapyBuild a ready-to-implement game plan for bringing lasers into ANY hygiene schedule, and level up your existing protocols with advanced laser techniques you can use immediately

title: Digital Dentistry
description: This course will highlight the various new digital tools and methodologies that are available for today's dental practice. Clinical surgical & prosthetic dentistry will be the focus, however various tools for your dental practice will also be showcased. Practical utilization of various digital tools will be discussed. Artificial intelligence, digital impressioning innovations, various surgical innovations and numerous prosthetic innovations will be highlighted, in both tooth based and implant based cases.
instructors: Bobby Birdi, DMD MS FRCDC FACP DABP

title: Evolving Paradigms in Endodontics: minimal access, enhanced debridement, and bioceramic innovations
description:
Endodontic practice continues to evolve, but not every new concept represents a true paradigm shift. This lecture critically examines three contemporary areas of interest to general dentists—minimal access, enhanced debridement and bioceramic innovations—through an evidence-based lens. The session will review current data on whether conservative access designs meaningfully improve the strength and longevity of endodontically treated teeth from a structural and restorative standpoint. Advances in debridement strategies will be discussed in the context of what is demonstrably improved versus what remains theoretical. Finally, the evolution of hydraulic calcium silicate cements will be traced, highlighting how these materials are reshaping obturation philosophy and clinical workflows. Throughout the lecture, published evidence will be used to distinguish what represents genuine progress, incremental change or enduring myth in modern endodontics.Learning Objectives: At the conclusion of this course, attendees will be able to:
1. Determine the optimal restorative approach for endodontically treated teeth to maximize longevity and tooth survival by identifying which structural, restorative and procedural parameters most significantly influence long-term outcomes.Adopt an enhanced root canal disinfection protocol tailored to the clinician’s level of experience and available resources, incorporating evidence-based strategies to improve cleaning efficacy and clinical predictability.Differentiate among available hydraulic calcium silicate cements by composition and clinical behavior and select materials optimized for specific endodontic obturation techniques and treatment objectives.

title: Fit for Leadership
description:
Want to learn about Zone 2 cardio, mixed methods workouts, aideal morning wake up stretching routine, functional exercise & itsergonomic impact on your miraculous body? This invigoratingpresentation was designed to give the health care professionalstrategies on how they can exercise regularly & develop a fitnessmodus operandi. The research shows that physically activeprofessionals have stronger resilience, faster reaction time, moreenergy and sharper cognitive ability than their inactivecounterparts. Most health care professionals are surprised touncover the robust science between their personal health andtheir professional performance. Finally, a different kind of programto help health care professionals perform better in & out of theoffice in 2026 and beyond.Learning Objectives: Learn insider strategies to improve your posture, release chronic muscle pain, improve your range of motion & work & play pain free again
Develop a fitness mindset of a) mixed methods workouts b) HIIT training, c) Zone 2 Cardio d) functional exercise patterns
Gain insight into how NEW nutrition scientific findings impact your body’s ability to respond to your functional fitness & exercise agenda

title: So You Think Your Practice is Doing Great - How Do You Know?
description: Having systems in place leads to improved efficiency and productivity. It pulls the team together, reduces stress and creates a practice that meets and exceeds patient expectations. Often, a dentist will answer the question about how the practice is doing with "It's doing great!!". When asked how they know, many can't give a definitive answer. Learn how to assess and evaluate strengths, weaknesses, and opportunities in the practice. Pull the team together in moving forward armed with knowledge. The sky's the limit!Learning Objectives: Identify opportunities for improvement in the health of the practiceGenerate daily/weekly/monthly/yearly reports that are crucial for you to stay on top ofRecognize the differences between Attitude/Skills/Knowledge and how they affect your systemDecrease stress while increasing productivity

title: Leveraging AI Smile Design from Simple to Complex Restorative Procedures (Lecture and Hands on)
description: Digital dentistry has rapidly advanced from simply replacing teeth to designing smiles that harmonize with the patient’s entire face. SmileCloud brings facial analysis, digital design, and restorative planning together into one collaborative platform that allows clinicians and technicians to communicate more effectively and predictably.In this lecture and hands-on workshop, participants will learn how to incorporate facially driven smile design into their digital workflow—from initial patient photos to final restorative planning. Using real clinical cases, attendees will see how SmileCloud integrates with common CAD platforms and how it can improve communication between the clinician, laboratory, and patient.

title: WORKSHOP: The Modern Full-Arch: Rethinking Value, Workflow & What's Possible
description: This presentation highlights the growing value of transitioning from analog to digital full-arch workflows and how that shift gives clinicians back what matters most: time and confidence in predictable outcomes. We’ll explore how digital tools and validated workflows, such as the OPTISPLINT® and the Digital AOX protocol enable clinicians to streamline treatment steps, improve precision, and minimize remakes while delivering consistently passive and accurate fits.This is a full day course. The morning (8:00 am - 11:00 am) will be lecture focused and the didactic learning portion. The afternoon (12:30 pm - 3:30 pm) will be a hands-on workshop where we walk through the workflow - luting the OPTISPLINT together and rotating through stations for scanning both OPTISPLINT and MUA scan caps.Learning Objectives: How digital workflows reduce appointments and save valuable chair time per caseWhy digital precision drives predictable, repeatable outcomes every timeThe value of simplification: less stress, more consistency, and stronger collaboration between lab and clinicianReal-world examples showing how digital AOX workflows produce measurable ROI and free up clinical capacity for growth

title: WORKSHOP: Extractions & Oral Surgery for the General Dentist
description: This action-packed extraction course, with two hands-on sessions and lectures, will provide a step-by-step blueprint for successful atraumatic extraction techniques with hand instruments and piezoelectric devices as wells as appropriate flap designs for surgical extraction procedures. The associated webinar will provide pre-op patient evaluation and safe patient selection and you will leave prepared to return to your practice and immediately provide these new techniques and procedures.You will learn how to select and demonstrate proficient use of the appropriate armamentarium (Periotomes, Proximators, Apical Retention Forceps, Piezotomes vs. conventional instruments) in order to remove and section teeth as well as determine and demonstrate case-appropriate basic and advanced suturing techniques.The course has been carefully designed to integrate didactic, and visual aides, and extensive hands-on attendee participation with a wide range of different exodontia and bone graft procedures (GBR, socket grafts) for each individual patient. The course ends with an interactive session about complications in oral surgery.Learning Objectives: Understand and apply non-surgical and surgical techniques used in modern exodontiaApply minimally invasive and alveolar ridge-protecting extractions in the general dental practiceManage common complications associated with tooth extractionsPerform current concepts and principles of GBR, including socket preservation, as related to pre-implantological extractions
instructors: Thomas G. Wiedemann, MD, PhD, DDS

title: Eugene Kelly Lecture: Advances in Management of Benign Maxillofacial Pathology
description: The management of benign maxillofacial pathology continues to evolve as advances in surgical technique, molecular diagnostics, and adjunctive medical therapies refine treatment protocols. This course will review contemporary surgical approaches to common benign maxillofacial lesions, emphasizing indications, anatomic considerations, and strategies to minimize morbidity and recurrence. Attendees will gain an understanding of evolving molecular pathogenesis, and will review established and emerging medical adjuncts that may supplement surgical care. The course is designed to enhance clinical decision-making for dentists and surgeons involved in the evaluation and management of benign maxillofacial disease.Learning Objectives: Understand updates in the surgical management of benign maxillofacial lesionsIdentify molecular or genetic assessments of regularly encountered benign maxillofacial lesionsDescribe existing and potential medical adjunctive treatment options for benign maxillofacial lesions

title: Restorative Requirements for Implant Restorations
description: Presentation and Objectives An area of controversy for implant restorative failures is often associated with the occlusion. This stems from differing occlusal philosophies and clinical principles for treating symptoms related to functional occlusal failures, whether as seen with implant-retained restorations or in routine dental care. Moreover, when there is no agreement among practitioners of an “ideal” functional occlusal scheme to address these functional failures, it sets a precedent for treatment by “trial and error”. The objective of this presentation is to reveal empirical data, both anatomical and physiological, that support an “ideal” functional occlusal scheme for a symptom-free masticatory function. Based on this information, this presentation will also outline an assessment and diagnostic protocol to illustrate how these clinical principles apply to routine dental care, with a focus on implant-retained restorations. The objective of this presentation is to explain and illustrate the following as they apply to implant restorations: -identify three clinical implant restorative conditions -the “ideal” occlusion scheme for each restorative condition -identify the appropriate functional treatment goal for each restorative conditionLearning Objectives: The reason for the controversy Address the controversy surrounding the treatment of functional occlusal failures across all dental care disciplines.Present factual data that supports a unified agreement for an “ideal” occlusion scheme for a symptom-free masticatory function.Identify the dental restorative conditions that affect the occlusion scheme in a clinical setting. Outline the distinctions between the three dental restorative conditions.List the restorative requirements for the functional occlusion treatment goal for each dental restorative condition.At the conclusion of this presentation, each dentist will be able to address all functional occlusal failures by treating any dysfunctional occlusal condition
instructors:
James M. Sagawa, DMD
Hannah Glazunov, DMD

title: Ensuring Culturally and Linguistically Competent Dental Care – a patient-centered approach
description: This 2-hour interprofessional session will provide attendees with an overview of culturally competent care for patients from diverse sociocultural and linguistic backgrounds. Participants will build skills to implement a person-centered approach that is relevant and useful for all patients, not just those whose cultural backgrounds are markedly different than the clinician’s.This approach, which emphasizes interpersonal communication skills, empathy, and human interaction, is especially valuable as technological advances are rapidly changing the healthcare environment, including new tools that make it easier to communicate across language barriers and ensure high quality care for patients with limited English proficiency and hearing disabilities.Using case analyses and breakout group discussion, this will be an interactive, two-part session focused on: (1) general approaches for ensuring culturally competent care and (2) how to address language barriers using professional methods of interpretation.Learning Objectives: Describe the core principles of culturally competent careExplore patients’ perspective on their health issues to personalize treatment discussionsIdentify mistrust and engage in trust-building strategiesRecognize social determinants of health that impact careDescribe the pitfalls of poor communication for patients with limited English proficiencyImplement strategies to work effectively with professional interpreter services

title: I Hate You - Please Help Me!
description: Every practice has challenging patients. Many are dealing with anxiety or control issues. Learn to recognize these patients and how to help them to become the most loyal patients you have.Learning Objectives: Recognize what category each difficult patient falls intoGain insight and tools on how to successfully win over your anxious or controlling patientsFeel confident in your ability to make a difference!

title: Oral Cancer: A Focus on Prevention
description: This course will provide oral health care providers with information regarding oral cancer. Potentially malignant conditions, particularly leukoplakia, will be discussed. Prevention of the disease is primarily the job of oral health care professionals; therefore, a focus will be made on preventing oral squamous cell carcinoma.Learning Objectives: Discuss the etiology of potentially malignant conditions of the oral cavityRecognize how potentially malignant conditions present in the oral cavityDetermine an appropriate treatment plan when potentially malignant conditions are discovered

title: RM: Shaping a Safe Future: Optimizing Care with Patient Education and Team Training
description: This course focuses upon essential risk management strategies, empowering dentists with the skills and resources to address key factors that optimize patient outcomes and mitigate potential liabilities. Through evaluation of actual TDIC cases, Shaping a Safe Future: Optimizing Care with Patient Education and Team Training explores the importance of establishing protocols to enhance patient safety. To protect the privacy of those involved, we have changed the names and some details in the cases discussed during this presentation. Additionally, this course provides best practices for obtaining current health histories, insights on essential staff roles and training, and steps for responding to office emergencies.Learning Objectives: Understand the significance of establishing and adhering to protocols and the importance of staff training in medical emergencies.Recognize your role in providing patient education to achieve optimal levels of care and patient compliance.Incorporate controls and procedures to reduce the potential for errors in documentation and increase patient safety.
instructors:
Viren R. Patel, DDS
Peter Tuenge

title: Role of Lasers in Hygiene
description: Are you curious about how lasers fit into dental hygiene—and how they compare to other modalities for managing periodontal disease? Whether you’re brand-new to lasers or already using them in your practice, this course provides a clear and approachable look at the role lasers play in modern hygiene care.We’ll explore lasers from the ground up: where they originated, how they work, and what newer technologies offer today’s clinician. You’ll learn how lasers differ from traditional periodontal therapies, and gain insight into how hygienists across the country are incorporating them into their workflow to support better patient outcomes and greater efficiency.You’ll discover how to identify ideal patients for laser therapy, understand what happens within the tissue at a biological level, see how timing within the hygiene visit can influence results, and learn what you can realistically expect based on current research. We’ll also discuss how lasers can contribute to practice growth and profitability without adding complexity to your day.You’ll leave this course with a strong foundational understanding of laser dentistry and a clear picture of how lasers can enhance your hygiene appointments, improve patient care, and support a modern, forward-thinking practice.Learning Objectives: Explore the latest trends in laser therapy and see how laser use has evolved over time, backed by current researchUnlock the science behind lasers—understand how they work and what’s happening inside the tissue to support optimal patient careIdentify ideal patients for laser therapy, set realistic expectations for outcomes, and discover how lasers can benefit your practice and workflowMaster tips to effectively implement lasers into every hygiene schedule and how to elevate your current laser therapy plan by introducing advanced laser modalities

title: Seeing is Healing—Transforming Periodontal Care Through Endoscopic Visualization
description: The first goal of periodontics is to produce a clean root surface—but our ability to do so is limited by what we can see. Traditional methods allow for debridement to a depth of only about four millimeters subgingivally, even in the best of hands.In this presentation, Dr. Lee N. Sheldon shares how his periodontal practice has successfully treated virtually every patient—including those with advanced bone loss—without surgery, by combining salivary diagnostics, periodontal endoscopy and EDTA for enhanced root surface conditioning. Attendees will see the exact protocols Dr. Sheldon and his hygienists use to achieve remarkable clinical outcomes, including documented cases of bone regeneration.The key is visualization. Once you can see it, you can clean it. This technology elevates the skilled hygienist to the central role in achieving complete periodontal healing.This course is designed for dentists and hygienists together who want to enhance their diagnostic precision, clinical effectiveness, and patient outcomes. If you want your patients to heal periodontally—and your hygienists to excel—this is a don’t-miss session that will redefine your in-office periodontal program.Learning Objectives: Understand the advantages and principles of periodontal endoscopy—how enhanced visualization transforms both diagnosis and treatment outcomes.Integrate advanced diagnostics and patient communication to guide comprehensive treatment planning and recognize perio-systemic implications.Discover how a simple adjunctive agent (EDTA) detoxifies root surfaces and promotes bone and attachment regeneration—without surgical intervention.

title: Work Life Balance
description:
Your nervous system is watching when you scroll on your phoneor watch the news. Your Immune system eavesdrops on yournervous system. If you don’t think you’re physical and emotionallyimpacted by stress you’re kidding yourself. Chronic social mediaattention to the global increase in military combat, human (foodshortage/ crime) and natural disasters can lead to thedevelopment of short term & long term psychological stressdisorders. You and your patients are experiencing this if you havepoor stress management strategies: low energy, anxiety, gloomand doom thinking, depression, brain fog, frequent colds andchronic pain.Learning Objectives: Recognize the warning signs and the implications of you and your patients being out of balance
Make the connection between STRESS and your patient’s ORAL HEALTH and TOTAL BODY HEALTH
The best 5 physical movements to do when waking up A new immune boosting eating strategy7 ways to lower cortisol in the evening so you can sleep deeper

title: Registration
Train-the Trainer: IPC Strategies for Portable and Mobile Dental Programs
description: Session Description: Infection prevention and control (IPC) is challenging enough in a traditional setting, but it can be almost impossible in some portable and mobile program settings. This session will discuss some common IPC challenges in the portable and mobile dental setting, program needs of public health dentistry, and real-world training scenarios. We will also provide practical advice and resources for programs to utilize to create the safest dental visit on the move. Attendance is required for recipients of CDC-RFA-DP24-0048 unless they have previously attended the training.Learning Objectives: Define the IPC challenges in the portable and mobile setting.Identify practical solutions to mitigate risk in the portable and mobile setting.Discuss unique public health program needs, including program partnerships, training, and processes.
instructors:
Sandy Tesch, RDH, MSHP
Sarah Stream, MPH, CDIPC, CDA, FADAA

title: A Social Media Bootcamp: Building Skills to Raise Oral Health Awareness
description: Session Description: Social media is where the public increasingly receives its health information. By establishing and building their presence on these platforms, oral health advocates can raise oral health awareness. Yet many advocates hesitate to use social media, primarily because it can be daunting to understand the culture and dynamics of these platforms. This workshop will present best practices for social media use, enhancing the confidence of advocates with low to moderate knowledge of social media. This workshop will strengthen advocates’ ability to write effective messages, use hashtags appropriately, and choose photos/graphics that draw interest. Best of all, this workshop will give participants a hands-on chance to practice the skills they will have just learned.Learning Objectives: Describe the culture and audience of the social media platform(s) on which you or your organization have a presenceWrite social media messages with clarity and brevity that are audience-centered and, therefore, more likely to generate engagementCreate or select graphics (photos, infographics, etc.) that draw the attention of your followers and reflect your organization’s brand and mission
instructors:
Heather Beavers, MM, RDH
Matt Jacob, BA

title: Interview Insights: How to Add Qualitative Research to Your Work
description: Session Description: Qualitative research is a rigorous and trustworthy approach that captures study participants knowledge, beliefs, experiences, and behaviors. Using qualitative approaches in public health and evaluation research adds value and depth to the “how and “why” of a situation or problem rather than just the “what”. There are many factors that you need to consider and document to ensure study participants are protected, data is collected accurately, and findings fairly depict those who participate. This workshop will provide attendees with a step-by-step process for creating, interpreting, and reporting an equitable and trustworthy qualitative research plan. Presenters will share case examples and qualitative interview techniques to demonstrate how to create an enriched qualitative experience. Attendees will leave with a clear understanding of how effective qualitative research can help improve program design, promote long-term program sustainability, and influence policy and funding decision-makers.Learning Objectives: Define key resources needed to design and implement qualitative research.Communicate key components of a qualitative data management plan.Describe a data dissemination plan that is ethical, equitable, and useful.Evaluate a qualitative research plan for rigor and trustworthiness using case examples.
instructors:
Kelly L. Schroeder, MS, RDH
Tamanna Tiwari, BDS, MDS, MPH
Gustavo G. Nascimento, DDS, PhD

title: Registration
Faculty Workshop: Integrating Public Health and Emerging Cariology Resources in Dental Education
description: Session Description: This workshop introduces dental educators to innovative resources designed to support evidence-based teaching. Participants will first explore the forthcoming updated public health curriculum from the American Association of Public Health Dentistry, aimed at strengthening faculty competencies and integrating public health principles into dental education. The session will then review the first of a series of digital lectures developed by CareQuest Institute and Kansas City University, currently focused on cariology, including slide decks, lecture notes, suggested activities, and sample assessments. Through interactive discussions and small group activities, attendees will learn strategies to incorporate these resources into their courses, customize content to meet their curricular needs, and enhance student learning. This workshop is ideal for dental faculty seeking practical, adaptable tools to enrich curricula with contemporary, evidence-based public health and cariology content.Learning Objectives: Describe the benefits of incorporating updated public health content from the American Association of Public Health Dentistry into dental curricula.Demonstrate how to access and use the CareQuest-Kansas City University cariology resources to enhance teaching.Adapt the provided resources to fit their own courses and student needs.
instructors:
Erinne Kennedy, DMD, MPH, MMSc
Josefine O. Wolfe, PhD, MBA, MPH, RDH
Hannah R. Archer, MPH, MAEd

title: Using a Structured Advocacy Framework to Inform and Educate Decision-makers
description: Session Description: This interactive weekend workshop will equip participants with the skills and tools to turn ideas into actionable oral health policy strategies using the forthcoming ASTDD Advocacy Guidebook. Built around the Structured Advocacy Framework (SAF), the session moves beyond theory—guiding attendees through a step-by-step process to identify stakeholders, map influence, and design engagement strategies tailored to their own advocacy priorities.Attendees will arrive with a real-world policy or program theme they want to advance. During the workshop, they will apply each section of the Guidebook to this theme, building a “skeleton” advocacy plan that can be refined and implemented after the conference. Participants will learn how to:• Align oral health priorities with broader public health and equity goals.• Use stakeholder mapping to identify allies, neutral parties, and potential opposition.• Select targeted engagement strategies that increase the likelihood of policy adoption.• Apply public value framing to strengthen messaging and demonstrate return on investment.The outcome is practical and immediate: each participant will leave with a customized, evidence-based advocacy plan anchored in proven strategies and tools. This plan can be taken directly to state agencies, legislative champions, coalitions, or community boards to advance oral health priorities.By the end of the session, participants will not only understand the content of the ASTDD Advocacy Guidebook but will have actively applied it to their own work. This ensures the workshop delivers tangible results—moving attendees from learning about advocacy to producing a plan ready for action. The session will also create a peer-learning environment, enabling participants to share strategies and challenges, building a network of advocates who can continue to collaborate long after the conference concludes.Learning Objectives: Understand how the creation of a structured advocacy framework (SAF) i
instructors:
Lyubov D. Slashcheva, DDS, MS
John Pournoor, PhD, MBA
Lori Kepler Cofano, BSDH

description: Session Description: Conduct a public session that brings together federal partners to explore innovative practices and models for advancing oral health in the US across the lifespan. Topics discussed at the workshop will include:Learning Objectives: Identify the progress that is needed to expand access to oral health careOutline critical preventive dental servicesExplain the broad impact of oral health disparitiesSummarize opportunities to implement models and strategies to reduce disparities and improve oral health in the United States.Describe the latest advancement in dentistry

title: A Message Reboot: What New Research Tells Us About Attitudes About Community Water Fluoridation
description: Session Description: A 2025 CareQuest Institute survey showed that most Americans support community water fluoridation, but nearly 3 in 10 adults are undecided. State and local policies will be influenced significantly by the ability of oral health advocates to educate communities. This workshop will summarize key findings from the research and reveal which messages were most persuasive to Americans. Participants will have an opportunity to discuss the findings and — based on these takeaways — consider how they would refine or improve on fact sheets, FAQs or other content they currently use. Participants are asked to bring with them at least one printed document that their program or organization now uses. The co-presenters will offer insights about how advocates might adapt what they say or do to preserve water fluoridation.Learning Objectives: Discuss if or how partisan differences were associated with opinions about water fluoridation.Articulate at least three messages that CareQuest Institute found to be persuasive in encouraging support for fluoridation.Identify the next tactical steps your state could take to ensure its partners are aware of the new research and its implications for how they talk about fluoridation.
instructors:

Judith A. Feinstein, MSPH

title: Evaluation Game Day: Navigating an Evaluation from Start to Finish
description: Session Description: So, you’ve been tasked with running an evaluation for your program. You’ve read the requirements, you’ve attended webinars or classes in the past, but how do you put it all together? In this workshop, attendees will have the chance to work through the evaluation process from start to finish through an interactive and collaborative oral health evaluation game. You’ll start with an oral health evaluation plan, and work collaboratively through an interactive, facilitated session with other attendees to figure out how to implement it. With hands-on opportunities to navigate common evaluation challenges and brainstorm solutions, participants will leave this workshop with insights and tools to guide their evaluation work.Learning Objectives: Use an evaluation plan to collect, analyze, and synthesize dataDescribe possible solutions to common evaluation challengesIdentify ways to adapt an evaluation as program circumstances change
instructors:

Maggie Pustinger, MPH, CHES

Strengthening the Oral Health Workforce: Collective Action through the Native Oral Health Network
description: Session Description: Persistent oral health inequities in Native communities are driven by systemic workforce shortages, limited access, and the absence of culturally responsive care models. The Native Oral Health Network (NOHN) has partnered with other public health programs and institutions to advance solutions grounded in Indigenous governance, collective action, and advocacy. This 90-minute session will highlight how this network mobilizes Tribal leaders, oral health professionals, academic institutions, policy advocates, and other partners to influence legislation, build sustainable and accessible educational programs, and strengthen workforce pathways. Presenters will share case studies on credentialing, funding, and collaboration, followed by interactive small-group discussions using a collective action framework. Attendees will leave with actionable strategies, policy tools, and resources to apply in their own regions. The session emphasizes the power of community-led collaboration in shaping equitable workforce development and improving oral health outcomes for all.Learning Objectives: Analyze how Indigenous governance, collective action, and advocacy are applied by the Native Oral Health Network to address oral health workforce inequities.Evaluate case studies on credentialing, funding, and intersectoral collaboration to identify effective strategies for strengthening oral health workforce pathways.Engage in facilitated discussions to exchange perspectives and identify practical approaches for advancing equitable oral health workforce development in diverse settings.

title: Break
Elevating Voices, Driving Change, Building National Impact
description: Session Description: Amid shrinking resources and growing health disparities, state-level advocacy has never been more important as a driver of community-informed oral health policy change. CareQuest Institute for Oral Health and American Network of Oral Health Coalitions are partnering to strengthen the capacity of state oral health coalitions to advocate for policies that expand access to care, address workforce challenges, and improve outcomes for underserved populations. National and state leaders will highlight strategies designed to elevate state voices, ensure coalitions have tools and resources they need, and connect local advocacy to broader national efforts. Presenters will share lessons from recent state-level policy successes, explore the challenges of navigating limited resources, and discuss how a united advocacy approach grounded in state expertise and national coordination can influence lasting change. Participants will understand why state-level advocacy is essential in today’s environment and how collective action can help us go farther together than alone.Learning Objectives: Describe why state-level advocacy is critical for advancing oral health in the context of shrinking federal resources.Identify strategies used by CareQuest Institute for Oral Health and ANOHC to strengthen the advocacy capacity of state oral health coalitions.Examine lessons learned from recent state-level policy successes that can inform future advocacy efforts.Analyze challenges and opportunities for sustaining oral health advocacy with limited resources.Discuss how aligning state expertise with national coordination can amplify collective impact and drive lasting policy change.
