title: 1 - Undiagnosed Oral Disease & Health Disparities Among Older Adults: Community vs. Institutional Care
description: Abstract: Background: Oral health is a vital component of overall well-being, particularly for older adults who are at increased risk for oral disease due to age-related physiological changes and chronic health conditions. Despite this vulnerability, limited research has focused on oral health in long-term care (LTC). This study examined disparities in oral disease diagnoses between older adults residing in LTC and those living independently in the community.Methods: A retrospective cross-sectional analysis was conducted using deidentified Medicaid claims data from the IBM MarketScan Research Databases (2018-2022). The study population included adults aged 65 and older, categorized by care setting (community-dwelling vs. LTC). In total, 5.6 million unique patients with over 524 million claims were analyzed.Results: Oral health diagnoses were uncommon overall and particularly rare in LTC. In 2018, 445,720 community-dwelling older adults generated 22.9 million oral health claims, while only 26 LTC residents generated 274 claims. By 2022, the diagnosis rate had risen to 7,117 per 100,000 in the community, compared to just 0.35 per 100,000 in LTC. The most frequently diagnosed conditions among community-dwelling adults included diseases of the pulp and periapical tissues (21.99 per 100,000), other disorders of teeth and supporting structures (14.55), and dental caries (12.06). Less frequent were diagnoses of tooth loss and prosthetic status (4.07) and cysts of the oral region (3.42).Conclusions: Findings reveal significant disparities in oral health diagnoses between care settings, suggesting gaps in access, screening, or documentation in LTC populations. Targeted interventions are needed to address this underrecognized public health concern.Source of Funding: This work was supported by the CareQuest Foundation.
instructors: Joohyun Chung, PhD, MStat, RN. Biostatistician

title: 2 - Increasing Access to Preventive Dental Care for Adults w/ Developmental Disabilities
description: Abstract: ABSTRACTIndividuals with developmental disabilities often face significant barriers to receiving preventive dental care, leading to dependence on hospital-based sedation dentistry and prolonged waitlists. The “Shorten the Line” program at California Northstate University (CNU) was developed to address the long waiting times people with disabilities commonly face when waiting for dental care, sometimes 1, 2, 3 or even 4 years. This program is shortening those waiting times through structured desensitization in partnership with the Alta California Regional Center (ACRC).The initiative applies a multi-phase approach that begins in the patient’s home, where familiar surroundings and caregiver support foster trust and predictability. Behavioral specialists and dental hygienists coach caregivers to gradually introduce dental tools and sensory exposure, followed by transitions to in-clinic appointments at CNU’s dental clinic. Standardized protocols, such as visual schedules, choice-based engagement, and predictable transitions, reduce anxiety and improve cooperation during care.Since spring of 2025, the CNU team provided desensitization support to nine ACRC clients. Of these, three individuals successfully completed dental care appointments in the clinic without the use of sedation. Caregivers also reported improved tolerance and participation in home-care routines. More clients are being referred to the program each week.The “Shorten the Line” model demonstrates that collaborative, community-based desensitization can meaningfully reduce sedation reliance and expand access to preventive dental services for adults with developmental and behavioral challenges. This offers a scalable public-health framework for dental schools and regional centers seeking to improve equity in oral-health access.Source of Funding: This work was supported by a grant from the Alta California Regional Center

title: 3 - Dental Care & Tooth Loss in Adults With Disabilities: Findings from Surveillance & Medicaid Data
description: Abstract: Objective: This study examines oral health status and dental service utilization among adults with and without disabilities in Nevada. Additionally, Medicaid claims data were analyzed to assess dental service use and permanent tooth extraction among adults with intellectual or developmental disabilities (ID/DD), highlighting geographic variations and informing state-level efforts to improve access.Methods: A cross-sectional analysis of the 2022 Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS) to obtain a representative sample of adults in Nevada (n=3,188). Weighted multivariable logistic regression models examined the association between past-year dental visit and permanent tooth loss and disability status. Separately, descriptive analysis of the 2022 Nevada Medicaid claims assessed dental visits, and permanent tooth extraction disaggregated by county of treatment. Results: One-third of adults (33.1%) reported at least one disability, with cognitive disability being the most common (15.8%). Adults with one (AOR: 1.5; 95% CI: 1.1-2.1) or two disabilities (AOR: 2.5; 95% CI: 1.6-3.9) had higher odds of a permanent tooth loss than individuals with no disability. There was no difference in service utilization (i.e., dental visit). A total of 7,865 Medicaid enrollees had an ID/DD diagnosis (0.85% of Medicaid enrollment and 0.25% of the state population), of which 12% had a permanent tooth extraction, with 87% recorded in Clark County in 2022. Conclusion: Adults with disabilities in Nevada have higher odds of permanent tooth loss, despite no significant differences in service utilization. Geographic concentration of extractions among adults with ID/DD underscores the need for targeted, county-level strategies to improve access to oral health services.Source of Funding: The study was funded by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, National Center for Chronic Disease Prevention and Health Promotion, State Promotion of Strategies to Advance Oral Health C

title: 4 - Evaluating Access to Medicaid Dentists for Young Children in San Francisco
description: Abstract: Objectives: This cross-sectional study assessed accessibility of dental appointments for young children in San Francisco. Methods: Trained callers contacted 78 dental clinics listed in the January 2025 DHCS Medi-Cal Provider Directory. Standardized calls were made in English, Spanish, and Cantonese, posing as parents seeking appointments for a 5-year-old with visible dental issues and an 18-month-old needing preventative care. Data collected included acceptance of new patients on Medicaid, wait times for appointments, denial reasons, and language accessibility. Descriptive analysis and Power BI were used to map neighborhood patterns. Qualitative observations from call logs were thematically analyzed to identify recurring barriers. Results: Of the 78 clinics, 52 (66.7%) were eligible and contacted. 26 clinics were ineligible, inflating the directory’s usefulness. Of the eligible list, reach rates were 55.1% (English), 52.6% (Spanish), and 44.9% (Cantonese). Among reachable clinics, 40.5% offered appointments for the 5-year-old and 32.4% for the 18-month-old. Denials were due to not treating children under 6 (32%), not accepting Medi-Cal (36%), or not accepting new patients (23%). Only 51.2% offered Spanish interpretation and 51% offered Cantonese interpretation. Average wait times were 15–18 days when appointments were available. Mapping revealed “dental deserts” in multilingual and minority neighborhoods. Qualitative findings highlighted age-based denials, assumptions about behavior, and a lack of professionalism. Conclusion: Inaccurate provider directories, low Medi-Cal participation, and limited language access create compounded barriers to pediatric dental care in San Francisco. Strengthening directory accuracy, multilingual staffing, and provider training is essential to reducing disparities in dental care access for young children.Source of Funding: California Department of Public Health; Moving California Oral Health Forward 2022 – 2027 Grant Number

title: 5 - Desensitization Techniques for Access to Dental Care for Patients with Neurodevelopmental Disorders
description: Abstract: Purpose/ objective: Patients with neurodevelopmental disorders (NDDs), including autism spectrum disorder, intellectual disabilities, and ADHD, often face significant barriers to accessing dental care due to sensory sensitivities, communication challenges, and heightened anxiety. This presentation reviews preclinical and clinical desensitization methods designed to improve dental care accessibility and outcomes for this population. Preclinical approaches include simulation-based exposure therapy, virtual reality environments, and caregiver-led behavioral modeling, which help familiarize patients with dental settings and procedures before clinical encounters. These methods aim to reduce anticipatory anxiety and build tolerance through gradual, controlled exposure.Clinical desensitization techniques involve structured, stepwise acclimatization protocols within dental office settings, often incorporating visual schedules, social stories, and sensory-adapted equipment. Techniques such as providing clinical desensitization and behavioral techniques, step by step procedures, time limited appointments, tell-show-do, positive reinforcement, and non-pharmacological behavior management strategies are emphasized to foster cooperation and minimize distress. Interdisciplinary collaboration between dental professionals and caregivers is critical for tailoring interventions to individual needs.The presentation also highlights the importance of training dental teams in neurodiversity-informed methods of care and integrating desensitization protocols into routine practice. Evidence suggests that these techniques not only improve patient experiences but also reduce the need for sedation or restraint, promoting safer and more ethical care. By bridging preclinical preparation with clinical implementation, desensitization strategies offer a promising pathway to equitable dental access for individuals with NDDsSource of Funding: None

title: Silver Diamine Fluoride in Primary Care Medicine: Three Pioneering Models & Lessons Learned To Date
description: Session Description: Silver diamine fluoride (SDF) application in primary care medicine holds the promise of improving much-needed access to care, reducing caries incidence and severity, and deepening medical-dental integration. The July 2023 activation of CPT code 0792T for SDF applied by non-dental professionals enabled oral health advocates in Maine and Ohio to pilot programs for children and adults and led the American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP) to develop guidance for and pilot SDF use by pediatric medical professionals. 1. The American Academy of Pediatrics will present its Guidance on SDF Application in the Pediatric Setting and lessons learned during a two-year pilot program in which three pediatric practices implemented SDF application.2. Health Partners of Western Ohio will present its medical dental integration project, Use of SDF by Medical Providers in an Ohio FQHC, describing how they trained and supported medical providers applying SDF in both pediatric and adult populations.3. The From the First Tooth program will present SDF Integration in a Large Health System in Maine, sharing selection, training and experience implementing SDF application in two practices within a large health system. During this session, each presenter will share: How the SDF program was started Strengths, challenges, unique aspects of the program Lessons learned & tips Following an introduction by the moderator, each presenter will have ~15 minutes to share program information. The moderator will then draw common themes and distinctions to engage the audience in Q&A and discussion.Learning Objectives: Explain the need for oral health interventions in medical settingsDescribe innovative and early oral health interventions by medical personnelDiscuss policy implications and roadmap for expansion
instructors:
Jamie Carder, RDH
Lyvia Gaewsky, MPH
Vinod Miriyala

title: Strategic Directions in Dental Public Health Research: Evidence Gaps and Opportunities
description: Session Description: This seminar will explore the American Association of Public Health Dentistry’s recently published Research Agenda that outlines progress and key evidence gaps on six key areas: oral health policy and legislation, delivery system innovations and care models, oral health workforce, DPH education, social determinants of health, and epidemiology and surveillance systems. A panel of leading scholars will unpack key priorities and evidence gaps within these six areas that are likely to have the greatest impact on population oral health, and how progress in these areas can guide future investigations, funding strategies, and policy development. Attendees will gain insights into aligning their work with national and global oral health priorities, contributing to a more robust and actionable evidence base. This session is ideal for researchers, public health professionals, and policymakers committed to transforming oral health through strategic, evidence-informed inquiry.Learning Objectives: Analyze current critical evidence gaps and understand their implications for future research, policy, and practiceEvaluate strategies for aligning individual and institutional research efforts with broader oral health prioritiesDiscuss the role of interdisciplinary collaboration in addressing social determinants of oral health and advancing public health dentistry

title: 1 - Uniting Medical and Dental Care for Diabetes Management
description: Abstract: ObjectivesSince 2020, National Network for Oral Health Access (NNOHA) has conducted national quality improvement collaboratives with 44 health centers to implement integration of diabetes and oral health care (IDOH) programs. The aim of this training program is to improve outcomes for people with diabetes by increasing the number of dual users of medical and dental services. MethodsNNOHA has worked with 44 health centers from 2020 to 2024 to provide training on diabetes management through oral health integration. Health centers also engaged in quality improvement training. Over 8 months, health centers submitted monthly data on various metrics focused on medical and dental integration. This included the number of patients with diabetes accessing both medical and dental services, patients with an A1c less than 9%, patients with diabetes with current A1c tests, and patients with an oral health evaluation completed by a medical professional. ResultsThe health centers saw an average 3% increase in the number of patients accessing both medical and dental services. Additionally, health centers reported increases in the number of patients with current A1c tests through increased promotion of diabetes management in the dental clinic.ConclusionUsing an established system-level framework for medical and dental integration, health centers implemented integration of oral health and primary care practice programs. The 44 health centers implemented programs to better manage diabetes care through oral health integration. Their monthly data demonstrated improvements in oral health access and diabetes monitoring for their patients.Source of Funding: HRSA Bureau of Primary Health Care
instructors: Candace Hsu Owen, RDH, MS, MPH

title: 2 - Diabetic Education & Tooth Loss in Nevada Adults: Results from Surveillance & Medicaid Claims Data
description: Abstract: Objective: The current study examines the association between diabetes status and permanent tooth-loss among adults in Nevada, and assessed the relationship between diabetic education and permanent tooth-loss, among adults with diabetes.Methods: A representative sample of adults in Nevada (n=3,188) was obtained from the 2022 Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS). Cross-sectional analysis was done using independent (past-year dental visit and any permanent tooth-loss) and dependent variables (diabetes diagnosis, and diabetic education). Weighted multivariable logistic regression models were done and Nevada 2022-2023 Medicaid claims analyzed.Results: A majority had a dental visit (60.8%), 7.2% reported type-2 diabetes diagnosis (T2-DM), and 56.6% reported no permanent tooth-loss. Individuals with T2-DM had 61% higher odds of permanent tooth-loss than those without T2-DM (95% CI: 1.02, 2.53). Among those with T2-DM, individuals who never received education to manage their diabetic condition had higher odds of permanent tooth-loss than those who received education or counseling (AOR: 2.41; 95% CI: 1.10, 5.27). Analysis of Medicaid data indicated that 6% (n=55,220) of enrollees in 2022 and 6.3% (n=57,975) in 2023 had a diagnosis of T2-DM. Permanent tooth extractions among enrollees with T2-DM were disproportionately concentrated in Clark County, accounting for 87% of extractions in 2022 and 91% in 2023.Conclusions: Adults with T2-DM had higher odds of permanent tooth-loss, while diabetic education was associated with lower tooth-loss. These findings underscore the potential protective role of diabetic education and highlight the need for integrated oral health and chronic disease management strategies, with attention to geographic disparities in care.Source of Funding: The study was funded by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, National Center for Chronic Disease Prevention and Health Promotion, State Promotion of Strategies to Advance

title: 3 - Data, Stakeholder Input, and Pilot Program to Inform Implementation of an Integrated Dental Program
description: Abstract: Objective: To describe the foundational population health data, stakeholder feedback, pilot program, and resulting implementation strategy that led to a sustainable dental program, integrated into medical primary care, within a safety-net healthcare system participating in Massachusetts’ Accountable Care Organization program.Methods: A data surveillance system was established to monitor population demographics and outcomes within a new 1115 waiver mandated statewide oral health screening and prevention program. Provider and patient feedback were collected through surveys conducted at one large family medicine department within the health system. Populations with the lowest dental care utilization were identified and targeted for a pilot program in which a public health dental hygienist was embedded within primary care. Feedback from this pilot informed the next phase of the implementation strategy.Results: Findings include data from 39,555 patients captured through surveillance, more than 200 provider and patient survey responses, and results from a pilot program that expanded access through a dental hygienist-led clinic. The pilot also produced data to guide the development of a telehealth-enabled model incorporating remote diagnostics.Conclusions: Integrating dental hygienists within primary care teams represents an important advance toward health and racial equity in oral healthcare. By focusing on populations most affected by disparities and embedding culturally competent practices, this model promotes equitable access to oral health services while remaining responsive to community needs.Source of Funding: CareQuest Institute for Oral Health
instructors:
John Ahern, MB BCh BDentSc PhD
Josefine O. Wolfe, PhD, MBA, MPH, RDH

title: 4 - Integrating Oral Health Assessment into Prenatal Care
description: Abstract: Objective: To describe the key demographic, utilization, and clinical findings following the implementation of an integrated oral health assessment program conducted by prenatal providers in a Teen and Pregnant clinic.Methods: Prenatal providers within a single urban prenatal teen clinic received standardized training on dental screening for caries, gingivitis, and other problems. From the total patient population seen between 2021 and 2025, a cohort of pregnant adolescents (N=262) was selected for analysis. Data collected included demographics, self-reported dental utilization, and clinical oral findings. Treatment urgency was categorized as routine, early, or urgent.Results: The study cohort had an average age of 18.5 years, with an age range of 13 to 23 years. The population was low-income and racially diverse, with the largest groups identifying as Black or African American (58%) and Latino/Hispanic (21%), followed by White (14%), and Bi-racial/Other (7%). 51% of respondents had not completed high school. Significant utilization gaps were noted: 56.5% reported no established dental care, and 59% had their last check-up or cleaning more than 12 months prior or never. Clinical assessments showed a low burden of visible disease (15% gingivitis, 6.6% caries). However, 33.3% of patients reported active oral symptoms (pain, broken teeth, cavities, sensitivity). Based on findings, 22.3% of the assessed group required timely intervention, categorized as urgent (0.39%) or early (21.9%), while 72.3% required routine care.Conclusion: The integration of oral health assessment by prenatal providers in this setting is feasible and effective for initiating the dental referral process among vulnerable pregnant adolescents.Source of Funding: Ohio Department of Health

title: 5 - Improving Access to Care for Chicago Families Through Community-Based, Patient-Centered Coordination
description: Abstract: ObjectiveThis presentation describes how the DentEquity IRIS Network strengthens access to dental care for underserved Chicago families by integrating oral health coordination with community-based, patient-centered approaches.MethodsBefore implementation, care coordinators found that children identified through school-based dental screenings often lacked a dental home for follow-up treatment, and families struggled to find providers accepting Medicaid. Follow-up relied on families locating their own dentist, leaving gaps in accountability and no way to confirm care occurred. Providers were hesitant to take on added administrative work, further limiting continuity. The DentEquity IRIS Network—a closed community of dental providers—was created to bridge these gaps. The web-based system enables coordinators to send and track city-wide referrals, confirm completion, and connect families to supportive resources when barriers arise. Partnerships with the Chicago Department of Public Health, Chicago Public Schools, and federally qualified health centers extend access to entire families.ResultsThe DentEquity IRIS Network streamlined transitions from school programs to community dental homes, providing visibility into referral outcomes and improving family engagement. Providers reported an easier, more efficient process with reduced administrative burden. Families gained access to Medicaid-accepting dental homes, and the program expanded to serve adults. Network data now inform strategies for equitable access and provider participation across the city.ConclusionsThe DentEquity IRIS Network shows how a community-driven, technology-supported model can close gaps in dental access by centering families’ needs, easing provider participation, and fostering interagency collaboration.Source of Funding: Illinois Children's Healthcare FoundationDelta Dental of Illinois Foundation

title: Small Steps, Big Impact: Practical Oral Health Projects in a Time of Limited Resources
description: Session Description: With shrinking federal funding and growing community needs, coalitions and partners are challenged to make meaningful progress without the resources once available for large-scale initiatives. This panel will showcase simple, low-cost, and replicable projects that deliver measurable results for oral health. Panelists from state coalitions will share examples of “quick wins” that require modest time and investment but build visibility, engage partners, and improve community health. Attendees will leave with practical ideas they can adapt to their own settings to keep momentum strong, even in an era of constrained resources.Learning Objectives: Examine case examples of small-scale oral health projects and the measurable outcomes they produced.Discuss approaches for leveraging partnerships, volunteers, and existing resources to maximize impact without requiring significant new funding.Apply lessons learned from panelists to design or adapt practical oral health initiatives within their own organizations or communities.
instructors:
Helen Hawkey, BSDH
Megan Reutter, DHSc, MSDH, RDH
Abby Richwine, RDH, BS
Caleb Stanton

title: Using Storytelling to Inspire Communities and Expand Care
description: Session Description: Storytelling is a powerful tool to change perceptions, inspire action, and expand impact. By harnessing the combined power of authentic storytelling and commercial marketing, the Pathways to Oral Health project takes a novel approach to increase dental provider participation in Medicaid and strengthen care for underserved populations. This session will showcase how innovative partnerships with marketing experts and the use of compelling stories featuring real patients, providers, and partners can reduce stigma and highlight the value of serving underserved communities. By delivering targeted, emotionally resonant messages, this campaign reframes Medicaid participation not as a burden, but as a professional and personal opportunity for impact. Presenters will share examples of messaging development, video campaigns, and lessons learned in reaching dental professionals with tailored messages. Participants will gain insights into how storytelling and marketing can be used to influence provider behavior and improve oral health outcomes for populations who are historically underserved.Learning Objectives: Explain how authentic storytelling can reduce stigma and shift perceptions about serving Medicaid patients.Identify ways commercial marketing strategies can be adapted to advance oral health access for underserved populations.Describe how targeted, emotionally resonant messages can motivate dental providers to participate in Medicaid.Apply lessons from the Pathways to Oral Health initiative to develop storytelling or marketing approaches in participants’ own settings.
instructors:
Tara Dimick, MBA, CCUE
Melanie s. Jacobs
Monica M. Turner, MHA
Tanya Dorf Brunner, MS

title: Water Fluoridation and Beyond: Navigating the Evolving Landscape of Caries Prevention
description: Session Description: With recent statewide bans on community water fluoridation in Florida and Utah, the national conversation around fluoride has intensified—raising both scientific and policy questions. This symposium will explore the evolving landscape of caries prevention in the United States and beyond, with a focus on current evidence, emerging alternatives, and future research priorities.This symposium will examine the association between fluoride exposure and neurodevelopmental outcomes, including the methodological challenges associated with this research. The session will highlight promising strategies for preventing dental caries, including silver diamine fluoride, antimicrobials, calcium-based approaches, and biomimetic mineralization therapies, among others. These alternatives will be discussed in the context of population reach, cost-effectiveness, and implementation feasibility. The symposium will also discuss the research gaps and opportunities to address these gaps. Attendees will gain a nuanced understanding of the complex interplay between science, public health, and community decision-making in this rapidly changing domain.Learning Objectives: Assess the current scientific evidence linking fluoride exposure to neurodevelopmental outcomes and their limitations.Explain the effectiveness and implementation considerations of non-fluoride strategies for dental caries prevention.Identify critical research gaps in caries prevention science to inform future studies and funding priorities.
instructors:
Margherita Fontana, DDS, PhD
Jayanth Kumar, DDS, MPH
Mark E. Moss, DDS, PhD
Jennifer Webster-Cyriaque, DDS, PhD

title: Break
Adding Value by Adding Dental to an Accountable Care Organization Serving People With Disabilities
description: Session Description: Discipline-specific fee for service payment models contribute to fragmentated care systems, disproportionally reducing the quality of care for populations with complex healthcare needs and deepening health disparities. In 2024, Altair ACO invited Apple Tree Dental to join their disability-led accountable care organization of Home and Community Based Service Providers. Alignment of mission, vision, and values led to Apple Tree becoming the first dental organization member. This session will provide a brief overview of Altair ACO, highlight the recent integration of oral health, and demonstrate the use of patient-reported outcome measures and total cost of care data to demonstrate the value of the ACO’s integrated care model to improve health outcomes for the people their members serve. Alternative payment models deployed in an interprofessional setting have the potential to demonstrate improved health outcomes for special care populations with potential for scaling these models to other populations.Learning Objectives: Describe an Accountable Care Organization.Recognize the opportunities created with the inclusion of oral health in collaborative service delivery to individuals with intellectual and developmental disabilities.Interpret total cost of care data as evidence of improved health outcomes in value-based care / pay for performance alternative models.
instructors:
Julie Dirth, BA, MS, ADT
Michael J. Helgeson, DDS
Deborah Jacobi, RDH, MA

title: Building a Data Backbone: The Colorado Oral Health Surveillance Dashboard
description: Session Description: This session introduces the Colorado Oral Health Surveillance Dashboard, a centralized resource for diverse oral health data. You'll learn how this innovative platform consolidates information from multiple sources to provide a comprehensive view of oral health trends, moving beyond traditional demographics. The presentation will detail the dashboard's unique structure, featuring custom dashboards for specific populations that uniquely integrate data on risk behaviors, comorbidities, social determinants of health and other influential health topics. We will also highlight the crucial role of stakeholder participation in the dashboard's development and long-term viability. The session will cover strategies for efficient maintenance and share future plans, ensuring its continued growth as a vital tool for oral health professionals, policymakers, and the public in Colorado.Learning Objectives: Describe the steps involved in the process of building a new public health data dashboard.Identify the variety of data sources, and corresponding data points, that contribute to the dashboard’s comprehensive datasets.Discuss strategies for ensuring the long-term viability and sustainability of a data surveillance tool like the dashboard.Summarize the key features that make the dashboard an efficient and user-friendly tool for oral health professionals and policymakers.Recognize the value of a centralized dashboard in facilitating data-driven decision-making.
instructors:
Ashleigh Kirk, MSW
Brett Lipshetz, MPH

title: Debt by Design: How Medical Credit Cards Fuel Economic Injustice and Medial Debt
description: Session Description: Medical debt remains a defining public health challenge in the United States. Nearly half of those with medical debt report dental expenses as a contributing factor, and more than 72-million adults lack dental insurance. The exclusion of dental services from Medicare, inadequate Medicaid coverage, and chronic underinvestment in oral health care have made dental debt a hidden but powerful force in deepening inequities. Beyond finances, debt worsens health outcomes, shortens life spans, and heightens mental health challenges, particularly in marginalized communities. The rise of medical credit cards in dental settings has intensified the problem. Marketed as patient-friendly solutions, these products often carry deferred-interest terms and rates exceeding 30-percent, trapping patients in cycles of debt, damaging credit, and driving bankruptcy, exacerbating health disparities. This session will examine how predatory dental financing systems reinforce economic and health inequity and discuss systemic reforms that protect patients and expand equitable access to oral health.Learning Objectives: Examine the scope of economic justice issues that impact, and are impacted by, oral health, including medical debt, coverage and affordability, and employmentDiscuss innovative strategies to address the intersections of oral health equity and economic justiceDefine and understand the critical role of community-led solutions in addressing structural inequities

title: Strengthening Perinatal Oral Health: Using a Strategic Framework and Examples to Guide State Efforts
description: Session Description: Inability to access oral health care before, during and after pregnancy can contribute to negative outcomes for women and infants. With support from the National Oral Health Resource Center, ASTDD updated its Perinatal Oral Health Best Practices Approach Report to help state oral health programs, and their partners better address this issue from an interdisciplinary stance. Come and learn about the Strategic Framework for Improving Perinatal Oral Health with associated actions to better facilitate improving health outcomes for pregnant women and ultimately their infants. Long standing obstacles have begun to break down as all states and the District of Columbia offer dental coverage via Medicaid through at least 60 days postpartum (as of 10/22). Oral health professionals and their medical colleagues are beginning to collaborate on whole person health care, which is a win/win for both the patient and the providers. Challenges remain, but the way forward is promising!Learning Objectives: Identify the components of the Strategic Framework for improving perinatal oral healthDescribe action steps that state oral health programs, and their partners can take to improve oral health outcomes for pregnant womenIdentify ways that an interdisciplinary approach to care can improve perinatal oral health
instructors:
Lori Kepler Cofano, BSDH
Matt Crespin, MPH, RDH, FADHA
Steven P. Geiermann, DDS, FICD, FACD

title: Exhibits Open
Poster Session #1
Community Water Fluoridation Updates
description: Session Description: This informational session will provide an opportunity for attendees to learn about models, techniques and strategies for managing and improving state community water fluoridation (CWF) activities and initiatives, through the experiences of three states.Learning Objectives: Summarize challenges in CWF management and opportunities for change and improvement.Discuss approaches to state level CWF data analysis, resources, and reports that can be used for informing stakeholders and decision-makers on the impact and benefit of optimally fluoridated water.Summarize how an integrated approach to water fluoridation training maximizes impact across multidisciplinary sectors.Describe how upgrades in technology for fluoridation monitoring streamline data collection and response time.
instructors:
Jenni Lansing, MBA, MPH, CPH, RDH
Gwen Sullentrup, MAT
Judith A. Feinstein, MSPH

title: Diplomates Dinner- Invitation Only
Continental Breakfast with Exhibitors
Registration
Welcome
ABDPH Symposium: What Agencies Can Do to Address the Loss of CDC's Division of Oral Health
description: Session Description: This symposium will describe how the CDC’s Division of Oral Health has supported a wide range of critical dental public health initiatives at the federal and state levels over its recent history. It will also explore how contemporary changes at the federal level have put those initiatives in jeopardy. A panel of speakers will set the Division of Oral Health’s past and present accomplishments in context and offer their thoughts about how program gaps can be addressed in the future. Discussion will focus on the potential roles that national dental public health agencies, like AAPHD, ASTDD, and NNOHA, will have. Panelists will also explore potential opportunities for other dental public health-focused organizations to be part of the solution.Learning Objectives: Describe dental public health initiatives supported by the CDC’s Division of Oral Health over its recent history.Discuss potential impacts to dental public health resulting from the recent elimination of the Division of Oral Health.Explore how various agencies might work together to address the most likely gaps.
instructors:
William Maas, DDS, MPH, MS
Mark D. Macek, DDS, MPH, DrPH, MS
Gina Thornton-Evans, DDS., MPH
Tooka Zokaie, MPH, MAS, CPH

title: Break
100 Million Mouths Campaign: Strengthening Oral Health Training in Primary Care through Partnerships
description: Session Description: Improving access to oral health care requires strengthening the primary care workforce through innovative and sustainable partnerships. The 100 Million Mouths Campaign (100MMC) has created Champions in 30 states and trained more than 12,000 learners across medicine, nursing, physician assistant, and other disciplines. Central to this success has been the support of diverse funders who have enabled growth, sustainability, and adaptation across a wide range of contexts. This session will highlight how funders, academic institutions, state coalitions, and other stakeholders collaborate to advance oral health equity and address local disparities. Presenters, including key funding partners, will describe strategies for engaging and sustaining multisector partnerships, as well as the roles of different stakeholder groups in creating impact. Small-group discussions will provide participants with opportunities to explore strategies for working with funders and partners, and to consider how they can engage with 100MMC efforts within their own states and programs.Learning Objectives: Explain the goals, structure, and national impact of the 100 Million Mouths Campaign in advancing oral health training in primary care.Analyze strategies used by funders, academic institutions, and state coalitions to build and sustain partnerships that address local oral health disparities.Identify opportunities to engage with the 100 Million Mouths Campaign and strengthen oral health training in participants’ own states.
instructors:
Maria Emanuel Ryan, DDS, PhD
Holli Seabury, EdD
Shenam Ticku, BDS, MPH

title: Assessing Antibiotics in Dentistry: The Collaboration to Harmonize Antimicrobial Registry Measures
description: Session Description: Literature suggests that dentists over-prescribe antibiotics which may compromise patient safety. However, these data may not truly reflect broad practice. The Collaboration to Harmonize Antimicrobial Registry Measures (CHARM) is a program that is funded by the Michigan and Illinois Departments of Public Health to track and assess antibiotic use is medical and dental practices. The purpose of this session is to provide an overview of CHARM and present data collected from community dental practices. Samples of the dashboards that are provided to dental offices will be reviewed and used to demonstrate how areas for prescribing improvement can be identified. Programs like CHARM can help dental prescribers to optimize antimicrobial prescribing, collect better data regarding their use of antimicrobials, and help to develop a more accurate narrative.Learning Objectives: Describe the CHARM program.Summarize the importance understanding dental antimicrobial prescribing practices.Interpret practice specific data to identify areas for improvement in antimicrobial prescribing.Explain how data provided by CHARM differs from data currently published in the literature.Discuss how to participate in CHARM.
instructors:
Kushal Dahal, MS
Michael Klepser, PharmD, FCCP, FIDP

title: Building Colorado’s Oral Public Health Infrastructure: Expanding School-based Oral Health Access
description: Session Description: Tooth decay is a widespread health concern among children in Colorado - over 60% of third-grade students and nearly 46% of kindergarteners have experienced cavities. Colorado utilizes a data-driven approach to expand access to oral health services in school-based settings through several effective methods, providing funding and technical assistance to local public health and safety-net health partners. To facilitate data collection, Colorado developed the Cavity Free Care Network electronic data collection tool, a complete electronic workflow designed for school-based oral health programs, allowing for communication between parents, schools, and providers. Expanded data collection and analysis enables Colorado to proactively allocate resources where they are most needed, ensure data reliability and effectiveness, and support long-term sustainability by strengthening the evidence base that guides oral health strategies and investments.Learning Objectives: Describe available oral health data sets that inform opportunities for school-based oral health expansionDefine school-based oral health models supported by Colorado, including the reach and impact of programs.Describe current opportunities for expansion provided through Colorado legislation and the implementation of the Colorado Universal Preschool Program.Demonstrate the Cavity Free Care Network electronic data collection tool, highlighting the system’s capabilities to support a full end-to-end preventive oral health services workflow in a school setting.
instructors:
Ashleigh Kirk, MSW
Jenni Lansing, MBA, MPH, CPH, RDH

title: From Crisis to Coverage: Medicaid Policy and Emergency Department Use for Dental Care
description: Session Description: Despite being largely preventable, dental-related visits to emergency departments (EDs) continue to rise—particularly among Medicaid enrollees—highlighting systemic gaps in access to routine oral health services. This panel will explore the intersection of Medicaid policy and emergency dental care utilization, focusing on how coverage limitations, access to care challenges, and provider shortages contribute to ED reliance for non-traumatic dental conditions.Panelists will discuss state-level variations in Medicaid dental benefits and share examples of how policy changes have impacted dental care access and outcomes among specific subgroups and subpopulations, using claims data, hospital discharge records, and other public health datasets. Attendees will gain insights into actionable policy levers that can improve oral health, reduce costly and avoidable emergency care, and better align coverage with patient needs and care-seeking behaviors.Learning Objectives: Understand how Medicaid policy impacts ED use among adults, and assess the burden of dental visits in the ED.Explore trends in ED use for dental problems among pregnant women and implications for Medicaid policy and service delivery.Learn about data sources and analytic approaches used to evaluate Medicaid dental coverage and emergency care utilization.
instructors:
Shillpa Naavaal, BDS, MS, MPH
Astha Singhal, BDS MPH PhD
Alejandra Valencia, DDS, MPH, MS

title: Break
Confidence in Every Smile: Tools and Training for Oral Hygiene Support
description: Session Description: Daily oral hygiene remains a major challenge for people with intellectual and developmental disabilities (IDD), often due to physical, sensory, and systemic barriers. Oral Health Kansas has developed a suite of resources to strengthen oral health confidence and skills among both individuals with disabilities and their caregivers.This session will highlight three complementary initiatives:• Confidence in Every Smile Video Series – seven short consumer-focused videos that help people with disabilities and their supporters overcome barriers to brushing and flossing.• Direct Care Staff Training Video – a structured tool for training teams to prepare direct support staff responsible for residents’ daily oral hygiene. A training director will share how the video is used in practice and the impact on staff performance and resident care.• Feeling Good About Your Smile Hands-On Learning Session – a 1-hour interactive class designed for adults with disabilities, emphasizing empowerment, self-advocacy, and practical oral hygiene skills.Together, these resources provide a multi-level approach—directly supporting people with disabilities, equipping their caregivers, and building confidence among direct care staff. Participants will leave with concrete ideas and adaptable tools for use in their own organizations and communities.Learning Objectives: Identify common barriers to daily oral hygiene for people with IDD.Describe the consumer-focused video series and how it supports independence and confidence.Understand how the direct care staff training video can be integrated into onboarding and ongoing training systems.Explore the structure and outcomes of the Feeling Good About Your Smile hands-on learning session.Identify strategies to implement or adapt these tools to improve oral health outcomes in their own communities.
instructors:
Kathy Hunt, RDH, ECPII, FADHA
Blake Harris, BSN, RN, IPC
Abby Richwine, RDH, BS
Tanya Dorf Brunner, MS

title: Finding Impact: What Decision-Makers Want to Know and How Evaluation Can Help Tell Your Story
description: Session Description: Funders, grantees, and other program decision makers want to know more than what a program does — they want to see the impact it creates. How can you find that impact? Theory of change (ToC) models, as part of an evaluation plan, provide a powerful way to visualize program objectives, influencing factors, and consequential outcomes that matter most for program sustainability, growth, and advocacy. During this session, attendees will learn about theory of change (ToC) models and how they can inform program or intervention impact and influence decision-makers. Through the voices of an oral health policy and change advocate, evaluation partners oral health funders, and an oral health grantee, attendees will learn how to build a strong evaluation framework that can capture meaningful data. They’ll leave with a clear understanding of how evaluation can be the critical bridge between optimistic vision and lasting results.Learning Objectives: Define evaluation and how it can help inform stakeholders of program progress.Describe the theory of change model.Recognize how evaluation can contribute to program growth and development.Map program objectives to program outcomes and impacts using a theory of change model.
instructors:
Karam H. Ahmad, MPH
Katinka Hakuta, MPH
Kelly L. Schroeder, MS, RDH
Eric P. Tranby, PhD

title: From Data to Decisions: Harnessing EHR Data and AI to Advance Oral Health
description: Session Description: Electronic Health Records (EHRs) are increasingly central to dental practice, research, and public health. However, challenges with data quality, standardization, and analysis limit their impact. At the same time, advances in artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning are creating new opportunities to enhance diagnostics, caries risk prediction, clinical decision support, and population oral health monitoring. This 90-minute seminar will provide an in-depth look at innovative approaches to EHR data analysis, opportunities for improving dental EHR interoperability, and the integration of AI into dental EHR analysis for assessing clinical outcomes or population oral health. Presenters will review strategies for improving EHR data quality and validation, highlight applications of AI in clinical and public health settings, and share real-world case studies where these methods have advanced patient care and research. Emerging innovations—including natural language processing, federated data systems, and data visualization dashboards—will also be discussed. Participants will gain practical tools and insights to apply EHR and AI approaches to improve outcomes and advance health equity.Learning Objectives: Identify key challenges and solutions in cleaning, extracting data, and analyzing EHR data.Understand how AI and machine learning can enhance caries detection, risk assessment, treatment outcomes, and population oral health monitoring.Discuss EHR interoperability challenges and opportunities for developing a common data model for harnessing dental EHR dataExplore examples where modern AI tools and informatics approaches can be used to improve patient outcomes and inform policy decisions.Discuss ethical, equity, and implementation considerations in applying AI and advanced analytics to dental public health.
instructors:
Sepideh Banava, DDS, MSc, MBA, MPH, DABDPH
Enihomo Obadan-Udoh, DDS, MPH, DMSc, DABDPH
Lauren Gritzer, DDS, MPH

title: Oral Presentation Session #3
instructors:
Heather Hawkins, DMD
Koua Her, MPH, MBA
Shillpa Naavaal, BDS, MS, MPH
Jinman Pang, PhD
Kathryn Rothas, DDS, MPH
McKenna Roudebush, MPH

title: 1 - Certifying Community-Based Non-Licensed Personnel as Oral Health-Focused Community Health Workers
description: Abstract: Community-based workforces are essential to improving oral health access, particularly in underserved populations where traditional delivery models fall short. This initiative describes the development of a Community Health Worker (CHW) Certification and Training Program designed to train non-licensed community members to serve as oral health–focused CHWs.The Medi-Cal Dental Advisory Committee (MCDAC) Oral Health CHW Certification Program provides a low-cost, accessible pathway to certification for individuals who wish to promote oral health, navigate care systems, and educate communities. Developed collaboratively by dental and public health partners, the program aligns with the California Department of Health Care Services (DHCS) CHW Benefit and HRSA Core Competencies.The curriculum includes six modules covering: (1) CHW roles, HIPAA, and mandated reporter training; (2) communication and motivational interviewing; (3) service coordination and referral tracking; (4) public health and advocacy; (5) oral health prevention across the lifespan; and (6) provider collaboration, billing, and risk management.Delivered through an on-demand learning management system, each module includes presentations, handouts, and assessments designed to ensure competency and scalability across California.This presentation will review the structure, implementation, and policy implications of certifying non-licensed personnel as oral health CHWs. Early outcomes demonstrate strong participant engagement, provider interest, and opportunities for integration with the Medi-Cal CHW reimbursement benefit, offering a replicable model for expanding the preventive oral health workforce and advancing health equity and access.Source of Funding: This project was supported in part by the Medi-Cal Dental Advisory Committee (MCDAC) and the Sacramento County Oral Health Program through in-kind collaboration.

title: 2 - Building an Age-Friendly Dental Workforce: Minnesota's Model for Geriatric Care Innovation
description: Abstract: Objective: Evaluate Minnesota’s statewide innovations: (1) workforce development anchored in the Age-Friendly Health Systems 4Ms Framework and (2) integration of collaborative practice dental hygienists (CPDHs) to expand access and staff training in long-term care facilities (LTCFs). Methods: The Minnesota Department of Health collaborated with the University of Minnesota School of Dentistry and Apple Tree Dental to implement programs. (1) Mini-Residency and Fellowship Programs included a four-day mini-residency for rural dental providers (n=17) and a post-graduate fellowship (n=4) in rural and geriatric oral health designed to embed the 4Ms principles into dental education and practice. Pre- and post-assessments measured knowledge and confidence gains.(2) Age-Friendly Oral Health Pilot in LTCFs is a pilot implemented at two long-term care facilities (n=59) where CPDHs provided preventive services and trained nursing staff in age-friendly oral health practices. Participants completed pre- and post-surveys assessing oral health knowledge (10-items) and confidence (5-items, 10-point Likert scale). Paired-samples t-tests and McNemar’s tests evaluated changes. Results: Knowledge scores increased significantly in the mini-residency (pre-M=6.87, post-M=10.00, p< 0.001), with reported greater confidence in clinical decision-making. Fellows reported 100% confidence in assessing oral health needs and addressing cognitive and mobility challenges. LTCF Pilot: Knowledge scores improved from pre (M=8.37) to post (M=9.56), t(58)=9.70, p< 0.001, d=1.26. Confidence scores increased from pre (M=7.29) to post (M=8.70), t(49)=9.00, p< 0.001, with significant gains in denture care and xylitol use (p < 0.01).Conclusion: Minnesota is transforming geriatric dentistry through integrated, age-friendly training and service delivery that strengthens provider competence, enhances staff capacity, and expands access for older adults.Source of Funding: HRSA

title: 3 - Common Barriers in Health Center Dental Residency Development
description: Abstract: Objective: To explore the common barriers to program development among Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) Teaching Health Center Planning and Development (THCPD) dental grant recipients. Methods: The HRSA implemented the THCPD Program in 2021 to facilitate the launch of new community-based residency programs in multiple medical and dental specialties, including general and pediatric dentistry residencies. THCPD grant recipients are required to engage with the Technical Assistance Center (TAC) for infrastructure and start-up support. The TAC assessed 23 THCPD dental grant recipients throughout their grant period using the Roadmap for Teaching Health Center Dental Program Development. The Roadmap contains a structured set of goals and objectives which guide advancement of programs through each of the five stages of development. Results: Barriers faced by community-based dental residency programs fell into 8 categories including Accreditation; Community and Health Center Engagement; Curricular Design and Evaluation; Faculty Development; Faculty/Staff Recruitment; Financial Planning; Resident Recruitment and Matriculation; and Space and Infrastructure. Barriers faced by dental programs varied by stage of development. This presentation will share summative findings on THCPD dental grant recipients’ experience of barriers within each category and include a case example of how a new teaching health center dental residency program overcame barriers to launch and train residents. Conclusions: Teaching health center dental residency programs can face many practical barriers in launching and becoming sustainable. This analysis may ease future program development by identifying common barriers and informing technical assistance resource and service refinement to increase support in overcoming common barriers.Source of Funding: Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA)
instructors:
Kathryn Rothas, DDS, MPH
McKenna Roudebush, MPH

title: 4 - Workplace Factors and Well-Being of Dental Assistants and Hygienists in HRSA-Funded Health Centers
description: Abstract: Objective: To evaluate the association between workplace factors and key aspects of well-being, including job satisfaction, engagement, burnout, and intention to stay, among dental assistants (DAs) and dental hygienists (DHs) at HRSA-supported health centers.Methods: Data were extracted from the 2022 HRSA Health Center Workforce Well-being Survey, including 1,435 DAs and 596 DHs. Outcomes for job satisfaction, engagement, and burnout were calculated as mean Likert scores and categorized into tertiles; intention to stay was dichotomized. Descriptive statistics summarized provider outcomes and health center characteristics. Multinomial logistic regressions estimated associations with 3-level outcomes (Relative Risk Ratios [RRR]); logistic regression assessed intention to stay (Odds Ratios [OR]). Models were stratified by provider type and adjusted for demographics and health center characteristics using backward stepwise selection.Results: DHs reported more positive well-being outcomes than DAs across multiple measures. For both, the workplace factor combining compensation and benefits was the strongest predictor of job satisfaction, while professional growth was particularly important for DAs (RRR=8.65). Engagement was positively associated with the health center’s mission, and training opportunities were significant for DHs (RRR=4.35). Burnout was primarily driven by workload in both, with moral distress uniquely significant for DAs (RRR=6.56). Compensation and benefits also strongly predicted intention to stay for both DAs (OR=2.15) and DHs (OR=4.54).Conclusions: Workplace factors, including compensation and benefits, influence well-being across both groups, while others have profession-specific effects. Tailored strategies, such as enhancing professional growth for DAs and training for DHs, may strengthen workforce well-being and retention in the safety net.Source of Funding: This research was funded by the US Health Resources and Services Administration

title: 5 - Utilization of Dental Services Post-Dental Benefit Expansion: Patterns and Predictors of Care Use
description: Abstract: Background and Objective: Virginia expanded dental care benefits from medically necessary tooth extractions only to comprehensive dental coverage for all Medicaid-enrolled adults in July 2021. Our aim was to examine dental service use under this expanded benefit and identify factors associated with any and preventive dental care useMethods: We combined Virginia Medicaid enrollment and administrative claims data from July 2021 to June 2023 to include non-pregnant, non-dual eligible adults aged 21-64 who were continuously enrolled for 180 days. Data from the Area Health Resource File was combined to account for community variables. Our primary outcomes were any and preventive dental visits, determined using diagnostic codes. We conducted descriptive analyses and used a two-part model to estimate the probability of any dental service use and the receipt of preventive care conditional on use. We also examined the impact of the Medicaid dental benefit.Results: In our study population (n=1.5 million), the mean number of visits and procedures among those who had at least one dental claim was 2.8 and 8.9 per year, respectively. The prevalence of any dental visit was higher among 50–64-year-olds; however, preventive dental visits were more common among 21-34-year-olds. Other factors, including race, ethnicity, sex, and location, were significant for both outcomes. The adjusted probability of any dental visit was 17.8%, while the probability for preventive dental visits was 10.8%.Conclusion: In the two years following the dental benefit expansion, about one in six Medicaid enrollees used dental care, and one in 10 used preventive dental services.Source of Funding: This research is supported by the National Institutes of Dental and Craniofacial Research grant/award K08DE032443. The content is solely the responsibility of the authors and does not necessarily represent the official views of the National Institutes of Health.

title: Networking Lunch
Non-CE Product Discussion- Elevate
Expanding Rural Oral Healthcare Capacity Through Community-Based Clinical Rotations in South Texas
description: Session Description: Texas is largely rural and faces critical shortage of dental providers. Limited access to oral healthcare in these communities underscores the need for innovative approaches to training and workforce development. To address these challenges, clinical rotations were designed and implemented for senior dental students and primary care dentistry residents at UT Health San Antonio in partnership with affiliated Federally Qualified Health Center (FQHC) sites. These rotations aimed to expand the capacity of future dentists to deliver high-quality oral health services in underserved rural areas while exposing them to the unique challenges of practicing in community-based settings. This presentation will describe the structure of the rotations, highlight key experiences reported by students and residents, and discuss the benefits and barriers encountered in this model of training. Findings will inform strategies for strengthening the dental workforce pipeline and improving access to care in rural regions.Learning Objectives: Explain the structure and goals of FQHC-based clinical rotations in rural settings.Identify challenges and benefits experienced by dental students and residents in community rotations.Understand how community rotations strengthen the dental workforce pipeline and propose one strategy to expand rural oral health access.
instructors:
Suman Challa, BDS, MSPH, MBA
Sohini Dhar, BDS,MPH, FICD
Rochisha S. Marwaha, BDS, MPH, FICD
Yashashri Urankar, BDS, MPH, FICD, FPFA

title: From Screenings to Referrals: Optimizing School Oral Health Programs
description: Session Description: School-based sealant and screening programs are essential for increasing access to preventive care, yet they face persistent challenges in managing urgent referrals, coordinating with schools, and sustaining effective reporting systems. This 60-minute seminar will highlight practical strategies to strengthen school oral health programs through streamlined workflows and the integration of Community Health Workers (CHWs).Presenters will share approaches for building strong partnerships with schools, navigating consent processes, and coordinating schedules. The role of CHWs will be emphasized, showing how they follow up on urgent referrals, connect families to dental homes, and address barriers such as transportation, language, and insurance navigation. The session will also outline daily, weekly, and monthly workflows that balance clinical care and communication, alongside best practices in data collection and reporting. Participants will leave with adaptable workflow tools, referral strategies, and reporting templates to enhance the reach and sustainability of school-based oral health programs.Learning Objectives: List key components of school-based oral health workflows for daily, weekly, and monthly program operations.Describe the role of Community Health Workers in managing urgent referrals and supporting families.Demonstrate strategies for data collection and reporting to track outcomes and communicate program impact.
instructors:
Linda Mann, RDH, EPDH
Mar Verastegui Espinoza, CHW
Lisa A. Balint, MPH, EPDH

title: Opportunities to Transform Pediatric to Adult Dental Care Transition for People with Disabilities
description: Session Description: Too many children with disabilities unjustly slip through the cracks upon reaching adulthood, no longer able to access the dental care they need. This is due to shortcomings in our current dental delivery, education, and financing systems, with more limited coverage, lower reimbursement, and fewer providers serving adults with disabilities. While the complex issue of pediatric to adult health care transition has not yet been mastered in medicine nor any other social or health service, the concept remains nascent in the dental community.Join us to discuss the experiences of patients with disabilities and their families attempting transition to an adult dental provider and the challenges indicated by pediatric and general dentists that prevent transition in their practices. We will then present opportunities to meaningfully improve pediatric to adult dental care transition, including the possibilities of new dental codes, developing a quality measure, revisiting and strengthening professional policies and best practices, and exploring opportunities for dental integration into disability service programs and policy.Learning Objectives: Describe the challenges experienced by patients with intellectual and developmental disabilities (IDD) and their families in the process of transitioning to an adult dental provider.Identify the barriers faced by pediatric and general dentists in successfully transitioning patients with disabilities,List the Six Core Elements™ (Got Transition) of the health care transition process.Discuss policy and payment opportunities to support pediatric to adult dental health care transition for people with disabilities.
instructors:
Chelsea Fosse, DMD, MPH
Lauren Mann, MPA
Mark Veazie, DDS, MPH

title: Pave the Policy Highway with a Multistate Environmental Scan to Inform Medical-Dental Integration
description: Session Description: The Health Resources and Services Administration, Maternal and Child Health Bureau funds the 4-year Maternal and Child Health–Improving Oral Health Integration (MCH-IOHI) projects in seven states and one jurisdiction to advance integration of preventive oral health care into primary care (henceforth, medical-dental integration (MDI)). Projects completed an environmental scan (ES) to gain knowledge about factors related to MDI, including the medical and dental provider workforce’s scopes of practice, Medicaid billing and reimbursement, and other policies and regulations.The seminar’s purpose is to share the ES process and results. The seminar’s goal is for participants to translate the process to their own states and identify opportunities for improving MDI-related rules, regulations, and policies. The session’s content will include an overview of the MCH-IOHI projects and the ES process and results. Three projects (Colorado, New York, Montana) will be highlighted. Participants will receive resources to support ES replication in their states.Learning Objectives: Identify environmental scan elements to inform, initiate, and accelerate the integration of oral health care into primary care at community health centers, private practices, and health systems.Use environmental scan results to inform policy change at the state legislative or agency level to better support MDI in their states.Translate the environmental scan process and findings to their own states and leverage states’ stakeholders to inform policy change that supports medical-dental integration.
instructors:
Patricia A. Braun, MD, MPH, FAAP
Tara J. Callaghan, MPH
Bridget A. Walsh, MPH

title: Break
Roundtables - (3) 20-min. Sessions
1 - Improvement of Consent Rates in Rural, School-Based Oral Health Programs
description: Description: Consent rates for public health programs are important for access to care and sustainability. School-based dental programs can be effective, however barriers to implementation, like low consent rates, exist (Rodriguez, Soto, & Martinez Mier, 2020, p. 232-239). Several reasons can result in low consent rates, including, parent knowledge, proper transfer or completion of form, low health literacy or other cultural factors (Rodriguez, Soto, & Martinez Mier, 2020, p. 232-239). This project aimed to increase consent rates in school-based oral health prevention programs in rural, eastern North Carolina. In five-years, tactics for consent were modified including: addition of an electronic consent, changing the paperwork color, timing of paperwork distribution, location of paperwork in student folders, incentives, and expanding school districts. During this time, there was a 19% increase in enrollment rates of eight elementary schools in two school districts. Additionally, in the last two school years, there was a 19% increase in the amount of returned consent forms (both consenting and declining program enrollment) and a 15% increase in consenting to program enrollment. For the 2024-2025 school year, 70% of consent forms were returned with an average of 40% program enrollment. Changes were effective to increase enrollment rates in the school-based oral health program. A study limitation is inability to differentiate the effectiveness of specific variables, as several changes were made simultaneously. However, the significant increase in opt-out forms allows opportunity for further research to identify reasons families may decline program enrollment.Source of Funding: : The Duke Endowment (Grant #217569-671411), The Blue Cross and Blue Shield Foundation of North Carolina (Grant #217259-671411), The Delta Dental Foundation (Grant #217604-671411), East Carolina University School of Dental Medicine, Bertie County Schools District and Jones County Public Schools Di

title: 2 - Accessible Community Care Using Mobile Dental Delivery Systems
description: Description: Access Dental Care (ADC) is a nonprofit organization dedicated to ensuring equitable access to comprehensive oral health care for individuals who are not usually served through traditional dental delivery systems. Using fully equipped mobile dental units and portable equipment, ADC brings high-quality dental care directly to residents of skilled nursing facilities, group homes for individuals with intellectual and developmental disabilities (IDD), Programs of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE), regional HIV/AIDS treatment centers, and accessible community sites and a senior center.By removing barriers of transportation, mobility, and complex medical or behavioral needs, ADC provides preventive, restorative, surgical and prosthetic dental care in environments where patients feel safe and supported. This innovative model depends on strong community partnerships with healthcare providers, long-term care organizations, and social service agencies that help identify patients in need and coordinate integrated care.Beyond improving patient access, ADC also addresses provider-level challenges by developing training, delivery systems and consultation that enable dental professionals to deliver care safely and confidently patient-centered care to all but especially those that are medically fragile and behaviorally complex. For over twenty-five years, ADC’s approach has demonstrated that community-based, mobile dental delivery can achieve high-quality outcomes while promoting dignity, comfort, and continuity of care for vulnerable populations. This model offers a replicable framework for expanding oral health equity and strengthening community health systems through collaboration, innovation, and compassion.Source of Funding: : Operations are supported by Medicaid, Medicare Advantage Plans, Private Insurance and Self-pay. ADC is also supported by public and private grants.
instructors: Betsy L. White, RDH, BS, FSCDH, FADHA

title: 3 - The Value of Public Health Dentistry to Patients
description: Description: “The Value of Public Health Dentistry to Patients” reviews the collective data of the impact Public Health Dentistry has for patients and communities.This project assesses the impact of providing dental services outside of the bricks and mortar dental practice. An analysis of a Community Care program including the utilization of a Community Health Worker/Qualified Interpreter in the field, patient surveys, quantitative data collection, and addressing Social Determinants of Health. This program has yielded many positive outcomes, including increased utilization by Medicaid patients, additional access points to dental care, reduced health disparities and promoted health equity for community members. Learning Objective(s): Describe how public health dentistry promotes whole health. Understand how patient and site surveys inform program success. Describe how public health dentistry can incorporate Community Health Workers.Source of Funding: : none
instructors: Amy D. Bell, BS, RDH, EPP

title: 4 - Healthy Smiles, Happy Students: Designing a Practical Playbook for School-Based Teledentistry
description: Description: School-based teledentistry is emerging as a promising strategy to expand preventive oral health care access for children, particularly in rural and underserved communities. However, building effective school-based programs requires practical guidance that can translate best practices and program design principles into real-world action. Virginia Health Catalyst is developing Healthy Smiles, Happy Students: A Practical Playbook for School-Based Teledentistry, a comprehensive how-to resource designed to help schools, clinics, and community partners plan, launch, and sustain school-based teledentistry programs. By April 2026, a draft of the core content will be largely complete. However, this roundtable presents an unparalleled opportunity to gather expert feedback on the Playbook’s design, toolkit, and dissemination strategy to ensure it is user-friendly and widely adopted. Participants will engage in a structured discussion around four central questions: (1) What types of implementation tools would make the Playbook most useful for end-users? (2) How can the Playbook best reflect the practical realities and diverse contexts of school-based settings nationwide? (3) What dissemination and promotion strategies would most effectively reach and engage potential users? (4) How can state oral health programs or dental public health leaders be equipped to champion and distribute the Playbook within their own networks? Input from participants will inform the final layout, features, and dissemination approach of the Playbook, helping ensure it serves as a practical, adaptable, and useful guide for expanding school-based teledentistry services across the country.Source of Funding: : This project is supported by the Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) of the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) under Grants to States to Support Oral Health Workforce Activities, Award Number 702F2500455.

title: 5 - Assessing rural/frontier counties for fluoride access: a case study from Nevada
description: Description: Nevada has a great expanse of rural/frontier counties that make up 87% of the state’s land mass. It also has mineral-rich soil – and groundwater - in many of its rural and frontier counties. In 2025, Nevada undertook an assessment to better understand the distribution of fluoride in groundwater and at the tap, with particular attention to smaller water systems. This is the first of its kind assessment for Nevada. This session will report on our findings. Attendees will leave with a better understanding of the unique oral health landscape for Nevada’s rural/frontier communities, Nevada’s regulatory and infrastructure dynamics around fluoride and fluoridation, and the complexities of working with smaller water systems.Source of Funding: : US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), cooperative agreement grant.

title: 6 - Feeling Good About Your Smile: An Oral Health Learning Session for People with IDD
description: Description: Adults with intellectual and developmental disabilities (IDD) often face unique barriers to maintaining good oral health, including limited access to providers, difficulties with daily oral hygiene, and stigma that impacts their confidence. Oral Health Kansas has developed Feeling Good About Your Smile, an interactive adult learning session designed specifically for people with IDD. The program blends practical oral hygiene instruction with self-advocacy, empowerment, and positive reinforcement strategies to help participants feel proud of their smile and more confident about oral health.This round table will provide an overview of the session design, share feedback from participants and caregivers, and invite colleagues to discuss strategies for adapting adult learning models in their own communities.By the end of this round table, participants will be able to describe the structure and key components of the Feeling Good About Your Smile learning session, identify strategies to adapt adult learning principles for people with IDD in oral health education, and share potential partnerships, venues, and methods for bringing similar sessions to their communities.Source of Funding: : This project is funded by the Kansas Department of Health and Environment – Division of Health Care Finance (KDHE-DHCF) and ARPA.

title: 7 - Assessing Lived Experience of Adults Enrolled in Maryland Medicaid Dental Coverage
description: Description: Rund-Al- Rusan (1), Ridhima Luthra (1), Arin Hanson (2), Mary Backley (3), Hosam Alraqiq(4)(My Colleague Rund and I both are first authors and would like to present if possible)In January 2023, Maryland implemented comprehensive adult dental coverage under Medicaid. This milestone, achieved after years of advocacy by the Maryland Dental Action Coalition (MDAC) and partner organizations, represents a major step toward reducing barriers to oral health care.In collaboration with MDAC, dental public health residents from the National Institute of Dental and Craniofacial Research (NIDCR) developed a project to assess the lived experience of Medicaid participants. Informed by A Guide to Monitoring Medicaid Using Lived Experience, developed by the Center on Budget and Policy Priorities, the project draws on lessons from more than 50 nonprofit organizations nationwide that engage directly with people who are on Medicaid.The project is being carried out in two stages. Stage one involved developing culturally and linguistically appropriate tools to assess how the coverage influences participants’ utilization of services as well as their social, financial, and overall well-being. These tools range from structured questionnaires to video storytelling directly addressed to lawmakers. In the second stage, the tools will be implemented at the care delivery partners level, where NIDCR residents will participate in collecting stories and gain hands-on experience working with this vulnerable population.This session will highlight lessons from MDAC collaboration, describe the development and application of participant-centered tools, and invite discussion on strategies to integrate lived experience into oral health systems change.Source of Funding: : None

title: 8 - Building Sustainable Partnerships & Targeted Communication Strategy to Support Community Engagement
description: Description: AIDPH has centered community engagement as the foundation of our research and educational programming. As this central value evolved over the past five years, AIDPH formalized our process for communicating with a diverse range of stakeholders, including community partners. Without clear, targeted, and accessible messaging, outcomes risk being shelved in publications rather than shaping policy, practice, and patient care. This roundtable will share the process, outcomes, and frameworks on communicating for patient-centered research and explore pathways for reaching diverse audiences. The discussion will examine strategies such as utilizing project management tools to streamline engagement, aligning messages and materials across channels, developing infographics and concise briefs, and leveraging partner networks to expand reach and credibility. Participants will also consider how to maintain trust in an environment challenged by misinformation, engage with micro- and community-based outlets, and co-create dissemination materials that are accessible and culturally affirming.
