Throughout the course of this roundtable, participants will explore two questions: (1) how do we help stakeholders understand why community engagement matters, and (2) how do we ensure people can implement research to make their communities stronger and healthier? This session invites participants to share their own communication challenges and successes, generating a shared toolbox of strategies for translating research into community impact. By focusing on communication as a lever for engagement, trust, and collective impact, this roundtable offers a roadmap for organizations seeking to embed equity, inclusion, and sustainability into community partnerships and to translate research into actionable change within the communities of greatest need.Source of Funding: : PCORI - Patient Centered Outcomes Research Institute

title: 9 - Beyond the Smile: What the Data Say About Senior Oral Health Inequities
description: Description: A smile can inspire confidence, connection, and health—yet for many lower-income older adults, dental care remains out of reach. Applied Survey Research partnered with Dientes Community Dental Care and Salud Para La Gente on a multi-year oral health assessment in Santa Cruz County to better understand the evolving needs of older adults with limited access to care. The discussion will draw on data from the 2023 Senior Survey and annual Patient Pulse Surveys (2024–2026), which together offer a rare longitudinal view of local oral health trends among lower-income seniors.Findings reveal persistent inequities in oral health, with over half of older adults in 2023 rating their oral health as fair or poor and reporting pain, difficulty eating, and social isolation. Cost, transportation, and mobility were cited as primary barriers. While preventive care rates have gradually improved since 2023, affordability continues to constrain access. By 2025, one in three patients reported receiving assistance from a caregiver, reflecting the growing intersection between oral health and aging support needs.This Roundtable discussion will share data-driven insights on how oral health outcomes among older adults have shifted over time and invite participants to discuss the broader implications for health equity, caregiving, and aging policy. Together, we will explore what the trends reveal about community-level gaps and opportunities—and how longitudinal data can guide future investments in equitable, accessible oral health care for older adults.Source of Funding: : none

title: Advancing Health Equity: Integrating The History of Systemic Racism in Predoctoral Dental Education
description: Description: The cultivation of culturally humble and socially just dentists who will advocate for and effect change towards oral health equity is advanced by intentionally educating them on the impacts of structural racism on population health. A literature review suggests that the etiologies of health inequities, such as social determinants of health, are commonly included in dental education. However, intentional inclusion of the impact of systemic racism on health inequities was found to be an area for development. California Northstate University College of Dental Medicine (CNU) prides itself on maintaining a humanistic culture that promotes health equity, community service, and social accountability. This predoctoral dental program surpasses the CODA requirements for cultural competency and diversity training by incorporating the history and impact of structural racism on population health, in addition to the traditional topics of cultural humility, DEIB, and oral health equity in the 3rd year Dental Public Health Course. This roundtable will review CNU’s 3rd year Dental Public Health Course, specifically highlighting the impact of the lesson "The History of Systemic Racism in Healthcare" on predoctoral dental students. This course’s outcomes reveal that the topic of systemic racism was effectively introduced, and that most students had not previously been exposed to this history. It will also report on the opportunities to address oral health inequities via inclusion of structural racism in predoctoral education, which were highlighted in our literature review.Source of Funding: : none

title: Climate Assessment on Executive Orders Targeting DEI: Views from AAPHD, ASTDD, NNOHA, AAPD Members
description: Description: In 2025, federal executive actions, state and local policies have impacted efforts related to diversity, equity, and inclusion. The American Association of Public Health Dentistry (AAPHD), in partnership with ASTDD (Association of State and Territorial Dental Directors), NNOHA (National Network for Oral Health Access), and AAPD (American Academy of Pediatric Dentistry), launched a collaborative climate assessment to examine how recent DEI-related changes affect members of participating organizations professionally, personally, and organizationally. Specific aims include: 1) to evaluate the personal and professional consequences of DEI-related executive actions and federal changes, including effects on professional environment, research capacity, patient care, dental education, and well-being, and 2) to identify actionable recommendations to most effectively support and advocate for dental public health professionals. Components of the climate assessment include an online survey of members of the participating organizations. This roundtable will provide a space to share survey results and facilitate discussion among members about their own experiences and opportunities for action. Results will help inform actionable recommendations to enhance support, advocacy, and policy engagement for dental public health professionals, as well as strategic goals for organizations.Source of Funding: : TBD

title: Silver Fluoride Therapy: Clinical Updates and Emerging Applications
description: Description: Silver fluoride therapy continues to gain prominence as a minimally invasive, cost-effective strategy for caries management and prevention, while being FDA cleared as a dentinal hypersensitivity treatment. This roundtable presentation will provide a timely update on the latest developments in silver fluoride formulations, including silver diamine fluoride (SDF) and silver fluoride gels, with a focus on new research, evolving clinical guidelines, and real-world implementation. Participants will explore advancements in delivery methods, expanded indications, and patient-centered communication approaches. Special attention will be given to its role in managing early childhood caries, treating root caries in older adults, and increasing access to care in underserved populations. Through open discussion, attendees will gain practical insights into integrating updated silver fluoride therapy into comprehensive caries management protocols.Source of Funding: : Elevate Oral Care

title: Synergizing Shots and Smiles: Developing a Toolkit to Implement Human Papillomavirus Vaccination
description: Description: Human papillomavirus (HPV) causes more than 37,000 cancers annually in the United States (CDC, 2025). Despite the vaccine’s effectiveness, adolescent HPV vaccination rates in Colorado remain below national goals. Because adolescents often see dental providers more frequently than medical providers, integrating HPV vaccination into dental settings offers an innovative, equity-focused strategy to expand access and prevent HPV-related cancers.Building on a prior medical-dental integration (MDI) pilot that increased HPV vaccination rates by 8.6% among more than 2,000 adolescents (Jack, 2025), this program aligns with Colorado’s new legislation that expands dental scope of practice to include vaccination in 2026. Strong partnerships with the Alliance for HPV Free Colorado, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment, and state dental associations support implementation and sustainability.This project leverages partnership with community members and dental providers to address vaccination disparities through creation of a comprehensive HPV vaccination toolkit. The toolkit includes provider training on vaccine counseling and hesitancy, patient education materials, and guidance on vaccine storage and billing. Development was informed by caregiver, adolescent, and dental advisory panels to ensure cultural relevance and practicality. Ten geographically diverse dental practices serving underserved populations will pilot the toolkit, with success defined as 80% of sites implementing the toolkit and achieving at least a 50% vaccination rate among eligible patients.By transforming dental clinics into vaccination access points, this initiative addresses health disparities and provides a scalable, community-driven model for cancer prevention and health equity innovation.Source of Funding: : Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment, Office of Health Equity

title: Navigating Perinatal Oral Health: What's Working and What is Not!
description: Description: Inability to access oral health care before, during and after pregnancy can contribute to negative outcomes for women and infants. The ASTDD Perinatal Oral Health Best Practice Approach Report describes the Strategic Framework for improving perinatal oral health and offers examples on how state oral health programs can facilitate such improvement. Yet there are often challenges to such good intentions. What has worked for your SOHP to improve perinatal oral health and what obstacles stood in the way of progress? Come and discuss Lessons Learned so that others can emulate your success.Source of Funding: : none
instructors: Steven P. Geiermann, DDS, FICD, FACD

title: Improving Dental Care for Individuals with HIV Through an Enhanced Education Online Curriculum.
description: Description: Come to this roundtable to learn about the online National HIV Curriculum (NHC) and explore the oral health education learning pathways within it. The NHC is a comprehensive online digital learning pathway currently used by medical, nursing and pharmacy students to attain core competency in HIV prevention, screening, diagnosis, and treatment. Dental educators were not initially included in this curriculum, until the Midwest AIDS Training and Education Center selected 4 dental advanced education programs to pilot relevant sections of the NHC curriculum for oral health learners. Designed to meet the diverse needs of healthcare providers, the curriculum covers key topics such as HIV epidemiology, treatment and prevention strategies,antiretroviral therapy guidelines, co-infections, and the psychosocial dimensions of HIV care. It aims to reduce HIV stigma among dental health professionals by increasing awareness of the changes and improvements in treatment and prevention of disease.The dental educational sites in this pilot have included post-doctoral students in several specialties, using different techniques to promote enhanced knowledge of individuals living with HIV. We will discuss our experiences integrating this curriculum into dental education and brainstorm how they can be expanded and improved with other programs as well as ways to measure its success in furthering the education of oral health providers. You can sign on and explore the lessons and see for yourself how this can help you and your organization improve care for those living with HIV.Source of Funding: : Funded by the Health Resources and Services Administration of the U.S. Department of Healthand Human Services.

title: Beyond Recognition: Dentistry and Eating Disorders Management
description: Description: Eating Disorders are a group of complex, life-threatening psychiatric conditions that remain perilously underdiagnosed and undertreated. With early recognition and timely treatment, a full recovery is possible; yet, as few as 20% of affected individuals receive the care they need. Detectable intra-oral, extra-oral, and behavioral signs of disordered eating can make dental providers among the first healthcare professionals to observe changes. However, the identification of signs and symptoms alone is not enough. A provider’s willingness and ability to raise concerns and connect patients to care can be among the most important steps in an individual’s road to recovery. To better understand readiness among dentists, we conducted research at Columbia University’s College of Dental Medicine examining knowledge, beliefs, self-efficacy, and practices related to the care of patients with a suspected eating disorder. Findings reveal gaps in knowledge and self-perceived ability to translate clinical observations into action, underscoring the urgent need for systematic integration of skill-based eating disorders education into dental training and consolidated systems to facilitate the connection of care between dental and psychiatric fields. Presentation of our results will serve as the basis for discussion on the role of dentistry in eating disorders management and opportunities to improve provider readiness and action. Participants will gain insight into novel strategies for embedding essential medical-dental integration into dental education and practice, empowering providers to act on observations with confidence and care. By actively involving dentistry in broader healthcare systems, we can improve access to psychiatric care, reduce stigma, and ultimately save lives.Source of Funding: : CVS Aetna

title: School-Based Dental Prevention and Medical-Dental Integration in Tennessee
description: Description: Tennessee’s School-Based Dental Prevention Program, funded by TennCare, Tennessee’s Medicaid program, and administered by the Tennessee Department of Health, has provided vital dental preventive services—including oral health screenings, fluoride varnish application, and silver diamine fluoride (SDF) treatment—to children in grades Pre-K-8, especially in rural and underserved areas. For many children, these visits represent their only professional dental encounter and their sole access to oral hygiene supplies during the year.In parallel, Tennessee’s state health departments have been successful in integrating oral and primary care. Dental teams offer brief counseling and referrals for HPV vaccination and tobacco cessation, and nurses have been trained to apply fluoride varnish, integrating oral health with chronic disease prevention and health promotion. This roundtable will explore current implementation strategies, operational successes and challenges, service statistics, and community impact for both school-based and clinic-based dental models. We will discuss workflow integration, training practices, outcomes tracking, and partnership with FQHCs and local schools.Source of Funding: : State and Federal funding for programs discussed.

title: Feasibility of Silver Diamine Fluoride Research at a Hawaii Federally Qualified Health Center
description: Description: There are 1.6 million Americans who identify as Pacific Islander (PI). Between 2013-2023, the population grew by 32.7% and PIs make up more than 1% of the population in five states. In Hawaii, 46% of PI children have untreated dental caries. Despite a growing population, little is known about how to treat their high caries rates given poor diets, high utilization of emergency care, lack of preventive dental care, and the impact of social determinants of health. Silver Diamine Fluoride (SDF), an easy-to-apply liquid fluoride, can be used to arrest non-cavitated carious lesions from becoming bigger and needing a filling, and could help treat high levels of caries. However, since SDF effectiveness is dependent on multiple applications, its effectiveness in PIs is unknown. The purpose of this study is to investigate the feasibility of conducting a Federally Qualified Health Center (FQHC)-initiated SDF study at a Hawaii FQHC with a large PI population. SDF is applied to incipient carious lesions on posterior teeth in children ages 5-12 years old who present for routine dental exam. Parents/guardians also complete a survey on oral health knowledge, oral hygiene practices, and dietary information. Children will be followed for 3-month intervals for reapplication of SDF and to assess effectiveness. Results include the logistics of conducting an FQHC-initiated clinical research study (staff training, clinic flow, recruitment), organizing academic resources and partnerships, and the messaging to a population that is often fearful of research. Results can impact larger oral health studies and interventions for the PI population.Source of Funding: : The project described was supported by grant number U54GM138062 from the National Institute of General Medical Sciences (NIGMS), a component of the National Institutes of Health (NIH), and its contents are solely the responsibility of the author and do not necessarily represent the official view of NIGMS or NIH.

title: Expanding the Frame: Integrating Social Science into Oral Health Research
description: Description: This roundtable explores the emerging role of qualitative research in oral health and dentistry, emphasizing how methods such as interviews, focus groups, and ethnography illuminate the social, cultural, and institutional contexts that shape oral health outcomes. The session begins with a brief overview of the disciplinary tensions that often arise between clinical designs to modify individual behaviors and social science approaches that situate those behaviors within broader frameworks of social meaning and constraint. These tensions, while sometimes challenging, are also generative—offering opportunities for methodological innovation, reflexivity, and interdisciplinary collaboration. Building on this foundation, the discussion will introduce the idea of bridge concepts: interpretive tools designed to hold together distinct disciplinary logics within a shared analytic frame. Bridge concepts support collaboration by enabling researchers and practitioners to engage oral health phenomena in ways that are both clinically actionable and socially grounded. The session will conclude with an open discussion in which participants are invited to reflect on their own experiences with qualitative methods, consider how bridge concepts might apply to their work, and explore strategies for integrating social science insight into oral health research and practice. By foregrounding the lived experience of oral health and the structural determinants that shape it, this roundtable aims to advance innovative, socially responsive approaches to oral health inquiry. It will be of particular interest to NOHC attendees engaged in research, education, and community-based care who seek to expand their methodological toolkit and foster interdisciplinary dialogue.Source of Funding: : NIH NRSA T-32 Training Grant
instructors: Frederick C. Howard, PhD, Anthropology; MA, Social Sciences

title: Leveraging BigMouth Data: Transforming Electronic Health Records into Population Health Insights
description: Description: The integration of electronic health records (EHRs) into population-level research provides an unparalleled opportunity to connect chairside clinical care with public health impact. The BigMouth Dental Data Repository, coordinated by UTHealth Houston, harmonizes de-identified dental EHR data from multiple U.S. dental schools, creating one of the nation’s largest oral health data resources. With more than 200,000 patient records spanning diverse populations and geographic regions, BigMouth offers a robust platform for surveillance, treatment pattern analysis, and data-driven innovation.This roundtable will explore how clinical EHR data can be transformed into actionable insights to advance oral health equity and inform policy. Through case examples, participants will learn how BigMouth has been used to investigate oral-systemic health intersections and to develop artificial intelligence (AI)-based predictive models. Presenters will address methodological, ethical, and practical considerations for working with large-scale EHR data and will demonstrate the process of accessing and analyzing BigMouth data for research and program evaluation.By highlighting real-world applications and fostering discussion around collaborative opportunities, this session will illustrate how BigMouth serves as a catalyst for interdisciplinary research, bridging clinical, academic, and public health practice. Attendees will leave equipped with practical strategies for leveraging large-scale clinical data to generate evidence for improving population oral health outcomes.Source of Funding: : This work was supported by internal research funds from participating academic institutions within the BigMouth Consortium.Co-Presenter: Faeq Quadri, BDS, MSc, PhD Assistant Professor, Director of Research, Woody L. Hunt School of Dental Medicine, El Paso, TX
instructors: Chukwuebuka E. Ogwo, BDS MIH MPH PhD

title: Oral Health Care for People with Special Health Care in Health Centers
description: Description: Oral health is considered one of the greatest unmet health needs for individuals with special health care needs (SHCN). Individuals with SHCN often seek oral health care in health centers and other safety-net settings. To understand the current landscape of oral health care delivery for individuals with SHCN, National Network for Oral Health Access (NNOHA) conducted a survey in Fall 2025 in partnership with the Jacobi Medical Center Dental Public Health residency program. The survey focused on facilitators and barriers to delivering oral health services as well as information about training experience, perceptions, and comfort level of dental providers. This round table session will include a high-level review of the survey results. Also, it will engage participants in open discussions on strategies and recommendations to close the gap for oral health care for people with SHCN.Source of Funding: : None

title: Found in Translation: An Organizational Approach to Enhance Language Services in Dental Clinics
description: Description: More than 1 in 5 people (22%) speak a language other than English in the U.S. Communication barriers lead to decreased care quality, adverse events, and poorer health outcomes. Organizational strategies used to meet patient language needs vary across dental settings. During this roundtable session, participants will discuss how to establish and integrate evidence-informed systems into their organizational structure to comprehensively address language services within their dental clinics. This discussion will begin with a brief case study highlighting a comprehensive, evidence-informed approach to language services in a dental setting (including electronic health record adaptation, dual role interpreters, ‘I Speak’ campaign, training, and evaluation). An organizational assessment instrument will be presented for participant review for consideration relevant to their respective institutions. Discussion involving federal policies followed by interactive brainstorming of shared and novel ideas, group problem-solving of potential challenges and/or innovative opportunities to enhance organizational approaches at home clinics will close out the session.Source of Funding: : Department of Health and Human Services Office of Minority Health - Award #: CPIMP221352

title: Building Strong Foundations: Tools for Oral Health Program Design & Evaluation
description: Description: Effective public oral health programs require thoughtful design and built-in evaluation strategies from the start. Yet many initiatives struggle to demonstrate measurable impact due to unclear objectives or limited evaluation planning. This round table discussion equips attendees with practical tools for program design and evaluation, ensuring initiatives are set up for long-term success.The presenter will introduce three core planning tools, SWOT analysis, logic models, and the Health Impact Pyramid, and demonstrate how each can guide program objectives, structure, and evaluation. A case study from a rural oral health initiative will show how SWOT identified barriers (transportation, language, workforce shortages), how a logic model structured activities and outcomes, and how the Health Impact Pyramid aligned objectives with population-level impact. Evaluation strategies highlighted both successes and challenges, offering valuable lessons for replication.Participants will learn how to apply each tool and leave with worksheets and templates for immediate use.Source of Funding: : none

title: Beyond the Build: Taking Digital Oral Health Training to Caregivers Where They Are
description: Description: Older adults face unique oral health challenges which are compounded by chronic conditions, medications, mobility and cognitive limitations, and limited care access. Poor oral health increases risks to overall health. Formal and informal caregivers are crucial for daily oral hygiene and early problem detection, yet literacy levels are low, and training and education to provide support is often non-existent. This roundtable shares findings from a two-year pilot informed from a larger, published statewide oral health initiative, aimed at improving caregiver knowledge and practices. Year One involved surveying caregivers and developing a tailored web-based educational resource, which demonstrated measurable learning gains. Year Two expanded outreach by partnering with caregiving communities to boost access and engagement. This session will share participant feedback, successes, challenges including feedback from community champions. Utilizing interactive and practical approaches, we’ll encourage attendees to provide feedback on the model and results while offering suggestions to broaden the reach. Attendees will be encouraged to share their experiences and insights related to this topic.Source of Funding: : CareQuest Institute for Oral Health and the Lunder-Dineen Health Education Alliance of Maine, a program of MGB

title: Building a Teledentistry Program in Iowa
description: Description: Teledentistry can extend the reach of dental professionals beyond in-person clinical settings and increase access to care for people who have the most difficulty receiving services and with Iowa having 61 out of 99 counties being designated as rural, the use of teledentistry would increase access to oral health services. Iowa Health and Human Services (HHS) designed a project plan to help hospital Emergency Department patients experiencing dental issues access the care they need. The project is being implemented through the I-Smile Silver program, which links adults in 10 Iowa counties with dental, medical, and community resources for health and wellness. Over time, these linkages will lead to less preventable and repeat emergency department visits.HHS has continued to explore teledentistry in Iowa and collaborated with Iowa Medicaid to implement the following teledentistry codes:Synchronous teledentistry (D9995), asynchronous teledentistry (D9996), Screening of a Patient (D0190), nutritional counseling (D1310), tobacco counseling (D1320), and oral hygiene instruction (D1330) through video communication. With the addition of teledentistry codes, HHS has expanded teledentistry to the I-Smile program. I-Smile is a statewide program for children and pregnant women, with an emphasis on early intervention and preventive oral health care beginning at or near the age of 12 months.Implementing teledentistry has been a long process and included the following: obtaining teledentistry codes, designing protocols for contractors to use when practicing teledentistry, research on necessary release forms, creating teledentistry forms and release of information documents, training contractors on teledentistry, and continuous technical assistance to contractors.Source of Funding: : HRSA Workforce grant and Title V Block grant.

title: Video Counseling Program: Motivational Interviewing in a Telehealth Program to Lower Lifestyle Risks
description: Description: The 2000 Surgeon General’s Report on Oral Health emphasized the risk of oral habits and lifestyle choices in oral disease prevalence and recurrence. Health promotion activities can use techniques like risk assessment and motivational interviewing, a patient-centered collaborative counseling approach to support positive behavior change. Workflows for clinical care provision are not ideal for effectively assessing and intervening on lifestyle risk factors. Teledentistry provides an accessible opportunity to provide counseling services in a safe, private environment to discuss sensitive topics, share educational material, and enhance patient engagement. With an awareness that these services may prevent initial and recurrent disease, many state Medicaid programs now provide coverage for these services, including Minnesota Health Care Programs. This roundtable describes the Virtual Counseling Program implemented by Apple Tree Dental, including education of all clinical staff about the value of referral to the program, health plan coordination of payment to support sustaining the program infrastructure, dental record adaptations for tracking the intervention, and showcasing the workflow for patients referred for the service. Descriptive measures of the program and feedback from health professionals and patients will be shared to inform best practices and lessons learned. Applying this service to key populations and programs will be discussed, including those serving high caries-risk populations such as children and adults with special care needs.Source of Funding: : There is no funding source for the program described.

title: Enhancing Access to Oral Health Care Through SDF-A Case Study: Republic of the Marshall Islands
description: Description: The Republic of the Marshall Islands (RMI) is an independent nation that is linked with the U.S. in the Western Pacific. Like many of the U.S. Affiliated Pacific Islands, it faces unique challenges in promoting oral health for its population. RMI’s population of just over 42,000 (2021) is spread across 24 islands and atolls and across 750,000 square miles of ocean. Confronting these challenges requires systematic, evidence-based approaches along with local and outside support and partnerships. Over the past several years, ASTDD has engaged RMI oral health programs (OHP) in an oral health infrastructure needs/capacity assessment and other technical assistance regarding surveillance (BSS) and fluoride varnish (FV) application. Also, ASTDD has helped to identify some foundational recommendations upon which the RMI OHP could promote and enhance oral health for its population. Data from a recent survey of school children in RMI showed that 46.8% of experienced dental caries versus 42% in the US; 35.9% had untreated caries versus 23% in the US. Data clearly demonstrated the need to enhance their efforts, e.g., to conduct oral health risk assessments, expand the applications of FV and adopt silver diamine fluoride (SDF) applications to prevent/arrest caries. This presentation will describe how the RMI OHP through training its care providers will improve access to oral health care by using FV and SDF in its dental clinics, school-based, and community-based settings.Source of Funding: : Health Resources and Services Administration’s Maternal and Child Health Bureau and the Bureau of Oral Health/Republic of the Marshall Islands and Pacific Islands Primary Care Association
instructors: Flora Nathan, Diploma in Dental Therapy

title: Dental Assistant Training Program Pilots Medical-Dental Integration Curriculum
description: Description: The Dental Assistant Training Program at Denver Health (DH), a large safety-net healthcare system in Denver, provides a 6-month dental assistant training focused on increasing the number of Black, Indigenous, or People of Color (BIPOC) dental professionals. In 2024, the program expanded the curriculum to include piloting the use of dental assistants in medical clinics as an approach to increase oral health access for DH’s patient population, many of whom are BIPOC, and to assess if the experience increased student interest in the dental field. The students learned about the benefits of integrated care and scope of practice in medical settings and spent time shadowing integrated dental hygienists, medical assistants, and medical providers. Two additional cohorts have been enrolled (Summer 2025 and Fall 2025). The evaluation of the program includes a quantitative survey and qualitative interviews of students and staff and assesses the feasibility of this medical-dental integration model, participants’ perceptions and experiences with this model, and the likelihood of the students staying in the dental field and increasing the dental workforce diversity. Roundtable participants will be able to describe the dental assistant training program’s medical-dental integration curriculum and rotation structure, will comprehend successes and challenges of integrating dental assistants students into a large federally qualified health center system, and will describe the dental assistant students perceptions and experiences. Source of Funding: Delta Dental of Colorado FoundationSource of Funding: : Source of Funding: Delta Dental of Colorado Foundation
instructors: Patricia A. Braun, MD, MPH, FAAP

title: In-house Dental Assistant Training Programs in Health Centers
description: Description: Health centers serve as community-centered organizations, dedicated not only to care delivery but also to advancing career development and strengthening local workforce capacity. With recent dental assistant (DA) shortages, health centers seek innovative strategies to recruit and retain DAs. In response, National Network for Oral Health Access (NNOHA), launched the DA Workforce Learning Collaborative (LC) in 2024. Guided by insights from NNOHA’s DA Needs Assessment, the LC is designed to support health centers to establish in-house DA training programs and build a sustainable pathway of qualified DAs. NNOHA has worked with 30 health centers nationwide to create a framework for DA training programs including budget planning, curriculum design, implementation strategies, and recommendations for long-term career growth opportunities for DAs. These programs strengthen the oral health workforce and reinforce the broader mission of health centers to support community wellness through career opportunities and access to care. 1. Review current challenges in recruiting and retaining dental assistants.2. Explain the impact of workforce shortages on care delivery and oral health access.3. Identify key aspects of in-house dental assistant training programs. 4. Describe lessons learned from the NNOHA Dental Assistant Workforce Learning Collaborative to design and enhance the dental assistant workforce.Source of Funding: : HRSA National Training and Technical Assistant Partnership (BPHC)
instructors: Candace Hsu Owen, RDH, MS, MPH

title: AAPHD Council Equips a Dental Public Health Workforce for 21st-Century Practice
description: Description: The American Association of Public Health Dentistry (AAPHD), a leading organization advancing the specialty of Dental Public Health (DPH), promotes equitable oral health through education, research, practice, policy, and advocacy. AAPHD’s Council on Practice and Workforce addresses critical gaps between DPH resources and evolving population needs. With the current oral health care system falling short in delivering preventive and whole-person care, the Council works to align workforce development with structural competencies that support oral health equity. Two workgroups advance this mission: one focuses on strengthening DPH training outputs and employment opportunities; the other workgroup aims to propose a definition for Population Oral Health, including nontraditional members of the workforce, models of care delivery, and roles across diverse sectors. These efforts aim to support a sustainable oral health infrastructure by promoting evidence-based policies, clarifying professional roles, and equipping the DPH workforce to lead in diverse care environments and delivery systems. This roundtable discussion will describe the key initiatives of the two Workgroups of this Council and seek input from stakeholders to inform its efforts.Source of Funding: : There is no funding source for the activities described in this presentation.

title: Navigating Dental Public Health Career Pathways Post Graduation
description: Description: Graduates of dental public health (DPH) residency programs are trained to address population-level health, yet many face challenges identifying viable employment opportunities after graduation. While academic and research roles remain prominent, pathways into state and local agencies, community health centers, integrated health systems, and policy organizations often present barriers. A central issue is that many non-academic career options require dental licensure, a process predicated on clinical experience and passage of licensing examinations, which are not part of the current DPH curriculum. This limits access to positions that could benefit from the unique skillsets of DPH-trained professionals. At the same time, most opportunities are currently identified through networking rather than structured recruitment, reflecting a broader disconnect between employers and the competencies gained in DPH programs. Employers may be unaware of the value DPH specialists bring in shaping policy, workforce development, and system-level integration. This roundtable will provide a forum to discuss these barriers and explore strategies to better elevate the specialty, strengthen mentorship and visibility, and improve alignment between training and workforce opportunities. Participants will share experiences in navigating career transitions, highlight the gaps between employer expectations and DPH competencies, and generate collective strategies to enhance career pipelines. By fostering dialogue among graduates, seasoned leaders, and educators, this session aims to reimagine how DPH career pathways are cultivated, supported, and recognized to expand the specialty’s impact across the health system.Source of Funding: : None.

title: Reimagining Dental Public Health Residency Curriculum Through Clinical Integration & Funding Reform
description: Description: Dental public health (DPH) residencies equip dentists to lead population-level prevention, policy, and systems change. Yet programs vary widely in structure, leaving graduates unevenly prepared for employers’ expectations. The absence of clinical components in the current curriculum presents challenges for graduates who encounter workforce barriers in settings that require active dental licensure or ongoing clinical proficiency. As a result, DPH specialists may be excluded from positions where their expertise in population health, workforce integration, and system transformation could be invaluable. This roundtable will explore the need for curriculum reform to incorporate clinical training components and consider models that combine DPH residencies with programs such as General Practice Residencies (GPRs) or Advanced Education in General Dentistry (AEGDs). These integrated pathways could ensure that DPH residents maintain clinical competency while advancing foundational knowledge in policy, prevention, and health equity.An equally critical component of this discussion is financial sustainability. Most residency programs rely on limited institutional or grant support, leaving DPH residencies vulnerable to underfunding. Exploring the potential for Graduate Medical Education (GME) funding, as well as blended funding models with other residency programs, is essential to building capacity and strengthening career pathways. Participants will engage in dialogue about innovative models for clinical integration, funding mechanisms to sustain and expand programs, and the long-term implications for the DPH workforce pathways. By fostering cross-sector conversation, this session aims to identify actionable strategies to modernize DPH education and better prepare graduates to serve as leaders across clinical, community, and policy settings.Source of Funding: : none

title: Expanding the Oral Health Workforce Through ROOTED: Frameworks for Rural Access
description: Description: Workforce development is one of the most pressing needs in dental public health, particularly for rural communities. To address this challenge, AIDPH launched ROOTED (Rural Oral health Opportunities to Expand Diversity, Education, and Training), an initiative designed to strengthen and diversify the oral health workforce through intentional, community-driven approaches.ROOTED serves as a flexible framework that can be adapted across multiple stages of professional development. It creates opportunities for students, trainees, and early-career professionals to connect with the realities of rural oral health, while also supporting partnerships with academic institutions, community organizations, and public health agencies. Through activities such as the ROOTED Seminar Series and collaborative learning forums, ROOTED introduces participants to the unique workforce challenges faced by rural communities and highlights strategies that connect education, service, and career development.This roundtable will provide a space for dialogue on how ROOTED’s framework can be applied and scaled to meet local workforce needs. Participants will explore strategies to engage future providers earlier in their training, develop meaningful community partnerships, and expand pathways into rural practice. Building on shared experiences, the session will surface practical and replicable solutions for advancing workforce development and improving access to care in underserved communities.Source of Funding: : Source of Funding: This project is partially funded by the Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) of the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) award Predoctoral Training in General, Pediatric, and Public Health Dentistry and Dental Hygiene HRSA 22-045 Award Number: D85HP45701‐03‐00.

title: West Virginia Meaningful Dentist Project
description: Description: Past studies find Medicaid adult dental coverage as an important part of low-income adults being able to access and use dental care (Singhal et al., 2017). The West Virginia Meaningful Dentist Project (WVMD) explored adult Medicaid enrollees’ (aged 21–64 years) access to dental care and aimed to define “the meaningful dentist” in the context of West Virginia, based on the number of providers enrolled with Medicaid, amount billed by each provider, and number of Medicaid enrollees seen by each provider annually. In partnership with the West Virginia Health Affairs Institute, the project generated maps, that identified the ratio of Medicaid enrollees, aged 21-64, per dentist along with defining what constitutes adequate, some, moderate, or substantial need of meaningful coverage in West Virginia.The WVMD Project defined “meaningful dentist” as a general dentist actively engaged in the Medicaid program, treating Medicaid-enrolled adults, billing for preventative and emergent care, and serving a substantial number of Medicaid enrollees annually.Four key criteria were used to determine whether dentists were meaningful: Provider Capacity, Observed Utilization, Cost Threshold, and Emphasis on Prevention. From 2020 to 2022, the number of counties meeting multiple criteria for meaningful dentists increased. From 2020 to 2022, the number of dentists meeting multiple criteria for meaningful dentist participation also increased.However, despite observed improvement in some areas there is still considerable need to ensure meaningful adult care for Medicaid enrollees across the state.Source of Funding: : Claude Worthington Benedum Foundation
instructors: Gina Sharps, MPH, RDH, CTTS, FADHA

title: Addressing Dentists’ Mental Health and Wellbeing in Iowa
description: Description: After a tragic loss of an Iowa dentist to suicide in 2020, a group of stakeholders was convened in 2023 with the intent of building a cross-sectional workgroup that would create a framework, plan and environment to facilitate early identification of mental health problems and encourage help seeking. A three-year strategic plan was developed to improve the comprehensive mental health and wellbeing of Iowa dentists focusing on prevention and early detection of mental health and wellbeing, building and sustaining positive work and learning environments, and provision of tools and resources. Collaborative efforts of the stakeholder workgroup have led to the following achievements: 1.) Updated Iowa Dental Board rules in June 2024 preventing mental health and substance use disorder discrimination in dentist licensure and credentialing. Dentists now answer a revised question about participation in a state monitoring program without fear of punitive action and 2.) Established an EPA like program for Iowa dentists to receive free counseling/therapy sessions. IADAPT (Iowa Dental Advocacy for Professional and Personal Thriving) was launched January 2025 in which each Iowa dentist can receive up to three, free virtual coaching/therapy sessions a year from a licensed mental health provider. Funding for IADAPT is provided through a grant to the Iowa Dental Foundation from Delta Dental of Iowa Foundation. Although great progress has occurred, there is still much work to be done by the stakeholder workgroup when it comes to changing attitudes and bringing about behavior change related to mental health and wellbeing of dentists in Iowa.Source of Funding: : Delta Dental of Iowa Foundation

title: Advancing Teledentistry in Michigan’s Oral Health Landscape
description: Description: The Michigan Department of Health and Human Services (MDHHS) Oral Health Program is working to expand access to dental care by supporting the use of telehealth in dental settings. In partnership with the Michigan Primary Care Association (MPCA), MDHHS conducted an environmental scan to better understand Michigan’s current telehealth policies and explore opportunities for applying them in dental care. This included a policy review and interviews with dental professionals across the state.This roundtable will take a closer look at Michigan’s teledentistry policy landscape. Key topics will include regulations, Medicaid reimbursement, workflow and staffing considerations, and technology readiness. The session will draw on provider and stakeholder perspectives to explore real-world challenges and emerging strategies for making telehealth work in dental practices.As part of this broader effort, MDHHS and MPCA are launching a pilot project in Fall 2025 in partnership with a Federally Qualified Health Center (FQHC). The goal is to test the feasibility, effectiveness, and long-term sustainability of telehealth in a community dental setting. Insights from the pilot will help shape future policy decisions, technical support, and strategies to improve access to care across Michigan.Participants will walk away with a clearer understanding of where Michigan stands on teledentistry, and join in a discussion about what’s working, what’s needed, and how telehealth can help close gaps in oral health access statewide.Source of Funding: : This project is funded by a HRSA grant T12HP46087.

title: Strengthening the Dental Pipeline for Underserved Communities: Insights from the CARE Program.
description: Description: Rural and underserved communities continue to face persistent oral health workforce shortages. Recruiting students from rural backgrounds and equipping them with the knowledge and skills to address disparities may improve long-term access to care. The CARE (Commitment to Access Resources and Education) Program was developed to recruit Doctor of Dental Surgery (DDS) students from rural areas and provide targeted lectures, mentorship, and training designed to foster service in underserved communities after graduation. Through the CARE Program, participating students receive structured lectures on oral health disparities, social determinants of health, and public health systems across their four years of training. The program also integrates community-based clinical experiences and mentorship from dental public health experts.This roundtable will invite participants to engage in dialogue on designing, implementing, and sustaining workforce pipeline initiatives. Discussion will highlight common challenges such as recruitment and retention of students, ongoing support during training, and evaluation of long-term program outcomes. Attendees with experience in similar initiatives are especially encouraged to contribute insights, successes, and lessons learned.By the end of this session, participants will gain a broader perspective on effective strategies for strengthening dental workforce pipeline programs and sustaining their impact, even during the early stages of development.Source of Funding: : none

title: Empowering Consumers: Using Advocacy to Recruit Dentists
description: Description: Access to dental care remains a significant barrier for people with disabilities and those relying on Medicaid. One promising strategy is to equip consumers, families, and community supporters with tools to confidently approach dentists about accepting Medicaid and treating people with disabilities. Oral Health Kansas developed a Consumer Toolkit for Recruiting Dentists that offers plain-language guidance, sample conversation starters, and resources to help individuals advocate effectively with local providers.This roundtable will introduce the toolkit, highlight how it has been used in Kansas, and invite discussion on how similar advocacy efforts can strengthen dental provider networks nationwide. Participants will share perspectives on empowering consumers, engaging dentists, and building sustainable systems of support.Learning objectives for participants in this session include understanding the components and purpose of the Consumer Advocacy Toolkit, exploring ways to engage consumers, families, and community organizations in dental workforce recruitment, and identifying opportunities to adapt or replicate advocacy approaches in their own states or networks.Source of Funding: : This project is funded by the Kansas Department of Health and Environment – Division of Health Care Finance (KDHE-DHCF) and ARPA.

title: Hygienist Inspired: Chairside Recruitment to Increase Diversity and Address Workforce Shortages
description: Description: Is your clinic struggling to fill hygiene positions because there just aren’t enough dental hygienists in your state? Created by the American Dental Hygienists’ Association (ADHA) in partnership with the Foundations of Delta Dental, Hygienist Inspired is an innovative chairside recruitment program that leverages the amazing relationships between dental hygienists and their patients to make a difference for the profession. Underrepresented populations often hear about a hygiene career for the first time from their own hygienist. Now hygienists have access to free resources to lead potential future hygienists to so they can start their education journey, find dental hygiene programs, scholarships and more.Learn about the resources available to bring this program to your clinic and start using the power of a personal conversation to address workforce shortages, improve access to care, and increase diversity in dental professions. Attendees will learn how hygienists in clinical practice can volunteer to be Allies to help raise awareness about the vital role of dental hygienists in the safety-net by doing what they do best: building relationships, sharing knowledge, and sparking interest in a rewarding career.Source of Funding: : Multiple Delta Dental Foundations, Delta Dental Institute

title: ASTDD Resources Galore!
description: Description: The Association of State and Territorial Dental Directors is a professional association that provides leadership to promote a governmental oral health presence in each state and territory, to formulate and promote sound oral health policy, to increase awareness of oral health issues and to assist in the development of initiatives for prevention and control of oral diseases. ASTDD's Members and Associate members are dedicated to public health and dental professionals. Through varies funding sources, ASTDD committees and consultants have developed competencies, guidelines, best practices, communication materials, evaluation tools, public health policy statements, basic screenings surveys for various age groups, and other significant resources. Visit this roundtable to find out how you and your program can access and use these resources. Why not join the organization so you can have access to additional members' only resources and participate in developing new resources?Source of Funding: : NONE

title: Empowering Oral Health Professionals Through Advocacy and Leadership Education
description: Description: Advocacy and leadership are essential competencies for oral health professionals seeking to influence policy, reduce disparities, and improve population health. There is a need within the dental public health workforce to strengthen these skills through structured education. This roundtable introduces participants to a graduate-level course developed at A.T. Still University’s College of Graduate Health Studies, designed to equip learners with practical tools to lead change in their communities and professional settings.This session will introduce the course design and content, and how it integrates evidence-based strategies in advocacy, coalition-building, policy analysis, and financial planning. Learners engage in real-world applications such as writing letters to policymakers, creating social media advocacy posts, conducting interviews with public health advocates, and participating in mock legislative hearings. Special emphasis is placed on oral health equity, Medicaid dental benefits, and culturally competent engagement.Roundtable participants will explore how to design and implement similar coursework or training modules tailored to oral health professionals. The session will highlight innovative teaching methods, including role-play simulations, AI-assisted communication exercises, and community-based project development.This presentation is highly relevant to ASTDD and AAPHD members, offering a replicable and scalable model for integrating advocacy and leadership into education and training. It demonstrates originality through its interdisciplinary approach and practical orientation, preparing oral health professionals to be influential leaders in advancing public health policy and practice.Source of Funding: : none
instructors: Jaana Gold, DDS, PhD, MPH, CPH

title: Promoting Oral Health for People with IDD Using Advocacy, Prevention, and Practice-Ready Strategies
description: Description: People with intellectual and developmental disabilities (IDD) face preventable oral health disparities driven by policy barriers, workforce readiness gaps, and limited preventive supports. This roundtable shares practice-ready strategies and early results from three AIDPH initiatives. Focusing on systems-level policy change, AIDPH’s toolkit “Strengthening State Medicaid Advocacy for People with Disabilities,” developed in partnership with the Coalition of Texans with Disabilities, equips advocates and providers with a five-part workbook on navigating political landscapes, engaging decision-makers, data for policy change, non-traditional partnerships, and storytelling. Taking a place-based view of oral health disparities, a Tennessee-based pilot with Harmony Health integrated novel technologies across IDD care settings to assess oral health and hygiene. In this population, 99% were moderate/high caries risk and participants who brushed twice daily for two minutes had 86.5% lower odds of high-risk categorization. Real-time brushing data supported caregivers in reinforcing habits, while salivary biomarkers informed targeted prevention. Further analysis of population-level data highlighted structural disparities and a workforce confidence gap in treating patients with IDD. This roundtable will provide a suite of research, education, and advocacy action points for both national and place-based solutions to addressing the unique oral healthcare needs of people with IDD. Participants will leave with: (1) a menu of advocacy tactics to expand adult Medicaid dental benefits, (2) implementation lessons for low-burden digital prevention supports in IDD settings, and (3) practical steps to build provider confidence. Discussion will focus on adapting these models to attendees’ states and networks.Source of Funding: : CareQuest Institute for Oral Health and Harmony Health

title: School-Based Dental Services: Insights on Barriers, Billing, and Opportunities for Improvement
description: Description: Whether provided by a school nurse, dental provider, or school-based health clinic, school-based dental services remain a vital safety net for children, yet challenges in workforce capacity, reimbursement, and follow-up limit their full potential. Drawing on findings from a project with certified school nurses which included a survey on dental challenges in schools and resources needed, focus groups, and a data pilot project, this roundtable explores how schools in Pennsylvania provide dental services, barriers faced in ensuring students receive needed treatment, and the impact of unclear Medicaid billing pathways. Participants will gain a clearer understanding of challenges that affect service delivery and strategies to strengthen collaboration, streamline referrals, and support sustainable reimbursement practices. The session emphasizes actionable recommendations to enhance school-based dental services and reduce disparities among children.Source of Funding: : CareQuest Institute for Oral Health

title: Protecting Medicaid Dental Access: Federal Changes, Education Impacts, and State Responses
description: Description: The 2025 Budget Reconciliation Act (H.R. 1) introduced sweeping changes to Medicaid, with significant downstream effects anticipated for oral health and dental education. This roundtable session will explore how federal policy shifts—ranging from Medicaid eligibility and financing to student loan borrowing and repayment—may disrupt access to care and workforce training. Chelsea Fosse, the Vice President of the Research & Policy Center at the American Academy of Pediatric Dentistry (AAPD) will examine the implications for families, providers, and the dental workforce, while also discussing strategies with attendees to safeguard oral health. While AAPD’s mission is optimizing the oral health of all children, we recognize the importance of dental coverage and access for the entire family. Dr. Fosse will summarize key provisions of H.R. 1 (and other budget reconciliation bills since) that relate to dentistry. She will then engage attendees in a roundtable discussion to gather insights on state-level and practice-level experiences. Ultimately, this session aims to foster collaboration, share lessons learned, and generate actionable strategies to protect access to dentistry and oral health care in Medicaid.Source of Funding: : AAPD

title: Reducing Dental ED Visits Through Patient Feedback, Outreach, and Targeted Interventions
description: Description: Emergency departments (EDs) continue to serve as a default destination for dental-related issues, absorbing a disproportionate volume of visits while leaving root causes unaddressed. This roundtable discussion explores why patients skip traditional dental care and seek help in EDs, drawing from recent survey data of hundreds of health plan members with dental-related ED visits.Through real patient feedback, we’ll examine key barriers—navigation challenges, long wait times, cost concerns, transportation gaps, dental anxiety, and systemic mistrust. These insights will guide a collaborative dialogue on targeted interventions, including same-day dental referrals, Tele dentistry, subsidized transportation, and community-based outreach.Participants will brainstorm scalable strategies that can thrive even under funding constraints, with the shared goal of reducing repeat ED visits and redirecting care to timely, effective, and equitable dental services.Source of Funding: : Advantage Dental from DentaQuest
instructors: Shana M. Palmer-Whalen, Certified Community Health Worker

title: Operationalizing NHANES Periodontal Case Definition for Transparent and Reproducible Analysis
description: Description: Periodontal data analysis using the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES) has remained limited despite the public availability of large, high-quality datasets. One key barrier has been the lack of transparency and operational guidance on implementing the CDC and AAP case definitions for periodontitis. While these definitions are widely cited, few researchers have successfully applied them due to the absence of practical instructions on their analytic implementation. This session aimed to develop and disseminate a detailed, step-by-step manual that enables researchers to operationalize NHANES periodontal case definitions accurately, thereby promoting reproducibility, methodological transparency, and open science in oral health research. We conducted a comprehensive review of CDC/AAP case definitions and reconstructed the analytic workflow required to apply them to NHANES periodontal data. The manual successfully reproduces CDC-reported prevalence estimates for periodontitis using NHANES data, demonstrating both methodological fidelity and the robustness of the Chisquares platform for complex survey data analysis. In addition to serving dental researchers, the manual offers a generalizable framework for operationalizing case definitions in other large-scale, complex datasets. This session will address a long-standing gap in periodontal epidemiology by translating theoretical case definitions into a transparent, reproducible analytic workflow that strengthens confidence in secondary data analyses using NHANES and similar surveys. By demystifying the operational steps behind widely used case definitions, this work advances open science principles and empowers oral health researchers to produce valid, comparable estimates of periodontal disease burden—ultimately improving the quality of evidence that informs public health and policy.Source of Funding: : none
instructors: Israel T. Agaku, DMD, MPH, PhD

title: The Collaboration to Harmonize Antimicrobial Registry Measures for Dental Antibiotic Stewardship
description: Description: Tracking antimicrobial use in dental practices can be challenging owing to competing priorities, limited staff, and difficulty accessing data from the electronic health record (EHR) system. However, inappropriate antibiotic selection and durations of therapy can result in increased rates of resistance and compromise patient safety. The Collaboration to Harmonize Antimicrobial Registry Measures (CHARM) is a program supported by the Michigan and Illinois Departments of Public Health to assist prescribers with the tracking and assessment of antimicrobial use in their practices, aiming to improve antimicrobial prescribing. This session will explain how CHARM works with dental practices to extract data from the EHR to provide an interactive dashboard that allows for easy tracking of antimicrobial use. During the session, attendees will use a sample dashboard to examine prescribing patterns and identify areas for prescribing improvement. Examples will be provided to demonstrate how understanding antimicrobial prescribing practices have resulted in improvement in clinical practice. Attendees will also learn how to enroll in CHARM.Source of Funding: : Michigan and Illinois Departments of Public Health

title: Building State Oral Health Surveillance Systems by Partnering with Public Health Dental Hygienists
description: Description: “Healthy People 2030” establishes a national public health infrastructure objective of increasing the number of states that have an oral health surveillance system but the related enabling projects such as conducting Basic Screening Survey can be costly to implement. This roundtable reviews an innovative supplemental approach from Massachusetts that utilizes reporting from the state’s Public Health Dental Hygienist workforce paired with analysis from the Office of Oral Health at the Massachusetts Department of Public Health to produce ongoing statewide oral health outcomes data. Timely oral health outcomes data is critical to informing statewide oral health policymaking.Source of Funding: : none

title: Exhibits Open
Poster Session #2
Continental Breakfast
Registration
Closing Loops, Opening Access: A Public Health Approach to Teledentistry
description: Session Description: Teledentistry has emerged as a transformative tool in advancing oral health equity, particularly for underserved populations facing barriers to access. The emergence of Telehealth networks demonstrate how virtual-first dental networks can extend the reach of oral health services into communities where geographic, financial, or workforce limitations persist. From a public health perspective, teledentistry networks strengthen community-level prevention programs, integrate with medical and social services, and provide scalable models for meeting state and federal screening requirements. By combining a nationwide network of licensed dentists with dedicated care coordination teams to close referral loops, improve oral health literacy, and support continuity of care a robust teledentistry network expands your reach beyond the clinic. As states and health systems seek innovative strategies to reduce disparities, teledentistry offers a sustainable pathway to broaden access, enhance outcomes, and embed oral health more fully into public health infrastructure.Learning Objectives: Describe how virtual-first dental networks integrate with public health programs to support screenings, referrals, earlier access to care, and its role in expanding oral health services in underserved populations.Identify key public health benefits of teledentistry, including improved oral health literacy, reduced disparities, and timely access to in-person dental treatment.Examine strategies for collaboration between teledentistry networks, medical and dental providers, and community organizations to strengthen statewide and national oral health initiatives.Evaluate scalability and sustainability models for implementing teledentistry within public health infrastructure, including policy, reimbursement, and workforce considerations.
instructors:
Brant Herman
Carla M. Bassett, Registered Dental Hygienist

title: Enhancing Pediatric Dentistry Clinical and Didactic Population Health Training with a HRSA grant
description: Session Description: This presentation explores how HRSA grant funding has transformed a pediatric residency training program through innovative population health approaches. Attendees will learn about our school-based clinic model, which equips residents to deliver efficient, minimally invasive care to disadvantaged populations in real-world settings. Teledentistry expands access and trains residents in remote diagnostics and treatment planning. Patient simulations further enhance clinical readiness. Didactic training includes a unique MS-MPH track, interdisciplinary public health rotations, and consultant-led specialized education for treating patients with disabilities. The program also invests in the future dental workforce through high school outreach, high school internships, dental student externships, and national conferences. Resident treatment data and survey insights since 2021 illustrate the program’s reach and impact. This session offers a dynamic look at how clinical innovation, public health integration, and workforce development intersect to shape the next generation of pediatric dental professionals.Learning Objectives: Recognize how alternative care models, such as school-based clinics and teledentistry, reshape resident approaches to solving dental access challenges.Identify how simulation-based and interdisciplinary public health rotations provide relevant, patient-centered, clinical education.Describe specific techniques used in school-based settings, including minimally invasive care, behavior management without nitrous oxide analgesia, and efficient scheduling.Explain how the MS-MPH track introduces new knowledge in population health for dental residents.Evaluate how mentorship, outreach, and externship opportunities can influence dentists’ long-term habits and commitment to underserved populations.
instructors:
Amy Baer, RDH, B.Ed
Kim Hammersmith, DDS, MPH, MS
Amanda K. Swanson, DDS, MS, MPH

title: More Closely Aligning Medicaid Policy and Needs
description: Session Description: Dental Medicaid rates increased significantly in 2022 in an effort to increase dental provider participation in Medicaid. The increased rates were beneficial to existing Medicaid providers and caught the attention of a few others, but the barriers that prevented dental providers from accepting Medicaid went beyond the rates. Once Missouri started listening to their dental providers and dental teams, the Missouri Coalition for Oral Health, Missouri Dental Association, and MO HealthNet were able to work together to make changes that allowed Missouri Dental Providers to provide better care to participants on Medicaid. Recent Medicaid changes have allowed adults with Medicaid coverage to establish a dental home and receive the routine care they need to prevent costly restorations. Working directly with dental providers has allowed the Coalition to identify the true barriers and work with partners to make changes where they are needed most.Learning Objectives: Attendees will learn to identify barriers that are important to dental providers.Attendees will hear about the Missouri Model to increase access to care.Attendees will understand the importance of a clinical perspective in policy decision making.
instructors:
Jessica Emmerich
Amanda Fahrendorf, BS
Ashley Herrman, MPH, CHES

title: Break
Digital Consent Forms: Boosting Data Collection and Enabling Targeted Patient Care
description: Session Description: This presentation will demonstrate how implementing electronic consent (eConsent) forms in a mobile dental program can improve data collection, streamline workflows, and enhance outreach efforts. It will also share lessons learned from engaging families and increasing participation among underserved populations. St. David’s Foundation Dental Program introduced eConsent forms to better track and serve uninsured students. The team integrated eConsent into existing workflows, ensuring compatibility with patient management systems while maintaining data security and accessibility. Customized outreach strategies were developed to overcome barriers such as digital literacy, limited internet access, and language differences. The program used data analytics to monitor consent return rates and guide ongoing adjustments. Implementation of eConsent forms led to a 32% reduction in data gaps within one year. Real-time tracking enabled responsive outreach, helping the program approach its goal of a 30% consent return rate per school. Despite initial declines in enrollment during the transition, process refinements and targeted outreach helped stabilize participation. Key changes in workflows resulted in improved scheduling efficiency and reduced administrative burden. Transitioning to eConsent forms was not just a technical upgrade but a strategic shift toward more efficient, data-informed, and family-centered care delivery. By meeting families where they are and using flexible outreach, the program improved consent return rates, enhanced service delivery, and increased access to dental care for uninsured children. This model offers a replicable approach for other mobile health programs seeking to improve equity and operational effectiveness.Learning Objectives: Gain the ability to effectively use electronic forms to improve data collection, enhance clarity, streamline workflows, and improve reporting for informed decision-making.Explore effective outreach st
instructors:
Melissa B. Belt, MPH
Nancy Lozoria, RDA
Tania Pelagio, RDA

title: From Provider Participation to Network Adequacy: Building Collaborations to Improve Access to Care
description: Session Description: Medicaid dental programs play a critical role in improving oral health access, yet provider participation and beneficiary utilization remain inconsistent across states. This session will review the current Medicaid landscape, including federal and state policy dynamics, and emerging strategies to maintain sustainable programs. Panelists will share lessons learned from the ADA’s six-state Medicaid Pilot Project, including success in using statewide reforms and multi-sectoral collaboration to significantly increase provider enrollment and beneficiary utilization in an MCO-delivered dental benefit. Discussions will also explore challenges faced by other pilot states where reforms did not translate into sustainable participation, underscoring the need for strong data, financial sustainability planning, and innovative cross-sector partnerships. This session will provide national and state leaders with actionable strategies, evidence-based resources such as the ADA’s Financial Sustainability Toolkit, and practical insights from pilot projects to strengthen Medicaid dental programs and advance oral health equity.Learning Objectives: Understand how the current Medicaid landscape has evolved and strategies to protect and maintain Medicaid dental programs.Identify key elements of effective collaboration among dental providers, MCOs, and Medicaid leadership, and understand how statewide reforms enhance provider participation, network adequacy and beneficiary utilization within Medicaid dental programs.Develop possible strategies for states to achieve sustainable change through data, policy, innovation, and collaboration.
instructors:
Deesha Bhaumik, PhD
Ian Hedges, M.S.
Jessica A. Meeske, DDS, MS

title: Scaling Access in Unstable Times: The Role of Dental Therapists
description: Session Description: Over the past year, we have seen unprecedented health care cuts that have strained health, oral health, and public health systems. Despite this, dental therapy – a proven, evidence-based workforce model – is transforming how oral health services are delivered, particularly in underserved and rural communities. This session will explore how dental therapy can build a more resilient, equitable oral health system, even amid political and economic instability. Participants will learn how communities, states, and Tribes are leveraging dental therapists in innovative ways to address workforce shortages and increase access to preventive care. This session will additionally highlight advocacy strategies that have successfully advanced dental therapy in politically diverse environments. Designed for health advocates, policymakers, and oral health professionals, this session offers both practical insights and policy perspectives on dental therapy as an opportunity amidst uncertain times.Learning Objectives: Participants will analyze the current political state of play and impacts of Federal and state policies are having on oral health across the country.Participants will describe how dental therapy is uniquely bi-partisan and uniquely positioned for progress during politically tumultuous times.Participants will assess an array of advocacy strategies that work across a diverse set of political environments.

title: Indigenous Oral Health: Providing Culturally Appropriate and Effective Care
description: Session Description: This program will detail current oral health disparities faced by indigenous people, primarily summarizing the 2023 CareQuest Institute for Oral Health’s White Paper: American Indian and Alaskan Native (AI/AN) Communities Face a ‘Disproportionate Burden of Oral Disease’, written in collaboration with the Society of American Indian Dentists (SAID). After establishing an understanding of the current burden of disease and oral health concerns facing the AI/AN population, the program will allow participants to identify obstacles to care and cultural considerations (traditions, expectations, hesitancies, and values) for indigenous patients in public and private health care settings. The bulk of the presentation will provide tangible and useful knowledge, information, and strategies to improve AI/AN oral health outcomes and treatment plan acceptance in any office through a personal history of receiving dental care as an indigenous person, as well as lessons learned with experience serving indigenous patients in a tribal setting.Learning Objectives: Describe current oral health disparities affecting indigenous peoples in the United States.Identify current obstacles faced by indigenous peoples in accessing oral health care.Understand aspects of native culture – language, beliefs, and expectations - that affect the indigenous dental experience and how to provide culturally appropriate care in your office.Identify strategies to increase treatment plan acceptance among indigenous patients.Access resources to expand knowledge of indigenous issues, proposed solutions to improve oral health and access to care, and contribute to a more equitable future.
instructors:
Rachel Meek, DMD
Tamana Begay, DDS

title: All TMJ Patients are NUTS, Right? How to Evaluate, Diagnosis & Treat the Most Common TMJ Problems - Part 1
description: TMJ disorders are one of those areas where almost every dentist feels uncomfortable. In dental school we didn’t get much classroom information, and little or no clinical experience. Our treatments in practice are often hit and miss, without clarity on why sometimes we hit, and other times we miss. In this lecture Dr. Spencer will review the relevant anatomy and discuss simple examination, diagnosis and treatment techniques that will allow your attendees to STUN their next TMD patients with their understanding of the patient's issues, and allow them to help many more people.Learning Objectives: A review or the relevant anatomy of the head and neck.Understand basic principles of focused examination and evaluation for TMD.Have a straight forward way to diagnose the most common TM disorders.Understand the treatments for the most common TM disorders.

title: Becoming A Special Needs Warrior: Equipping Yourself to Treat Patients With Special Needs - Part 1
description: It doesn’t matter your experience or training; everyone has questions and fears when it comes to treating patients with special needs. Dr. Dent breaks down these common barriers for dental providers by equipping you with the knowledge and how-to in a fun and practical way that will build your confidence and empower you to be a special needs warrior in your practice.Learning Objectives: Define various forms of Special Needs and their common characteristics.Understand associated behaviors and dental concerns of each covered disability.Discuss effectiveness of behavior management techniques and behavior modification strategies.

title: Better Composite Restorations - Part 1
description: Until now, our composite went “in” the tooth. Now we can go “around” the tooth.
