Particular emphasis will be placed on the workﬂow that team members can support.Learning Objectives: Understand the current printing technology and choose the best option for your practice.Explore opportunities for 3D printing in general dentistry.Create a printing workﬂow that has a strong ROI.Utilize the skills of your team to perform digital tasks.

title: Odontogenic Sinusitis: What Dentists Need to Know
description: The pathological extension of dental infection into the maxillary sinuses is often overlooked in clinical medical and dental practice, with its sequelae often misdiagnosed as rhinogenic sinusitis. This lecture explains and illustrates the similarities, distinguishing differences, and frequent inter-relationship between odontogenic sinusitis and rhinogenic sinus infections. The associated clinical and radiographic findings of odontogenic sinusitis are explained using many real-world clinical case examples with an update on the current literature. Emphasis is placed on the importance of CBCT imaging, magnification, proper terminology, and clinical expertise for rendering both non-surgical and surgical endodontic treatment when indicated. The goal is to help practitioners distinguish rhinosinusitis from odontogenic sinusitis in an effort to improve the health of patients through accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment, and improved communication between general dentists, endodontists and ENT specialists.Learning Objectives: Describe the clinical symptoms and radiograph similarities and differences between rhinogenic sinusitis and the various forms of odontogenic sinusitis.Describe the progression and pathogenic effects of apical periodontitis on the sinus tissues and the associated clinical and radiographic findings using current terminology.Understand the unique non-surgical and surgical challenges in maxillary posterior teeth proximate to the maxillary sinus.Understand the importance of proper endodontic management and post-operative follow up of ODS and when to collaborate with an ENT physician.

title: Between the Threads: Uncovering Implant Therapy for the Dental Hygienist - Part 1
description: The American Academy of Implant Dentistry estimates about 3 million dental patients in the United States have dental implants with a projected 500,000 implants being placed each year. Join periodontal hygienist, speaker, author and consultant Katrina M Sanders RDH, BSDH, M.Ed, RF as she uncovers the modalities of complex care for the dental implant patient.This comprehensive program looks between the threads to better understand the imperative role of the dental hygienist in identifying potential dental implant candidates, evaluating systemic conditions affecting dental implant success, assessing stable and diseased periimplant sites, diagnosing Stage IV and periimplant diseased cases, maintaining single, multiple or all-on-four/all-on-six patient cases, and actively treating periimplant diseased patients. Let’s establish and maintain a stable foundation of dental implant protocols while threading through the research and uncovering new trends and techniques aimed to elevate comprehensive standards in the provision of dental implant care… together.Learning Objectives: Review the Process of Care as it relates to assessing, diagnosing, planning, implementing and evaluating the dental implant candidate.Describe the AAP standards regarding the diagnostics of periimplant disease, including therapeutic opportunities for a Stage IV patient.Identify appropriate preventive and therapeutic modalities as well as advanced periodontal procedures designed for dental implant patients.Discover futuristic trends in the provision of dental implant therapeutics.
instructors: Katrina M. Sanders, RDH, BSDH, M.Ed, RF

title: Beyond the Hype: Practical AI for Oral Heath
description: Since 2022, generative artificial intelligence (AI) has rapidly transformed the technological landscape, becoming a focal point of innovation across industries. As AI becomes increasingly integrated into oral healthcare, providers must develop an understanding of its fundamentals, capabilities, and limitations. This session will explore the evolution of AI, its applications in oral healthcare and beyond, and immediate, practical use cases for private practice.Learning Objectives: Define AI and AI adjacent terms.Analyze current AI use in oral healthcare.Identify opportunities to further integrate AI into oral healthcare.

title: Dental Service Organizations & Their Impact on Dentistry in Washington State
description: This presentation will address state and federal laws and regulations governing dental service organizations (“DSO”) arrangement as well as practical considerations for dentists and dental practices considering entering into DSO arrangements, including pros and cons of DSO arrangements and contracting and negotiating tips when entering into DSO arrangements.Learning Objectives: Legal and regulatory considerations related to DSO arrangements.Pros and cons of DSO arrangements.Tips for contracting and negotiating with DSOs.

title: Digital Dentures: Scanning, Milling & Printing
description: Since the introduction of digital dentures, many aspects have improved. Such improvements include streamlined workflows, easy-to-use software, and new and improved manufacturing techniques. This presentation will demonstrate the clinical workflows and use of intraoral scanning, milling and 3D printing in the fabrication of dentures.Learning Objectives: Review diagnostic principles for edentulous patients.Demonstrate clinical workflows for digital dentures.Discuss how to utilize intraoral scanners to streamline your digital denture workflow.

title: Geriatric Medicine: What’s Old is Now New - Part 1
description: We are living longer! The fastest growing segment of the population are seniors. The dental team must recognize which medical conditions may influence your dental treatments, and conversely; how your dental treatments may affect your patient’s medical status. This lecture will highlight the changing physiology of the elderly and how it impacts your dental care. Discussions include the aging pulmonary, cardiac and neurologic systems, as well as the plethora of prescriptions, over-the-counter medications and herbal supplements consumed by seniors, including their effect upon the oral cavity. With the established link between the mouth and the body, this course is must for the entire dental team!Learning Objectives: Understand the medical changes that occur from natural aging versus the disease process.Understand the plethora of medications and herbal supplements that our patients consume and their effect on our dental treatment.Demystify conditions such as Alzheimer's and Parkinson's Disease.

title: Increase Dental Hygiene Production by Improving CDT Procedure Code Selection
description: Accurate, evidence-based diagnosis is the best method to determine appropriate, medically necessary dental treatment. Once this diagnosis is determined, the clinician will develop a treatment plan. This includes selecting the correct CDT procedure codes for the treatment. Too often, the procedure code selected may be inaccurate because dental software programs ONLY include the code number and title, NOT the descriptor.Many clinicians are unaware of the ADA’s Current Dental Terminology (CDT) descriptors for each procedure code which must be used by the business staff to correctly bill patients and their insurance carriers. Inaccurate billing can not only cause the practice to undercharge and lose production but can also result in patients having to pay more out-of-pocket dollars then they were expecting to pay, which then can lead to mistrust. This can further lead to patients refusing to have ANY treatment completed because they cannot believe the “estimate” which they were given.As technology and treatment modalities evolve, new CDT procedure codes are developed, and current CDT procedure codes are amended by the ADA’s Code Maintenance Committee (which meets every March). Over the past 5 years, there has been a significant increase in procedure codes specific to preventive products used and recommended by hygienists. This course will provide the latest nomenclature and descriptors for procedures which can be performed by dental hygienists. In Washington state, there are over 100 procedures/codes from which hygienists can document. DO you know what they are?And what about the ICD-10 Diagnostic Codes for dental and dental hygiene procedures (over 700) listed in the current CDT Procedure Codes Manual? How and why do we need to know these? So many questions!Learning Objectives: Describe the specific elements to be completed for the CDT Procedure Codes for Evaluations, Preventive Services and Periodontal Therapy, procedures provided by dental hygienists.Discuss the basi
instructors: Kathy S. Forbes, RDH, BS, FADHA

title: Let’s Start with the Temporomandibular Joint & Finish with a Big Smile - Part 1
description: TMD, splint therapy, occlusion, and case treatment planning to final esthetics. Let’s make it simple and bring it all together by learning more about the Temporomandibular Joint and how we use the information to create smiles. Understanding the Temporomandibular Joint and related muscles can impact final restorative outcomes. Starting from the TMJ evaluation to the final design of the smile can restore form, form, and esthetics through knowledge in appliance therapy, occlusion, and equilibration.Learning Objectives: Review of cases from treatment of the TMD to final smile.Review of principles of Joint anatomy, Muscles, and occlusion.Discuss how Splint therapy can treat TMD and aid in treatment position.Discuss posterior and anterior determinants of occlusion in a comprehensive case review.How to communication and collaborate with your team and patients in this process.

title: Minimally Invasive Pediatric Dentistry: Caries Disease Management Models - Part 1
description: The dental profession has typically viewed dentistry as traditional surgical treatment of the disease versus managing the chronic nature of the disease process surrounding dental caries. Dentistry is currently moving away from surgical approaches for treatment of caries as new innovative caries disease management techniques provide dentists with options in offering non-surgical modes of therapy for their patients. Dentists have the obligation to provide the risks, benefits and alternatives for both surgical and non-surgical therapies in managing dental caries. Participants will be able to identify appropriate candidates for minimally invasive dentistry by reviewing the following techniques: Silver Diamine Fluoride (SDF), Silver-Modified Atraumatic Restorative Technique (SMART), Interim Therapeutic Restoration (ITR), Papacarie, and Hall Crown Technique.Learning Objectives: Understand the supporting literature for non-invasive/minimally-invasive dental therapies for pediatric patients and those with special healthcare needs.Identify appropriate candidates for pediatric minimally invasive dentistry.Understand and apply minimally invasive techniques: Silver Diamine Fluoride, Silver-Modified Atraumatic Restorative Technique, Interim Therapeutic Restoration, Papacarie, and the Hall Technique.
instructors: Jacy Stauffer, DMD, FAAPD, AADSM-Qualified Dentist

title: Navigating the Insurance Maze Like a Pro! - Part 1
description: Love it or hate it, insurance is part of dentistry. Whether you are in network or out, discover these shortcuts to make navigating insurance a dream instead of a nightmare including: Miscoded! Coding updates (CDT codes) and how to maximize reimbursement. Cut claim submission time in half. Strategies for claim submissions, narratives and rejections. Disallowed vs. Denied - what's the difference and what to do about it. Maximize revenue Now! underutilized CDT codes.Learning Objectives: Manage time efficiently by submitting claims that do not require follow up. Only handle it once.Explain the difference between insurance disallowed and insurance denied. When can we bill the patient and when are we required to write off.Assess underutilized CDT codes in their practice.
instructors: Pamela Peterson, President, CEO Total Patient Service Inst.

title: Oral Surgery for the General Practitioner: How to Make it Flow Better in Your Clinic - Part 1
description: This presentation is to discuss and demonstrate oral surgical procedures that can be performed by the GP safely in office with ease and confidence. I will discuss my own journey in this discipline and go over tips, tricks and mentorship I have gained in my 20+ year career as a wet-fingered GP. We will be using a combination of cases with photos, videos, CBCT, radiographs and history and physical exam data to learn how to extract teeth, raise flaps, place implants and grafts, suture well, draw blood for PRF, interpret blood tests, perform biopsies, expose teeth for orthodontics and do frenectomies.Learning Objectives: How to extract teeth, both erupted and impacted with confidence.How to venipuncture a patient to draw blood to make PRF in your office.How to use blood tests to augment the surgical outcomes for your patients.How to perform various surgical procedures in office like frenectomies, biopsy and ortho exposures.How to deal with complications and post-op infections and manage your surgical patients.
instructors: Waji Khan, BSc, DDS, MBA, MEd

title: Platelet Rich Fibrin: The Ideal Way to Maximize Grafting Results & Wound Healing
description: The world of biologics in oral surgery have exploded since the 1990’s. Concurrently so has the world of natural medicine. Where these two worlds meet, autologous platelet products offer a natural boost to the body’s natural healing capacity. Surgery relies on tried and tested protocols, and natural biologics have a wealth of literature support as well as clinical applications. From improved healing to enhanced materials handling, there is no reason not to incorporate platelet products as we care constantly seeking for ways to increase our odds of success. We will cover the biology, literature support, and different uses of PRP (platelet rich plasma) and PRF (platelet rich fibrin) as an adjunct to the concepts covered in the first lecture.Learning Objectives: Centrifuge concepts.Review the history of platelet aggregates.Discuss the biology of platelet aggregates.Common uses of PRF.Demonstrate the process of creating platelet products.Clinical case examples.

title: Practice, Financial & Tax Strategies for Newer Dentists - Part 1
description: This lecture will cover practice, financial, and tax strategies for associates and newer practice owners who are intent on planning successful careers and successful practices.Learning Objectives: Understand best practices surrounding managing finances and debts prior to and early practice ownership.Address the basics of financial planning objectives for young dentists, from basic documents to timing for buying a house and practice.Understand practice valuation, operating overhead, profit, and due diligence in purchasing a practice.Understand the importance of tax management and tax planning for your practice and personally.

title: The Changing Epidemiology of Oral Cavity Cancer: An Update
description: This presentation will discuss the changing epidemiology of oral cavity cancer, with reference to the emerging epidemic of oral tongue cancer in young adults. It will highlight novel and emerging risk factors including poor oral hygiene, microbiome, sugar-sweetened beverages, and diet. In addition, a discussion regarding differing risk profiles and risk modification between different ages, races, and sexes.Learning Objectives: Describe the changing epidemiology of oral cavity cancer in the US and globally.Discuss recurrence and survival in early-onset and average-onset oral cavity cancer.Discuss emerging etiopathologies for early-onset and average-onset oral cavity cancer.Differentiate risk profiles for oral cavity cancer between different ages, races, and sexes.Identify challenges in treatment of oral cavity cancer in young adults and barriers to further investigation.

title: The Questions You Hate & The Answers You’ll Love: A Guide For Dealing With Difficult Patients - Part 1
description: “OH NO! Not that one again!” After all your training, and years of hard work and tremendous sacrifice, don’t you just hate it when you get those heart wrenching questions and resistance to case acceptance over and over again? Your team and you work so hard, and care so deeply, so why is it that often our patients don’t ask for optimal care and enthusiastically accept our treatment plans? Get ready to laugh, learn, and get fired up for a dramatic improvement of your case acceptance and your enjoyment of your practice!Learning Objectives: Learn the power of high impact new patient experience.Review ten questions you must ask of each new patient.Commit to obtaining the necessary evidence for predictable case presentations.Roleplay with your team the most common questions you absolutely HATE to hear, and learn the most predictable answers you’ll LOVE to give them.

title: The Simple Approach to Occlusal Stability
description: The concepts of occlusion have most dentists confused. However, they really are simple. We just have to apply them appropriately for each patient. The most frequent questions posed by dentists include: Which teeth need to touch for stability? What bite (CR or IP) is appropriate for this patient? How do I know when to equilibrate? When is wear on teeth a problem? What do I do with the uneven edges of teeth? What do I do if the teeth are moving during function? This presentation will give you knowledge to easily plan and restore the “everyday” patients that are a large part of your practice. What’s more, you will be able to diagnosis and plan treatment with more ease and conﬁdence for those “troubling” cases that sit on your lab bench top.Learning Objectives: Learn the contributing factors to occlusal disease.When equilibration is important and when it is not.The essentials of diagnosis and treatment planning for achieving occlusal stability.When intercuspal position (IP) is the reasonable restorative position.

title: Understanding & Managing Dental Fear & Anxiety
description: Dental care-related fear and anxiety is common, and it can be a major challenge for patients and the dental team. This session will introduce attendees to key background information and foundational concepts necessary for understanding and addressing fears and anxieties about dental treatment. The session will also cover approaches for assessing and managing this fear and anxiety.Learning Objectives: Describe the epidemiology, etiology, and consequences of dental care-related fear and anxiety as well as the key conceptual considerations relevant to its management.Identify and apply evidence-based approaches for assessing dental care-related fear and anxiety to inform treatment planning.Identify and apply basic and advanced evidence-based approaches for managing dental care-related fear and anxiety.

title: What’s New? Materials & Tech that Expedite Excellence: Digital & 3D Printing
description: With a constant stream of new products that claim to be bigger, better, faster flooding the industry, it is challenging to know which are effective and efficient…and which are just hype.In this fast-moving market, advertising makes every product appear to be the panacea. However, some of the best products and services don’t enter the market with much fanfare. Dental professionals can become bogged down or confused when trying to decipher which new materials and technologies are right for their practices.As editorial director with Dental Economics magazine, Dr. Joshua Austin authors the column Pearls for Your Practice and has personally worked with most new products and technologies that have entered the market. In this interactive, fast-moving course, Joshua shares the current trends in dental materials and technologies. Learn how materials have changed over the past few years and how those changes can affect your procedures and practice.This session will focus on intraoral scanning and 3D printing.Learning Objectives: Explore the latest innovations and enhancements in intraoral scanning.Learn how the improved scanning amplifies practice productivity.Discover how 3D printing fits in dental practices in 2026.Discover how these devices can close the gap of patient communication.Discuss when luting is an appropriate choice instead of bonding.Explore many other products and items that can make your life easier and your dentistry faster.

title: "Hurts So...Swell!" Dental Pain Management For the Entire Dental Team - Part 1
description: Qualifies for MATE requirement hours.Management of acute odontogenic pain is accomplished through a clinical approach that successfully incorporates both opioid and non-opioid analgesics. However, moderate to severe dental pain often occurs well after office hours and away from our immediate supervision. Thus, it is critical for clinicians to be able to accurately identify a patient's need for pain control, develop an individualized pain management plan and assess the efficacy of analgesic agents employed.This program will provide strategies for effectively managing dental pain with opioid and non-opioid analgesics and analgesic adjuvants, while also mitigating associated risks. Special emphasis will be given to appropriate practices for prescribing analgesics as well as identification and treatment of opioid analgesic abuse.Learning Objectives: Understand the pharmacology and mechanism of action of opioid and non-opioid analgesics, as well as their potential for abuse.Discuss best practices for prescribing opioid analgesics as part of an individualized patient pain management plan.Describe techniques useful in recognizing and identifying potential addiction as well as avoiding potential drug diversion.Learn current protocols for the management and treatment of opioid addiction.Examine guidelines for proper storage and disposal of unused dosages.
instructors: Thomas Viola, RPh, CCP, CDE, CPMP

title: Production as a Mirror: Leadership, Strategy & Smart AI Adoption in Dentistry’s Next Era
description: In dentistry, production isn’t just a number, it’s a reflection of your culture, leadership, and the health of your team. In this session, Dr. Thomas Kang will share the journey of building Puget Sound Periodontics from a single location, three operatory denovo in 2006 into a thriving, specialty practice by 2025. Attendees will learn how to identify the right daily and weekly metrics to guide decision-making, why allocating intentional time for leadership and strategic planning is critical, and how to create a culture where growth follows clarity and accountability. Dr. Kang will also explore how emerging AI tools and intelligent agents are reshaping practice operations from documentation to patient experience and how dentists can harness these technologies to stay agile, profitable, and independent in a rapidly changing landscape.Learning Objectives: Learn how to allocate your time effectively for both production and leadership.Understand the metrics you should be tracking to measure your success.Know what AI implementation you can apply (next day) to help your office efficiency.

title: Basic Life Support Training for Healthcare Providers | Hands-On Workshop ($95)
description: Attend this hands-on workshop to learn how to perform CPR and airway management and be better equipped to handle emergencies in your office and at home. Participants will receive their CPR certification course completion card (cards expire every two years).Learning Objectives: Be able to perform CPR and airway management.Be able to handle emergencies at work and home.Receive a CPR certification course completion card.

title: Extraction & Guided Bone Regeneration | Hands-On Workshop ($395)
description: This course is a combination of instruction, demonstration, and hands-on (pig-jaw) workshop. Participants will learn basic principles of bone augmentation surgery (in conjunction with extractions) for private practice. Indications, considerations, and surgical principles will be discussed.Supplies to be supplied by participants: Loupes (optional).Learning Objectives: Learn principles of patient evaluation, clinical, and diagnostic considerations for ridge augmentation of extraction socket defects.Be familiar with relevant surgical and anatomic principles for ridge preservation surgery.Practice and develop hands-on skills to gain experience in performing ridge preservation flap surgery associated with an extraction site.

title: Modern Monolithic Injection Molded Composite Dentistry: Concepts, Materials, Instruments, Techniques | Hands-On Workshop ($495)
description: Additive dentistry is quite simply fun to do! Say goodbye to layering, condensing, and burnishing! Join Dr. Clark to experience a unique approach to modern resin dentistry. Participants will perform injection molding to create ideal margins, rounded emergence profiles, and mirror smooth restorations in two Anterior exercises, Deep Caries and Black Triangles and two Class II exercises.Supplies to be supplied by participants: Loupes and headlights if participants use them.Learning Objectives: The 6 steps to achieve rock solid posterior composite contacts in even the most difficult cases.How can you best combine flowable and regular composites with the injection molding technique, and when to use bulkfills.Treating the dreaded black triangle, especially common after tray aligner therapy.How to handle heated composites.

title: Practice Transition Planning Session | Hands-On Workshop
description: Transitioning out of practice ownership is one of the most important and complex business decisions a dentist will make. This hands-on workshop is designed to prepare you for a successful transition.A key challenge in any transition is understanding and evaluating the financials numbers. Practice valuation results may differ from expectations. This workshop will show how to interpret financial statements and provide tips for improvement. You’ll gain insight into the entire transition process—from maximizing your practice value, to the legal considerations you need to address. This deep dive includes the operational and personal considerations involved in selling your practice and moving toward retirement.By the end of this session, you’ll have the tools and confidence to make informed, strategic decisions about your practice transition—ensuring both financial success and peace of mind for your next chapter while avoiding costly mistakes that can delay or derail your transition.Supplies to be supplied by participants: 2025 Profit & Loss Statements.Learning Objectives: How your practice will be valued and what valuation methods are used.Gain a clear understanding of EBITDA and its impact on practice value.How to identify the right buyer for your practice.Evaluate staffing costs to determine the right balance between overhead and productivity.Learn how to read production reports and identify underlying causes of inefficiency or mismanagement.Identify the relationship between a practice’s financial health, growth potential, and overall transition readiness.How to sell your practice for the maximum price.Will you have enough money to retire when you sell and when can you retire?How to identify the right buyer for your practice.What is the truth behind unrepresented offers.
instructors:
Rodney Johnston, MBA, CMA
Frank Sciabica, DDS
Jen Bennett

title: The Complete Face: Botox Live-Patient Training Certification Course ($1,600)
description: If you're looking for a Botox course that actually teaches the whole face—not just a fraction of it—The Complete Face is that course. This program is the only training that combines upper and lower facial cosmetic treatment and therapeutic Botox for TMJ in one comprehensive seminar. No multiple “levels.” No return trips to learn the rest. One course. Thorough and practical.This is anatomy-first, evidence-based education—not a commercial demonstration course. You’ll learn to evaluate, plan, dose, and inject with clarity and intention so you can immediately offer meaningful, safe, and high-value tox treatments in your practice.To enroll, dentist candidates must supply:Valid dental licenseMalpractice insurance declarations page1. Dentists provide their own patient models for the afternoon hands-on session. 2. All dentists who wish to inject patients at the event must demonstrate proof of holding a valid Washington State dental license or dental license in another state (Under state law -RCW 18.32.030, dentists from out-of-state are allowed to participate in continuing education events sanctioned by WSDA ) 3. All dentists must provide proof of current malpractice insurance in the form of a declarations page.Supplies to bring for afternoon session:Your own patient modelCamera (preferably not phone)Loupes/readers (optional)Learning Objectives: Identify specific areas of the aging face and features that can be improved with tox treatment.Discuss indications for therapeutic treatment with botulinum toxins in the head and neck.Conduct a thorough patient consultation to evaluate the potential need for and assess the suitability of facial injectables.Avoid, recognize and manage adverse events.Demonstrate accurate photo-documentation and written documentation.

title: CBCT in Action: Software Navigation, 3D Anatomy, Clinical Applications & Pathology Diagnosis | Hands-On Workshop ($495)
description: This immersive, hands-on workshop takes CBCT interpretation from theory to practice. Participants will learn to navigate advanced imaging software step by step, explore 3D head and neck anatomy in real clinical datasets, and apply systematic viewing protocols to identify pathology with precision and confidence. Guided by real-world case examples, the course emphasizes diagnostic workflow, image enhancement tools, and practical applications in implant planning, endodontics, and pathology detection equipping clinicians with the skills to translate CBCT findings into confident, evidence-based treatment decisions.Learning Objectives: Become familiar with CBCT imaging software tools and navigation.Locate vital anatomical structures related to the area of implant planning and how to utilize CBCT in planning stage to prevent nerve injury.Be able to discover the etiology of dental pain associated with endodontic and non endodontic related pathology (review of cases).Identify incidental findings that have major clinical significance.
instructors: Setarah Lavasani, DDS, MS, FICD, FGDIA

title: Smile Session: Next Level Practice Ownership: The Secrets to Scaling Your Practice
description: Please note: PNDC doesn't not award CE for Smile Session courses. CE may be obtained directly from the speaker onsite at the session. Ready to shift gears from just surviving and start dominating your dental practice? This high-energy, lecture-based continuing education course delivers the exact playbook you need to transform your practice into a profitable, stress-reduced powerhouse. This presentation is designed specifically for dentists who are serious about stepping fully into a Chief Executive Officer role and take complete control of their future. Built around the three proven pillars of elite practice success; Leadership, Business Mastery, and Bulletproof Systems. This course gives you immediately implementable strategies that drive real results. Clinical excellence alone no longer guarantees success. In today’s competitive market, the dentists who win are the ones who lead confidently, understand their numbers, and operate with world-class systems. This course hands you the proven roadmap. No fluff, no theory... but battle-tested tools used by top 1% practices across the country.

title: Headache Disorders in the Dental Chair: What Every Dentist Should Know
description:
Dentists are uniquely positioned to be the first point of contact for patients experiencing head and facial pain, since they often see patients regularly for preventive and routine care. Headache is among the most common health complaints, yet it is frequently misunderstood in the dental setting. While many patients and dentists attribute head pain to “TMJ,” not all headaches are related to temporomandibular disorders, and misdiagnosis can lead to unnecessary treatment, frustrated patients, and delayed medical care. In this session, Dr. Hemamalini Chandrashekhar, will provide practical insights into headache disorders most relevant to dental practice. Attendees will learn how to recognize and differentiate common headache types such as migraine and tension-type headache, as well as less common but clinically significant conditions such as trigeminal autonomic cephalalgias that may contribute to recurrent orofacial and dental pain. The course will also review red flag symptoms requiring referral, illustrated through case-based examples. This session emphasizes the role of the dentist and dental team in early recognition, patient education, and interdisciplinary collaboration. By the end, participants will have a clear framework to identify headache disorders and contribute to comprehensive, patient-centered care, within their scope of practice.Learning Objectives: Identify the most common headache disorders in the dental setting.Differentiate headache pain from temporomandibular disorders (TMD) and dental pain using history, symptom
patterns, and chairside screening tools.Recognize red flag symptoms and clinical presentations that require prompt referral to medical providers.
instructors: Hemamalini Chandrashekhar, BDS, MDS (OMFS), MDSc, DABOP, DABDSM

title: Between the Threads: Uncovering Implant Therapy for the Dental Hygienist - Part 2
description: The American Academy of Implant Dentistry estimates about 3 million dental patients in the United States have dental implants with a projected 500,000 implants being placed each year. Join periodontal hygienist, speaker, author and consultant Katrina M Sanders RDH, BSDH, M.Ed, RF as she uncovers the modalities of complex care for the dental implant patient.This comprehensive program looks between the threads to better understand the imperative role of the dental hygienist in identifying potential dental implant candidates, evaluating systemic conditions affecting dental implant success, assessing stable and diseased periimplant sites, diagnosing Stage IV and periimplant diseased cases, maintaining single, multiple or all-on-four/all-on-six patient cases, and actively treating periimplant diseased patients. Let’s establish and maintain a stable foundation of dental implant protocols while threading through the research and uncovering new trends and techniques aimed to elevate comprehensive standards in the provision of dental implant care… together.Learning Objectives: Review the process of care as it relates to assessing, diagnosing, planning, implementing and evaluating the dental implant candidate.Describe the AAP standards regarding the diagnostics of periimplant disease, including therapeutic opportunities for a Stage IV patient.Identify appropriate preventive and therapeutic modalities as well as advanced periodontal procedures designed for dental implant patients.Discover futuristic trends in the provision of dental implant therapeutics.
instructors: Katrina M. Sanders, RDH, BSDH, M.Ed, RF

title: CDT Coding Demystified: Answers to Common CDT Procedure Code Questions
description: This interactive course will provide dental professionals with the opportunity to not only discuss common questions related to dental hygiene CDT procedure code selection but hear answers as well. (And there may be even more than one answer!) Participants will examine how accurate, evidence-based diagnoses are crucial in determining medically necessary treatment. In addition, common questions related to dental hygiene treatment and CDT code selection for insurance alignment will be addressed, questions such as "How do I handle it when my office manager asks me to change a procedure (D4346) to D1110 because the patient's insurance doesn't cover D4346?"Many practices are unaware of the changing landscape of the CDT procedure codes and their descriptors. And what about diagnostic codes? Since 2020, over 700 ICD-10-CM diagnostic codes have been added to the CDT Manual, yet many practices are unaware of their significance. And now, some carriers are asking for these diagnostic codes on claim forms. Participants will be provided a basic understanding of ICD-10-CM fundamentals and how applying the correct codes can improve reimbursements.Learning Objectives: Discuss solutions to common questions related to dental hygiene treatment and the CDT procedure codes selected to support it.Describe how a dental hygienist can use procedures codes/report to identify his/her value to the dental practice.Discover the importance of using appropriate CDT procedure codes.
instructors: Kathy S. Forbes, RDH, BS, FADHA

title: Diagnosis & Treatment Planning in Today’s Dental Practice
description: Dentists and team members strive for simple and eﬀective strategies for diagnosis and treatment planning with their patients. However current challenges include the patient’s lack of awareness of their current health status and dependence on dental insurance.This course will review the esthetic, functional, structural and biological components of thorough hygiene and doctor exams with particular emphasis on clear patient communication and shared decision making, making “YES” a part of your patient response.Learning Objectives: Understand the essentials of diagnosis and treatment planning for achieving occlusal stability.Master the components of the collaborative oral health examination and discovery process.Engage your team to help facilitate the patient’s engagement during each and every visit to your practice.

title: From Classroom to Clinic: How AI is Reshaping Oral Health Education
description: Artificial Intelligence (AI) has been widely regarded as one of the most significant modern educational disruptors. While public attention has focused on generative AI, other powerful tools like computer vision and machine learning are fundamentally changing clinical education. This session will explore a dental school’s experience using an AI-powered clinical decision support (CDS) tool in a comprehensive clinical care model to improve the identification of common oral pathologies, optimize the instructional experience, and impact educational outcomes. Attendees will gain insights into the practical application of these tools, potential challenges, and benefits, ultimately aiming to elevate the quality of clinical education and patient care.Learning Objectives: Describe the fundamentals of artificial intelligence and its relevance to oral health education.Explore the capabilities of clinical decision support tools in an educational setting.Apply strategies for leveraging AI technologies to enhance student learning and clinical outcomes.

title: Geriatric Medicine: What’s Old is Now New - Part 2
description: We are living longer! The fastest growing segment of the population are seniors. The dental team must recognize which medical conditions may influence your dental treatments, and conversely; how your dental treatments may affect your patient’s medical status. This lecture will highlight the changing physiology of the elderly and how it impacts your dental care. Discussions include the aging pulmonary, cardiac and neurologic systems, as well as the plethora of prescriptions, over-the-counter medications and herbal supplements consumed by seniors, including their effect upon the oral cavity. With the established link between the mouth and the body, this course is must for the entire dental team!Learning Objectives: Understand the medical changes that occur from natural aging versus the disease process.Understand the plethora of medications and herbal supplements that our patients consume and their effect on our dental treatment.Demystify Alzheimer's and Parkinson's Disease.

title: Interdisciplinary Topics: Functional & Esthetic Crown Lengthening through Perio & Restorative Teamwork
description: Patient needs often entail teamwork between the restorative team and corresponding specialty teams. As the industry has sobered from titanium excitement there is a resurgence of a desire to save teeth instead of extracting. This lecture will focus on the science and strategies of crown lengthening in the posterior and in the anterior regions.Learning Objectives: Review the biology of the supra crestal attached tissue.Explore literature regarding submarginal restorations.Clinical decision making strategies.Interdisciplinary communication and sequences.Surgical approaches for functional and esthetic crown lengthening.Healing processes.Case presentations.

title: Let’s Start with the Temporomandibular Joint & Finish with a Big Smile - Part 2
description: TMD, splint therapy, occlusion, and case treatment planning to final esthetics. Let’s make it simple and bring it all together by learning more about the Temporomandibular Joint and how we use the information to create smiles. Understanding the Temporomandibular Joint and related muscles can impact final restorative outcomes. Starting from the TMJ evaluation to the final design of the smile can restore form, form, and esthetics through knowledge in appliance therapy, occlusion, and equilibration.Learning Objectives: Review of cases from treatment of the TMD to final smile.Review of principles of Joint anatomy, Muscles, and occlusion.Discuss how Splint therapy can treat TMD and aid in treatment position.Discuss posterior and anterior determinants of occlusion in a comprehensive case review.How to communication and collaborate with your team and patients in this process.

title: Mental Health & Oral Health: Considerations & Management Approaches for Dentistry
description: Mental health and oral health are connected. With increasing awareness of the importance of mental health for good oral health outcomes—and an increasing focus on comprehensive, patient-centered care—the dental team must be equipped to understand and address mental health in a sensitive and effective manner. This session will introduce attendees to key background and practical approaches for appreciating and managing patient mental health as relevant to dental care.Learning Objectives: Understand the complex associations between mental health and oral health.Describe common mental health conditions that are relevant to dental care and oral health outcomes.Identify and apply evidence-based, patient-centered approaches for recognizing and managing clinically relevant mental health problems.

title: Minimally Invasive Pediatric Dentistry: Caries Disease Management Models - Part 2
description:
The dental profession has typically viewed dentistry as traditional surgical treatment of the disease versus managing the chronic nature of the disease process surrounding dental caries. Dentistry is currently moving away from surgical approaches for treatment of caries as new innovative caries disease management techniques provide dentists with options in offering non-surgical modes of therapy for their patients. Dentists have the obligation to provide the risks, benefits and alternatives for both surgical and non-surgical therapies in managing dental caries. Participants will be able to identify appropriate candidates for minimally invasive dentistry by reviewing the following techniques: Band Stabilization, Curodont, Resin Infiltration, and Etch-Bleach-Seal. Comprehensive cases will be discussed to apply the various techniques learned from Parts 1 and 2.Learning Objectives: Understand the supporting literature for non-invasive/minimally-invasive dental therapies.Understand and apply minimally invasive techniques: Band Stabilization, Curodont, Resin Infiltration, and Etch-Bleach-Seal.
.Apply the various minimally invasive techniques learned in Parts 1 and 2 with comprehensive cases.
instructors: Jacy Stauffer, DMD, FAAPD, AADSM-Qualified Dentist

title: Navigating the Insurance Maze Like a Pro! - Part 2
description: Love it or hate it, insurance is part of dentistry. Whether you are in network or out, discover these shortcuts to make navigating insurance a dream instead of a nightmare including: Miscoded! Coding updates (CDT codes) and how to maximize reimbursement. Cut claim submission time in half. Strategies for claim submissions, narratives and rejections. Disallowed vs. Denied - what's the difference and what to do about it. Maximize revenue Now! underutilized CDT codes.Learning Objectives: Manage time efficiently by submitting claims that do not require follow up. Only handle it once.Explain the difference between insurance disallowed and insurance denied. When can we bill the patient and when are we required to write off.Assess underutilized CDT codes in their practice.
instructors: Pamela Peterson, President, CEO Total Patient Service Inst.

title: Oral Cavity Cancer Reconstruction from A to Z
description: This presentation will discuss the surgical management of oral cavity cancers and reconstructive methods. It will highlight the evolution of healing following oral cavity cancer treatment.Learning Objectives: Review oral cavity cancer ablative surgical procedures. Discuss relevant oral cavity cancer reconstructive methods.Illustrate the evolution of reconstructed wounds throughout the head and neck cancer treatment journey.

title: Oral Surgery for the General Practitioner: How to Make it Flow Better in Your Clinic - Part 2
description: This presentation is to discuss and demonstrate oral surgical procedures that can be performed by the GP safely in office with ease and confidence. I will discuss my own journey in this discipline and go over tips, tricks and mentorship I have gained in my 20+ year career as a wet-fingered GP. We will be using a combination of cases with photos, videos, CBCT, radiographs and history and physical exam data to learn how to extract teeth, raise flaps, place implants and grafts, suture well, draw blood for PRF, interpret blood tests, perform biopsies, expose teeth for orthodontics and do frenectomies.Learning Objectives: How to extract teeth, both erupted and impacted with confidence.How to venipuncture a patient to draw blood to make PRF in your office.How to use blood tests to augment the surgical outcomes for your patients.How to perform various surgical procedures in office like frenectomies, biopsy and ortho exposures.How to deal with complications and post-op infections and manage your surgical patients.
instructors: Waji Khan, BSc, DDS, MBA, MEd

title: Practice, Financial & Tax Strategies for Newer Dentists - Part 2
description: This lecture will cover practice, financial, and tax strategies for associates and newer practice owners who are intent on planning successful careers and successful practices.Learning Objectives: Understand best practices surrounding managing finances and debts prior to practice ownership.Address the basics of financial planning objectives for young dentists, from basic documents to timing for buying a house and practice.Understand practice valuation, operating overhead, profit, and due diligence in purchasing a practice.Understand the importance of tax management and tax planning for your practice and personally.

title: The Art & Science of Digital Implant Overdentures
description: Treating edentulous patients can be an overwhelming process with patients lacking the retention and confidence they desire. A review of the key diagnostic principles will be reviewed to help determine if a patient will be a good denture patient and how implant overdentures can help improve their quality of life. This lecture will discuss the clinical workflows for digital dentures and how we can use intraoral scanners to streamline the process.Learning Objectives: Review diagnostic principles.Demonstrate clinical workflows for digital dentures and overdentures.Understand the benefits of implant overdentures.

title: The Questions You Hate & The Answers You’ll Love: A Guide For Dealing With Difficult Patients - Part 2
description: “OH NO! Not that one again!” After all your training, and years of hard work and tremendous sacrifice, don’t you just hate it when you get those heart wrenching questions and resistance to case acceptance over and over again? Your team and you work so hard, and care so deeply, so why is it that often our patients don’t ask for optimal care and enthusiastically accept our treatment plans? Get ready to laugh, learn, and get fired up for a dramatic improvement of your case acceptance and your enjoyment of your practice!Learning Objectives: Learn the power of high impact new patient experience.Review ten questions you must ask of each new patient.Commit to obtaining the necessary evidence for predictable case presentations.Roleplay with your team the most common questions you absolutely HATE to hear, and learn the most predictable answers you’ll LOVE to give them.

title: What’s New? Materials & Tech that Expedite Excellence: Direct Restorative & Bonding
description: With a constant stream of new products that claim to be bigger, better, faster flooding the industry, it is challenging to know which are effective and efficient…and which are just hype.In this fast-moving market, advertising makes every product appear to be the panacea. However, some of the best products and services don’t enter the market with much fanfare. Dental professionals can become bogged down or confused when trying to decipher which new materials and technologies are right for their practices.As editorial director with Dental Economics magazine, Dr. Joshua Austin authors the column Pearls for Your Practice and has personally worked with most new products and technologies that have entered the market. In this interactive, fast-moving course, Joshua shares the current trends in dental materials and technologies. Learn how materials have changed over the past few years and how those changes can affect your procedures and practice.This session will focus on direct restorative dentistry and bonding.Learning Objectives: Illuminate growing trends and discover the best new products and innovations on the market.Explore the impact new products/technologies have on the practice’s bottom line.Explore the three etching techniques and learn when each are appropriate.Analyze how bulk fill posterior composites compare to their traditional counterparts.Discuss time saving placement techniques for bulk fill posterior composites.Review universal adhesives and their advantages.Discuss when luting is an appropriate choice instead of bonding.Explore many other products and items that can make your life easier and your dentistry faster.

title: "Hurts So...Swell!" Dental Pain Management For the Entire Dental Team - Part 2
description: Qualifies for MATE requirement hours.Management of acute odontogenic pain is accomplished through a clinical approach that successfully incorporates both opioid and non-opioid analgesics. However, moderate to severe dental pain often occurs well after office hours and away from our immediate supervision. Thus, it is critical for clinicians to be able to accurately identify a patient's need for pain control, develop an individualized pain management plan and assess the efficacy of analgesic agents employed.This program will provide strategies for effectively managing dental pain with opioid and non-opioid analgesics and analgesic adjuvants, while also mitigating associated risks. Special emphasis will be given to appropriate practices for prescribing analgesics as well as identification and treatment of opioid analgesic abuse.Learning Objectives: Understand the pharmacology and mechanism of action of opioid and non-opioid analgesics, as well as their potential for abuse.Discuss best practices for prescribing opioid analgesics as part of an individualized patient pain management plan.Describe techniques useful in recognizing and identifying potential addiction as well as avoiding potential drug diversion.Learn current protocols for the management and treatment of opioid addiction.Examine guidelines for proper storage and disposal of unused dosages.
instructors: Thomas Viola, RPh, CCP, CDE, CPMP

title: Smile Session: CEO vs DSO: The Pros and Cons of Partnering with a DSO vs Maintaining Private Ownership
description: Please note: PNDC doesn't not award CE for Smile Session courses. CE may be obtained directly from the speaker onsite at the session. If you are weighing independent ownership against a DSO partnership, this workshop is designed to give you clarity and confidence in your decision. We will break down real data so you can truly understand what your practice is worth and how different transition models impact your long-term wealth. With rising student debt and increasing corporate presence in dentistry, knowing how to evaluate cash at close, bonuses, equity, and long-term earning potential has never been more important. This session will equip you with practical tools, real-world comparisons, and strategic insight to help you make one of the most significant financial decisions of your career.

title: Cracked Teeth & Vertical Root Fractures: Rethinking Diagnosis & Treatment
description: Cracked teeth and vertical root fractures are increasingly recognized as major contributors to dental pain and tooth loss. This lecture presents an updated, evidence-based overview of their etiology, classification, and management. It examines the roles of occlusal forces, restorative design, and aging in the initiation and progression of tooth cracks, while highlighting the diagnostic value of advanced tools such as transillumination and microscopy. Management strategies focus on early detection, full-coverage restoration, and the application of the Iowa Staging Index to inform prognosis and guide treatment decisions. By challenging traditional concepts, this lecture provides a clear and structured framework for understanding and managing cracked and fractured teeth, thereby enhancing long-term clinical outcomes.Learning Objectives: Recognize early signs of fractures, and apply advanced imaging and visualization tools.Identify key etiologic and diagnostic factors, and determine prognosis.Develop a conservative treatment plan prioritizing tooth preservation.

title: Getting Diagnosis Right in Endodontics: Common Mistakes & Misconceptions
description:
Accurate endodontic diagnosis remains one of the most challenging aspects of dentistry. Drawing on his experience teaching and collaborating with referring dentists, Dr. Telmen Tumen Ulzii will highlight common diagnostic errors that lead to unnecessary or delayed treatment. This lecture reviews a step-by-step approach to pulpal and periapical diagnosis, emphasizing proper use of Endo Ice, EPT, percussion, palpation, and probing to avoid false results. Dr. Tumen Ulzii will also discuss how to distinguish endodontic from periodontal disease, occlusal trauma, and referred pain, and how to interpret radiographs and CBCT findings in context. Attendees will gain practical tools to improve diagnostic accuracy and confidence in daily practice.Learning Objectives: Identify common diagnostic errors made by dental students and general practitioners, and explain how to avoid false
positives and negatives when using Endo Ice, electric pulp testing, percussion.Differentiate endodontic pathology from periodontal disease, occlusal trauma, and referred pain through a systematic
evaluation of clinical findings and radiographic interpretation.Apply a step-by-step diagnostic framework that integrates clinical testing, radiographic assessment, and patient
history to improve diagnostic accuracy and treatment planning in daily practice.

title: Advanced Tax, Transition & Income Planning for Before, During & After Practice Transition - Part 1
description: This lecture is valuable for mid- to late-career dentists who are intent on plotting out a successful course for profitability, practice value, well-planned practice transition and a successful retirement utilizing advanced tax, financial and transition planning.Learning Objectives: Understand the power of written goals, plans and implementation for long-term success through transition and beyond.Learn how to keep more of your hard-earned money by understanding key tax traps and advantages surrounding transition and beyond.Learn how to plan a successful retirement with practice, transition, global cash flow and tax planning.Utilize advanced tax, financial and transition planning to make your retirement years successful and comfortable.

title: AI in Practice: Navigating the Legal & Technical Maze
description: As Artificial Intelligence (AI) moves from theory into operatories, clinicians face a critical challenge: how to adopt powerful new tools responsibly and safely. While AI promises to enhance diagnostics and streamline operations, it also introduces new questions about data security, diagnostic liability, and regulatory compliance. This session provides a practical framework for navigating this landscape and help establish clear principles for vetting and implementing AI technology, covering everything from vendor due diligence and HIPAA-compliant data handling to effective staff training. Attendees will learn how to integrate these tools to improve patient care without compromising security or complicating clinical workflows.Learning Objectives: Describe legal obligations for technology in healthcare.Create strategies for safe technology adoption.Apply strategies to AI tools.

title: Appliance Therapy in Everyday Dentistry - Part 1
description: Various types of appliances, applications of each appliance, and principles of appliance therapy in joints, muscles, and teeth. Utilizing bite splint therapy and understanding various appliance designs can be an invaluable advantage in a more comprehensive approach to treatment planning. A step by step overview of the different appliances will be discussed and simplified for applications in everyday dentistry. Learn the indications, contraindications, and benefits to the different appliances with applications in dental cases and applied to predictable dental outcomes.Learning Objectives: Review of TMJ and muscle examination and anatomy.Review of Occlusal Muscle Disharmony.Focus on Principles and Design of Bite Splint therapy.Discussion of all various appliance designs and advantages and disadvantages to appliance selection.Focus on Principles and Design of Bite Splint therapy.Discussion of all various appliance designs and advantages and disadvantages to appliance selection.Discussion of integration of appliance therapy, principles of occlusion, and equilibration.How to communicate and collaborate with your team and patients with Appliance Therapy.

title: Dealing with Complications in Implant Dentistry: A Case Journey - Part 1
description: Complications in dentistry are never a good thing! If the greatest crime the devil ever created was making you think that there are no complications in implant dentistry, we have a lot to talk about. This presentation is all about complications and there are many of them to discuss and how you can avoid them. Shooting yourself in the foot is a bad thing, it's even worse when you reload the gun. So let's share our experiences in a journey.Learning Objectives: 4 different types of complications.5 ways to avoid complications.3 examples of cases which failed and how to avoid that!
instructors: Waji Khan, BSc, DDS, MBA, MEd

title: Guardians of Health: The Essentials Of Disease Prevention - Part 1
description: As healthcare continues to evolve in the post-COVID-19 era, so does the professional and ethical responsibility of the dental hygienist. Today’s patients are more informed than ever about infection control, disease risk factors, and the importance of prevention. This is the moment to reaffirm our profession, focusing on the critical role dental hygienists play as preventive and infection control specialists in allied healthcare.Join Katrina M Sanders, RDH, as she dives into the latest advancements in disease prevention. This dynamic course explores cutting-edge techniques available to dental hygiene professionals to control, prevent, and manage disease. Participants will walk away with advanced knowledge of futuristic methods in disease prevention, ready to elevate their practice and patient outcomes.Special Note: While this is a clinical course, it’s one that all dental team members enjoy taking, as it covers the diverse ways in which dentistry plays a vital role in preventing disease. From hygienists to dental assistants, each team member can benefit from understanding the broader impact of disease prevention strategies, patient education, and how their roles contribute to long-term patient health. This course offers a comprehensive look at the intersection of clinical practice and preventive care, making it relevant and engaging for the entire dental team as they work together to improve patient outcomes.Learning Objectives: Evaluate the current landscape and subsequent evolution of preventive dentistry.Discuss modern trends, techniques and advances in the prevention and control of disease transmission.Identify available techniques and products for use in the disease prevention as well as futuristic trends for the dental professional.
instructors: Katrina M. Sanders, RDH, BSDH, M.Ed, RF

title: Immediate Implant Placement & Provisionalization
description: Restoration of a non-restorable anterior tooth is one of the most challenging treatments. Immediate implant placement is one of the best treatment options we can provide for these situations, however, there are specific principles that must be followed. This lecture will discuss the key factors associated with proper implant placement to set us up for success. Following implant placement, a custom-made provisional can be fabricated chairside to support biologic and esthetic tooth replacement. Techniques to fabricate these immediate provisional will be discussed.Learning Objectives: Review keys to set up success for immediate implant placement.Discuss implant placement procedures.Understand multiple ways to fabricate immediate provisionals.

title: Motivational Interviewing & Advanced Communication for the Dental Setting
description: Effective communication is a cornerstone of high-quality, patient-centered healthcare. Motivational interviewing is an evidence-based approach for effectively communicating with patients to facilitate treatment adherence and health behavior change. This session will introduce attendees to motivational interviewing and other advanced communication techniques that support a good patient-provider relationship and good clinical outcomes.Learning Objectives: Describe the role of effective communication in patient care.Identify and apply motivational interviewing techniques to effectively promote treatment plan acceptance, adherence to clinical recommendations, and oral health-related behavior change.Identify and apply advanced communication strategies to effectively build rapport with patients, facilitate dental treatment and oral health, and navigate difficult conversations.

title: Secrets to Hearing "Yes" to the Fee! - Part 1
description: Patient perception of insurance and cost of treatment can derail case acceptance. Discover proven ways to help your patients say "Yes" to the fee no matter the cost or what their insurance will cover including: Stop saying this! Terms you may be using that turn patients away from treatment. Start saying this! Proven verbal skills to hearing "Yes" to treatment. Financial arrangements secrets to hearing Yes to the fee. "Will my insurance cover all of this?" Answer the most challenging insurance questions. "I can't afford it!" How to turn financial objections into opportunities for a "Yes" to treatment.Learning Objectives: Build lasting relationships with patients converting a No to a Yes regarding treatment acceptance.Overcome patients financial objections with great verbal skills.Demonstrate presenting financial arrangements.
instructors: Pamela Peterson, President, CEO Total Patient Service Inst.

title: The Changing Epidemiology of Oral Cavity Cancer: An Update (Repeat)
description: This presentation will discuss the changing epidemiology of oral cavity cancer, with reference to the emerging epidemic of oral tongue cancer in young adults. It will highlight novel and emerging risk factors including poor oral hygiene, microbiome, sugar-sweetened beverages, and diet. In addition, a discussion regarding differing risk profiles and risk modification between different ages, races, and sexes.Learning Objectives: Describe the changing epidemiology of oral cavity cancer in the US and globally.Discuss recurrence and survival in early-onset and average-onset oral cavity cancer.Discuss emerging etiopathologies for early-onset and average-onset oral cavity cancer.Differentiate risk profiles for oral cavity cancer between different ages, races, and sexes.Identify challenges in treatment of oral cavity cancer in young adults and barriers to further investigation.

title: The Great Debate: To Save or Not to Save
description: Two periodontal specialists — Leroy Horton, DDS, MS, DICOI and Aierress Davis, DDS, MHS, Cert Perio — going head to head, each assigned a particular stance on a generic case presented to the audience. One defending the ideology of saving a tooth at all costs, while the other arguing the merits of extraction and implant therapy. The audience views the case and votes on it prior to any of the presentation. They vote after the presentation, and whichever speaker garnered the greatest positive change in percentage wins the debate. This is entertaining, informative, engaging, and regardless of who wins, the audience learns from both presenters.Learning Objectives: Present the case for extracting teeth with poor prognosis to initiate implant therapy.Highlight the limitations of restorative treatments for compromised teeth.Review the options available and the benefits of maintaining natural teeth.Provide literature and case presentation to demonstrate multi-disciplinary therapies on both sides of the debate.
instructors:
Leroy Horton, DDS, MS, DICOI
Aierress Davis, DDS, MHS, Cert Perio

title: The Ultimate FIVE STAR New Patient Experience - Part 1
description: Maybe you need more new patients? Or...maybe you just need to slow down, ask better questions, OUT-LISTEN the competition, use cutting edge diagnostic photography with advanced caries detection, and apply time-tested human relations principles to thrive during turbulent times.Enjoy a fast-paced, interactive experience where we will master the art of an exceptional new patient experience, offer a comprehensive treatment plan, and deliver a bullet-proof case presentation resulting in an increased case acceptance, more optimal care dentistry, and FUN!!Learning Objectives: Master the ten key questions you must ask each New patient and Emergency patient!Teach your team how to systematically and predictably take quality photographs.Demonstrate a treatment planning sequence that leads to predictable case acceptance.

title: Tips & Tricks to Make Your Practice Senior-Friendly - Part 1
description: This course is designed for the dental team that wants to treat the fastest growing demographic group within North America. Those patients 65 and over have the greatest dental needs, and this course will highlight how to treat them in a comfortable and dignified manner. This course will explore how to transform your office into a welcoming and safe environment for the aging population. Topics to be discussed include treatments of the demented patient, prevention and treatment of root caries, xerostomia, elder abuse and hospice care.Learning Objectives: Truly grasp the importance of the senior patient to your practice.Know how to market your practice to seniors.Treat root and coronal caries.Aid the hospice patient.

title: What’s New? Materials & Tech that Expedite Excellence: Digital & 3D Printing (Repeat)
description: With a constant stream of new products that claim to be bigger, better, faster flooding the industry, it is challenging to know which are effective and efficient…and which are just hype.In this fast-moving market, advertising makes every product appear to be the panacea. However, some of the best products and services don’t enter the market with much fanfare. Dental professionals can become bogged down or confused when trying to decipher which new materials and technologies are right for their practices.As editorial director with Dental Economics magazine, Dr. Joshua Austin authors the column Pearls for Your Practice and has personally worked with most new products and technologies that have entered the market. In this interactive, fast-moving course, Joshua shares the current trends in dental materials and technologies. Learn how materials have changed over the past few years and how those changes can affect your procedures and practice.This session will focus on intraoral scanning and 3D printing.Learning Objectives: Explore the latest innovations and enhancements in intraoral scanning.Learn how the improved scanning amplifies practice productivity.Discover how 3D printing fits in dental practices in 2026.Discover how these devices can close the gap of patient communication.Discuss when luting is an appropriate choice instead of bonding.Explore many other products and items that can make your life easier and your dentistry faster.

title: Will Vaping Challenge The Dental Professions as Smoking Has In The Past? - Part 1
description: Although cannabis smoking among young adults has declined markedly over the past several decades, there has been a substantial increase in the use of vaporizing devices. While originally designed as alternative delivery vehicles for nicotine in place of tobacco, advances in technology and lack of strict regulation have given rise to the use of these devices as delivery vehicles for cannabis. Unfortunately, very little information is available about the safety of the active and inactive ingredients present in cannabis products, especially those used for vaping. This course will provide participants with an overview of cannabis, vaping, and their overall systemic adverse effects. Special emphasis will be given to the oral complications associated with vaping, as well as patient care planning strategies.Learning Objectives: Describe the pharmacology of cannabis, including its mechanism of action, routes of administration and available formulations.Compare smoking and vaping as alternate delivery systems for introducing drugs like nicotine and cannabis into the lungs.Describe the oral and systemic complications encountered by patients who vape.Explain current available treatment strategies and patient care planning techniques for patients who vape cannabis.
instructors: Thomas Viola, RPh, CCP, CDE, CPMP

title: Managing Preventable Incidents in Dentistry: Swallowed Instruments & Wrong Tooth Treatment
description:
Preventable clinical incidents are one of the fastest growing categories of dental malpractice claims drivers. This course provides a comprehensive overview of two high-risk event categories: swallowed and aspirated instruments and wrong tooth treatment. Through real-world case studies, practical workflow improvements, and risk-management insights, participants will learn how these incidents occur, the most effective strategies for prevention, and effective risk management techniques and patient communication strategies for when these incidents do occur. This presentation will be provided by Jacqueline Clarke, J.D., a litigation attorney, who currently serves as MedPro's Lead Dental Risk Consultant. MedPro insureds who attend this presentation may be eligible to earn a 5% risk management premium credit* for the next policy renewal. *Approval by MedPro does not imply acceptance by a state or provincial board. Premium credit eligibility and amount subject to state insurance filings and policy type. Completion of a risk management course does not imply or guarantee renewal.Learning Objectives: Recognize current trends in malpractice litigation, including the most common types of procedures, and related injuries, which lead to lawsuits.Identify both the patient and situational risk factors that increase the likelihood of a swallowed / aspirated instrument situation. Recognize the emergency protocol for swallowed and aspirated instruments.Develop incident-response protocols to manage emergencies effectively, and reduce downstream harm.
Apply pre-procedure verification steps to prevent wrong-tooth or wrong-sided treatment.Improve team communication and documentation to support patient safety and mitigate the chances for preventable outcomes.

title: Mastering Dental Photography With Your Smartphone | Hands-On Workshop ($295)
description: In this fun and lively hands-on workshop, participants will have the opportunity to practice taking the images that have the greatest impact in communication with the patient and the dental team using a smartphone and speciﬁc accessories. All clinical intra- and extra-oral views will be demonstrated and emphasis will be placed on integration of the photographs with increased improvement in case presentation.A limited number of smartphone attachments, mirrors and retractors will be made available but participants are asked to bring sterile supplies (if they own them) to maximize the learning experience.Supplies to be supplied by participants: Favorite mirrors and retractors if you have them. A limited supply will be provided by the instructor.Learning Objectives: Master six essential dental photographic images.Learn problem solving techniques with emphasis on mirror and retractor placement.Utilize digital imaging for patient education and case acceptance.Integrate digital photography easily in your practice.

title: Food for Thought: Nutrition’s Influence on Oral Disease & Recovery
description:
Successful oral health care doesn’t stop at the procedure—it starts long before and continues well after. Nutrition plays a critical yet often overlooked role in surgical recovery, tissue repair, immune function, and overall patient outcomes. This session explores the vital connection between nutrition and oral health, highlighting strategies for optimizing patients prior to surgery, tailoring approaches for special populations, and implementing postoperative nutrition plans that accelerate healing. We’ll also discuss practical ways providers can integrate nutritional considerations into everyday practice and how to collaborate effectively with nutritionists to enhance patient care. Attendees will leave with actionable tools to support their patients through every stage of treatment, from preparation to recovery, with an emphasis on evidence-based nutrition strategies that improve outcomes and elevate the standard of care.Learning Objectives: Understand the role of nutrition in preoperative optimization, postoperative healing, and long-term oral health.Identify special considerations for high-risk or medically complex patients where nutrition plays a critical role.Apply practical, evidence-based strategies to incorporate nutrition counseling and interdisciplinary collaboration into
daily practice.
instructors: MIna D. Fahmy, DDS, MS, Dip ABOMS

title: From Analog to Automation: Complete Digital Workflows for Full-Arch Implant Rehabilitations
description: Implant-supported prostheses have long been recognized as an effective modality to restore function and esthetics for patients with edentulous arches or terminal dentition. With rapid advances in digital technology, full-arch implant rehabilitation has evolved from traditional analog methods to fully integrated digital and automated workflows. These innovations have greatly enhanced the precision, efficiency, and predictability of both surgical and restorative phases. This presentation will systematically outline and compare analog and digital workflows for full-arch implant rehabilitation, supported by clinical case examples and the latest scientific evidence. Key topics include: interdisciplinary, restoratively- and surgically-driven treatment planning strategies; the use of optical jaw-tracking systems for diagnosis; dynamic navigation–assisted implant surgery; and the application of photogrammetry and intraoral scanning to achieve precise digital implant impressions and fabricate chairside 3D-printed interim prostheses. The presentation will also discuss the advantages, limitations, and clinical decision-making considerations of contemporary digital workflows, while emphasizing the continued relevance of traditional analog approaches.Learning Objectives: Apply an interdisciplinary, restoratively- and surgically-driven treatment planning approach for predictable full-arch implant rehabilitation.Understand the fundamental principles and workflow differences between analog and digital approaches in full-arch implant rehabilitation.Implement static computer-assisted surgical guides and dynamic navigation–assisted implant surgery to improve surgical accuracy and predictability.Integrate facial scanning, intraoral scanning, optical jaw-tracking, and photogrammetry technologies into digital workflows.Design and fabricate CAD/CAM and chairside 3D-printed interim prostheses to optimize restorative efficiency and clinical outcomes.Evaluate the clinical advantages and limi
instructors:
Yen-Wei Chen, DDS, MSD
Kanako J. Nagatomo, DDS, MSD, PhD

title: Achieving Personal Well-being for a Fulfilling & Sustainable Career in Dentistry
description: While life as a dental professional is often invigorating, rewarding, and fun, it can also be demanding and quite stressful. Excessive and unmanaged stress can lead to difficulty and dissatisfaction with work, burnout, and mental and physical health problems. This session will introduce attendees to evidence-based principles and practical skills for personal stress management as well as strategies that foster well-being and a positive, healthy culture for the entire dental team.Learning Objectives: Define stress and its causes and consequences, distinguishing between acute and chronic stress.Explain and apply evidence-based strategies for coping with stressors and achieving well-being.Describe and employ approaches for promoting wellness among colleagues and staff.

title: Advanced Tax, Transition & Income Planning for Before, During & After Practice Transition - Part 2
description: This lecture is valuable for mid- to late-career dentists who are intent on plotting out a successful course for profitability, practice value, well-planned practice transition and a successful retirement utilizing advanced tax, financial and transition planning.Learning Objectives: Understand the power of written goals, plans and implementation for long-term success through transition and beyond.Learn how to keep more of your hard-earned money by understanding key tax traps and advantages surrounding transition and beyond.Learn how to plan a successful retirement with practice, transition, global cash flow and tax planning.Utilize advanced tax, financial and transition planning to make your retirement years successful and comfortable.

title: All-on-4: From Treatment Planning to Final Restorations
