title: The Art and Science of Tooth Whitening
description: Tooth whitening has become an over-looked procedure by dental practices, but not by patients! How can you bring whitening to life in your practice? New systems are being brought to the market at a dizzying pace. Should I do chair side or take-home bleaching? Do over-the-counter whitening products work and how do I incorporate them into clinical practice? What about a combination of chair side and take-home? Are they worth it? In this course, we will review the different systems on the market as well as those on the horizon. The pros and cons of bleaching will be covered as well as the use of light sources to speed up the whitening process. The combination of white spot removal and bleaching will be demonstrated through clinical slides. Issues of sensitivity, mechanism of action and longevity will be discussed. Material in this course will rely on scientific evidence from the literature as well as from our clinical experiences.
instructors:
Connie Kugel, RDH
Gerard Kugel, DMD, MS, PhD
requirements: Become comfortable with the scientific details related to whitening Understand the mechanism of action of whitening and white spot removal Incorporate whitening into the practice as a benefit for patients and the practice Discover the different types of whitening available Identify the causes of whitening sensitivity and how to treat it

title: Anterior Esthetics: How to Satisfy The Patient
description: Formulas, guides, measurements, and "tricks" do not always result in the patient viewing the final result and feeling emotional satisfaction. This course will review lessons learned from decades of treatment on both teeth and implants in the anterior sextant. Analysis and management of the smile and the gingival frame will be explored to enhance the esthetic outcomes. Selection and limitations of different ceramic materials will be investigated as well as resin bonded cementation. Both CAD/CAM and analogue techniques will be used and their selective applications applied to maximize both the satisfaction of the patient and the dentist.
requirements: Identify the patient's goals to avoid potential disappointment Discuss the diagnosis and treatment of an esthetic versus a biologic dental issue Implement step-by-step clinical techniques to make better temporary restorations and impressions Establish the case for the dental technician to succeed and not fail

title: Dental Management of Sleep Apnea: Pediatrics and Oral Appliance Therapy
description: This course provides dental professionals with a practical overview of managing obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) across patient populations, with an emphasis on pediatric cases and oral appliance therapy. Attendees will learn to recognize the signs of OSA in children, understand the screening and diagnostic process, and explore non-invasive treatment options such as oral appliances. The course highlights the critical role of dentists in a multidisciplinary care team addressing sleep-related breathing disorders.
instructors:
Umakanth Katwa, MD
Leopoldo Correa, MS, BDS
requirements: Understand the fundamentals of obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) in children and adults Identify appropriate screening and diagnostic tools for OSA in the dental setting Illustrate the principles of oral appliance therapy, including selection and customization Integrate collaborative, multidisciplinary approaches to the management of OSA Recognize the dentist’s role in improving patient outcomes through sleep apnea intervention

title: Pediatric and Dental Sleep Medicine: A Clinical Introduction for Dentists
description: This course offers an engaging introduction to the field of dental sleep medicine with a focus on pediatric patients. Dentists play a critical role in identifying and managing sleep-related disorders that impact both children and adults. Participants will explore sleep physiology, common pediatric sleep disorders, and the dental implications of conditions such as obstructive sleep apnea (OSA). The course emphasizes practical screening tools and the importance of interdisciplinary collaboration in delivering comprehensive care.
instructors:
Umakanth Katwa, MD
Leopoldo Correa, MS, BDS
requirements: Understand basic sleep physiology with emphasis on pediatric patients Recognize the impact of sleep on growth, development, and overall health Identify common pediatric sleep disorders and their dental relevance Outline the dentist's role in screening and managing sleep-related breathing disorders, including obstructive sleep apnea Collaborate with medical professionals for comprehensive patient care

title: Dawson Academy 2 Day Program: Occlusion and Smile Design
description: Master Occlusion, Smile Design, and Boost Your Bottom Line! Core 1: Occlusion and Smile Design Two Full Days: Friday, January 30, 2026: 8:30 am – 12:30 pm; 1:30 – 4:30 pm AND Saturday, January 31, 2026: 8:30 am – 12:30 pm; 1:30 – 4:30 pm In this course, attendees will delve into the fundamentals of functional occlusion. Designed to elevate your dental practice, this course offers a comprehensive integration of foundational occlusal principles with cutting-edge workflows. Beyond mastering the intricacies of esthetic dentistry, this course provides practical strategies to reduce insurance dependency, ensuring a robust bottom line. Redefine dental excellence and business success, positioning yourself as a leader in your community with Core 1. Over two days, you’ll learn the requirements for occlusal stability to improve your practice from examination and records to treatment planning to practice management.

description: In this course, attendees will discover efficient and predictable patient treatment using digital technology. This hands-on course provides an opportunity to digitally design smiles and apply new techniques and materials for single-visit cosmetic treatments.
requirements: Assess the complete digital and clinical process for single visit composite veneers Understand case selection and advantages of this treatment Acquire practical tips for enhanced workflows Explore the latest material and technique advancements with composite veneers Gain hands-on experience with the matrix system to build confidence for future cases

title: Expedited Excellence: Increase Efficiency with Direct Restorations
description: In this course, the instructor will discuss direct restoration procedures for the posterior. Attendees will learn how to increase your efficiency and results with your posterior restorations, using unique techniques. Attendees will participate in all steps of different posterior restorations using bulk-fill composite with correct curing light procedures and several different techniques.
requirements: Explore the advantages of using bulk fill posterior composite Discuss and perform several placement techniques for bulk fill posterior composites that will save time and increase predictability Implement quick and effective polishing techniques for posterior restorations Examine different matrix systems and their best uses Use techniques for performing two adjacent Class II lesions efficiently Review universal bonding agents and their best uses in restorative dentistry

title: Digitally Designed Composite Veneers
description: In this course, attendees will discover efficient and predictable patient treatment using digital technology. This hands-on course provides an opportunity to digitally design smiles and apply new techniques and materials for single-visit cosmetic treatments.
requirements: Assess the complete digital and clinical process for single visit composite veneers Understand case selection and advantages of this treatment Acquire practical tips for enhanced workflows Explore the latest material and technique advancements with composite veneers Gain hands-on experience with the matrix system to build confidence for future cases

title: The Dos and Don’ts of Porcelain Laminate Veneers
description: This course will help dentists incorporate veneers into their everyday practices. Clinical cases will demonstrate preparation design based on patients’ needs, reduction guides, silicone temporary shells to improve the final results, and tips for enhancing cementation.
instructors:
Chad Anderson, DMD, MS
Gerard Kugel, DMD, MS, PhD
requirements: Understand treatment planning and case selection for veneers Recognize and avoid preparation and cementation pitfalls Identify common problems associated with porcelain veneer cases Demonstrate the techniques involved in the preparation, temporization, and cementation processes Fabricate a veneer preparation reduction guide along with a silicone temporary shell Evaluate a step-by-step review of cementation

title: The Dos and Don’ts of Porcelain Laminate Veneers
description: This course will help dentists incorporate veneers into their everyday practices. Clinical cases will demonstrate preparation design based on patients’ needs, reduction guides, silicone temporary shells to improve the final results, and tips for enhancing cementation.

title: Crown Lengthening
description: Crown lengthening procedures may involve osseous resective therapy in addition to surgical management of soft tissue. These hard- and soft-tissue alterations may be provided in order to obtain a ferrule height and establish a biologic width. This course will utilize a plastic typodont to provide a practical exercise in exposing a maxillary premolar with a subgingival fracture. Techniques for incisions, osseous therapy, and suturing will be reviewed. Indications and contraindications to surgical exposure will be discussed.
requirements: Practice crown lengthening techniques on a typodont Design a flap for a crown lengthening surgical procedure Utilize ostectomy and osteoplasty to achieve adequate tooth exposure Identify how to eliminate osseous deformities Suture the buccal and lingual flaps for closure Provide postoperative management Determine when crown lengthening is contraindicated *Tuition includes cost of models that attendees will keep at the conclusion of the course.

title: Esthetics from 30,000 Feet
description: In this course attendees will be introduced to the diagnostic process utilized to restore biologic tooth form and function to the worn dentition. This course will walk through the process of diagnosis and principles of temporomandibular joint stability, the importance of guidance in occlusion and how form and cosmetics go hand-in-hand. A closer look into key anatomical features that should be in every restoration, from a single unit to full mouth rehabilitations. This course will help in identifying common themes related to occlusal dysfunction and its relationship to breakdown in the esthetic zone and aid in foundational ways to treatment plan. Additionally, this course will walk attendees through a step-by-step process of stability, diagnosis, re-establishing the form on the models and the techniques used to restore biologic form to the teeth.
requirements: Explain the process of diagnosis and principles of temporomandibular joint stability Determine the importance of guidance in occlusion Identify common themes related to occlusal dysfunction and its relationship to breakdown in the esthetic zone Recognize the key anatomical features that should be in every restoration

title: Esthetic Techniques with Anterior Crowns and Veneers
description: This course is tailored for dental professionals who have a foundational understanding of esthetic dentistry but seek to refine their skills and address specific challenges related to crowns, veneers and esthetic techniques. Step-by-step protocols, materials selection, cementation and digital design will be discussed. A hands-on exercise utilizing an intraoral scanner will be provided.
requirements: Master the fundamentals of smile design and esthetic evaluation Identify ideal candidates for veneers and effectively communicate treatment options and expectations Understand the latest advancements in material science Demonstrate preparation and cementation methods for anterior crowns and veneers for optimal fit and esthetics

title: Pain Management for the Dentist
description: The Opioid epidemic continues to claim lives at an alarming rate. The current characteristics of this crisis will be defined at the national and state levels. The various types of orofacial pain and the appropriate treatment strategies for each diagnosis will be discussed together with a comparison of the use of opioids, non-opioid medications, and non-pharmacologic options for safe and effective pain management. Best practice guidelines for pain control after different types of dental procedures will be evaluated. The risks, benefits, alternatives, storage, and disposal of controlled substances will be detailed. Simple means of identifying and managing patients at risk for substance misuse will be described. Regulatory and legal requirements will be documented with emphasis on the Prescription Drug Monitoring Program. A Resource List for the safe use of opioids will be provided.
requirements: Summarize the current national and local prescription opioid crisis Discuss acute and chronic orofacial pain conditions and the safe and effective pain management strategies for each diagnosis Evaluate the risks, benefits, and alternatives of a wide variety of pain medications including the risks of abuse and addiction associated with opioid medications Define the role of the dentist and dental team in pain management, risk assessment, and mitigation

title: Esthetic Techniques with Anterior Crowns and Veneers
description: This course is tailored for dental professionals who have a foundational understanding of esthetic dentistry but seek to refine their skills and address specific challenges related to crowns, veneers and esthetic techniques. Step-by-step protocols, materials selection, cementation and digital design will be discussed. A hands-on exercise utilizing an intraoral scanner will be provided.
requirements: Master the fundamentals of smile design and esthetic evaluation Identify ideal candidates for veneers and effectively communicate treatment options and expectations Understand the latest advancements in material science Demonstrate preparation and cementation methods for anterior crowns and veneers for optimal fit and esthetics

title: Full-Arch Fundamentals and Esthetics, Complications, and Conversion
description: FULL-ARCH FUNDAMENTALS: THE WHAT, WHO, AND HOW Welcome and Intro – Ancy Verdier, DMD Course overview and framing: why this workflow, why now Surgical Execution for Predictable Full-Arch Outcomes – Steven Litwin, DDS Flap design, implant positioning, bone reduction, and prosthetic space Overview of All-on-4 vs. pterygoid-based options Digital Workflow and Patient Selection – Matthew Annese, DMD Diagnostic workups, scanning, design, and case selection Prosthetic design considerations for long-term success Complication-Proofing Your Treatment – Benjamin Keyser, DMD Managing common surgical and prosthetic issues Maintenance and troubleshooting strategies 12:00 – 1:00 pm- Lunch Break GOING DEEPER: ESTHETICS, COMPLICATIONS, AND CONVERSION FP1 and Tissue Management – Matthew Annese, DMD Create natural-looking esthetics and soft tissue support in FP1 designs Advanced Complications and Rescue Strategies – Benjamin Keyser, DMD Manage broken frameworks, esthetic failures, and peri-implantitis Refining the Patient Conversation – Steven Litwin, DDS Build trust, manage expectations, and present full-arch cases with clarity Closing Comments & Q&A – Ancy Verdier, DMD Summary of key takeaways Audience questions and collaborative wrap-up
instructors:
Benjamin Keyser, DMD
Matthew Annese, DMD
Steven Litwin, DDS
Ancy Verdier, DMD
requirements: Understand surgical execution for predictable full-arch outcomes Establish digital workflow and patient selection Discover how to complication-proof your treatment Analyze FP1 and tissue management Determine advanced complications and rescue strategies Explain how to refine patient conversation

title: Pharmacologic Management of Acute Dental Pain
description: In this course, attendees will learn the scope and responsibilities of prescription writing privileges as it applies to the practice of dentistry. The history of pain management and dentistry's responsibility in combating the prescription drug epidemic will be discussed. Pharmacologic pain management options available to dentists will be reviewed including opioids and non-opioids including principles of oral dosing regimens and side effects, contraindications and clinically significant drug-drug interactions. Basic pain physiology will be reviewed as well as the mechanisms of action of commonly prescribed analgesics. The dentist’s responsibility in combating drug diversion will be presented including how to identify drug seeking patients.
requirements: Identify analgesic prescribing guidelines in connection with the effective management of acute dental pain Understand the origin of the prescription opioid epidemic and dentistry’s role in combating substance abuse Determine the risks of substance abuse and addiction associated with opioid analgesics Recognize and counsel patients at risk for substance abuse Discuss how to counsel patients about the side effects, security, addictive nature and proper storage and disposal of controlled substances

title: Basic Surgical Incisions, Flap Management, and Suturing Techniques
description: Utilizing pig jaws, this course will review incisions utilized for surgical access around the natural dentition. Attendees will learn techniques for flap elevation, flap positioning, and suturing for closure. Anatomical considerations associated with flap elevation, periosteal fenestration, and incision closure will be discussed. Attendees will learn how to coronally advance a flap, reposition a flap, and apically position a flap will be explained, . The principles of osseous resection for pocket reduction as well as periodontal regeneration will be reviewed. Surgical instrumentation and various suture materials will also be discussed.
requirements: Practice surgical techniques on a pig jaw Discuss the rationale for surgical access Identify how to make incisions for surgical access and how to elevate a flap Examine the technique for periosteal fenestration for flap advancement Review suturing techniques for flap closure

title: Bioceramic Based Root Canal Obturation: The Anatomical Continuum
description: Bioceramics and bioceramic sealers represent a significant advancement in endodontic obturation, offering exceptional biocompatibility, dimensional stability, and antimicrobial properties. This course provides a comprehensive, evidence-based exploration of bioceramic-based root canal obturation, emphasizing the material science, clinical indications, and outcomes associated with their use. Attendees will gain a deep understanding of the physicochemical characteristics that differentiate bioceramics from traditional sealers, as well as their role in promoting periapical healing and long-term treatment success. Through detailed case discussions and step-by-step procedural breakdowns, attendees will develop a strong understanding of bioceramic materials thus refining their approach to obturation and developing a framework for integrating these techniques seamlessly into routine endodontic care.
requirements: Comprehend the scientifically-based biologic requirements for endodontic success Determine limitations and drawbacks of traditional sealers and obturation methods Understand the biological advantages and usages of bioceramics in root canal therapy and specifically in obturation

title: Single Implant Mastery: From Placement to Precision
description: This course provides a focused and practical approach to mastering single dental implant placement. It covers immediate implant placement protocols and the clinical criteria for case selection. Participants will explore the interaction between implant systems and bone biology to enhance surgical outcomes. The course emphasizes decision-making, precision, and predictability in single-tooth implant procedures. Practical insights into the strengths and limitations of different implant systems will be discussed. Designed for clinicians aiming to elevate their implant practice, this course blends theory with clinical relevance.
requirements: Understand the key principles of single implant placement Identify appropriate cases for immediate implant placement Gain knowledge of how bone quality and volume influence implant success Evaluate various implant systems and their compatibility with different clinical scenarios Develop skills to plan and execute single implant procedures with greater confidence Foster critical thinking to manage challenges in implant dentistry effectively

title: The Anatomical Edge in Endodontics
description: Modern endodontics is evolving—beyond mechanical shaping and traditional access designs—toward a biologically and anatomically centered approach that respects the natural complexity of the root canal system. This course is designed for practitioners who want to elevate their endodontic outcomes by understanding and working with the tooth’s internal anatomy, not against it. Through a combination of concise theory and extensive hands-on training, participants will learn how to recognize, interpret, and treat the three-dimensional complexities of root canal systems using evidence-based, anatomy-guided techniques. Topics include conservative canal instrumentation compromising less dentin, simple and comprehensive irrigation protocol, and obturation strategies that fill—not force—into anatomical spaces. Attendees will not only learn the principles behind anatomically centered endodontics but also practice and test these techniques on simulated and extracted teeth, gaining practical confidence in tools, materials, and decision-making for everyday clinical scenarios.
requirements: Understand the latest generation of minimally invasive technologies Recognize and interpret complex root canal anatomies Master anatomically centered endodontics and apply it in clinic

title: Anatomically Centered Endodontics
description: The goal of any endodontic treatment is to prevent or treat apical periodontitis- microbial control. This goal is achieved through the synergistic effect of the famous endodontic triad: Instrumentation, irrigation, and obturation of the root canal anatomy. Anatomically Centered Endodontics, ACE, is a philosophy that embraces all 3 components of the endodontic triad while allowing clinicians to reach microbial control in a minimally invasive, safe, and efficient manner- streamlining endodontic procedures. The ACE philosophy incorporates adaptive instrumentation, the XP-3D+, which cleans the canal safely and three-dimensionally reaching areas where traditional round NiTi files are unable to reach. It also involves a special irrigation mixture of NaOCl, chelating agents and surfactants that promotes deep disinfection with the simultaneous dissolution of organic and inorganic matter. This ultra-conservative and disinfected canal can then be obturated using bioceramic technologies that allow for zero shrinkage and bond to dentin thus offering a fluid-tight seal.
requirements: Comprehend the scientifically based biologic requirements for endodontic success Understand the meaning of minimally invasive endodontics Determine the limitations of files that creates a round shape Appreciate the latest generation of minimally invasive technologies that allows us to clinically obtain the essential biologic goals

title: Overdentures Reimagined: Integrating Tradition, Innovation, & Digital Precision
description: KOSHER STUDY CLUB LECTURE & BRUNCH Overdentures are no longer just a solution for the edentulous—they’re a dynamic treatment option at the intersection of tradition and technology. This lecture explores the full spectrum of overdenture therapy, from conventional tissue-supported to implant-supported designs and cutting-edge digital workflows. Participants will gain practical insights into diagnosis, treatment planning, and execution of overdentures using both analog and digital protocols. Whether you're revisiting removable prosthodontics or expanding your implant offerings, this lecture will equip you to deliver predictable, high-quality outcomes with confidence.
requirements: Compare traditional and digital workflows for implant overdentures Apply the three critical diagnostic and treatment planning principles to enhance clinical decision-making in overdenture cases Understand digital protocols for impression taking, clinical records, and prosthesis fabrication *Tuition includes a Kosher brunch from 11:00 - 11:30 am.

title: Periodontic Specialty Group Program
description: Designed by Specialists, Open to All Optimizing Clinical Outcomes with Perio, Ortho, and AI Collaboration This program is designed for dental professionals seeking to integrate cutting-edge periodontal innovations and interdisciplinary orthodontic collaboration into their daily workflow. The first segment will focus on Surgically Facilitated Orthodontic Therapy (SFOT) and Miniscrew-Assisted Rapid Palatal Expansion (MARPE) , highlighting their everyday clinical applications in collaboration with general dentists and orthodontists. Emphasis will be placed on advanced treatment planning, workflow integration, and predictable interdisciplinary outcomes. The second segment will explore the global application of Artificial Intelligence in periodontal practice, including the emerging concept of the Digital Dental Hygienist. Attendees will gain insight into AI-driven patient engagement, and data management tools that can be implemented to enhance clinical efficiency and patient care.
requirements: Apply advanced SFOT and MARPE techniques in collaboration with orthodontists and general dentists to achieve optimal periodontal and orthodontic results Understand global AI technologies and the Digital Dental Hygienist concept in relation to everyday periodontal practice for enhanced periodontal maintenance care Develop interdisciplinary treatment protocols that combine surgical innovation, orthodontic biomechanics, and AI-assisted clinical decision-making for predictable patient outcomes * Tui

title: Oral Surgery Specialty Group Lecture for the OMS and Team Members
description: Designed by Specialists, Open to All MORNING SESSIONS 8:00 - 9:00 am: Network with colleagues over coffee and breakfast pastries before the course begins. From New Patient Visit to Verdict: The Course of a Nerve Injury Lawsuit 9:00 - 11:00 am Judith Feinberg Albright This course will follow the course of a hypothetical lingual nerve injury case from the initial patient encounter through third molar surgery and into litigation, all the way to trial. We will outline the course of litigation, discuss tricks and traps along the way, and talk both about common pitfalls and about key strategy points for success. We will discuss the factors that assist in the defense of a lingual nerve injury, and the factors that can complicate it. The course will set key litigation events in the hypothetical, and provide real-world learning opportunities. Nerve Anatomy, Injury Classification, and Diagnostic Follow-Up 10:45 am - 12:00 pm Daniel Oreadi, DMD This course provides a comprehensive review of trigeminal nerve anatomy relevant to oral and maxillofacial surgery. It will cover the mechanisms of nerve injury, including surgical trauma, iatrogenic injury, and oncologic resection. Attendees will learn the widely accepted classifications of nerve injury severity and their clinical implications. The course will also explore evidence-based diagnostic strategies to assess neurosensory dysfunction, including clinical neurosensory testing, imaging, and adjunctive modalities. The course culminates in a stepwise, “first-contact-to-resolution” management algorithm. The luncheon will be held from 12:00 - 1:15 pm. AFTERNOON SESSIONS Contemporary Trigeminal Nerve Surgery: Modern Techniques and Case Review 1:15 - 2:15 pm Jeffrey Hajibandeh, DDS, MD At the conclusion of this course, attendees will understand indications for surgical versus non-surgical intervention for trigeminal nerve injuries, modern surgical techniques for repair of trigeminal nerves, case review of operative and non-operative c

Daniel Oreadi, DMD, FACS
Jeffrey Hajibandeh, DDS, MD, FACS

title: BORID Update 2026
description: This course will be presented by current Board members and the Board’s Executive Director will summarize the Board’s licensure requirements including licensure renewal and continuing education, the nature of complaints filed with the Board, and the “top ten” reasons licensees are sanctioned by the Board for regulatory and statutory violations. The course will also include a summary of “hot topics” being considered by the Board, the use of teledentistry, updates to 234 CMR and changes to the clinical competency exams required by the Board for initial licensure. A brief question and answer period will be included at the end of the course.
instructors:
Jacyn Stultz, MS, RDH
Sabina Malla, DDS
Thomas Trowbridge, DDS, MD
Michael Scialabba, DDS
Barbara Young, RDH, JD
requirements: Review BORID's Regulations at 234 Understand requirements for initial licensure as a dentist, dental hygienist, or dental assistant Explain requirements for licensure renewal, including continuing education and mandatory course work Determine requirements for clinical practice, i.e. infection control, spore testing, BLS certification, etc. List "top ten" complaints considered by BORID Discuss BORID "hot topics"

description: Designed by Specialists, Open to All TADs and MARPE: Orthodontic and Orthopedic Use of Skeletal Anchorage 8:30 - 10:15 am: TAD Success and the Power of Incremental Improvements 10:30 - 11:45 am: Cross-slot mechanics: Simplicity is the Ultimate Sophistication 1:45 - 3:00 pm: An Introduction to MARPE 3:15 - 5:00 pm: Implementing MARPE in Private Practice

title: Pediatric Specialty Group Program: Lecture for Dentist
description: Designed by Specialists, Open to All MORNING SESSION Medical Management of Dental Caries: Using a Chronic Disease Management Mindset 8:00 am - 12:30 pm Joel Berg, DDS This course will provide the attendee with the most up-to-date information regarding emerging technologies to manage dental caries in your practice, also in non-restorative ways. Specifically, the course will talk about the science behind MMC and how to approach dental caries management from a chronic disease management mindset. AFTERNOON SESSION Dental Protocols for Medically Compromised Children 2:00 - 5:00 pm Juan Yepes, DDS, MD This course offers attendees the latest insights into the evolving and dynamic field of oral medicine in pediatric dentistry. Through an interactive, case-based format, participants will explore a broad range of common medical conditions in children that have significant implications for dental care. Topics will include asthma, diabetes, congenital heart disease, autism, ADHD, and various syndromes, with a focus on evidence-based dental management strategies. Special emphasis will be placed on tailoring treatment protocols to ensure safe and effective care for medically complex pediatric patients.
instructors:
Juan Yepes, DDS, MD
Joel Berg, DDS
requirements: MORNING SESSION OBJECTIVES: Discover new caries lesion detection technologies and how they will detect caries lesions at an early stage Describe the science of dental caries from early lesions to large cavitated lesions Understand case selection for early vs. late caries lesion management Create and install a workflow for caries management in your practice AFTERNOON SESSION OBJECTIVES: Identify common systemic conditions in pediatric patients that influence dental care and treatment planning App

description: Are you a licensed dental assistant or interested in becoming licensed as a dental assistant? This course presented by current Board members and the Board’s Executive Director will summarize the licensure requirements for all dental assistants including licensure renewal and continuing education. The course will also include a summary of the Board’s regulations regarding the permissible scope of practice for licensed dental assistants. A brief question and answer period will be included at the end of the course.

title: Keratinized Tissue: What? Why? Where? When?
description: We will discuss the cosmetic, biological, and functional significance of keratinized tissue around dental implants and natural teeth. We will also discuss different techniques to restore keratinized tissue around implants and teeth. A video presentation will also be provided.
requirements: Assess periodontal versus peri-implant tissue in health and disease Compare keratinized tissue versus alveolar mucosa Identify different surgical techniques to restore keratinized tissue Understand autogenous soft tissue augmentation versus allograft and xenograft tissue grafts Examine outcomes of treatment and prognosis based on the health of soft tissues

title: Anterior Implant Placement: Handling Cosmetic Zone Surgical Challenges
description: In this course, we will discuss several surgical approaches in dealing with challenges we often encounter in anterior cosmetic region implant therapy. Implant treatments in the anterior region are generally more challenging than the treatment at the posterior region. It requires extensive experience and techniques to handle not only white (teeth) restoration, but also the pink (soft tissue) restoration. Some situations, such as severe atrophied alveolar ridge from either congenital reasons or severe periodontal disease, will make the cases even more difficult. With presentations of our real cases, we will discuss some surgical techniques to overcome these difficulties and achieve better clinical outcomes.
requirements: Understand unique features in cosmetic zone implant therapy Discuss challenges of implant placement in the anterior region Assess approaches on how to evaluate anterior implant cases Apply treatment planning protocols Identify how to implement bone grafting and soft tissue grafting Describe PET/socket shield technique Determine restorative options for the best outcomes

title: Surgical-Restorative Digital Workflow
description: Utilizing a “Surgical-restorative” digital workflow and treatment efficiency model will help provide more affordable care for patients. This course is to help dentist to minimize the unknown variables in the traditional implant therapy that often drive up costs and reduce efficiency. The “Surgical-restorative” digital workflow simplifies the treatment process, incorporates digital tools for greater accuracy and predictability, and helps the dentist become more efficient.
requirements: Discuss the "surgical-restorative" workflow concept Describe how "surgical-restorative" digital workflow can create a collaborative experience, cut down the chair time, and increase patient satisfaction Understand the use of digital technology in dentistry for dental implants and restoration Identify digital surgical-restorative workflow Review the true need for using digital technology Develop easy and cost-effective access for dentists

title: When an Infection Walks In Your Office (and Ruins Your Day!)
description: In this course, attendees will be presented with a brief history of infections, followed by a summary of orofacial infection workup, diagnosis and surgical management. Case studies will be presented, emphasizing the importance of the timeliness of care and proper airway management. Pertinent facial anatomy will be reviewed, as well as medical and surgical treatment. Proper radiographic workup will also be presented, including plain films and CT scan. Importance will be placed on prompt diagnosis and treatment.
requirements: Explain the importance of timely treatment of odontogenic infections Recognize the most common fascial spaces involved with odontogenic infections Understand what entails a proper workup for suspected infection List the most common radiographic studies used for proper infection diagnosis Compare and contrast the most common and effective antibiotics used for orofacial infections Describe the various surgical approaches to the facial skeleton when treating infections Review the decision tree for

title: Dental Management of Medically Compromised Patients
description: As our patient population ages, along with the complexities of managing caries, periodontal disease and prosthetic concerns, concomitant medical issues which can impact dental therapy need to be assessed. A team approach in identifying the patient’s medical considerations allows dental providers to tailor treatment plans that meet the patient’s specific needs. This program will discuss the importance of a thorough medical history and medical risk assessment. Consultations with the patient’s medical team to facilitate appropriate management of the patient’s medications will be reviewed. To facilitate a practical approach to medically compromised patient care, this program will focus on dental management relative to common systemic disorders such as diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease.
requirements: Obtain a thorough medical history and identify medical conditions Compare risk assessment of the patient’s medical condition relative to dental care Understand medication adjustments and modifications to procedures Identify esthetic considerations Examine diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease

title: Where Art Meets Technology: Digital Esthetics in Modern Dentistry
description: A beautiful smile is never an accident — it’s the result of artistic vision, clinical skill, and today, digital precision. In this lecture, I’ll take you behind the scenes of a few of my favorite esthetic cases, where technology became the paintbrush, but the art remained in the hands of the clinician. Together, we’ll explore how digital tools like AI and 3D design don’t replace creativity — they amplify it — helping us craft smiles that are not only functional, but full of personality, harmony, and emotion.
requirements: Identify key differences between lab-crafted mockups and digitally designed smiles Implement working faster and more clearly with lab technicians through digital communication Create Digital Smile Designs (DSD) for both clinical planning and patient Compare esthetic principles with technology to design smiles that feel natural Understand how to bring a full digital workflow in their practices without overcomplicating daily routines Improve patient trust and treatment through digital smile simula

title: Implant Maintenance: Parts, Chemistry, and Compatibility in Dental Hygiene
description: We are now 40 years since professor Brånemark introduced his research on osseointegration to the dental community. Dental Implants provide our patients with successful long-term restorative solutions for function, comfort, and aesthetics. Today, Dental Hygienists are seeing more patients with dental implants. Implant manufacturers have introduced new implant designs, implant surfaces, and surface chemistry that affect biocompatibility and maintenance protocols. Therefore, there is a concern if what we are using to reduce the peri-implant inflammation may negatively affect the biocompatibility of the implant surface and the implant’s long-term success. This class will review dental implant component materials and design and the biological compatibility of commonly used instruments, technology, and therapeutic materials.
requirements: Review the history of osseointegration and dental implant therapy Identify dental implant components for single-tooth and full-arch treatment concepts Implement maintenance protocols for the prevention of peri-implantitis Understand biologically safe instrument materials to use on dental implants Compare home care products and techniques safe for implant maintenance

title: “Oh No!” Managing and Preventing Prosthetic Complications
description: Prosthetic complications can occur at any time and place for new and existing patients. It is vital to know how to manage these complications when we see them. Even more critical for long-term success for our patients is our ability to predict future outcomes and complications for our patients prior to, during, and after treatment. In this course, a variety of fixed and removable prosthetic complications that may be commonly encountered will be discussed and how to manage and prevent them.
requirements: Identify common fixed and removable prosthetic complications seen in daily clinical practice Understand risk factors and considerations for prosthetic rehabilitation Examine dental material choices to prevent complications and increase predictability Incorporate emergency procedures that increase daily productivity Recognize the cost associated with prosthetic complications Develop protocols to manage and predict these complications

title: From Scan to Smile: Optimizing Workflow Pathways for Full-Arch Implants
description: In this course, attendees will explore the evolving landscape of workflow options for full-arch implant fixed prostheses at different stages of treatment-from initial diagnostics to final prosthesis delivery. We will compare analog, digital, and hybrid approaches, emphasizing clinical decision-making, lab coordination, and patient-centered outcomes. Attendees will gain practical insight into treatment planning and digital smile design, different conversion protocols for immediate loading, and workflow options for final prosthetic design and delivery. Real-world case examples and outcomes will be used to highlight where each approach excels, and where limitation arises. By the end of the course, clinicians will be equipped to evaluate and adapt their workflow based on their practice setup for predictability, efficiency, and patient satisfaction.
requirements: Compare analog, digital, and hybrid workflows for full-arch implant fixed protheses Identify key clinical and laboratory steps that influence treatment outcomes and efficiency Understand indications for guided vs. freehand surgical approaches within different workflows Utilize digital scanning and 3D printing technologies to enhance the accuracy and efficiency Recognize common complications and how workflow choices can prevent or contribute to them Examine practical criteria to select an appropr

title: “Sole (Practitioner)” Confessions of a Small-Town Dentist
description: This course will discuss current labor shortages affecting small dental practices. It will offer applicable clinical and business advice to reduce hardships being faced by small and start-up dental practices. The course will examine current labor trends within the dental field and cover policy contributions and continuous work by organized dentistry in the current business environment.
requirements: Discuss overall staffing and business models in the small practice field Understand the trends of the current labor shortage of auxiliary personnel Identify which tasks are allowed to be delegated and performed by auxiliary personnel Apply existing technology to improve workflow

title: Risk Management for the Entire Dental Team
description: This informative, interactive lecture is designed for the entire dental team. Risk management involves providing safe and effective dental care while minimizing potential liabilities. This course will focus on the dental practice as a whole and preventing those communication, informed consent, and documentation errors that often lead to malpractice claims.
requirements: Understand the importance of effective communication in every patient encounter Recognize the role of risk management for every member of the office Comprehend the significance of thorough documentation in preventing and defending against malpractice claims Master the basic principles of proper chart and record documentation, along with the responsibilities of safeguarding patient health information Implement effective, office-wide procedures to ensure patient safety Grasp the fundamentals of in

title: Safeguarding Your Practice: Avoiding and Defending Dental Malpractice Claims
description: Using a series of real case studies and also responding to questions and scenarios from the audience, the panel will discuss key patient risk management principles to avoid Board complaints and litigation.
instructors:
Alexander Terry, Esq
Kevin Reidy, Esq
Nipa Thakkar, DMD
Mr. Mark Robitaille
Katherine Panikian, Esq
requirements: Realize the role of patient selection in meeting the standard of care Learn techniques for resolving patient complaints early Discover effective behaviors for communicating with difficult patients Understand how patient noncompliance can lead to malpractice claims Recognize the importance of documentation in completing patient care and defending malpractice actions *Attendees will be eligible for 5% risk management discount on their professional liability premium with EDIC.

title: Advancing Oral Health Through Minimally Invasive Dentistry and Medicaid
description: This course explores how minimally invasive dentistry and disease management principles can be applied in dental practices that serve Medicaid populations. Rooted in prevention and early intervention, minimally invasive care focuses on preserving natural tooth structure, improving long-term oral health outcomes, and reducing the need for invasive restorative procedures. Medicaid dental programs promote oral health equity by providing essential dental services to low-income individuals and underserved communities, ensuring that everyone has access to quality oral health care regardless of socioeconomic status. Participants will learn how to integrate caries management techniques and patient education strategies into care models that align with Medicaid guidelines and goals. While this course will focus primarily on the Massachusetts Medicaid program (MassHealth), the concepts and strategies discussed are broadly applicable to Medicaid programs in other states. All are welcome to attend.
instructors:
Hong Tran Rajak, RDH
John Fraone, DMD, MS
Samantha Jordan, DMD, MPH
requirements: Describe the core principles of minimally invasive dentistry and a disease-management approach and their relevance to caring for patients with Medicaid coverage Gain an understanding of the key principles and goals of Medicaid dental programs in promoting oral health equity and addressing the needs of low-income and underserved populations Learn that Medicaid provides health coverage to millions of Americans, including eligible low-income adults, children, pregnant women, elderly adults and peop

title: Effective Scheduling in CHCs to Maximize Productivity
description: Join this course to explore strategies for effective scheduling in community health centers (CHCs) to maximize provider productivity and overall clinic efficiency while maintaining high-quality patient care. Attendees will learn how to align scheduling practices with clinical workflows, optimize appointments, and reduce no-show rates. Special emphasis will be placed on scheduling techniques that support provider efficiency by minimizing downtime, reducing administrative burdens, and balancing patient demand. Real-world examples and data-driven approaches will be shared to illustrate practical improvements that lead to sustainable operational success.
requirements: Identify common scheduling challenges in CHC settings and their impact on productivity Explore best practices for aligning appointment scheduling with provider and patient needs Apply strategies to improve schedule efficiency, reduce patient wait times, and enhance provider workflow

title: Inclusive Dental Care: Patients with Intellectual and Developmental Disabilities
description: This course is designed to help dental providers and their teams create a more inclusive, compassionate, and effective care environment for individuals with intellectual and developmental disabilities (IDD). Participants will explore the unique oral health needs and challenges faced by patients with IDD and gain practical strategies to improve comfort, communication, and overall care. This course features powerful insights from individuals with lived experience - including provider, patient, and caregiver perspectives - to highlight real-world barriers and solutions. Attendees will learn how to adapt clinical practices, use supportive tools and techniques, and foster strong relationships built on trust and understanding. All dental members are encouraged to attend and contribute to building a practice culture rooted in respect and inclusion.
instructors:
Samantha Jordan, DMD, MPH
Ms. Maura Sullivan
Darren Drag, DMD
requirements: Describe the benefits of providing care for patients with intellectual and developmental disabilities (IDD) Understand the challenges individuals with IDD face in accessing dental care by hearing firsthand perspectives Build trust, ease anxiety, and improve interactions with patients and caregivers using effective communication strategies Identify and implement practical modifications to the dental setting that enhance comfort and accessibility for individuals with IDD Gain confidence in using b

title: Humanizing Oral Healthcare in Community Health
description: In a collective partnership funded by a CareQuest Institute for Oral Health grant and convened by the MassLeague, the Caring Health Center’s Tania M. Barber Learning Institute designed and delivered a four-part seminar on humanizing and community-centered approaches to oral health care. The interactive, asynchronous seminar was developed by and for members of the community health center workforce. It engaged oral healthcare workforce from two MA FQHCs in a six week reflective learning experience that integrated critical analysis of theory and frameworks, community-based learning, and reflection of their experiences in dental practice as an opportunity for personal, professional and intellectual growth. This presentation will provide a snapshot of the 6-week seminar and will provide actionable tools for daily practice that incorporate the history and founding of community health centers (CHC), definitions of oral health equity and foundational frameworks of humanizing and community centered approaches in dental practice to improve the care of diverse patient populations and communities.
requirements: Learn how the community health center oral health workforce training was developed and implemented Analyze the importance of the realities and strategies for engaging in this form of praxis Define oral health, oral health disparities and foundational frameworks of humanizing and community centered approaches in dental practice. Provide tools to understand and incorporate into daily practice core tenets aimed at taking action in self and community care as a part of humanizing oral health and dent

title: Leadership Essentials for Office Managers and Team Leads
description: Strong leadership and management skills is essential for a high performance and high functioning dental practice. This highly interactive course will equip the office manager and team leads with the core skills and tools needed to lead confidently, communicate effectively and support team performance, practice productivity and improved patient experiences. Learn how to manage daily operations, resolve workplace challenges, track performance metrics and inspire a culture of accountability. Whether you're new to the role or looking to sharpen your leadership edge, this course will provide practices strategies you will use immediately.
requirements: Understand core responsibilities of office managers and team leads and how their roles impact practice performance Identify key performance indicators that drive operational success Utilize effective communication techniques to develop exceptional teams Develop strategies for overcoming workplace challenges Create a personal leadership action plan Discover how to coach team members and establish a conflict resolution plan Implement an effective growth conference protocol that provides clarity an

title: Introduction to Dental Assisting in Oral Surgery 101: Where to Start
description: This course introduces dental assistants to the essential skills and responsibilities of an oral and maxillofacial dental assistant. Attendees will learn how to prepare a sterile field, simulate a surgical scrub, and practice sterile gloving techniques. The course covers performing simulated suture removal. Additionally, attendees will define biopsy procedures, understand different types of biopsies, and practice completing a brush biopsy requisition form. Emphasis is placed on identifying surgical instruments used in basic oral surgical procedures as well as passing instruments to prepare for real-world surgical settings. Through hands-on practice and simulations, dental assistants will develop the necessary competencies for assisting in oral surgery.
instructors:
Tamar Iskenderian, CDA
Maria DiLuigi, CDA, RDH
requirements: Summarize the qualities of an oral and maxillofacial dental assistant Prepare a sterile field for instruments and supplies with simulating a surgical scrub and performing sterile gloving Discuss assisting with suture placement, identify types of techniques, and perform simulated suture removal Define biopsy and describe the different biopsy procedures Identify surgical instruments for basic surgical procedures

title: Level Up: Professional Growth and Power Moves for the Dental Assistant
description: This highly interactive course empowers dental assistants of all experience levels to take charge of their careers. Through storytelling, skits, group coaching, and activities, attendees will explore real-world strategies for career growth, certification opportunities, personal branding, networking, and becoming indispensable in the dental office. Whether you’re just starting out or leading the back office, you’ll leave inspired, informed, and equipped with a game plan to level up your professional journey.
requirements: Identify pathways for career advancement and professional recognition in dental assisting Evaluate certification and licensure options to support career mobility and leadership roles Apply personal branding techniques to stand out as a dental assistant in today’s workforce Demonstrate effective communication and networking skills for interviews, salary negotiation, and professional events Develop a personalized career action plan and vision board to map future growth

title: Empowering Teams: How Office Managers Guide Team Success
description: In this course, attendees will learn how to guide, support and elevate your team to reach new levels of performance and cohesion. Attendees will explore actionable leadership strategies including how to set clear expectations, hold effective one-on-one evaluations, communicate with purpose and overcome conflict and objections. This course will provide tools to create a culture of accountability, collaboration, and continuous growth - empowering managers to lead with confidence and drive meaningful team success.
requirements: Discover how to facilitate a productive team meeting that generates action Establish communication tools to reduce confusion and improve performance Utilize coaching skills to overcome resistance to change Identify three key leadership behaviors to influence a positive and productive team culture Recognize early signs of disengagement or conflict and learn proactive strategies to address Create team goals and accountability measurements

title: Challenging the Falsehoods of Fluoride
description: As healthcare professionals, optimal community water fluoridation is probably something we have believed in most of our lives. Having something so accepted, suddenly challenged, can put us at a terrible disadvantage and leave us wondering. We think we know it’s a common good, but we forget why. This course will give you a foundation on which to build so that you can inform your patients of the current correct science and prepare you with strategies should this become an issue in your local community at large.
requirements: Explain the history of water fluoridation Identify and expose confirmation bias Refute the misinformation concerning optimal community water fluoridation Engage counter opposition to optimal community water fluoridation Participate in promoting optimal fluoridation strategies as healthcare professionals Provide care strategies for all patients based on the highest levels of evidence Understand remineralization and carious lesions

title: Lead Like a Pro: Real-World Leadership for Dental Assistants
description: This course is designed for dental assistants at all levels, from new graduates to the seasoned professional. Packed with storytelling, humor, interactive activities, and real-life scenarios, this session will help attendees develop confidence, communication skills, and problem­ solving strategies needed to step up and stand out in the dental office. Whether leading a team or just getting started, attendees will leave this course with tools to influence the practice’s success and inspire others-without needing a fancy title.
requirements: Identify key leadership traits Explain how dental assistants can demonstrate leadership without a formal title Develop effective communication skills Utilize conflict resolution strategies in common workplace scenarios Demonstrate practical team leadership skills such as delegation, motivation, and dentist support Apply techniques to positively influence practice success

description: This course introduces dental assistants to the essential skills and responsibilities of an oral and maxillofacial dental assistant. Attendees will learn how to prepare a sterile field, simulate a surgical scrub, and practice sterile gloving techniques. The course covers performing simulated suture removal. Emphasis is placed on identifying surgical instruments used in basic oral surgical procedures as well as passing instruments to prepare for real-world surgical settings. title: The Role of CBCT in the Modern Dental Practice
description: In this course, the instructor will highlight the indication of cone beam CT and guide how to prescribe imaging based on the patient's needs. The critical role of imaging in diagnosis and treatment planning will be emphasized. The impact of medical and dental history on diagnosis will also be discussed.
requirements: Understand the capabilities of two-and-three dimensional imaging modalities Identify the indications, advantages, and limitations of CBCT Discuss the impact of clinical history on patient care Refine their approach toward radiographic interpretation with three dimensional imaging Recognize the impact of three-dimensional imaging with CBCT

title: AI-Driven Dentistry: The New Tools Shaping the Future of Care
description: At present, AI is already being used in dental practices. AI helps read 2-D dental images and AI is used by insurance companies to determine whether to pay on dental care submitted on claims. AI also helps dentists design cases. The only limitation presently on AI is our imagination. This course will explore what AI really means. We will explore how AI is being used today. Finally, we will discuss how AI will affect us in the future, including in the 3-D world. Participants will have a better idea how AI will affect their future practices and when to engage with AI. Join us for an unbiased overview of what dentists need to know about the applications of artificial intelligence in dentistry.
instructors:
Maxine Feinberg, DDS
Richard Huot, DDS
requirements: Compare AI tools that are currently available Recognize the impact AI tools have on clinical care and practice management Understand the 2D and 3D diagnostic capabilities of AI Identify the strengths and weaknesses of AI systems Discuss the future capabilities of a fully integrated AI system in your office Handouts are available for this course: Download Now

Attendees will expose and evaluate the radiographs on manikins. They will also mount conventional dental x-ray film and digital images on the computer. They will be able to self-analyze and rectify radiographic technical and processing errors. The instructor will provide a review of dental occlusal, panoramic, and cephalometric radiographs. title: The Power of Non-surgical Periodontal Therapy
description: This course will highlight the current concepts and techniques for providing effective non-surgical management of periodontal diseases in both a lecture and hands-on format. The etiology of periodontal diseases along with skills to help motivate patients to control dental plaque will be briefly reviewed with an understanding that periodontal-peri-implant diseases are systemic. Diagnostic techniques for accurate probing measurements and calculus detection will be discussed coupled with the hands-on use of a novel dental model with a stimulated attachment apparatus. The rationale of periodontal hand instrumentation will be presented with hands-on experience using a universal curette and Gracey curettes. Hands-on experience in instrument sharpening using diamond sharpening cards will be provided. The theory and use of magnetostrictive and piezoelectric ultrasonics will be presented with hands-on exercises using a variety of tips. The emerging role and use of air polishing with the latest agents and supragingival and subgingival tips will be presented along with the opportunity to use air polishing. The effective sequencing of appointments for initial periodontal therapy and the development of an effective maintenance program will be discussed.
instructors:
Patricia Cohen, RDH
Joseph Montesano, DMD
Mr. Kyle Wright
Ronald Fried, DMD, MSc
Daniel Coleman, DMD, MS
Paul Levi, DMD
requirements: Understand the etiology of periodontal diseases Improve accuracy in periodontal pocket measurement Utilize skills in calculus detection and hand instrument sharpening Use hand and power instruments to enhance skills in scaling and root planning Develop a protocol for comprehensive Phase I non-surgical periodontal therapy and for creating an effective maintenance program Handouts are available for this course: Download Now

title: Understanding the Dental Insurance Landscape: Maximize Your Success
description: What drives insurance companies to design programs that seem so mysterious to dental offices? Let's close the disconnect between the way dental offices think and the way insurance/benefit companies set up their dental benefit plans. In this course attendees will gain the skills to be able to think like the insurance companies and help streamline your daily operations and package your submissions to produce less frustration and increased success.
requirements: Understand what drives insurance company policies Discuss why insurance plans don't make sense Discover how offices can change their thinking to be more in line with how insurance companies operate Identify how to streamline your operations for better billing success Use actual insurance processing guidelines to look at common billing scenarios Handouts are available for this course: Download Now

title: Keeping Up with Recent Dental Code Changes
description: Dental procedure codes change every year. Many practices miss billing and reporting opportunities to describe properly the services they are providing. In this course, the instructor will discuss new and recent codes, how to use them properly in the practice for improved claims success and which codes are being scrutinized and why. Peek into common insurance company policies and why certain codes have increased scrutiny. Understand utilization management basics. Eliminate common code mistakes that interfere with being paid. This course is great for the entire team to understand how best to report and get paid for what they do.
requirements: Examine the dental coding system and discuss how to use the various coding guides Compare new codes from the last several years and those that have been expired or deleted Discuss which codes are being scrutinized by payers and why and how to manage them Understand how utilization management is affecting the dental benefits industry Identify how to avoid common coding mistakes, how to report the services provided, and how to enhance payment for services rendered Handouts are available for this c

title: HPV: Not the Hanky Panky Virus the Media Portrays It to Be
description: New evidence has linked the presence of HPV in an oral mucosal infection to the increased risk of oral and oral pharyngeal cancer. It has been a challenge in the past to identify those individuals with these risk factors until now. In this course, attendees will learn about the SOSA™ Screening Protocol, the Screening Oral Systemic Abnormalities™ Exam, a proprietary clinical screening protocol developed by the instructor. Attendees will discover how to optimize their verbal skills and tactile skills to co-diagnose and educate the patient about self-examination techniques without invoking the fear of cancer. We will review the importance of consistently conducting an effective oral abnormality visual and tactile "lump, bump & bulge” screening exams as well as distinguishing between adjunctive fluorescent screening technologies. The instructor will also share her referral protocol to enhance collaboration with specialists. New information will be highlighted as to why oral systemic health and wellness are key to oxidizing viral infections. Finally, attendees will review a check list of potential products to dispense to patients based on their individual conditions and why it changes throughout treatment and recovery.
requirements: Confidently discuss the necessity of utilizing emerging technology to screen patients preventively vs. finding late-stage oral cancer Recognize changes in the patient's mouth without dismissing a single small spot or watching what happens in between appointments Provide comfort for patients with cancer therapy and support their journey as an advocate and a friend

title: The ABCs of CBD: What You Need to Know as a Healthcare Provider
description: CBD, or cannabidiol, is the most prominent naturally occurring cannabinoid component found in cannabis, constituting up to 40% of the plant. CBD uses range from inflammatory disease treatment, dietary support, cancer, ADD, ADHD as well as pet health. With so many varieties, do you know how it will affect your patients during dental treatment, and can it assist long term? This course will empower you! In today’s dental environment, we must be creative in having our patients disclose their complete medical history without fear of scrutiny and judgement. Through this course the attendees will learn pharmacokinetics, mechanisms of action and current legal regulations surrounding CBD usage for management of dental related conditions like TMJ, oral pain, and anxiety. The instructor will deliver the key points of HEMP CBD, its components, its delivery systems and many beneficial uses. Upon completion, dental professionals will be able to effectively counsel patients on the risks, benefits, contraindications and appropriate use of CBD products as part of a comprehensive, patient-centered care plan.
requirements: Understand the pharmacology and legal status of CBD Evaluate the evidence based therapeutic application of CBD in dentistry Develop patient communication and integration strategies for CBD usage in the modern dental practice

title: Ethical Considerations While Addressing Oral Health Throughout the Life Spectrum
description: In this course attendees will learn about the unique needs and concerns that routinely arise when treating pediatric and geriatric patients in a variety of practice settings. They will also learn how to manage ethical and legal concerns by using expert opinions, techniques and existing data to avoid common errors in practice and focus on enhancing patient care.
instructors:
Talia Miller, DMD
Catherine Sarkis, JD
Kadambari Rawal, BDS, MScD
requirements: Navigate intricate ethical issues when treating the most vulnerable patients Understand the nuances of patient communication and ethical problem solving Discover various legal considerations tied to geriatric and pediatric oral health and well being Know how to prioritize patient welfare for children and older adults Evaluate concepts surrounding abuse and neglect for these patient populations Recognize your reporting obligations as a healthcare provider Presented by the American College of Dent

title: Preventive Equipment Maintenance
description: Take care of your practice by learning how to take care of your equipment. Local full-service dealers, along with their service technicians and representatives, will cover basic preventive maintenance and safety requirements for office equipment. Benco Dental, Henry Schein Dental, and Patterson Dental will be presenting this course.

Mr. Spencer Smith
Mr. Scott Hicks
Mr. Joe Mugford
Mr. Peter Furey
requirements: Review methods to properly maintain sterilizers and perform spore testing Understand how to properly maintain dental unit waterlines, dental air compressors, and suction systems Identify techniques to keep suction lines and cuspidor drain lines clean Comply with safety requirements for dental equipment Thank you to Benco Dental, Henry Schein Dental, and Patterson Dental for their collaboration on this program.

title: HIV and Oral Health: What Every Dental Professional Needs to Know
description: ADHA MA LECTURE & LUNCHEON HIV and Oral Health: What Every Dental Professional Needs to Know This course provides dental professionals with essential knowledge about HIV and its implications in dental care. Attendees will explore the basics of HIV transmission, oral manifestations of the disease, and evidence-based infection control practices to ensure the safety of patients and providers. The course emphasizes practical strategies for managing the oral health needs of people living with HIV and dispelling common myths to reduce stigma in clinical settings.
requirements: Explain the basics of HIV/AIDS, including modes of transmission and the impact of antiretroviral therapy (ART) on disease progression Identify oral manifestations of HIV and describe their clinical significance, diagnosis, and management Apply CDC-recommended standard precautions and best practices for infection control to minimize risk in dental settings Assess risk and respond appropriately to occupational exposures, such as needlestick injuries, including the use of post-exposure prophylaxis

title: Work-Life Harmony: Balance, Success, and Fulfillment
description: Achieving true work-life balance starts with mindset—and it starts at home. This course will reveal how mindset-driven strategies help you reduce stress, boost productivity, and create lasting harmony between work and family life. By reprogramming your brain’s algorithms, you’ll lead by example, inspiring those around you—at home and in the workplace—to adopt the same mindset shifts. Empowering family members and coworkers to achieve the same fulfillment, results, and smiles. Create a ripple effect that spreads well-being across every area of life.
requirements: Shift your mindset for lasting balance Transform your thinking to create sustainable harmony between work and family life Lead with positivity and inspire change Set an example that motivates family members and coworkers to adopt practices that promote balance and success Create a contagious ripple of positivity Empower others to embrace these practices, spreading positivity, productivity, and happiness throughout your personal and professional circles

title: Orofacial Myofunctional Therapy: Beneficial for More Than “Just” Tongue Thrust
description: This course is for dental hygienists and dentists who wish to expand their understanding of Orofacial Myofunctional Therapy (OMT) and what can be achieved with OMT. Understanding how OMDs (Orofacial Myofunctional Disorders) can affect facial growth and appearance, mouth breathing as well as Sleep Disordered Breathing (snoring, mild to moderate Obstructive Sleep Apnea etc. Attendees will also receive hands-on learning regarding the location of the major craniofacial muscles and their function. A couple of very basic exercises will be included with the understanding that they are just examples and not meant to be utilized without further training/certification.
requirements: Review how and why to screen for Orofacial Myofunctional Disorders (OMT) Perform an assessment and evaluation of airways and tongue ties Discuss goals of Orofacial Myofunctional Therapy Utilize skull models to locate the major craniofacial muscles Evaluate lip posture, tongue posture, mentalis strain, and discuss why they are important Demonstrate basic OMT exercises

title: The Sales Mindset: Elevating Performance, Morale, and Results
description: Sales success is deeply rooted in mindset. This course will explore how cultivating a powerful, positive mindset can transform not just individual performance but also team morale and client relationships. By shifting how employees view themselves, their colleagues, and their clients, they’ll boost their confidence, engagement, and outcomes. Learn how adopting a success-driven mindset fosters a high-performing environment where morale thrives, relationships strengthen, and sales soar—leading to long-term results and business growth.
requirements: Reprogram your brain for resilience Shift your mental algorithms to quickly bounce back from rejection, bad days, or difficult interactions, and maintain focus on your goals Boost team morale by leading with positivity Become a positive influence within your team, using mindset techniques that create lasting motivation and collaboration Cultivate lasting results with daily practices Gain the tools to consistently apply mindset exercises and brain shifts, ensuring continued success and high perfo

description: This course is for dental healthcare providers and recommended for all office staff. The presentation encompasses guidelines from OSHA, ADA, the CDC on Workplace Safety, Infection Control, Blood and Aerosol Borne Pathogens and Barrier Precautions. Discussion includes written policies and protocols regarding On-Site Inspection Compliance for Massachusetts dental practices.

title: Dental Care for Young Children: Expand Your Practice and Enhance Your Revenue
description: This course will provide dentists (or dental practices) with the knowledge, skills and confidence to expand their practices to include children three years old and younger. In this course, the instructors will discuss rationale and strategies for general dentists (or dental practices) to meet the American Academy of Pediatric Dentistry and the American Association of Pediatrics guidelines regarding routine dental care for young children, how to seamlessly integrate the age one dental visit into your practice, when and how to use minimally invasive dentistry techniques, and practical behavior management techniques.

Michelle Henshaw, DDS
Keri Discepolo, DDS, MPH
Catherine Hayes, DMD

title: BLS CPR for Health Care Providers: Thursday PM – In-Person Program
description: This American Heart Association (AHA) Basic Life Support CPR for Healthcare Providers renewal program reviews cardiopulmonary resuscitation, AED use, and choking emergencies in adults, children, and infants. This course includes a required written exam and skills assessment. Because this is a traditional lecture-style class lasting three (3) hours, there is no online component. IMPORTANT: AHA CPR certificates are now issued as eCards; they will be emailed to you following successful completion of CPR training. Would you like to own a copy of the BLS CPR manual? Available for purchase at: https://shopcpr.heart.org/bls-provider-manual
requirements: Identify and learn emergency treatment for cardiac arrest in adults, children, and infants Operate an automated external defibrillator (AED) unit Identify and manage choking incidents for adults, children, and infants

title: Basic Orthodontic Assisting
description: This course is directed at the already experienced dental assistant who has had little or no exposure to orthodontic assisting. Attendees will receive hands-on experience with the most up to date techniques of both clear aligner therapy and conventional braces. Techniques will include the following: digital scanning of typodonts, placement of clear aligner buttons, conventional bracket and band placement, archwire placement, and placement of additional orthodontic appliances such as Hawley, Hyrax etc.

description: Participants will compare the clinical limitations of traditional oral antimicrobials with evidence-based botanical alternatives. The presentation highlights essential oils and herbs that support tissue healing and patient comfort. Current trends, research, and practical applications of natural antimicrobial agents will be explored.
